content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγος_ἀνθρώπων_1">, as Archil. fr. 86 <quote xml:lang="grc">α<*>νός τις ἀνθρώπων ὅδε</quote>: <ref n="Pind. O. 7">Pind. <title>O.</title>7. 54</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνθρώπων παλαιαὶ ῥήσιες</quote>, id. <ref n="Pind. N. 9">Pind. <title>N.</title>9. 6</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔστι δέ τις λόγος ἀνθρώπων</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 664">Soph. <title>Ai.</title>664</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ βροτῶν παροιμία. <emph>ἀρχαῖος</emph> </quote> goes adverbially with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φανείς_1">, ‘put forth of old’; cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 593">Soph. <title>Ant.</title>593</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρχαῖα</quote>... | ...<quote xml:lang="grc"> πίπτοντ’</quote>: and <emph>ib.</emph> 621 <quote xml:lang="grc">σοφίᾳ γὰρ ἔκ του κλεινὸν ἔπος πέφανται</quote> (n.). L's accentuation, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔστ_1">, is right: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστὶ φανείς</quote> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">πέφανται</quote> would be weak here. For the order of the words (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνθρώπων</quote> separating <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρχαῖος</quote> from <quote xml:lang="grc">φανείς</quote>), cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 944">Soph. <title> Ant.</title>944</ref> f. As to the <quote xml:lang="grc">γνώμη</quote> itself, see <ref n="Soph. OT 1529">Soph. <title>O. T.</title>1529</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θάνῃ_3">. The <emph>v. l.</emph> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θάνοι_3"> would be possible only if <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote> were absent. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 164">Cp. 164</ref> cr. n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτ_τῳ_3">: for <quote xml:lang="grc">τῳ</quote> in the second clause, cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 257"> <title>Ant.</title>257</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγὼ_ἐμόν_4"> <quote xml:lang="grc"> κ.τ.λ.</quote>: for the tribrach, cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1232"> <title>Ph.</title>1232</ref> n.—She can dispute the old saying, because she forebodes that her life will be bitter to the end. The pathos here depends less on retrospect than on presentiment: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 37">cp. 37</ref>, 46.— This passage illustrates Aristotle's remark that a person who speaks with strong feeling (<quote xml:lang="grc">παθητικῶς</quote>) may effectively impugn the truth of popular maxims (<quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ δεδημοσιευμένα</quote>): <ref n="Aristot. Rh. 2.21"> <title>Rhet.</title>2. 21</ref>§ 13. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατρὸς_μὲν_6"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> No <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote> answers to this <quote xml:lang="grc">μέν</quote>. The antithesis is between her woes before and after marriage; of the latter she begins to speak at v. 27. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ναίουσ_Πλευρῶνι_7">. This insertion of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔτ_7"> is the best remedy. The word is forcible, as marking that her sorrows began while she was still a young maiden. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 23"> <title>Ph.</title>23</ref>, where, as here, the text of L has lost <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔτ’</quote> before a word beginning with <quote xml:lang="grc">ε</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλ_7"> at the beginning of the next word; though such lengthening would have been legitimate in the epic hexameter. Cp. W. Christ, <title>Metrik</title> § 18 (2nd ed.).—Paley reads </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ναίουσα_δ_7"> (with B): but the <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote> would be weak here.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Πλευρῶνι_7">. The ancient Pleuron stood in a fertile plain of Aetolia, near the mountain called <quote xml:lang="grc"> Κούριον</quote>, a few miles W.N.W. of Calydon. About 230 B.C. that site was deserted, and a new Pleuron was founded more to the S.W., not far from the modern Mesolonghi. (<ref n="Strab. 10.451">Strabo 10. 451</ref> : <ref>Leake, <title>North. Gr.</title> I. 115 ff.</ref>) In the <title> Iliad</title> Pleuron figures among the chief Aetolian towns (2. 639, with four others: 13. 217, with Calydon only).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄκνον_7">. The <emph>v. l.</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄτλον_7"> (‘trouble,’ rt <quote xml:lang="grc"> ταλ</quote>) is a less fitting word here: the point is the anguish of her dreadful <emph>suspense</emph> (15, 24). Though <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀτλεῖν</quote> is not rare, the noun occurs only in <ref n="Aesch. Seven 18">Aesch. <title>Th.</title>18</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">παιδείας ὄτλον</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λγιστον_8">. The positive would be more usual, since </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴ_τις_8"> follows; but the superl. is not redundant, if taken as absolute (‘very grievous’), and not relative (‘<emph>the most</emph> grievous’). Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1006"> <title>O. C.</title>1006</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ τις γῆ θεοὺς ἐπίσταται</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τιμαῖς σεβίζειν, ἥδε τῷδ’ ὑπερφέρει</quote>: and <ref n="Eur. Andr. 6">Eur. <title>Andr.</title>6</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν δ’, εἴ τις ἄλλη, δυστυχεστάτη γυνή</quote>. Soph. has <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴπερ τις ἄλλος</quote> in <ref n="Soph. OT 1118">Soph. <title>O. T.</title>1118</ref>, but more often <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴπερ τις</quote> simply (as <ref n="Soph. OC 1664">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 1664</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 488">Soph. <title>Ai.</title>488</ref>); and so <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 934.">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title>934.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔσχον_8">, not </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἶχον_8">, because she thinks of the ordeal, not as a process, but as a past moment of life; cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 225"> <title>Ant.</title>225</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὰς γὰρ ἔσχον φροντίδων ἐπιστάσεις</quote>. This is better than to give <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔσχον</quote> its commoner sense, ‘<emph>came to</emph> have’ ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1229"> <title>Ant.</title>1229</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1420"> <title>Ph.</title>1420</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μνηστὴρ_9">: this legend had already been treated by Archilochus (<emph>c.</emph> 670 B.C.), and by Pindar: see Introd.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἀχελῷον_9">. The Acheloüs rises at the centre of Pindus, in Mount Lacmon, the great watershed of northern Greece, and, after a course of some 130 miles from N. to S. , flows into the Ionian Sea. Its lower waters formed the boundary between Acarnania on the west and Aetolia on the east. The modern name, ‘White River’ (Aspropotamo), is due to the yellowish colour which the stream derives from a clayed bed.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_μορφαῖσιν_10">. The power of self-transformation, which Greek fancy gave especially to deities of water, was a lively symbol of the unstable element. Proteus exerts that power against Menelaus ( <ref n="Hom. Od. 4.456"> <title>Od.</title>4. 456</ref>), Nereus against Heracles ( <ref n="Apollod. 2.5.11">Apollod.2. 5. 11</ref>), Thetis against Peleus (schol. <ref n="Pind. N. 3">Pind. <title>N.</title>3. 55</ref>, Soph. fr. 155 and 556). Each is desperate, and must try every resource. And so, here, self-change expresses passionate importunity. Mythology found a reflex in daily speech when Greeks said, <quote xml:lang="grc">παντοῖος γίγνεται δεόμενος</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐναργὴς_11">, in visible form, before the eyes of Oeneus: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 224">cp. 224</ref>. The word suggests that sense of awe which came to a Greek at the thought of a <quote xml:lang="grc">δαίμων</quote> actually appearing to a mortal: <ref n="Hom. Il. 20.131"> <title>Il.</title>20. 131</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χαλεποὶ δὲ θεοὶ φαίνεσθαι ἐναργεῖς</quote>: ‘'tis perilous when a god is seen face to face.’ <ref n="Hom. Od. 16.161"> <title>Od.</title>16. 161</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γάρ πω πάντεσσι θεοὶ φαίνονται ἐναργεῖς</quote>: <ref n="Hom. Od. 3.420">ib. 3. 420</ref> (Athena) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥ μοι ἐναργὴς ἦλθε</quote>. <cit> <bibl n="Verg. A. 4.358">Verg. <title>Aen.</title> 4. 358</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">ipse deum manifesto in lumine vidi.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote: ipse deum manifesto in lumine vidi.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταῦρος_11">. This regular embodiment of a river-god symbolised both the roar of the torrent, and, as Strabo adds, the twistings of the stream (<quote xml:lang="grc">καμπαί</quote>), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἃς καλοῦσι κέρατα</quote> <ref n="Strab. 10.458">(10. 458)</ref>. Coins of Acarnania (after 300 B.C.) show Acheloüs as a bull with human head; and Soph. may have had this type in mind, for it appears on coins of Magna Graecia as early as 500 B.C.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰόλος_ἑλικτός_11">. The image is peculiarly appropriate, since the Acheloüs, in parts of its course, is so tortuous. For <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰόλος</quote>, ‘gleaming,’ cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1157."> <title>Ph.</title>1157.</ref>A vase-painting shows the Acheloüs, in combat with Heracles, as a serpent with the head and arms of a man, and an ox's horns (Gerhard, <title>Auserl. Vasenbilder</title>, vol. 2, no. 115).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνδρείῳ_βούπρῳρος_11"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> A human figure, with human face, and a shaggy beard, but with the forehead, horns, and ears of an ox. The Acheloüs appears thus on an archaic coin of Meta pontum in Lucania (Millingen, <title>Anc. Coins of Greek Cities and Kings</title>, pl. 1, no. 21). The words <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ἐκ δὲ δασκίου γενειάδος</emph>, κ.τ.λ.</quote>, coupled with such evidence, make it clear that </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βούπρῳρος_11"> means, ‘with <emph>front</emph>’ (not, ‘head’) of ox. In this sense, it is fitter than </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βούκρανος_11">: and Strabo 's reading (cr. n.) is thus confirmed.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κύτει_11">. The word <quote xml:lang="grc"> κύτος</quote> (rt <quote xml:lang="grc">κυ</quote>) means ‘a cavity,’ then ‘a vessel’: hence, fig., the human body as encasing the vital organs: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Tim. 74a">Plat. <title>Tim.</title> 74 A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπὸ τῆς κεφαλῆς διὰ παντὸς τοῦ κύτους</quote> </cit>. See Appendix. </gloss>

data.quote: ἀπὸ τῆς κεφαλῆς διὰ παντὸς τοῦ κύτους

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διερραίνοντο_14">, ‘were sprinkled abroad’: a word applied by Arist. to the ‘diffusion’ of fire by rapid movement, <title> Meteor.</title> I. 3 (p. 341 <emph>a</emph> 30) <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ...πῦρ...διαρραίνεσθαιτῇ κινήσει.—<emph>κρηναίου ποτοῦ</emph> </quote>, the water as it flowed from the <quote xml:lang="grc">κρήνη</quote>, fount, of the river. This phrase recurs in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 21"> <title>Ph.</title>21</ref>, and (plur.) in fr. 559. Hesiod <title> Theog.</title> 340 calls the Acheloüs <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀργυροδίνην</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσδεδεγμένη_15">, ‘expecting’ such a suitor; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, looking forward to his becoming her husband (525). Cp. the usage of the epic aor. partic., <ref n="Hom. Il. 10.123"> <title>Il.</title>10. 123</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμὴν ποτιδέγμενος ὁρμήν</quote>. The normal Attic sense, ‘having received,’ is inadmissible. She could not yet be doomed to the visits of a wooer who had not even gained her father's consent. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆσδε_κοίτης_17">. Though the compound <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμπελάζειν</quote> elsewhere (as in 748) takes a dat., it can also take a gen., like the simple verb ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1327"> <title>Ph.</title>1327</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πελασθεὶς φύλακος</quote>). So a gen., instead of the more usual dat., stands with <quote xml:lang="grc">συντυχών</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 320"> <title>Ph.</title>320</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐντυχών</quote> (<emph>ib.</emph> 1333), <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπαντήσας</quote> (<emph> ib.</emph> 719). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέν_δέ_18">: not, indeed, soon enough to prevent the anguish of which she has spoken (v. 16), yet to her joy.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀσμένῃ_μοι_18">: <ref n="Soph. OT 1356"> <title>O. T.</title>1356</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκλύεται_21">, here simply=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκλύει</quote> (cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1112"> <title>Ant.</title>1112</ref> n., and <ref n="Soph. OT 1003"> <title>O. T.</title>1003</ref>), rather than ‘delivers for himself,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, to be his bride.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διείποιμ_21">, tell clearly: <ref n="Soph. OT 854"> <title>O. T.</title>854</ref> n. The place of the first </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἂν_21"> serves to emphasise </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τρόπον_21"> ( <ref n="Soph. OT 339"> <title>O. T.</title>339</ref> n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόνων_21">, of warfare, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 248"> <title>Ph.</title>248</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆς_θέας_23">: for the gen., cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 885"> <title>O. T.</title>885</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Δίκας ἀφόβητος.—<emph> ὅδ’</emph> </quote>, after </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅστις_23">, as in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 463"> <title>Ant.</title>463</ref> f. The drawback to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_δ_23"> here is that it would be unduly emphatic: see <ref n="Soph. Phil. 87"> <title>Ph.</title>87</ref>, Appendix.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξεύροι_23">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 287"> <title>Ph.</title>287</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γαστρὶ μὲν τὰ σύμφορα</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τόξον τόδ’ ἐξηύρισκε</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ζεὺς_ἀγώνιος_26">, the supreme arbiter in all trials of strength,—as at Argos he was <quote xml:lang="grc">σθένιος</quote> (<ref n="Paus. 2.32.7">Paus. 2. 32 § 7</ref>). So Hermes is <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγώνιος</quote> ( <ref n="Pind. I. 1">Pind. <title> I.</title>1. 60</ref> etc.), as patron of the palaestra. The <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγώνιοι θεοί</quote> of <ref n="Aesch. Supp. 189"> Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title> 189</ref>, besides these two, are Apollo and Poseidon,—who presided respectively over the Pythian and Isthmian <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγῶνες</quote>, as Zeus over the Olympian and Nemean: see <ref n="Aesch. Supp. 182"> <emph>ib.</emph> 182—194</ref>, and Prof. Tucker's note on v. 163 (=189 Dind.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_δὴ_26"> is sceptical, as that of <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴπερ</quote> is usu. confident: cp. <ref n="Eur. Orest. 17">Eur. <title>Or.</title>17</ref>(quoted by Schneidewin), <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ κλεινός, εἰ δὴ κλεινός, Ἀγαμέμνων</quote>. The pause after the second foot suits the pensive stress on <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δὴ καλῶς</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 658">Soph. <title>Ant.</title>658</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ κτενῶ. πρὸς ταῦτ’ ἐφυμνείτω Δία</quote>, etc.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέχος_26">, nom., in the sense of ‘bride’ (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 360">cp. 360</ref>, and <ref n="Soph. Aj. 211"> <title>Ai.</title>211</ref>). The accus. in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 491"> <title>Ai.</title>491</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ σὸν λέχος ξυνῆλθον</quote> (‘came into thy bed’), is warranted by the verb of motion, as in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 817">Soph. <title>Ph.</title>817</ref> , <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ δὲ σύναιμον λέχος ἦλθεν</quote>. But <quote xml:lang="grc">λέχος ξυστᾶσά τινι</quote> could not well mean ‘joined to him in marriage’ (as though <quote xml:lang="grc">λέχος</quote> were a kind of cognate acc.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κριτὸν_26">, chosen by himself (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 245">cp. 245</ref>), is also best suited to <quote xml:lang="grc">λέχος</quote> as= ‘bride.’ For <quote xml:lang="grc"> ξυστᾶσ’</quote> cp. <cit> <bibl n="Isoc. L. 4.8">Isocr. <title>Ep.</title> 4 § 8,</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπειδὴ ξυνέστηκέ μοι</quote> </cit> (‘since he has been associated with me’). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προκηραίνουσα_29"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">κῆρ</quote>), feeling anxiety about him, <quote xml:lang="grc">μεριμνῶσα κατὰ τὸ κέαρ</quote> (schol.). The compound occurs only here: Eur. has the simple <quote xml:lang="grc">κηραίνω</quote> as = ‘to be anxious’ ( <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 223">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title>223</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Her. 518">Eur. <title>H. F.</title>518</ref>). Distinguish the other <quote xml:lang="grc">κηραίνω</quote>, from <quote xml:lang="grc">κήρ</quote>, ‘to harm’ ( <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 128"> Aesch. <title>Eum.</title>128</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">δρακαίνης ἐξεκήραναν μένος</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νὺξ_εἰσάγει_29"> <foreign xml:lang="grc"> κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: ‘for one night ushers in a trouble, and another, in succession (to the former night), expels it,’ —to make room for some fresh anxiety. This is a poetical amplification of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀεί τιν’ ἐκ φόβου φόβον τρέφω</quote>. Each night torments her, as she lies awake, with some new surmise as to her husband's fate.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόνον_29"> is governed by both verbs.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διαδεδεγμένη_29"> is used absolutely: its object, if expressed, would have been <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν προτέραν νύκτα</quote>: cp. <ref n="Hdt. 8. 142">Her.8. 142</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς δὲ ἐπαύσατο λέγων Ἀλέξανδρος, δια δεξάμενοι</quote> (‘in their turn’) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔλεγον οἱ ἀπὸ Σπάρτης ἄγγελοι</quote>. Thus <quote xml:lang="grc">διαδεδεγμένη</quote> serves at once (<emph>a</emph>) to show that the words <quote xml:lang="grc">νὺξ εἰσάγει καὶ νὺξ ἀπωθεῖ</quote> refer to different nights; and (<emph>b</emph>) to suggest the new <quote xml:lang="grc">πόνος</quote>—not expressly mentioned—which the second night brings; since the task in which it is <quote xml:lang="grc"> διάδοχος</quote> to the first is that of harassing the sufferer's mind. See Appendix. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὓς_ποτε_31"> <quote xml:lang="grc"> κ.τ.λ.</quote> The point of the comparison, which has been prompted by the word <quote xml:lang="grc">σπείρων</quote>, is merely the rarity of the visits. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποτέ_31">=‘at some time or other’ (cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀψέ ποτε, χρόνῳ ποτέ</quote>), or perhaps ‘formerly’—while the labours for Eurystheus were going on: it could not, by itself, mean ‘<emph>only</emph> now and then.’ The sentence begins as if <quote xml:lang="grc">ποτέ</quote> were to be followed by some such general phrase as <quote xml:lang="grc">διὰ χρόνου</quote>:—<quote xml:lang="grc">οὓς κεῖνός ποτε...διὰ χρόνου προσεῖδε</quote>, ‘whom he saw only at uncertain intervals.’ The interposed simile, however, leads the poet to employ a phrase adapted to the special case of the <quote xml:lang="grc">γῄτης</quote>,—viz., </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σπείρωνμόνον_ἅπαξ_31">. The <quote xml:lang="grc">γῄτης</quote> sees his distant field only twice a year. But it is not meant that Heracles visits his home just twice a year. Nor has </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξαμῶν_31"> any figurative application to him, such as ‘reaping the joy’ of seeing his children. It is an irrelevant detail. This is quite Homeric. See, <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>, <ref n="Hom. Il. 13.62"> <title>Il.</title>13. 62</ref> ff., where Poseidon, soaring into the air, is likened to a bird which soars <quote xml:lang="grc">διώκειν ὄρνεον ἄλλο</quote>: though the sea-god is pursuing no one.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἅπαξ_31"> seems best taken with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξαμῶν_31_2"> only. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰὼν_34">, fortune in life; <ref n="Soph. Phil. 179."> <title>Ph.</title>179.</ref>— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰς_δόμων_34">: this order of words is the most forcible: no sooner did he regain his home, than he had to leave it again. The reversed order (which Brunck prefers) would give greater prominence to his moments of rest.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῳ_34">: Eurystheus (1049), whose name she shrinks from uttering. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄθλων_τῶνδ_36">, the labours for Eurystheus.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπερτελὴς_36">, rising clear of them: <ref n="Eur. Ion 1549">Eur. <title>Ion</title>1549</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴκων...ὑπερτελής</quote> (appearing above it): <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 359">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title>359</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπερτελέσαι</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μέγα δουλείας</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">γάγγαμον ἄτης.—<emph>ἔφυ</emph> </quote> seems to be here no more than <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγένετο</quote> (as in <ref n="Soph. El. 236">Soph. <title>El.</title>236</ref>, and often). Some, however, understand, ‘now that his inborn force has prevailed,’ etc.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταρβήσασ_ἔχω_36">=<quote xml:lang="grc">τετάρβηκα</quote>. The periphrasis is somewhat rare when the verb is intrans.; but cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 731">Soph. <title>O. T.</title>731</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέ π<*> λήξαντ’ ἔχει</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔκτα_38">, the only Sophoclean example of this form (on which see Monro, <title>Hom. Gr.</title> § 13), though we have <quote xml:lang="grc">συγ κατακτάς</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 230."> <title>Ai.</title>230.</ref>Both Aesch. ( <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 460"> Aesch. <title>Eum.</title>460</ref>) and Eur. ( <ref n="Eur. Ba. 1290"> Eur. <title>Bacch.</title>1290</ref>, etc.) use <quote xml:lang="grc"> κατέκταν</quote> in dialogue, but not <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔκταν.— <emph>Ἰφίτου βίαν</emph> </quote>: for the periphrasis, cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 314.">Soph. <title>Ph.</title>314.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_Τραχῖνι_39">. Heracles was dwelling at Tiryns when he slew Iphitus, as related in vv. 270 ff. Then, with Deianeira and his children, he removed from Tiryns to Trachis, and soon afterwards Zeus sent him forth into servitude (276).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνάστατοι_39"> ( <ref n="Soph. OC 429"> <title>O. C.</title>429</ref> n.) alludes to compulsion used by Eurystheus: the word would not suit a voluntary migration. This had happened fifteen months ago. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξένῳ_ἀνδρὶ_40">: Ceÿx, king of Trachis, who is not named in this play. <title>Scut.</title> 353 (Heracles speaks) <quote xml:lang="grc">Τρηχῖνα δέ τοι παρελαύνω</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς Κήϋκα ἄνακτα· ὁ γὰρ δυνάμει τε καὶ αἰδοῖ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">Τρηχῖνος προβέβηκε</quote>. The Hesiodic <quote xml:lang="grc">Κήϋκος γάμος</quote> described a marriage-feast given by that king, at which Heracles was a guest. Apollodorus (2. 7 § 7) and Diodorus (4. 36 § 57) mention Ceÿx.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπου_40">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅποι</quote>, since <quote xml:lang="grc">βέβηκεν</quote> implies, ‘is now’: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 52."> <title>O. C.</title>52.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλὴν_41"> cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1643"> <title>O. C.</title>1643</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἕρπεθ’</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς τάχιστα· πλὴν <*> κύριος</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">Θησε<*> παρέστω.—<emph>ὠδῖνας</emph> </quote>: cp. <*>25.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὑτοῦ_41">, <*>ctive gen.: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 858"> <title>Ant.</title>858</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> πατρὸς...ο<*>κτο<*></quote> (<emph>about</emph> him): <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1039"> <title>Ph.</title>1039</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κέντρον...ἐμοῦ. <emph> <*>οῦ</emph> </quote> is clearly right: the h<*>shness of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτ_41"> may be measured by supposing that, <*>stead of it, we had <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἡρακλέους</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀνδρος</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέκα_έντε_44">. Twelve years before this time, the <*>acle at Dodona had told Heracles th<*>. at the end of twelve years (824), he <*>ould have rest. Fifteen months befo<*> this time, Heracles had given Deian<*> the <quote xml:lang="grc">δέλτος</quote> on which h<*> had written <*>own that oracle (1167). <*>e had then to<*> her that, if he did not return at the <*> of fifteen months, she might assume th<*> he was dead (164 ff.)<*></gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκήρυκτος_44">. No herald has come, either to announ<*> his approach, or to give any tidings of <*>im.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἄστιν_44">, not on <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινόν</quote>. When she ponders the <*>cle, her grave misgiving (43) becomes <*>rtitude.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοιαύτην_44">, giving the ground for a statement: <ref n="Soph. OC 747"> <title>O. C.</title>747</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὴν_47">, a rare instance of the art. used as relat. pron. in dial gue without metrical necessity: see <ref n="Soph. OC 747"> <title>O. C.</title>747</ref> n. The motive here may have been a wish to avoid four consecutive endings in <foreign xml:lang="grc">ν</foreign> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πημονῆς_ἄτερ_47">. If the <quote xml:lang="grc"> δέλτος</quote> should prove to have foretold the death of Heracles, then she would have received it <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν πημονῇ</quote>: it would have been a harbinger of woe.—As to Wunder's rejection of vv<*> 44—48, see Appendix. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολλὰ_δ_49">: the thought is, ‘though hitherto I have been silent, now I must speak.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γοωμένην_49"> takes </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολλὰ_ὀδύρματα_49"> as ‘inner’ (or ‘cognate’) accus., and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὴν_ἔξοδον_49"> as object: Schneidewin cp. <ref n="Eur. Med. 205">Eur. <title>Med.</title>205</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄχεα μογερὰ βοᾷ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ἐν λέχει προδόταν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φρενοῦν_52">, pres., since the act may be conceived as continuing or re peated; but </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φράσαι_52">, aor., with ref. to the particular utterance: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 95"> <title>Ph.</title>95</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξαμαρτεῖν...νικᾶν</quote> : <emph>ib.</emph> 667 f. <quote xml:lang="grc">θιγγάνειν... δοῦναι</quote> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀμὲ_52"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> Two constructions are possible: I prefer the first. (1) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καί_52">= ‘and,’ depending on </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_52">, and the apodosis begins with the direct question, <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς κ.τ.λ.</quote> (2) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καί_52_2">=‘even’ (cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 719"> <title>Ant.</title>719</ref> n., <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀπ’ ἐμοῦ</quote>), and the apodosis begins with <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀμὲ χρή</quote>. But the first is more deferential; and the very abruptness of <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς κ.τ.λ.</quote> is natural here.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φράσαι_σόν_52">, ‘to prescribe <emph>thy part</emph>’ (<title>O.C.</title> 625 n.), <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, to say what it becomes thee to do. There is only a verbal resemblance to <title>I. A.</title> 1167 (compared by Schneid.), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ )μὲ χρὴ λέγειν τὰ σά</quote> <emph>;</emph> ‘am I to make thine answer for thee?’—The <emph>v. l.</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τόσον_52"> is weaker, whether taken to mean ‘so bold a speech,’ or (with the schol.) ‘just thus much.’ And the form itself is rare in Sophocles ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 277"> <title>Ai.</title>277</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δὶς τόσ’</quote>: <emph> ib.</emph> 185 lyr. <quote xml:lang="grc">τόσσον</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοσοῖσδε_54">. Besides Hyllus, the eldest child of Deianeira, legend gave her three other sons, and one daughter (Apoilod. 2. 7 § 8: <ref n="Diod. 4. 37">Diod.4. 37</ref>: <ref n="Paus. 1.32.5">Paus. 1. 32 § 5</ref>). Cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1153">vv. 1153 ff</ref>. For the parataxis (<quote xml:lang="grc">πληθύεις μέν, ἀτὰρ οὐ πέμπεις</quote>), cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 419"> <title>O. T.</title>419</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατὰ_ζήτησιν_54">: <cit> <bibl n="Isoc. 17.4">Isocr. or. 17 § 4</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χρήματα δοὺς ἐξέπεμψεν ἅμα κατ’ ἐμπορίαν καὶ κατὰ θεωρίαν</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
χρήματα δοὺς ἐξέπεμψεν ἅμα κατ’ ἐμπορίαν καὶ
							κατὰ θεωρίαν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_δοκεῖν_56">: instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ νέμοι τιν’ ὤραν τοῦ τὸν πατέρα...δοκεῖν</quote>. The gen. </gloss>
lemma: εἰ πατρὸς νέμοι τιν’ ὤραν τοῦ … δοκεῖν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατρὸς_56">, placed at the beginning of the clause, illustrates the normal Greek tendency to announce the subject of the statement at the outset (as in <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτον οἶσθ’ εἰ ζῶν κυρεῖ</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 444"> <title>Ph.</title>444</ref> n.). The second gen., </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_δοκεῖν_56">, is ‘epexegetic,’ as defining the <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤραν</quote>. But it is not in apposition with <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρός</quote> (‘care for his father,—<emph>that is</emph>, care for his being deemed,’ etc.). Rather the two genitives are linked to <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤραν</quote> with slightly different shades of meaning;— ‘care, on his father's account, for his being deemed.’ Instead of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_δοκεῖν_56_2">, we might have had a relative clause, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως ἂν...δοκῇ</quote>. But, since <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤραν</quote> could take a gen., that constr. was preferred as more compact. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 2.4">Dem. or. 2 § 4</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτων οὐχὶ νῦν ὁρῶ τὸν καιρὸν τοῦ λέγειν</quote> </cit> (the speaking-time for these things). <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 5.22">Id. or. 5 § 22</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λαβεῖν ἐβούλετο τὴν δόξαν τοῦ πολέμου τοῦ δοκεῖν δἰ αὑτὸν κρίσιν εἰληφέναι</quote> </cit> .</gloss>

data.quote: τούτων οὐχὶ νῦν ὁρῶ τὸν καιρὸν τοῦ λέγειν

λαβεῖν ἐβούλετο τὴν δόξαν τοῦ πολέμου τοῦ
							δοκεῖν δἰ αὑτὸν κρίσιν εἰληφέναι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δοκεῖν_56"> here=‘be believed to be’: cp. <ref n="Thuc. 6. 17">Thuc.6. 17</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕως...ὁ Νικίας εὐτυχὴς δοκεῖ</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">ε<*>ναι</quote>, ‘while he <emph>has the reputation</emph> of being successful’ (not ‘seems’: he really was so). The meaning is, ‘Hyllus ought to go in search of news, if he cared to dispel our painful anxiety.’ The <quote xml:lang="grc">τροφός</quote> chooses words which avoid any suggestion of disaster to Heracles, and say only that his welfare has yet <emph>to be ascertained.</emph> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νέμοι_56"> is better attested than </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νέμει_56">, and also fitter, as implying the deferential <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰκὸς ἂν εἴη</quote>, not the blunt <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰκός ἐστιν</quote>. See Appendix. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρτίπους_58">, with <emph>opportune</emph> foot (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρτίως καὶ ἡρμοσμένως τῷ καιρῷ πορεύεται</quote>, schol.). Cp. the similar phrases for a timely arrival<emph>; </emph> <ref n="Soph. OT 78"> O. T.78</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς καλόν</quote> : <ref n="Soph. Ant. 386"> <title>Ant.</title>386</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς δέον περᾷ</quote>: 387 <quote xml:lang="grc">ποίᾳ ξύμμετρος προὔβην τύχῃ</quote> <emph>; </emph> <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1168"> Ai.1168</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς αὐτὸν καιρόν</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. Seven 373">Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 373</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς ἀρτίκολλον ἀγγέλου λόγου μαθεῖν</quote>. Elsewhere </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρτίπους_58_2">=‘with sound foot’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἅρτιος</quote>, well-compacted), as <ref n="Hom. Il. 9.505"> <title>Il.</title>9. 505.</ref>And so some take it here, as if it were meant to suggest his fitness for the mission: but this seems frigid. The poet was perhaps thinking of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρτι</quote> rather than of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρτιος</quote>: and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρτι</quote> certainly occurs in composition, not only with verbs (as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρτιθανής</quote>), but also with nouns, as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρτίδακρυς</quote> ( <ref n="Eur. Med. 903">Eur. <title>Med.</title>903</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρτίπλουτος</quote> ( <ref n="Aesch. Supp. 742"> Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title>742</ref>). Still, <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀρτίπους</quote>, as used here, could be taken from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρτιος</quote>, in the sense of ‘fitted’ to the occasion, <quote xml:lang="grc">καίριος.—<emph>θρῴσκει δόμους</emph> </quote> : <ref n="Soph. OC 643">Soph. <title>O. C.</title>643</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δόμους στείχειν ἐμούς</quote>. He is hastening to tell his mother the news which he has just heard (67). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_καιρὸν_59">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 325"> <title>O. T.</title>325</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_τ_59"> is clearly right: Deianeira can at once act on the counsel by sending Hyllus. With </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_59"> simply, or </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_γ_59">, the sense would be much weaker: ‘Hyllus can do as I suggest.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_παῖ_61">, an affectionate form of address, as in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 260"> <title>Ph.</title>260</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Hec. 172">Eur. <title> Hec.</title>172</ref>, etc.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀγεννήων_61">, prop. ‘not begotten’ ( <ref n="Soph. OC 973"> <title>O. C.</title>973</ref>), then, ‘of no birth,’ ‘low-born,’ like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγεννής</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀγενής.— <emph>καλῶς πίπτουσιν</emph> </quote>, fall happily,—a metaphor from dice: <ref n="Soph. El. 1100">Soph. <title> El.</title>1100</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ μὲν γὰρ εὖ</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ δ’ οὐ καλῶς πίπτοντα δέρκομαι βροτῶν.—<emph> ἐλεύθερον</emph> </quote>=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλευθέριον</quote>: cp. Eur. fr. 828 <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλοῖσι δούλοις τοὔνομ’ αἰσχρόν, ἡ δὲ φρὴν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν οὐχὶ δούλων ἔστ’ ἐλευθερωτέρα</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διδακτά_64">: for the plur., cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 524"> <title>Ph.</title>524</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">αἰσχρά</quote>), and <ref n="Soph. OC 554"> <title>O. C.</title>554</ref> n. The sing. occurs below<ref n="Soph. OC 671."> O. C., 671.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὲ_πυθέσθαι_65">: for the place of <quote xml:lang="grc">σέ</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 710"> <title>Ant.</title>710</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἄνδρα, κεἴ τις ᾖ σοφός, τὸ μανθάνειν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλλ’ αἰσχρὸν οὐδέν</quote>. It is needless to conjecture <*>.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξενωμένου_65">: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 865"> <title>El.</title>865</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξένος...κέκευθεν</quote> (‘he has been buried in a foreign land’). <cit> <bibl n="Shak. h8 2.2.129">Shaksp. <title>H. VIII.</title> 2. 2. 129</bibl> <quote xml:lang="eng">Kept him a foreign man</quote> </cit> (=kept him out of England).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποῦ_στιν_65">: for this mode of writing, cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 16"> <title>Ph.</title>16</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φέρειν_65"> is a certain correction of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φέρει_65">: in answer to his question, she is quoting the slave's speech. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μύθοις_67">, L's reading, is as good as </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μύθοις_γ_67">, though no better. L has lost <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote> in some other places (as <ref n="Soph. Ant. 648"> <title>Ant.</title>648</ref> <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1241"> Ant., 1241</ref>): but, on the whole, it seems best not to assume such a loss here. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἱδρῦσθαι_68">. The length of his absence prompts her conjecture that he has fixed his abode somewhere: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 101">cp. 101</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κλιθείς</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὲν_69"> here is not answered by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_69"> in 72.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄροτον_69">, ploughing-season ( <ref n="Hes. WD 448">Hes. <title>Op.</title>448</ref>), hence, ‘year’: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 825">cp. 825</ref>. So <quote xml:lang="grc">πόα</quote>=‘summer,’ Rhianus <emph>ap.</emph> <ref n="Paus. 4.17.6">Paus. 4. 17 § 6</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χείματά τε ποίας τε δύω.—<emph>ἐν μήκει χρόνου</emph> </quote>, ‘at the full length of that period,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, from beginning to end of the year.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Λυδῇ_69">: Omphalè: <ref target="cl252">see on 252</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_ἔτλη_71">: ‘if he <emph>indeed</emph> bore this.’ <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote> here emphasises <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτ’ ἔτλη</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1127"> <title>Ai.</title>1127</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινόν γ’ εἶπας, εἰ καὶ ζῇς θανών</quote>: and <ref n="Soph. OT 305"> <title>O. T.</title>305</ref> n. If <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote> were taken with <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτο</quote> only (‘<emph>even</emph> this’), it would imply former disgraces. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1218">Cp. 1218</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλά_72">, like ‘well,’ here refers to D. 's bitter comment: <emph>that</emph> disgrace, at any rate, is past. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_θανὼν_73">: a fine touch. She is prepared to hear anything now; even that he is dead. And <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐξαφεῖται</quote> was ambiguous. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Εὐβοῖδα_74"> (as in Aesch. fr. 29), contr. for <quote xml:lang="grc">Εὐβοΐδα</quote>, acc. of <quote xml:lang="grc">Εὐβοΐς</quote>. In <ref n="Soph. El. 442">Soph. <title>El.</title>442</ref> the MSS. give <quote xml:lang="grc">Εὐβοΐδας</quote>, for which Seidler wrote <quote xml:lang="grc">Εὐβοῖδας</quote>. For the nom., the longer form <quote xml:lang="grc">Εὐβοιΐς</quote> is used below (237<ref n="Soph. El. 401">Soph. El., 401</ref>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόλιν_74"> is in appos. with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Εὐβοῖδα_χώραν_74">. Oechalia in Euboea was the seat of Eurytus, but it is in accor<*>nce with epic precedent to regard him as reigning over the whole island,— like Chalcodon in the <title> Philoctetes</title> (489 n.), and Elephenor in the <title>Iliad</title> (2. 536 ff.).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_ἔτι_74">, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιστρατεύσειν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OC 1074"> <title>O. C.</title>1074</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρδουσ’ ἢ μέλλουσιν</quote> <emph>;</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔλειπε_76"> seems to differ from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔλιπε</quote> here only as being somewhat more vivid, —<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, as serving to suggest the moment when he <emph>was doing</emph> the act (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 47">cp. 47</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δέλτον λιπὼν ἔστειχε</quote>). See Appendix.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆσδε_πέρι_76">. There is no reason to suspect <quote xml:lang="grc">χώρας</quote>. The oracle said that, at this time, he was to go through his last labour. The Euboean war, she infers, is that labour. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀγνοῶ_78">. As Heracles had long spared Deianeira a knowledge of the prophecy (158), so she had hitherto spared her son. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τελευτὴν_τελεῖν_79">: cp. Theognis 1166 <quote xml:lang="grc">εὖτ’ ἂν ὁδοῦ τελέῃς τέρματ’ ἐπ’ ἐμπορίην</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄρας_80">, having taken up, as a burden to be borne. The midd. would be usual in this sense ( <ref n="Eur. Ion 199"> Eur. <title>Ion</title>199</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰρόμενος πόνους</quote>): but the act. is also admissible, just as in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 706"> Soph. <title>Ph.</title>706</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ φορβὰν...αἴρων</quote> (n.). So in <ref n="Hom. Il. 23.736"> <title>Il.</title>23. 736</ref> we have the act. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀέθλια δ’ ἶσ’ ἀνελόντες</quote> (‘having won like prizes’), but <emph>ib.</emph> 823 the midd., <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀέθλια ἶσ’ ἀνελέσθαι</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰς_ὕστερον_80">, Reiske's simple correction of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰς_ὕστερον_80_2">, is much the best. <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ὕστερον</quote> cannot be defended by understanding <quote xml:lang="grc">χρόνον</quote>: the two passages in which <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ἀεί</quote> has been explained as <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ἀεὶ χρόνον</quote> are both corrupt ( <ref n="Soph. OC 1584"> <title>O. C.</title>1584</ref>, <ref n="Soph. El. 1075"> <title>El.</title>1075</ref>).—The redundancy of <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν λοιπὸν ἤδη</quote> after <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς τό γ’ ὕστερον</quote> is not greater than that in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1103"> <title>Ph.</title>1103</ref> ff. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃς ἤδη μετ’ οὐδενὸς ὕστερον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνδρῶν εἰσοπίσω τάλας...ὀλοῦμαι</quote>: where the text is certain. For other conjectures, see Appendix.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βίοτον_εὐαίων_80">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 518"> <title>O. T.</title>518</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βίου...τοῦ μακραίωνος</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_κειμένῳ_82">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1510"> <title>O. C.</title>1510</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν τῷ δὲ κεῖσαι τοῦ μόρου τεκμηρίῳ</quote> <emph>;</emph> (‘what sign of thy fate holds thee in suspense?’)—answering the words, <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥοπὴ βίου μοι</quote>. Alcaeus <emph>ap.</emph> <title>Vesp.</title> 1235 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντρέψεις ἔτι τὰν πόλιν· ἁ δ’ ἔχεται ῥοπᾶς</quote> (‘its fate hangs in the trembling scale’). For <quote xml:lang="grc"> ῥοπή</quote> cp. also <ref n="Soph. OT 961"> <title>O. T.</title>961</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡνίκ_ἅμα_85">. Verses 83 and 85 are probably right as they stand, while v. 84 is spurious. The original form of the interpolation was, however, I think, <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ πίπτομεν σοῦ πατρὸς ἐξολωλότος</quote>, intended to <emph>follow</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰχόμεσθ’ ἅμα</quote>, in order to supply the condition opposed to <quote xml:lang="grc">κείνου βίον σώσαντος</quote>. Then it struck a reviser that the passage would be more forcible if <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ πίπτομεν</quote> were changed to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ πίπτομεν</quote>, and v. 85 were omitted.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_οἰχόμεσθ_85"> cannot be strictly paralleled: but cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 535"> <title>Ant.</title>535</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ μὴ εἰδέναι</quote>. I had thought of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_κείμεσθ_85">, which derives some support from the fact that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ κ</quote> (<emph> sic</emph>) <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰχόμεσθ’</quote> occurs as a variant (cr. n.): but <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰχόμεσθ’</quote> is better, and is probably sound. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νῦν_ξυνήθης_88"> <quote xml:lang="grc"> κ.τ.λ.</quote> The <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν</quote> here, and the <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν</quote> in v. 90, are both right: only here we must read </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴα_88"> for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐᾷ</quote>, with Vauvilliers. The repetition of <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν</quote> is excused by the change of sense: in v. 88 it means, ‘<emph>as it was</emph>’: in v. 90, simply ‘<emph>now.</emph>’ Cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 1334"> <title>El.</title>1334</ref> ff. (‘if I had not taken care, ye would have been lost,’) <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν δ’ εὐλάβειαν τῶνδε προὐθέμην ἐγώ</quote>. | <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ νῦν ἀπαλλαχθέντε κ.τ.λ.</quote>: where the senses of <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν</quote> change just as here. It is well to note that repetitions of common words, which would otherwise be awkward, are often justified by such variations of meaning; see, <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>, the double <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 524"> <title>Ph.</title>524</ref> ff., and <emph>ib.</emph> 645 ff.; and the fourfold <quote xml:lang="grc">δῆτα</quote> <emph>ib.</emph> 757 ff.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_μὴ_90">: it is unnecessary to write <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ μὴ <emph>οὐ</emph> </quote> : <ref n="Soph. Trach. 742">cp. 742</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OT 1387"> <title>O. T.</title>1387</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἂν ἐσχόμην</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ μὴ )ποκλῇσαι</quote> (n.): <ref n="Soph. Ant. 443"> <title>Ant.</title>443</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἀπαρνοῦμαι τὸ μή</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 348"> <title>Ph.</title>348</ref> ff. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_ὑστέρῳ_92">=<quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ</quote> (‘<emph>even</emph>’) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑστέρῳ γάρ</quote>. This use of <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ γάρ</quote>,—where <quote xml:lang="grc"> καί</quote> affects a following adj.,—is somewhat rare; but cp. fr. 86. 9 <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ γὰρ δυσειδὲς σῶμα καὶ δυσώνυμον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">γλώσσῃ σοφὸν τίθησιν</quote> etc.: <ref n="Soph. OT 334"> <title>O. T.</title>334</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ γὰρ ἂν πέτρου</quote> etc.: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 669"> <title>Ai.</title>669</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ γὰρ τὰ δεινὰ</quote> etc. More often, in such cases, <quote xml:lang="grc">γάρ</quote> follows that which <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote> affects, as <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1268"> <title>Ph.</title>1268</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ τὰ πρὶν γάρ</quote> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τό_εὖ_92"> | </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πράσσειν_92">: for the place of the art., cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 265"> <title>O. C.</title>265</ref> n. ‘Even to one who is late, good fortune, if he should ever hear of it, brings gain.’ The general sentiment, ‘better late than never,’ is adapted to the particular case. Hyllus is going in search of tidings; and even now, if he hears good tidings, he will have his reward. The words <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεὶ <emph> πύθοιτο</emph> </quote> make it clear (I think) that <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ εὖ πράσσειν</quote> has here its ordinary sense, ‘faring well,’—not the much rarer sense, ‘acting aright’ (like <quote xml:lang="grc">πράσσοντα καλῶς</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OC 1764"> <title>O. C.</title>1764</ref> n.). The optat. gives abstract generality, which suits a <quote xml:lang="grc">γνώμη</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 666"> <title>Ant.</title>666</ref> n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμπολᾷ_92">. Any profitable action may be said, by a metaphor from trading, to ‘bring in’ gain. The bold phrase here is qualified by the fact that <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ εὖ πράσσειν</quote> is followed by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεὶ πύθοιτο</quote>. It is not, strictly, the thing ascertained, but the act of ascertaining it, that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμπολᾷ κέρδος</quote>.— Distinguish the phrase in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 303"> <title>Ph.</title>303</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξεμπολήσει κέρδος</quote> (‘sell off wares at a profit’). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰόλα_94">, ‘gleaming’ with stars: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 11">cp. 11</ref>: Eur. fr. 596 <quote xml:lang="grc">περὶ δ’ ὀρφναία</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">νὺξ αἰολόχρως, ἄκριτός τ’ ἄστρων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄχλος.—<emph> ἐναριζομένα</emph> </quote> might be merely ‘slain,’ but seems here to have its proper sense, ‘slain and despoiled.’ One point which favours this view has not been noticed. The inverted order of the words (‘chiasmus’) has its usual effect for the ear,—viz., to indi cate that </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φλογιζόμενον_94"> balances </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐναριζομένα_94">, as </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατευνάζει_94"> balances </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίκτει_94">. And this is so, if <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐναριζο<*>ένα</quote> implies, not only ‘slain,’ but ‘despoiled,’—thus serving, with <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰόλα</quote>, to suggest that bright panoply which Night is still wearing when the Dawn comes to vanquish her,—ere the Sun-god has yet issued from her womb. Cp. <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 279"> Aesch. <title>Ag.</title>279</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς νῦν τεκούσης φῶς τόδ’ εὐφρόνης</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατευνάζει_φλογιζόμενον_94">. The passage is marred by placing the comma, as some do, after <quote xml:lang="grc">τε</quote>, and taking the partic. with <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰτῶ</quote>. Cp. Byron, <title>Corsair</title>, canto III.: ‘Slow sinks, more lovely ere his race be run, | Along Morea's hills the setting sun; | Not, as in northern climes, obscurely bright, | But one unclouded blaze of living light.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτο_97"> is in appos. with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τον_ναίει_97">. The objection to taking </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καρῦξαι_97"> as governing a double acc. (like <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγειν τινά τι</quote>) is, here, that the emphasis on </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτο_97_2"> would then be unsuitable; since, under the circumstances, the knowledge which they desire <emph>about</emph> Heracles can be only, <quote xml:lang="grc">πόθι ναίει</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόθι_παῖς_98">. In the MS. reading, <quote xml:lang="grc">πόθι μοι πόθι μοι παῖς</quote>, either the second <quote xml:lang="grc">μοι</quote>, or <quote xml:lang="grc">παῖς</quote>, must be omitted: the antistrophic words are <quote xml:lang="grc">βλεφάρων πόθον, ἀλλ’</quote> (107). The strong reason for retaining </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πας_98"> is that, as the constr. would have been so clear without it, it is very unlikely to have been inserted; while the repetition of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοι_98"> would have been a most easy error. For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_Ἀλκμ_98">, followed by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παῖς_98"> in the relat. clause, Schneidewin cp. <ref n="Eur. Her. 840">Eur. <title>H. F.</title>840</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γνῷ μὲν τὸν Ἥρας οἷός ἐστ’ αὐτῷ χόλος</quote>, and id. fr. 1039. 3 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁρᾷς τὸν εὐτράπεζον ὡς ἡδὺς βίος</quote> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_φλεγέθων_99">: for this direct invocation (continued in 102), following <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἅλιον αἰτῶ</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 164"> <title>O. T.</title>164</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">προφάνητέ μοι</quote> (after <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἄρτεμιν</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc"> Φοῖβον</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στεροπᾷ_99">, usu. ‘lightning,’ here, ‘flashing light’; so the word is used of flashing armour ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 11.83"> <title>Il.</title>11. 83</ref>, etc.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_κλιθείς_100">. The general sense is simply, ‘where is he on sea or land?’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποντίας_100">, rather than </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποντίους_100"> (see cr. n.), is probably right. According to Athenaeus (p. 189 D), <quote xml:lang="grc"> αὐλών</quote> is masc. in Attic prose, but fem. in poetry: he quotes Soph. (fr. 503) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπακτίας αὐλῶνας</quote>, and Carcînus (fr. 1) <quote xml:lang="grc">βαθεῖαν εἰς αὐλῶνα</quote>. Although, then, <quote xml:lang="grc">πόντιος</quote> could be used as an adj. of two terminations, Soph. may have preferred the distinctively fem. form here. In <ref n="Aesch. PB 731">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title> 731</ref>, however, the word is masc., <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐλῶν’ ἐκπερᾶν Μαιωτικόν</quote> (of the Cimmerian Bosporus).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόθι_100"> (=</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="που_100">) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ναίει_κλιθείς_100">; lit., ‘where he is situated, either on the sea-straits, or in a resting-place on one of the two continents.’ </gloss>
lemma:
ναίει ἢ ποντίας
							αὐλῶνας ἢ δισς. ἀπείροις κλιθείς

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ναίει_100"> thus governs an acc. in the first clause, while in the second it stands intransitively with a partic. For a similar difference in form between the clauses after </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_100"> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἤ_100">, cp. <ref n="Thuc. 4. 5">Thuc.4. 5</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ὀλιγωρίᾳ ἐποιοῦντο</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς Ἀθηναίους</quote>), <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς...ἢ οὐχ ὑπομενοῦντας σφᾶς, ἢ ῥᾳδίως ληψόμενοι βίᾳ</quote>: where the acc. <quote xml:lang="grc"> ὑπομενοῦντας</quote> (governing <quote xml:lang="grc">σφᾶς</quote>) is better taken as depending on the verb than as absol. For <quote xml:lang="grc">ναίειν</quote> as=merely ‘to be in a place,’ cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 117">Soph. <title>O. C.</title>117</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῦ ναίει</quote>, said, as here, of a wanderer.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δισς_κλιθείς_100">, lit., ‘resting upon’ them, as on a support; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, having found an abode on land, instead of roaming over sea. The phrase was suggested by the epic use of <quote xml:lang="grc">κέκλιμαι</quote>, as said (<emph>a</emph>) of land which <emph>slopes down</emph> to the water's edge,—thus, as it were, ‘resting on’ the water; <ref n="Hom. Od. 13.234"> <title>Od.</title>13. 234</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκτὴ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">κεῖθ’ ἁλὶ κεκλιμένη</quote>: (<emph>b</emph>) of a person who <emph>dwells on the edge</emph> of water; <ref n="Hom. Il. 5.709"> <title>Il.</title>5. 709</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λίμνῃ κεκλιμένος Κηφισίδι</quote> (where see Leaf): <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 15.740"> <emph>ib.</emph> 15. 740</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">π)όντῳ κεκλιμένοι</quote> </cit> (the Greeks ‘leaning on’ the sea): <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 16.17"> <emph>ib.</emph> 16. 67</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥηγμῖνι θαλάσσης | κεκλίαται</quote> </cit>. So in <ref n="Pind. O. 1">Pind. <title>O.</title> 1. 92</ref> the buried Pelops is described as <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀλφεοῦ πόρῳ κλιθείς</quote>, ‘resting by’ (lit. ‘upon’) ‘the stream of the Alpheus.’ Here, however, Soph. has modified the usage,—the dat. denoting land, not water; and the sense is not, dwelling ‘on the shore of’ either continent, but simply, anywhere within their limits.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δισσαῖσιν_100"> is possible only because </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόθι_100_2"> precedes. We could not say (<foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>), <quote xml:lang="grc">δισσαῖς ἠπείροις οἰκεῖ</quote>, meaning ‘he dwells in one of the two continents.’ But it is correct to say, <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῦ δισσαῖς ἠπείροις οἰκεῖ</quote> <emph>;</emph> meaning, ‘where in (either of) the two continents is his home?’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποντίας_αὐλῶνας_100"> is merely a general expression for the sea. The phrase was suggested by the Aegaean, with its <cit> <quote xml:lang="lat">interfusa nitentes | … aequora Cycladas</quote> <bibl n="Hor. Carm. 1.14.19">(Hor. <title>C.</title> 1. 14. 19)</bibl> </cit>. Paley understands:—‘Is he near home, in the Euripus (<quote xml:lang="grc">αὐλῶνας</quote>), or midway between both continents, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, in the Hellespont?’ Mr Whitelaw, too, thinks that the Hellespont is meant, and that <quote xml:lang="grc">δισς. ἀπείροις</quote>=‘on a slope looking towards both continents,’—the sea being regarded as an eminence. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρατιστεύων_ὄμμα_102">: cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 3.277"> <title>Il.</title>3. 277</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἠέλιός θ’, ὃς πάντ’ ἐφορᾷς καὶ πάντ’ ἐπακούεις</quote>. For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατά_102"> , <ref n="Soph. Trach. 379">cp. 379</ref>; <title>O.T.</title> 1087 <quote xml:lang="grc">κατὰ γνώμαν ἴδρις</quote>, n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποθουμένᾳ_103"> = <quote xml:lang="grc">ποθούσῃ</quote>, a midd. found only here, yet not suspicious, since the context excludes the pass. sense. The ‘longing mind’ is clearly Deianeira's; <quote xml:lang="grc">ποθουμένᾳ</quote> could not well denote the ‘anxious’ or ‘tender’ feeling of the Chorus. As </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πυνθάνομαι_103"> is devoid of emphasis,—like a parenthetic ‘so I hear,’—the order of the words is not too bold. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰν_ἀμφινεικῆ_104">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 527">cp. 527</ref>: <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 686">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title>686</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰν δορίγαμβρον ἀμφινεικῆ θ’ Ἑλέναν</quote>. Not, ‘with <emph>two</emph> suitors’ (Paley).— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀεί_104"> belonged, in the poet's thought, to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τρύχεσθαι_104">, but is cut off from it by the adversative form in which the sentence is worked out (<quote xml:lang="grc">οὔποτ’ εὐνάζειν..., ἀλλ’</quote>, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔποτ’ εὐνάζουσαν</quote>). It could not well be taken with <quote xml:lang="grc">ποθουμένᾳ</quote>: still less with <quote xml:lang="grc">πυνθάνομαι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄρνιν_105">. The nightingale may be meant (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 963">cp. 963</ref>, <ref n="Soph. El. 148"> <title>El.</title>148</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἃ Ἴτυν αἰὲν Ἴτυν ὀλοφύρεται</quote>); but it is also possible that the image is general, as in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 423"> <title>Ant.</title>423</ref> ff. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀδακρύτων_106"> proleptic: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1200"> <title>Ant.</title>1200</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀργὰς εὐμενεῖς κατασχεθεῖν</quote>, and <emph>ib.</emph> 791 n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βλεφάρων_πόθον_106">: cp. fr. 729 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀμμάτειος πόθος</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὔμναστονδεῖμα_ἀνδρὸς_106">, though the adj. might go with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνδρός</quote>, and <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖμα</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁδοῦ</quote> only. Casaubon's emendation </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τρέφουσαν_106"> (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 28">cp. 28</ref>) has been generally received. But the MS. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φέρουσαν_106"> must not be lightly rejected. If right, it means ‘bearing’ as a burden; cp. <title>O.T.</title> 93 <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶνδε γὰρ πλέον φέρω</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ πένθος</quote>. The word is, however, much more suitable to <quote xml:lang="grc">πένθος</quote> than to <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖμα</quote>. And we cannot compare passages in which <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρειν</quote> is said of the temper or mood which a person ‘carries’ within him, as <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 118">Eur. <title> Hipp.</title>118</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σπλάγχνον ἔντονον φέρων</quote> (cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 705">Soph. <title>Ant.</title>705</ref> n., and <emph>ib.</emph> 1090). A scribe might easily have written <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρουσαν</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">τρέφουσαν</quote> by a mere slip,— as the true <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔβαλ’</quote> became <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔλαβ’</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 680">Soph. <title> Ph.</title>680</ref>, or as in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 180">Soph. <title> Ant.</title>180</ref> the true <quote xml:lang="grc">φόβου</quote> seems to have been made in L from <quote xml:lang="grc">σοφου</quote>: cp. also the variant <quote xml:lang="grc">μένειν</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">νέμοι</quote> below, in 163. On the whole, I believe that </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τρέφουσαν_106_2"> is right. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐνθυμίοις_τρύχεσθαι_110">, lit., is afflicted by that desolateness of her bed which is always in her thoughts,=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνθυμουμένην εὐνὰς ἀνανδρώτους τρύχεσθαι</quote>. This may be freely rendered, ‘pines on her anxious, widowed couch.’ But the dat. is really causal, not locative; and the schol.'s explanation of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνθυμίοις</quote> by <quote xml:lang="grc">μεριμνητικαῖς</quote> (‘full of care’) assumes a sense which seems neither necessary nor tenable. Everywhere else <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνθύμιος</quote> means ‘dwelling in the mind,’ and is said of that which lies heavy on the soul, as a cause of misgiving or anxiety. So <title>O.T.</title> 739 <quote xml:lang="grc">τί δ’ ἐστί σοι τοῦτ’, Οἰδίπους, ἐνθύμιον<emph>;</emph>—<emph>ἐλπίζουσαν</emph> </quote>, of evil foreboding, as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλπίζει</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 799."> <title>Ai.</title>799.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δύστανον_110">, Deianeira. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολλὰ_ἴδοι_112">. For <quote xml:lang="grc">κύματα ἢ νότου ἢ βορέα</quote> (waves belonging to, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> raised by, them), cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 2.396"> <title>Il.</title>2. 396</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν δ’</quote> (<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">σκόπελον</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔ ποτε κύματα λείπει</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">παντοίων ἀνέμων, ὅτ’ ἂν ἔνθ’ ἢ ἔνθα γένωνται</quote>. Note the last clause as parallel with the mention of two opposite winds here,—showing that Sophocles had that passage in mind.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κύματ_ἴδοι_112"> is clearly right: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐρέϊ_πόντῳ_112"> is a locative dat. of a common kind, like <ref n="Soph. El. 174"> <title>El.</title>174</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέγας οὐρανῷ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζεύς</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ν_112">, not </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἂν_112">, should be inserted after <quote xml:lang="grc">κύματ’</quote>, and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴδοι_112"> taken as a potential opt., ‘might see.’ But in Attic poetry the opt. is so used only where there is some stress on the notion of the possible or conceivable; as in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 605"> <title>Ant.</title>605</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς...κατάσχοι</quote> <emph> ;</emph> (n.): see <title>O. C.</title>, Append. on 170, p. 275 (2d ed.). (2) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_112"> is to be inserted, but </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴδοι_112_2"> changed to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴδῃ_112">, an epic subjunct. of comparison, as in <ref n="Hom. Il. 2.474"> <title>Il.</title>2. 474</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc"> ὥστε...διακρίνωσιν</quote>. But there is no Attic example of this; for in <ref n="Eur. Hec. 1026">Eur. <title>Hec.</title>1026</ref> the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκπέσῃ</quote> of the MSS. should be <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκπεσεῖ</quote>. (3) The objection to Zippmann's compromise—<quote xml:lang="grc">κύματ’ <emph>ἐν</emph>...βάντ’ ἐπιόντ’ <emph> ἂν</emph> </quote>—is the harsh asyndeton, which is foreign to the poet's manner.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βάντ_τ_112">, lit., ‘having passed by, and coming on.’ The spectator sees wave after wave go by.—Others understand, ‘driven <emph>back</emph>, and then coming on again’ (Blaydes, ‘ebbing and flowing’). This gives a forced sense to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βάντ_112">. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὕτω_δὲ_116">: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 25"> <title>El.</title>25</ref> ff. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥσπερ γὰρ ἵππος...ὡσαύτως δὲ σύ.—<emph>τὸν Καδμογενῆ</emph> </quote> (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1"> <title>O. T.</title>1</ref>, n.), not merely because he was born at Thebes (<quote xml:lang="grc">Θηβαγενής</quote>, <ref n="Hes. Th. 530"> Hes. <title>Th.</title>530</ref>); but because, though Alcmena and Amphitryon were Argive Perseidae, the youthful Heracles had been adopted into the ‘Cadmean’ nobility of Thebes. This was symbolised by the tripod dedicated on the boy's behalf in the Ismenion, after he had served as <quote xml:lang="grc">δαφναφόρος</quote> of the Ismenian Apollo. ( <ref n="Paus. 9. 10. 4.">Paus.9. 10. 4.</ref>)</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βιότου_πολύπονον_116"> (<quote xml:lang="grc"> πέλαγος</quote>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὥσπερ_Κρήσιον_116">, (<quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ μὲν</quote>) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στρέφει_Καδμογενῆ_116">. With </gloss>
lemma:
στρέφει τὸ δ’
							αὔξει τὸν Καδμογενῆ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Κρήσιον_116"> cp. <title>C.</title> 1. 26. 1 ff. The image is that of a strong swimmer buffeting a rough sea. One wave twists him aside (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στρέφει_116">) from his course: the next sweeps him onward, lifting him on its crest. It is characteristic of Sophocles that, in the second clause, he has preferred </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὔξει_116"> to <quote xml:lang="grc">αἴρει</quote>, through thinking of that which the <emph> uplifting</emph> wave figures,— viz., the <emph>honour</emph> won by the hero. For the omission of <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ μέν</quote> (implied by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_δ_116">) before <quote xml:lang="grc"> στρέφει</quote>, cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 22.157"> <title>Il.</title>22 157</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῇ ῥα παραδραμέτην, φεύγων, ὁ δ’ ὄπισθε διώκων</quote>. Remark that </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βιότου_πολύπονον_116_2"> could not stand for <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ βιότου πολύπονον</quote>: and the <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ δ’</quote> before <quote xml:lang="grc">αὔξει</quote> in 1.0 way alters this fact. It is therefore necessary, as it is easy, to supply <quote xml:lang="grc">πέλαγος</quote> from what follows.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στρέφει_116_2"> (due to Reiske) are Dindorf, Nauck, Wecklein, Hartung. The last-named, however, takes it as = ‘overturns,’ referring it to the swimmer being <emph>plunged down</emph> into the trough of the sea. For this sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">στρέφειν</quote>, see on <ref n="Soph. OC 1453"> <title>O. C.</title>1453</ref> f. But here the idea of ‘turning aside or back’ better suits the image of reverses alternating with triumphs. <quote xml:lang="grc">στρέφειν</quote> was said of the wrestler who ‘twists back’ his foe (Pollux 3. 155: cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποστρέψας</quote> in <ref n="Aristoph. Kn. 264">Ar. <title> Eq.</title>264</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τρέφει_116">, we may observe:—(1) <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 367">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title>367</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ πόνοι τρέφοντες βροτούς</quote> may fairly be quoted to show that the sense here might be, ‘troubles make up the life of Heracles.’ (2) But the context seems to show that, instead of this, we require a word (<emph>a</emph>) which shall convey the idea of <emph> vexing</emph>, and (<emph>b</emph>) which can be opposed to <quote xml:lang="grc">αὔξει</quote>. For other views of the passage, see Appendix. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλά_119">: (<emph>though</emph> he is harassed), <emph>yet</emph> he is not suffered to perish (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 88">cp. 88</ref>). Since the words </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_αὔξει_119"> may be regarded as parenthetical, the idea of <emph>trouble</emph> remains the dominant one in the sentence before <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλά</quote>: hence the antithesis is logical.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀναμπλάκητον_119">, ‘unerring,’ in the sense, ‘not stumbling or failing,’ <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄπταιστον</quote> (schol.), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀσφαλῆ</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 472"> <title>O. T.</title>472</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Κῆρες αναπλάκητοι</quote>. As to the forms with and without <quote xml:lang="grc">μ</quote>, see n. there.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐρύκει_119">, a somewhat strange phrase (though <quote xml:lang="grc">θάνατον ἐρύκειν τινός</quote> would be natural), since it might suggest that he wished to reach Hades: cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 18.126"> <title>Il.</title>18. 126</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδέ μ’ ἔρυκε μάχης</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧν_122">, causal gen.: <ref n="Hom. Il. 1.65"> <title>Il.</title>1. 65</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐχωλῆς ἐπιμέμφεται</quote>: <ref n="Thuc. 8. 109">Thuc.8. 109</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέμψηται...τῶν ...γεγενημένων</quote>. The pron. refers back to vv. 103—111, which spoke of Deianeira's laments. There is no real obscurity in this, since her grief is the main theme of the ode, and the second strophe (112— 121) referred to the fate of Heracles as the cause of that grief.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰδοῖα_123">, Musgrave's correction of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἁδεῖα_123">, is certain. In L the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁ</quote> of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁδεῖα</quote> is at the end of a v., and the loss of <quote xml:lang="grc">ι</quote> after it would have been peculiarly easy (see Autotype Facsimile, p. 66 A). The difficulty of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἁδεῖα_123_2"> is not the construction, which, if somewhat harsh, is quite possible: ‘I will counsel in a pleasant vein’ (the adj. used adverbially), ‘though the counsel is adverse.’ The objection is the sense. ‘In a pleasant vein’ must mean, ‘suggesting thoughts of comfort’: as in <ref n="Soph. OT 82"> <title>O. T.</title>82</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡδύς</quote>, ‘pleasant,’ = ‘bringing good news.’ But, since </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντία_123"> expresses remonstrance against her <emph>despair</emph>, there is then no proper antithesis with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἁδεῖα_123_3">. Further, the word required by the context is clearly one which shall temper opposition with deference: as </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰδοῖα_123_2"> does.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἴσω_123">, <emph>proferam</emph>, ‘bring forward,’ ‘suggest’: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 166"> <title>O. C.</title> 166</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγον εἴ τιν’ οἴσεις | πρὸς ἐμὰν λέσχαν</quote> </cit> . <cit> <bibl n="Isoc. 7.6">Isocr. or. 7 § 6</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτων ἐνεγκεῖν ἔχω παραδείγματα</quote> </cit>. Not, ‘give an adverse judgment,’ like <quote xml:lang="grc">ψῆφον φέρειν</quote> . </gloss>

data.quote: λόγον εἴ τιν’ οἴσεις | πρὸς ἐμὰν λέσχαν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γὰρ_124">, prefacing the statement ( <ref n="Soph. OT 277"> <title>O. T.</title>277</ref> n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀποτρύειν_124">, ‘fret away.’ The midd. occurs in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 339"> <title>Ant.</title>339</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γᾶν...ἀποτρύεται</quote>. (Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Tac. Hist. 2.76">Tac. <title>Hist.</title> 2. 76</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">si quid... ferociae habuit,...commissationibus deteritur.</quote> </cit>)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐλπίδα_ἀγαθὰν_124">, that brighter forecast which the case permits: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 606"> <title>Ai.</title>606</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κακὰν ἐλπίδ’ ἔχων</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνάλγητα_126">, a lot with no pain in it; for the absol. neut. pl., cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 8.413"> <title>Od.</title>8. 413</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοὶ δέ τοι ὄλβια δοῖεν</quote>. Elsewhere <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνάλγητος</quote>=‘insensible to pain,’ or ‘un feeling.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδ_κρ_126"> <foreign xml:lang="grc"> κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: ‘a painless lot <emph>not even Zeus</emph> hath appointed,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘Zeus <emph>himself</emph> hath not appointed.’ It is the will of Zeus himself that mortals should have pain along with joy. For this use of <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέ</quote>, emphasising a person, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 280">cp. 280</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OC 590"> <title>O. C.</title>590</ref>(n. on <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲ σοὶ</quote>). In <ref n="Hom. Il. 5.22"> <title>Il.</title>5. 22</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲ γὰρ οὐδέ κεν αὐτὸς ὑπέκφυγε</quote> (as in <ref n="Hom. Od. 8.32"> <title>Od.</title>8. 32</ref>, a like case), it is the second <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέ</quote>, belonging to <quote xml:lang="grc"> αὐτός</quote>, that is parallel with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδ_126"> here.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπέβαλε_126">: since the reference is to an eternal law, it seems best to take the aor. as=a perfect, rather than as gnomic (‘usually imposes’). For the sense, cp. <ref n="Eur. Med. 1112"> Eur. <title>Med.</title>1112</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς οὖν λύει</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">τήνδ’ ἔτι λύπην</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">θνητοῖσι θεοὺς ἐπιβάλλειν</quote> <emph>; </emph> <ref n="Hom. Il. 6.357"> Il.6. 357</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷσιν ἐπὶ Ζεὺς θῆκε κακὸν μόρον</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπὶ_κυκλοῦσιν_129">=<quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐπικυκλοῦσι</quote>, by tmesis: ‘come round in turn’ to all. Others prefer to join </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπὶ_πᾶσι_129">, ‘over the heads of all,’ thinking that this suits the imagery (from stars) better; but the first view seems more in accord with idiom. There is no other sound instance of an intrans. <quote xml:lang="grc">κυκλεῖν</quote> in a writer of the 5th cent. B.C.; for in <ref n="Soph. El. 1365"> <title>El.</title>1365</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κυκλοῦνται</quote> is certainly right; it was so written by the first hand in L, and then altered by another to <quote xml:lang="grc">κυκλοῦσι</quote>. But Arist. uses <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνακυκλεῖν</quote> intransitively: <title>De Gen. et Corr.</title> 2. 11 (p. 338 <emph>a</emph> 4) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνάγκη</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν γένεσιν</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνακυκλεῖν καὶ ἀνακάμπτειν</quote>: and so again in <title>Meteor.</title> 1. 3 (p. 339 <emph>b</emph> 28). In later Greek, too, this usage was current, as appears from <ref n="Plut. Septem 160f">Plut. <title>Mor.</title>160</ref>F (<quote xml:lang="grc">δελφῖνες...κυκλοῦντες</quote>) There is no reason, then, for doubting that Soph. admitted the use here; cp. the intrans. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπινωμᾶν</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">προσενώμα</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 168">Soph. <title>Ph.</title>168</ref> <ref n="Soph. Phil. 717.">Soph. Ph., 717.</ref>Nauck, holding with Herm. that </gloss>
