<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κυκλοῦσιν_129"> must be transitive, adopts his </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χαρὰν_129"> for </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χαρὰ_129">, and further changes </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἷον_129"> to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰὲν_129">, thus destroying the beautiful simile, and reducing <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρκτου...κέλευθοι</quote> to an equivalent for <quote xml:lang="grc">περιτελλόμεναι ὧραι</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄρκτου_κέλευθοι_129">. As the Great Bear moves ever round the pole, so joy and sorrow come round in unceasing rotation. The peculiar fitness of the comparison is in the fact that the Bear never disappears below the horizon: <ref n="Hom. Il. 18.487"> <title>Il.</title>18. 487</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρκτον τ’...ἥ τ’ αὐτοῦ στρέφεται</quote>, ‘that revolves <emph>in its place</emph>,’—‘having no share in the baths of Ocean.’ <cit> <bibl n="Ov. Met. 13.293">Ov. <title>Met.</title> 13. 293</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">immunemque aequoris arcton.</quote> </cit> Cf. Soph. fr. 399 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρκτου στροφάς τε καὶ κυνὸς ψυχρὰν δύσιν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτ_αἰόλα_132"> (94) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νὺξ_132"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>, the ‘paratactic’ form, instead of, ‘<emph>as</emph> night does not abide, <emph>so</emph> neither does woe,’ etc.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κῆρες_132">, here merely ‘calamities,’ <quote xml:lang="grc">συμφοραί</quote>, a sense recognised by Hesych. <emph>s. v.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">κῆρες</quote>. The sing. oft. has this meaning (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 454">cp. 454</ref>): but the plur. usu. denotes either (<emph>a</emph>) ‘the Fates,’ as in <ref n="Soph. OT 472"> <title>O. T.</title>472</ref>, or at least ‘<emph>death</emph>-dooms,’ as in <ref n="Hom. Il. 12.326"> <title>Il.</title>12. 326.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_βέβακε_132">: the subject is <emph> each</emph> of the preceding nouns, the verb agreeing in number with the nearest ( <ref n="Soph. OC 8"> <title>O. C.</title>8</ref> n.): ‘but (each) is suddenly gone (from one), while joy, and the loss of it, come to <emph>another</emph> man in his turn.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῷδ_132"> is opposed to the <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ μὲν</quote> implied in the preceding clause. It is true that the main point is the changing experience of the individual, rather than the transference of joy or woe to his neighbour. But these two notions are closely linked here by the image of joy and woe coming round, as the Bear revolves about the pole. Cp. <ref n="Hdt. 1. 207">Her.1. 207</ref>(Croesus to Cyrus), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκεῖνο πρῶτον μάθε, ὡς κύκλος τῶν ἀνθρωπηίων ἐστὶ πρηγμάτων, περιφερόμενος δὲ οὐκ ἐᾷ αἰεὶ τοὺς αὐτοὺς εὐτυχέειν</quote>.—For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χαίρειν_καὶστέρεσθαι_132"> as nomin. (without art.) to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπέρχεται_132">, cp. <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 181">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 181</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">παρ’ ἄκοντας ἦλθε σωφρονεῖν</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἃ_137">, ‘as to which things,’ ‘wherefore’: <ref n="Isoc. 8.122">Isocr. or. 8 § 122</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἃ καὶ πάντων μάλιστ’ ἄν τις θαυμάσειεν ὅτι προχειρίζεσθε δημαγωγούς</quote>. So the sing. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅ</quote>, <ref n="Thuc. 2. 40">Thuc.2. 40</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃ τοῖς ἄλλοις ἀμαθία μὲν θράσος λογισμὸς δὲ ὄκνον φέρει</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴσχειν_137">, and that </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάδ_137"> is pleonastic. But this view is not proved by the alleged examples. They are:—(1) <ref n="Eur. Andr. 1115">Eur. <title>Andr.</title>1115</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧν Κλυταιμνήστρας τόκος</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">εἷς ἦν, ἁπάντων τῶνδε μηχανορράφος</quote>. Here, however, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧν</quote> is masc., referring to the <quote xml:lang="grc">λόχος</quote> mentioned just before, and a comma should follow <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦν</quote>. (2) <title>I. A.</title> 155 <quote xml:lang="grc">σφραγῖδα φύλας<*> <*>ν ἐπὶ δέλτῳ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τήνδε κομίζεις</quote>. Here the <emph>v. l.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῇδε</quote> is clearly right.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέγω_137"> as=‘command,’ with acc. and inf., cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 101"> <title>Ph.</title>101</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐλπίσιν_ἴσχειν_137">: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν</quote> would usu. be added to the dat.: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 897"> <title>Ant.</title>897</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ἐλπίσιν τρέφω</quote>. So <ref n="Thuc. 2. 8">Thuc.2. 8</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀργῇ εἶχον...τοὺς Ἀθηναίους</quote>, instead of the regular <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ὀργῇ</quote> (id. 2. 18 etc.). Cp. too <ref n="Soph. OC 1678">Soph. <title>O. C.</title>1678</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν</quote> [MSS. <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ</quote>] <quote xml:lang="grc">πόθῳ λάβοις</quote>, with <ref n="Plut. Alc. 18"> Plut. <title>Alc.</title>18</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀργῇ δ’ ἅμα καὶ φόβῳ τὸ γεγονὸς λαμβάνοντες.—<emph>τὰν ἄνασσαν</emph> </quote>, wife of the son of Zeus, and so having the better reason to hope. — </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄβουλον_137">, having no <quote xml:lang="grc"> πρόνοια</quote> for them: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 546">Soph. <title>El.</title> 546</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀβούλου...πατρός</quote> (alluding to Agamemnon's sacrifice of his daughter). Racine has an unconscious echo of this verse, <title>Athalie</title>, acte 2, sc. 7, ‘Dieu laissat-il jamais ses enfants au besoin?’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπεικάσαι_141">: cp. <ref n="Eur. Orest. 1298"> Eur. <title>Or.</title>1298</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἑλένης τὸ κώκυμ’ ἐστίν, ὡς ἀπεικάσαι</quote>. These are isolated examples of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπεικάζω</quote> so used: for in <ref n="Soph. OC 16">Soph. <title>O. C.</title>16</ref>(where see n.) we must read <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς σάφ’ εἰκάσαι</quote>. Elsewhere <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπεικάζειν τι</quote> is ‘to express the likeness of a thing,’ either in art, or (as in Soph. fr. 154. 2) by a comparison. Hence Herm. wished to read here the usual word <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐπεικάσαι</quote> (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 1220">cp. 1220</ref>). He dismissed the example in the <title>Orestes</title> by saying that there <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἀπεικάσαι</quote> means, ‘to <emph>compare</emph> the voice heard with Helen's voice’: but that is obviously a forced explanation. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεικάζω</quote> is <*> to ‘enter upon’ conjecture, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπί</quote> giving the notion of advance, as in <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπινοῶ</quote>: <*> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπεικάζω</quote>, when used as here, is rath<*> throw off,’ or ‘hazard,’ a guess,—<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπ<*></quote> <*>ing used as in <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποκινδυνεύω</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θυμοφ_142">, from the epic <quote xml:lang="grc">θυμοφθόρος</quote>, occurs only here: for the form cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ψυχορραγῶ</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μήτ_εἶ_143">: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘mayest <*> remain ignorant,—as thou now art <*> the combination of a wish with a fact <*> 582 ff., <ref n="Soph. Ant. 686"> <title>Ant.</title>686</ref> n.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νῦν_δ_143"> has <*> authority than </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νῦν_τ_143">. Greek express<*> had a pervading bent towards antith<*> and this tendency sometimes asserted <*> after a sentence had begun in the <*>actic’ form. Thus here, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νῦν_δ_143_2"> sprang <*> the thought, ‘you may, indeed, know <*> the future,—though I trust that you will not,—but now, at least, you do <*>’ I therefore keep <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν δ’</quote>. Each trad<*>nal instance of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τε_δέ_143"> should be care<*> weighed before changing <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote> to <quote xml:lang="grc">τε</quote>. <*> 285 f.<ref n="Soph. Ant. 333"> Ant., 333</ref> f.<ref n="Soph. Ant. 1151"> Ant., 1151</ref> ff.: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1096"> <title>Ant.</title>1096</ref>, <title>P</title><<title>*>12 f.</title><<title>*> and for the negative </title><<title>quote lang=greek>mh/te</title><<title>/quote> followed by </title> <<title>quote lang=greek>de/</title><<title>/quote>, </title> <<title>title>O. C.</title> 421 f. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τ_νεάζον_144"> <quote xml:lang="grc"> κ.τ.λ.</quote> The young life grows in ‘regions of its own,’— sheltered, like some tender plant, from scorching heat, from violent rain, and from rough winds. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοιοῖσδε_144"> refers to the preceding words, <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν δ’ ἄπειρος εἶ</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘such’=‘thus untroubled.’ For this retrospective <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιόσδε</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 148."> <title>Ai.</title>148.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βόσκεται_144">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 558"> <title>Ai.</title>558</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τέως δὲ κούφοις πνεύμασιν βόσκου, νέαν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ψυχὴν ἀτάλλων. <emph>χώροισιν αὑτοῦ</emph> </quote>: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς ἰδίοις αὑτοῦ τόποις</quote>. He notices the other reading </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτοῦ_144">, which Paley supports by the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοῦ ἐνὶ Τροίῃ</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 2.237"> <title>Il.</title>2. 237</ref>), etc.: but here it would be both weak and obscure. For the image of the sheltered plant, cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 18.56"> <title>Il.</title>18. 56</ref>(Thetis of Achilles), <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ δ’ ἀνέδραμεν ἔρνεϊ ἶσος</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν μὲν ἐγὼ θρέψασα, φυτὸν ὣς γουνῷ ἀλωῆς κ.τ.λ.</quote> —</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θάλπος_πνευμάτων_144">: Schneid. cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 5.478"> <title>Od.</title>5. 478</ref>(of <quote xml:lang="grc">θάμνοι</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς μὲν ἄρ’ οὔτ’ ἀνέμων διάη μένος ὑγρὸν ἀέντων</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε ποτ’ ἠέλιος φαέθων ἀκτῖσιν ἔβαλλεν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτ’ ὄμβρος περάασκε διαμπερές</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καί_144"> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νιν_144">—which most of the conjectures assume to be corrupt—is confirmed by a fragment of the orator Antiphon (no. XXVIII. 10 in Sauppe, <title>Oratt. Att.</title> vol. II. p. 151), where he speaks of education as a permanent influence:—<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν νέῳ σώματι ὅταν τις τὴν παίδευσιν γενναίαν ἐναρόσῃ, ζῇ τοῦτο καὶ θάλλει διὰ παντὸς τοῦ βίου, καὶ αὐτὸ οὔτε ὄμβρος οὔτε ἀνομβρία ἀφαιρεῖται</quote>. The last sentence is manifestly a reminiscence of <quote xml:lang="grc">καί νιν οὐ θάλπος θεοῦ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτ’ ὄμβρος κ.τ.λ.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡδοναῖς_147">, a dat. of attendant circumstance, ‘amid’ them.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξαίρει_βίον_147">, ‘uplifts its life’; a phrase suggested by the image of the plant shooting up (cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 18.56"> <title>Il.</title>18. 56</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνέδραμεν</quote> , <ref n="Hom. Od. 6.163"> <title>Od.</title>6. 163</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρνος ἀνερχόμενον</quote>), but also implying, ‘<emph>exults in</emph> its life’: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1066"> <title>Ai.</title>1066</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδὲν δεινὸν ἐξάρῃς μένος.—<emph>ἕως</emph> </quote> without <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote>, as <ref n="Soph. Phil. 764"> <title>Ph.</title>764</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 77"> <title>O. C.</title>77</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 555"> <title>Ai.</title>555</ref>: but <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕως ἄν</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1000"> <title>Ph.</title>1000</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 834"> <title>O. T.</title>834</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 114"> <title>O. C.</title>114</ref>, fr. 736. 5. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_νυκτὶ_149">, though virtually equiv. to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐννυχίων</quote>, belongs by constr. to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λάβῃ_149">: in the (sleepless) night she receives her portion of those cares which haunt a wife. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 29">Cp. 29</ref> f.: <ref n="Aristoph. Kn. 1290">Ar. <title>Eq.</title>1290</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐννυχίαισι</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">φροντίσι</quote>.—Not: ‘on the marriage-night.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἤτοι_ἢ_149">, as <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1182"> <title>Ant.</title>1182</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 662">Aesch. <title> Ag.</title>662</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Ion 431">Eur. <title>Ion</title>431</ref>: but <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ... ἤτοι</quote> ( <ref n="Pind. N. 4">Pind. <title> N.</title>4. 5</ref>) does not occur in </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_φοβουμένη_149">, lit., ‘feeling a fear from <emph>the quarter of</emph> her husband’; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘fearing on his account.’ Cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 783"> <title>El.</title>783</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν δ’ ἀπηλλάγην φόβου</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς τῆσδ’</quote>, where the last three words cohere. It is needless to take the partic. as pass. (‘alarmed <emph>by</emph> him’). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὑτοῦ_151">, the masc., because, though thinking of a wife, she puts her thought in an abstract form: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 771"> <title>El.</title>771</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲ γὰρ κακῶς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πάσχοντι μῖσος ὧν τέκῃ προσγίγνεται</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 463"> <title>Ant.</title>463</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">ὅστις</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακοῖσιν_οἷς_151">=<quote xml:lang="grc">κακὰ οἷς</quote>. The antecedent, when attracted into the case of the relat. pron., usu. follows it ( <ref n="Soph. OC 56"> <title>O. C.</title>56</ref>), <emph>unless</emph> it stands at the beginning of the sentence, in acc. (as below<ref n="Soph. OC 283"> O. C., 283</ref> n.), or, more rarely, in nom. ( <ref n="Soph. OC 1150"> <title>O. C.</title>1150</ref>). But the peculiar form found here can be paralleled. <ref n="Plat. Meno 96a">Plat. <title>Men.</title> 96 A</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχεις οὖν εἰπεῖν ἄλλου ὁτουοῦν πράγματος οὗ οἱ μὲν φάσκοντες διδάσκαλοι εἷναι...ὁμολογοῦνται κ.τ.λ.</quote> <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 2.2">Dem. or. 2 § 2</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ μόνον πόλεων καὶ τόπων ὧν ἦμέν ποτε κύριοι φαίνεσθαι προϊεμένους</quote> </cit>. [<quote xml:lang="grc">προΐεσθαι</quote> never takes a gen., like <quote xml:lang="grc">μεθίεσθαι</quote>.]—It is also possible to take </gloss>

data.quote:
μὴ μόνον πόλεων καὶ τόπων ὧν ἦμέν ποτε κύριοι
							φαίνεσθαι προϊεμένους

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακοῖσιν_οἷς_151_2"> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">οἵοις κακοῖς</quote>: for this use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅς</quote>, see on <ref n="Soph. OC 1171"> <title>O. C.</title>1171</ref>, and cp. <ref n="Dem. 18.16">Dem. or. 18 § 16</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑτέρῳ δ’ ὅτῳ κακόν τι δώσομεν ζητεῖν</quote>. But I prefer the other view. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὲν_δὴ_153">: the only Sophoclean instance of this formula, which was always rarer than either <quote xml:lang="grc">μὲν οὖν</quote> alone, or <quote xml:lang="grc">μὲν δή</quote> (627). <quote xml:lang="grc">δή</quote> here really=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἤδη</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 823"> <title>Ant.</title>823</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤκουσα δή</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦμος_155">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1134"> <title>O. T.</title>1134</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὴν_τελευταίαν_155">: when he left home (for Lydia), fifteen months before: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 39">cp. 39</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέλτον_157">: the tablet mentioned in 47, recording the oracle given to Heracles at Dodona.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγγεγραμμένην_157"> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυνθήμαθ_157">, ‘inscribed with tokens,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, the writing in which Heracles had taken down the oracle (1167). The acc. with the pass. partic. denotes the object of the act. verb (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγγράφω ξυνθήματα δέλτῳ</quote>): cp. <ref n="Hdt. 7. 69">Her.7. 69</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λεοντέας ἐναμμένοι</quote>: <ref n="Xen. Anab. 5. 4. 32">Xen. <title>An.</title>5. 4. 32</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστιγμένους ἀνθέμια</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Verg. Ecl. 3">Verg. <title>Ecl.</title> 3. 106</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">inscripti nomina.</quote> </cit> The word </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυνθήματα_157"> recalls the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">σήματα λυγρά</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 6.168"> <title>Il.</title>6. 168</ref>),—now generally held to denote some kind of alphabetic or syllabic writing (<title>Introd. to Homer</title>, p. 112, n. 1). In later Greek <quote xml:lang="grc">συνθήματα</quote> meant a preconcerted cipher: <ref n="Plb. 8.17">Polyb. 8. 17. 9</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">συνθ. λαβὼν καὶ πίστεις</quote> (‘a letter in cipher, and credentials’): cp. <ref n="Plb. 8.18">id. 8. 18. 9</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">συνθηματικὰ γράμματα</quote>. There is possibly a touch of designed archaism in the poet's phrase; he may have felt that it suited the heroic age to speak of writing as a mystery. This is more likely than that he thought of Heracles as using secret symbols. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀγῶνας_ἐξιών_159">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 290"> <title>Ai.</title>290</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφορμᾷς πεῖραν</quote> : <ref n="Thuc. 1. 15">Thuc.1. 15</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">στρατείας...οὐκ ἐξῄεσαν</quote>: <ref n="Dem. 19.163">Dem. or. 19 § 163</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτε...τὴν προτέραν ἀπῄρομεν πρεσβείαν.—<emph>οὔπω</emph> </quote>, after </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_159">, the compound negative after the simple, is normal (like <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἔστιν οὐδέν</quote>, etc.); not an irregular repetition, like that in 1014 (n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔτλη_159">, brought himself to do it: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1070">cp. 1070</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὥς_δράσων_160">: for <quote xml:lang="grc">δρᾶν τι</quote> (to do something notable), cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 732"> <title>O. C.</title>732</ref> n.: for the place of <quote xml:lang="grc">τι</quote>, <emph>ib.</emph> 280 n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔτ_ὤν_161">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1217"> <title>Ph.</title>1217</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔτ’ οὐδέν εἰμι</quote>: and, for the place of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔτ’</quote>, also <ref n="Soph. OT 24"> <title>O. T.</title>24</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἶπε_δ_161">: epanaphora: <ref n="Soph. OC 610"> <title>O. C.</title>610</ref> n. The <quote xml:lang="grc">δέλτος</quote> (157) contained the oracle only. Heracles first expounded this (hence the <emph>aor.</emph> part. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προτάξας_161"> in 164): then he gave his testamentary directions,—not in writing, but merely by word of mouth.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἶπε_κτῆσιν_161">, ‘he said what I was to take for myself as marriage-property,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘as my property in right of our marriage.’ This means, in accordance with the Attic usage of the poet's age, that she was to take as her own the dowry (<quote xml:lang="grc"> προῖξ</quote>) which she had brought to her husband, together with any gifts that he might have made to her. Thus a widow is described as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπολιποῦσα τὸν οἶκον καὶ κομιο αμένη τὴν προῖκα</quote>, [Dem. ] or. 40 § 7. The bride's father (or other representative before the law, <quote xml:lang="grc">κύριος</quote>) kept a record of the <quote xml:lang="grc">προῖξ</quote>, with a view to its recovery at the husband's death, or in the event of a divorce: Isaeus or. 3 § 35 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐὰν ἀπολίπῃ ἡ γυνὴ τὸν ἄνδρα, ἢ ἐὰν ὁ ἀνὴρ ἐκπέμψῃ τὴν γυναῖκα, οὐκ ἔξεστι πράξασθαι τῷ δόντι</quote> [<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> the father, or <quote xml:lang="grc"> κύριος</quote>] <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃ μὴ ἐν προικὶ τιμήσας ἔδωκεν</quote>: ‘which, when he gave it, he did not record at a certain value, as part of the dower.’ Thus in [Dem. ] or. 47 § 57 a widow claims some pieces of property on the ground <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτι αὑτῆς εἴη ἐν τῇ προικὶ τετιμημένα</quote>.</gloss>
lemma: εἶπε … ὅ τι χρείη μ’ ἑλέσθαι λέχους κτῆσιν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἣν_νέμοι_161">: ‘what share of their father's land he assigned by division to his sons,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘assigned to them severally.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διαιρετὸν_161">: for the verbal adj. of two terminations, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 384"> <title>O. T.</title>384</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρόνον_προτάξας_164">, having first prescribed the time [for the division of the property],</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_164"> (saying) that,</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡνίκα_κἀνιαύς_164">., when he should have been absent from the country, after his departure, for fifteen months,</gloss>
lemma: ἡνίκα χώρας ἀπείη βεβώς τρίμ. χρόνον κἀνιαύς

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τότε_ζῆν_164"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> The words <quote xml:lang="grc">χρόνον προτάξας</quote> refer to his having expounded the oracle to her <emph> before</emph> he gave the directions as to his property: </gloss>
lemma: τότε χρείη σφε ἢ θανεῖν τῷδε τῷ χρ., ἢ … ζῆν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_164_2"> depends on the notion of ‘saying’ contained in <quote xml:lang="grc">προτάξας</quote>: and the sentence, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς, ἡνίκα...ἀπείη, χρείη</quote>, explains <quote xml:lang="grc">χρόνον προτάξας</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τρίμηνον_κἀνιαύσιος_164"> unaltered, because it is conceivable that, while <quote xml:lang="grc"> τρίμηνον</quote> was prompted by the <quote xml:lang="grc">χρόνον</quote> before it, <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀνιαύσιος</quote> should have been adapted to <quote xml:lang="grc">βεβώς</quote>. Cp. the personal constr. with <quote xml:lang="grc">χρόνιος</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OC 441"> <title>O. C.</title>441</ref> n.), <quote xml:lang="grc">χθιζός, παννύχιος</quote>, etc. But I should prefer </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀνιαύσιον_164">.—The repetition </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρόνον_χρόνου_164"> does not warrant a suspicion (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 554"> <title>O. C.</title>554</ref> n.): it expresses her anxiety to be precise as to the all-important point.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπεκδραμόντα_164"> is lit., ‘having run out from beneath,’ having ‘eluded’ the imminent danger: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1086"> <title>Ant.</title>1086</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν σὺ θάλπος οὐχ ὑπεκδραμεῖ</quote>. <ref n="Hdt. 1. 156">Her.1. 156</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢν τὸ παρεὸν ὑπεκδράμωσι</quote>. As the </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρόνου_τέλος_164"> is here a perilous crisis, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπεκδρ_164">. is more forcible than the conjecture </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπερδραμόντα_164">, which would mean simply, ‘having passed.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοιαῦτ_πόνων_169">. Among the various explanations of the gen. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_πόνων_169">, two seem better than the rest; and I prefer that which I place first.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἱμαρμένα_ἐκτελευτᾶσθαι_169"> rather than with either word alone. ‘He said that such things were destined to be accomplished <emph>in regard to</emph> the toils of Heracles.’ This is only a rarer and bolder form of the gen. used in poetry after verbs of ‘speaking <emph>about</emph>’ ( <ref n="Soph. El. 317"> <title>El.</title>317</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ κασιγνήτου τί φής</quote> <emph>;</emph>), ‘asking about’ ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 439"> <title>Ph.</title>439</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φωτὸς ἐξερήσομαι</quote>), ‘hearing about’ ( <ref n="Soph. OC 307"> <title>O. C.</title>307</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κλύων σοῦ</quote>). And there is another passage of Sophocles which shows a similarly bold use of it, viz., <ref n="Soph. OC 355"> <title>O. C.</title>355</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">μαντεῖα</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἃ τοῦδ’ ἐχρήσθη σώματος</quote>, ‘the oracles that had been given <emph>concerning</emph> me’: an example which (to my mind) strongly confirms this view.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκτελευτᾶσθαι_169">: destined to be accomplished as part of (in the number of) his toils. For this we might compare such uses of the partitive gen. as <quote xml:lang="grc">πλεῖν τοῦ πρώτου στόλου</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 73"> <title>Ph.</title>73</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξετάζεσθαι τῶν συγχαιρόντων</quote> ( <ref n="Dem. 21.202">Dem. or. 21 § 202</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀριθμεῖσθαι τῶν μακάρων</quote>, etc.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόνων_169"> depends on </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸςθεῶν_εἱμαρμένα_169"> as if it were a subst. <quote xml:lang="grc"> εἱμαρμένην</quote>:— ‘he said that such <emph>a doom for the toils of H.</emph> was to be fulfilled.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόνων_169_2"> depends on </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκτελευτᾶσθαι_169_2"> as equiv. to <quote xml:lang="grc">τελευτὴν γίγνεσθαι</quote>: ‘he said that such events were <emph>to be accomplished as the end of</emph> his toils.’ I do not think that the Greek words will bear either of the two latter versions. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_φηγὸν_171"> <quote xml:lang="grc"> κ.τ.λ.</quote> A note on the Oracle at Dodona, illustrative of this passage and of vv. 1166—1168, will be found in the Appendix. The signs were taken from the movement and rustling of the oak's leaves; and these signs were interpreted by the priestesses called <quote xml:lang="grc"> Πελειάδες</quote>. Cp. fr. 414 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰς θεσπιῳδοὺς ἱερίας Δωδωνίδας</quote>. Euripides spoke of three such priestesses; but Pindar, like Sophocles, gave the number as two (schol. here). In saying that the oak ‘<emph>spake</emph>’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">αὐδῆσαι</quote>) by their mouths, he follows the established mode of expression with regard to it. See, <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>, Lucian <title> Amor.</title> 31 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ ἐν Δωδώνῃ φηγὸς...ἱερὰν ἀπορρήξασα φωνήν</quote>. Constantine Porphyr. 2. 55 <quote xml:lang="grc">Δωδώνη, ἐφ’ ἧς ἡ δρῦς ἡ φθεγγομένη τὰ τῶν δαιμόνων μυστήρια</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐδῆσαι_ἐκ_171">. It was through the inspired <emph>lips</emph> that the utterance of the oak became a ‘voice.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Δωδῶνι_171">, as in frr. 413, 415: so fr. 412 <quote xml:lang="grc">Δωδῶνος</quote>. The nom. <quote xml:lang="grc">Δωδών</quote> is not extant; unless it should be restored to a verse which Byz.Steph. , <emph>s.v.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">Δωδώνη</quote>, quotes from Simmias of Rhodes (<emph>c.</emph> 320 B.C.?), <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζηνὸς ἕδος Κρονίδαο μάκαιρ’ ὑπεδέξατο Δωδώ</quote>. For the locative dat., cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 900">Soph. <title>O. T.</title>900</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν Ἀβαῖσι ναόν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ναμέρτεια_173">: for the Doric form, see on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 715"> <title>Ant.</title>715</ref> n. Cp. <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 246"> Aesch. <title>Pers.</title>246</ref>(dial.) <quote xml:lang="grc">ναμερτῆ λόγον</quote> (so Porson for <quote xml:lang="grc">νημερτῆ</quote>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶνδε_173">=the predictions (of the alternative issues, prosperity or death): </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ναμέρτεια_173_2"> =‘precision,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> the precise term of fifteen months. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συμβαίνει_173">=‘comes right,’ ‘tallies’ (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 1174">cp. 1174</ref>; and with dat., 1164). Thus the sense is:—‘The precise term foretold by these prophecies tallies [with the period which has actually elapsed] at the present time,’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_173"> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε</quote> ) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τελεσθῆναι_χρεών_173">, ‘so that they must be fulfilled.’ (With <quote xml:lang="grc">χρεών</quote> we may understand either <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστί</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶναι</quote>: the former is simpler: for the ellipse, cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 668"> <title>Ai.</title>668</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρχοντές εἰσιν, ὥσθ’ ὑπεικτέον</quote>.) In other words, ‘This is precisely the time when the fulfilment of these predictions falls due.’ The schol. saw that </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_173_2"> is for <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε ὁπότερον πραχθῆναι</quote>). The change to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ᾧ_173"> is needless, and worse.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡδέως_175"> has been variously altered, from a feeling that it is out of harmony with the tone of 29 f. and 149; but the word is well fitted to express that <emph>even</emph> a sound sleep, when it came to her, was apt to be suddenly broken.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φόβῳ_175"> goes with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταρβοῦσαν_175">, which it strengthens: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 65"> <title>O. T.</title>65</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕπνῳ γ’ εὕδοντα</quote> (n.): <ref n="Soph. Ant. 427"> <title>Ant.</title>427</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γόοισιν ἐξῴμωξεν</quote>. ( <ref n="Soph. OC 1625"> <title>O. C.</title>1625</ref> and <ref n="Soph. Phil. 225"> <title>Ph.</title>225</ref> are not similar.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐφημίαν_178"> refers to the ominous <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστερημένην</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 361"> <title>Ai.</title>361</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">ΑΙ....ἀλλά με συνδάϊξον</quote>. | <quote xml:lang="grc">ΧΟ. εὔφημα φώνει.—<emph> καταστεφῆ</emph> </quote>, with a wreath of laurel: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 83"> <title>O. T.</title>83</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_λόγων_178"> refers to <quote xml:lang="grc">καταστεφῆ</quote>: ‘in view of (suitably to) joyous news.’ Brunck's reading, <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς <emph>χάριν</emph> λόγων</quote>, would be weaker (‘on account of his tidings’). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρῶτος_ἀγγέλων_180">, forestalling Lichas: the words mark his eagerness to assert his claim on her gratitude (190 f.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γὰρ_181"> as in 155.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρατοῦντα_181">, the pres. (=‘is victorious’), as oft. <quote xml:lang="grc">νικῶν</quote>: cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. OT 437."> <title>O. T.</title>437.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπαρχὰς_181"> refers more especially to the train of <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰχμαλωτίδες</quote> which the Messenger had seen with Lichas, but can also include the spoils which were to come later with Heracles. For <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπαρχή</quote> said of human beings, cp. Arist. fr. 443 (p. 1550 <emph>b</emph> 39) <emph>ap.</emph> <ref n="Plut. Thes. 16">Plut. <title>Thes.</title>16</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Κρῆτας...ἀνθρώπων ἀπαρχὴν εἰς Δελφοὺς ἀποστέλλειν</quote>. So in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 202">Soph. <title>Ph.</title>202</ref> the captive <quote xml:lang="grc">Φοίνισσαι</quote> describe themselves as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκροθίνια Λοξίᾳ.—<emph>θεοῖσι</emph> </quote> (for the synizesis, <title>O.C.</title> 964 n.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖςἐγχωρίοις_181">, esp. Zeus <quote xml:lang="grc">Οἰταῖος</quote> (200), Apollo, and Artemis Ortygia (210 ff.). <ref n="Soph. Trach. 245">Cp. 245</ref> (of the captives) <quote xml:lang="grc">αὑτῷ κτῆμα καὶ θεοῖς κριτόν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίνα_τόνδε_184">: <ref n="Soph. OC 68"> <title>O. C.</title>68</ref> n., <ref n="Soph. Phil. 441."> <title>Ph.</title>441.</ref>The Messenger has been explicit; but she is bewildered with joy. So in <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 268">Aesch. <title> Ag.</title>268</ref> the Chorus makes the <quote xml:lang="grc">κῆρυξ</quote> repeat his tidings. Cp. below<ref n="Aesch. Ag. 876">Aesch. Ag., 876f</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολύζηλον_185">, in this context, is best taken as=‘exciting much <quote xml:lang="grc">ζῆλος</quote>,’ ‘admired by all’: though it could also mean merely, ‘very prosperous.’ In <ref n="Soph. OT 381"> <title>O. T.</title>381</ref>(n.) it means, ‘full of emulation.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φανέντα_185"> adds vividness to the thought of the joy that awaits her: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 199">cp. 199</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμφανῆ</quote>, 224 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐναργῆ.—<emph> σὺν</emph> </quote> of attendant circumstance ( <ref n="Soph. OT 17"> <title>O. T.</title>17</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κράτει_νικηφόρῳ_185">: <quote xml:lang="grc"> κράτος</quote> is the superior strength, the mastery ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 594"> <title>Ph.</title>594</ref> n.), which <quote xml:lang="grc">vίκην φέρεται</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 497">cp. 497</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OC 1088"> <title>O. C.</title>1088</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σθένει )πινικείῳ</quote> (‘triumphant might’). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀστῶν_ξένων_187">, <foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign> ‘from whom in the world?’ Cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 975"> <title>El.</title>975</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς γάρ ποτ’ ἀστῶν ἢ ξένων</quote>. So far as the <quote xml:lang="grc">ξένοι</quote> are definitely conceived here, they may be supposed to arrive from Euboea. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βουθερεῖ_188">: only here. Hesychius gives the right sense,—<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ᾧ βόες θέρους ὥρᾳ νέμονται</quote>. A poet might feel that a simple compound of <quote xml:lang="grc">βοῦς</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">θέρος</quote> would suffice for a picturesque epithet of <quote xml:lang="grc">λειμών</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign>, ‘the meadow <emph>of the oxen's summer</emph>’ would readily suggest ‘the meadow which is the summer pasture of oxen.’ Those who object to such a compound seem to try it by the standard of prose.—Hesychius adds to his explanation of <quote xml:lang="grc"> βουθερεῖ</quote>: <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ <emph>βουθόρῳ</emph> τὸ αὐτό</quote>. Wecklein adopts this, as=‘in which oxen jump about’: but surely they must be <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰστροπλῆγες</quote> to behave so. The word occurs only in <ref n="Aesch. Supp. 301">Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title> 301</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βουθόρῳ ταύρῳ</quote> (=qui vaccam salit).—The <quote xml:lang="grc">λειμών</quote> was in the plain of Malis, between Trachis and the Malian Gulf: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 194">cp. 194</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοι_190"> implies that the motive was a natural one, which she will readily comprehend: cp. the frankness of the messenger in <ref n="Soph. OT 1005"> <title>O. T.</title>1005</ref>, and of the <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἔμπορος</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 552."> <title>Ph.</title>552.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴπερ_εὐτυχεῖ_192">: if he comes with good news, and may therefore expect a cordial welcome (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 229">cp. 229</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_χρώμενος_192">, because he does not enjoy much facility (for moving forward). For the partic. in a reply, cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1228."> <title>Ph.</title>1228.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Μηλιεὺς_194">: for the Ionic form, cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 4"> <title>Ph.</title>4</ref> n. Trachis was on a rocky spur under the heights (‘Trachinian Rocks’) which bound the plain of Malis on s. and W.; the distance from the (ancient) coastline of the Malian Gulf was about six miles.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἅπας_194">: not only the <quote xml:lang="grc"> Τραχίνιοι</quote> (the highlanders of Malis), but the <quote xml:lang="grc">Παράλιοι</quote> also. As to Malis, cp. Introd. <title> Ph.</title>p. ix. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρίνει_195">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνακρίνει</quote> : <ref n="Soph. Trach. 314">cp. 314</ref>, 388, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 399."> <title>Ant.</title>399.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παραστάς_195">: a crowd has gathered round him (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κύκλῳ_195">); and the eager people keep pressing close up to him, to put their questions. So this partic. is used of one who comes close up to a person, in a threatening way: <ref n="Soph. OC 992"> <title>O. C.</title>992</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ τίς σε</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">κτείνοι παραστάς</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 295"> <title>El.</title>295</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βοᾷ παραστᾶσ’</quote>. Thus, while the conjecture </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="περιστάς_195"> would merely supplement <quote xml:lang="grc">κύκλῳ, <emph>παραστάς</emph> </quote> really adds a new touch.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βῆναι_195">, aor. <emph>set</emph> forward from the place where he is halting: stronger than <quote xml:lang="grc"> βαίνειν</quote>, <emph>keep moving</emph> on. Cp. <quote xml:lang="grc"> βῆναι</quote> said of death, <ref n="Soph. OC 1226."> <title>O. C.</title>1226.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_ποθοῦν_196"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> I leave </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_ποθοῦν_196_2"> in the text, not feeling certain that it is corrupt; though I am disposed to read, with ThomasE. , </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_ποθείν_196">. A discussion of other views will be found in the Appendix. Here I note these points.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_ποθοῦν_196_3"> is sound, it means, ‘<emph>the feeling of desire</emph>’ in the questioner's mind. It cannot mean ‘his desire’ in the sense of ‘that which <emph>is desired</emph> by him’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ ποθούμενον</quote> schol.). This, at least, is the inference from all the evidence available: see nn. on <ref n="Soph. OC 267"> <title>O. C.</title>267</ref> <ref n="Soph. OC 1604."> O. C., 1604.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_ἐκμαθεῖν_196"> cannot mean, then, ‘to learn what is desired.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκμαθεῖν_196">, if it is to govern </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_ποθοῦν_196_4">, must be explained as having a pregnant sense, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκπλῆσαι μαθών</quote>, ‘to <emph>satisfy</emph> the desire <emph>by learning.</emph>’ Some analogies might be quoted ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 399"> <title>Ant.</title>399</ref>): but the phrase seems impossibly harsh.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_ποθοῦν_196_5"> as an acc. of reference: ‘<emph>with regard to</emph> his curiosity, wishing to be fully informed.’ This is awkward; but it is not inconceivable. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐχ_δὲ_198">: the omission of <quote xml:lang="grc">μέν</quote> is like that in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 971"> <title>Ph.</title>971</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ εἶ κακὸς σύ, πρὸς κακῶν δ’ ἀνδρῶν μαθὼν κ.τ.λ.</quote> Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 276"> <title>Ant.</title>276</ref>(the <quote xml:lang="grc">φύλαξ</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">πάρειμι δ’ ἄκων</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐχ ἑκοῦσιν</quote>. Here, too, perhaps, the conceit is meant to be a trait of homely humour. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_λειμῶν_200">. The uplands of Oeta were sacred to Zeus (1191). Lands dedicated to gods might be cultivated for the profit of the temples (238 n.). Sometimes, however, they were left idle, or served merely for ornament. It was in such cases more especially that they were said to be <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνειμένα</quote>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 761c">Plato <title>Legg.</title> 761 C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ τί που ἄλσος ἢ τέμενος περὶ ταῦτα ἀνειμένον ᾖ, τὰ ῥεύματα ἀφιέντες εἰς αὐτὰ τὰ τῶν θεῶν ἱερὰ κοσμῆσαι</quote> </cit>. Athen. p. 503 C <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς ἀλσώδεις καὶ συσκίους τόπους τοὺς τοῖς θεοῖς ἀνειμένους</quote>. So Demeter reproves the wood-cutter in her grove: <cit> <bibl n="Call. Cer. 47">Callim. <title>Hymn. Cer.</title> 47</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τέκνον, ὅτις τὰ θεοῖσιν ἀνειμένα δένδρεα κόπτεις, | τέκνον, ἐλίνυσον</quote> </cit>. The exquisite verses of Eur. ( <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 75">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 75</ref> ff.) describe an inviolable meadow of Artemis: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔνθ’ οὔτε ποιμὴν ἀξιοῖ φέρβειν βοτά</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτ’ ἦλθέ πω σίδηρος, ἀλλ’ ἀκήρατον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μέλισσα λειμῶν’ ἠρινὸν διέρχεται</quote>. In a Cretan precinct of the Dictaean Zeus, it was forbidden to keep flocks or sheepfolds, to sow, or to cut timber (<title>C. I. G.</title> II. p. 1003). With </gloss>

data.quote:
εἴ τί που ἄλσος ἢ τέμενος περὶ ταῦτα ἀνειμένον
							ᾖ, τὰ ῥεύματα ἀφιέντες εἰς αὐτὰ τὰ τῶν θεῶν ἱερὰ κοσμῆσαι

τέκνον, ὅτις τὰ θεοῖσιν ἀνειμένα δένδρεα
							κόπτεις, | τέκνον, ἐλίνυσον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄτομον_200"> cp. Hesych. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀδρέπανον· ἄδρεπτον· θεοῖς ἀνακείμενον. Σοφοκλῆς</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλὰ_201">, ‘at least’; 320, <ref n="Soph. OC 1276"> <title>O. C.</title>1276</ref> n.: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺν_χρόνῳ_201"> , <ref n="Soph. Aj. 306"> <title>Ai.</title>306</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμφρων μόλις πως σὺν χρόνῳ καθίσταται</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OC 1653."> <title>O. C.</title>1653.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴσω_202">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἔνδον</quote>, as 867, and oft.; but it properly implies motion (336, 492, 693, 900). The form <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴσω</quote> is here used, as in <ref n="Soph. OC 18"> <title>O. C.</title>18</ref>, without metrical necessity; and it has been held that the form </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔσω_202"> (which does not occur in Ar. ) was admitted in Tragedy only when metre required it: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 491">Soph. <title>Ant.</title>491</ref> is, however, an exception.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στέγης_αὐλῆς_202">: the second word here is a mere synonym for the first; hence Kvíčala conjectures <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτῆς</quote>: but see n. on <ref n="Soph. OC 1501."> <title>O. C.</title>1501.</ref>Those ‘within’ are her handmaidens; those ‘without,’ the Chorus.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄμμα_ἀνασχόν_202">: for the fig. sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄμμα</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 987"> <title>O. T.</title>987</ref> n. As said of sunrise, etc., <quote xml:lang="grc"><*>σχω</quote> is more usual than <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνέχω</quote>: yet cp. Bekk. <title>Anecd.</title> p. 400. 4 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνέχειν· τὸ ἀνατέλλειν τὸν ἥλιον ἢ τὴν σελήνην</quote>.</gloss>
lemma: ὄμμα φήμης τῆσδε, ἄελπτον ἐμοὶ ἀνασχόν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ἀνασχόν_202">, is so suggestive of an acc. abs. ( <ref n="Soph. OC 380"> <title>O. C.</title>380</ref> n.), that it had occurred to me, as to Mr Blaydes, to ask whether </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆσδε_202"> ought not to be </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆσδ_ὃ_202">: but the answer, I think, is that this would practically make </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄελπτον_202"> too prominent; the surprise would be more emphasised than the joy. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνολολυξάτω_205"> has been recognised by almost all recent critics and metrists as a certain correction of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνολολύξετε</quote> (L) or <quote xml:lang="grc">‐ατε</quote>. But I should keep the MS. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δόμοις_205">, merely reading </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἁ_205"> for </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_205"> with Erfurdt. The clue to a right interpretation here depends on two points in the context. (1) Deianeira has called for a joyous cry from the women <emph>in the house</emph>, and from those <emph>outside of it</emph> (203 f.). The first words of the Chorus accordingly appeal to the women <emph>in the house</emph>,—as is marked, not only by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δόμοις_205_2">, but by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐφεστίοις_205">, adding that the <emph> men</emph> of the household are to join in. Then, at v. 210, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁμοῦ δὲ...παιᾶν’...ὦ παρθένοι</quote>, the maidens <emph>of the Chorus</emph> are invited to raise the paean. (2) The words </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_ἀρσένων_205"> <foreign xml:lang="grc"> κ.τ.λ.</foreign> could not have been used unless a reference to <emph> women</emph> had <emph>preceded;</emph> it is not enough that it should <emph> follow</emph>, in <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ παρθένοι</quote>, at v. 210.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἁ_μελλόνυμφος_205">, ‘she whose nuptials are soon to come,’ is a poetical phrase for <emph>virgo nubilis</emph>, and denotes the maidens of the household generally. Nauck, reading <quote xml:lang="grc">δόμοις...<emph>ὁ</emph> μελλόνυμφος</quote>, gives this sense to it, but admits that the masc. (‘<emph>quisquis</emph> nubilis est’) is awkward: rather it is impossible. (2) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἁ_μελλόνυμφος_205_2">=‘she who is soon to be (re-)united to a husband,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> Deianeira. This is a forcing of the Greek word which can easily be smoothed over in an English paraphrase, but which would probably have seemed very strange to a Greek. (3) Reading </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δόμος_μελλόνυμφος_205">, ‘the household of maidens,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> ‘the maidens of the household.’ This seems an untenable usage: moreover the metre condemns <quote xml:lang="grc">δόμος</quote>.—Another version of this reading, ‘the house which is soon to receive the husband,’ not only strains <quote xml:lang="grc">μελλόνυμφος</quote>, but fails to supply the necessary antithesis to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρσένων</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνολολυξάτω_205_2">: the <quote xml:lang="grc"> ὀλολυγή</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀλολυγμός</quote> was a cry to the gods, usually expressive of joy or hope, in prayer or sacrifice: and it is especially said of women (<emph>e.g. </emph> <ref n="Hom. Il. 6.301"> Il.6. 301</ref>, <ref n="Hom. Od. 3.450"> <title>Od.</title>3. 450</ref>: <ref n="Aesch. Seven 268">Aesch. <title> Theb.</title>268</ref> etc.). But this verb denotes a cry of horror in <ref n="Soph. El. 750.">Soph. <title>El.</title>750.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δόμοις_205_3">, rather ‘<emph>for</emph> the house’ (dat. of interest) than merely ‘in it’; cp. <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 27">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title>27</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δόμοις</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀλολυγμὸν εὐφημοῦντα τῇδε λαμπάδι</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπορθιάζειν.—<emph> ἀλαλαγαῖς</emph> </quote>, probably due to Triclinius, has been received instead of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλαλαῖς_205"> by many recent edd., in order that the first foot of the verse may be a tribrach (see Analysis Metr.). <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλαλή</quote> was the more frequent form; but the other occurs as a <emph>v. l.</emph> in <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 335">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title>335</ref>, as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλαλαλαί</quote> is a <emph>v. l.</emph> for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλαλαί</quote> in <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 1761">Ar. <title> Av.</title>1761</ref>: and a loss of <quote xml:lang="grc">αλ</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">αγ</quote> would of course have been easy. The <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλαλή</quote> was a cry of triumph ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 133">Soph. <title>Ant.</title>133</ref> n.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοινὸς_207">, fem.: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 751"> <title>O. C.</title>751</ref> n. —The acc. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸνεὐφ_Ἀπ_207">. depends on </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴτω_κλαγγὰ_207"> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὑμνείτω</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 123"> <title>El.</title>123</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τάκεις ...οἰμωγὰν</quote> | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀγαμέμνονα</quote>: <emph>ib.</emph> 556 <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δέ μ’ ὦδ’ ἀεὶ λόγους</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξῆρχες.—<emph>Ἀπόλλω</emph> </quote>, the shorter form of the acc., as in <ref n="Soph. OC 1091"> <title>O. C.</title>1091</ref>(lyr.): like <quote xml:lang="grc"> Ποσειδῶ</quote>, it was used chiefly after <quote xml:lang="grc">νὴ τὸν, μὰ τὸν.—<emph>προστάταν</emph> </quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 637"> <title>El.</title>637</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Φοῖβε προστατήριε</quote>,—with reference to his image being placed in front of houses. Paus. (1. 44. 2) saw at Megara a hieron of Apollo <quote xml:lang="grc">Προστατήριος</quote>. C. O. Müller (<title>Dorians</title> bk II. ch. 2 § 6) points out that the title <quote xml:lang="grc">προστάτης</quote> was given to Apollo in the Ionian colonies of Miletus, on the shores of Pontus. So, as protector of roads, he is <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγυιεύς</quote>. Artemis, too, is called <quote xml:lang="grc">προστατηρία</quote>, <ref n="Aesch. Seven 449.">Aesch. <title>Th.</title>449.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνάγετ_211">: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 125">Soph. <title> El.</title>125</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴθι τὸν αὐτὸν ἔγειρε γόον</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄναγε πολύδακρυν ἁδονάν</quote>: id. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1350">Soph. <title>Ph.</title>1350</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνάγετ’ ἀνάγετε κωκυτόν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἄρτεμιν_Ὀρτυγίαν_213">. The epithet was usu. understood as meaning ‘born in Ortygia.’ That name, like Nysa, was associated with various places (as Syracuse; Aetolia, schol. <ref n="Apollon. 1.419">Apoll. Rh. 1. 419</ref>; Ephesus, <ref n="Strab. 14.639">Strabo 14. 639</ref>); but most frequently with Delos, as a name either for that island itself, or for some islet near it (Rheneia?): cp. <ref n="HH 16"> <title>Hom. h. Apoll.</title> 16</ref>, which describes Artemis and Apollo as born, <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν μὲν ἐν Ὀρτυγίῃ, τὸν δὲ κραναῇ ἐνὶ Δήλῳ</quote>. The epithet is parodied by <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 870">Ar. <title>Av.</title>870</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Λητοῖ ὀρτυγομήτρᾳ</quote> (‘Quail mother’) Asteria, Leto's sister, was said to have escaped from Zeus by taking the form of a quail (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὄρτυξ</quote>): <ref n="Apollod. 1.2">Apollod.1. 2</ref>§ 2. One theory explains the name Ortygia as simply ‘abounding with quails’; another (Preller vol. I. p. 238) supposes that the <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄρτυξ</quote> was taken as the type of a good mother; but the question remains uncertain.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐλαφαβόλον_213"> (like <quote xml:lang="grc"> θαναταφόρα</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OT 180"> <title>O. T.</title>180</ref>): see on <ref n="Soph. OC 1092"> <title>O. C.</title>1092</ref> f.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμφίπυρον_213">: with a torch in each hand: see on <ref n="Soph. OT 207."> <title>O. T.</title>207.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Νύμφας_213">: the <quote xml:lang="grc">Μαλιάδες νύμφαι</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 724"> <title>Ph.</title>724</ref> ff., n.) of Malian hills. woods, and streams. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀείρομ_216">. Homeric verse admits the elision of <quote xml:lang="grc">αι</quote> in the verbal endings <quote xml:lang="grc">‐μαι, ‐σαι</quote> (except in the infin.), <quote xml:lang="grc">‐ται, ‐σθαι</quote>. There is no other example of it in Tragedy; but it does not seem impossible that Sophocles should have used the familiar epic licence in a lyric passage. If we read <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ἀείρομαι</emph> οὐδ’ ἀπώσομαι</quote>, a cyclic dactyl is substituted for a trochee; which does not seem very likely in this metrical context. (Cp. H. H. SchmidtJ. , <title>Compositionslehre</title> p. cxlii., and W. Christ, <title>Metrik</title> p. 378.) In the lemma of the schol. we certainly find <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀείρομαι οὐδ’ ἀπώσομαι</quote>: but that proves nothing.—For the sense (<quote xml:lang="grc">μετεωρίζομαι ἐν τῷ χορεύειν</quote> schol.), cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Eccl. 1179">Ar. <title>Eccl.</title> 1179</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἴρεσθ’ ἄνω, ἰαὶ εὐαί</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_αὐλόν_217">, the instrument associated with religious enthusiasm, and more esp. with the Dionysiac worship. In Attic Tragedy the lyre seems to have been the older instrument (cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 1304">Ar. <title>Ran.</title>1304</ref> <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 1285">Ar. Ran., 1285</ref>): but after the time of Aeschylus, at least, the <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐλός</quote> was the regular one. Cp. MA. üller, <title>Gr. Bühnenalt.</title> p. 192 n. 3.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_τύραννε_217"> clearly refers to the <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐλός</quote> (for the change to the voc., <ref n="Soph. Trach. 99">cp. 99</ref>)—not to Apollo or Dionysus.—The words </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τᾶς_φρενός_217"> bring out the spiritual sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">τύραννε</quote>, and express the compelling influence of the flute. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰδού_μ_218">: this <quote xml:lang="grc">μ’</quote> must depend on <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰδού</quote>: that after </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐοῖ_218"> depends on </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀναταράσσει_218">, the shriek being here literally an ‘interjection.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_κισσός_218">: the ivy was sacred to Dionysus, who is styled <quote xml:lang="grc">κισσεύς</quote> (<ref n="Paus. 1.31.6">Paus. 1. 31 § 6</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">κισσοφόρος, κισσοχαίτης</quote>, etc.: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Ov. Fast. 3.767">Ovid <title>Fasti</title> 3. 767</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">hedera est gratissima Baccho.</quote> </cit> It was worn by bacchanals ( <ref n="Eur. Ba. 81">Eur. <title>Bacch.</title> 81</ref>); though there seems to be no proof that it was worn, at least ordinarily, by tragic choreutae. Here, however, the Trachinian maidens <emph> imagine</emph> themselves to be bacchanals; the music of the <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐλός</quote> suggests the spell of the <quote xml:lang="grc">κισσός</quote>: and they speak as if the ivy on their brows was sending its mystic power through their whole frames, stirring them to the dance. Just so the laurel was the symbol of poetical inspiration.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βακχίαν_ἅμιλλαν_218">, the Bacchic competition of eager dancers, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, the swift dance itself. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅμιλλα</quote> is oft. thus associated with eager speed: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1062"> <title>O. C.</title>1062</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥιμφαρμάτοις ἁμίλλαις</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 861"> <title>El.</title>861</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χαλαργοῖς ἐν ἁμίλλαις</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1065"> <title>Ant.</title>1065</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τρόχους ἁμιλλητῆρας.—<emph>ὑποστρέφων</emph> </quote>, lit., ‘whirling a little’ (cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑποκινεῖν</quote>): <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, just beginning to set the dance in movement. Not, ‘bringing <emph>back.</emph>’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰὼ_Παιάν_221">: the refrain (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφύμνιον</quote>) of the paean: the whole Chorus would strike in here (cp. n. on 205—224). Dindorf adds a second <quote xml:lang="grc">Παιάν</quote>, on the assumption that the verse is an iambic dimeter, comparing <ref n="Aristoph. Ach. 1212">Ar. <title>Ach.</title> 1212</ref>, where the MSS. have <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰὼ ἰὼ Παιὰν Παιάν</quote> (<title>Metra Aeschyli</title> 1. etc., p. 119). But the MS. reading is kept by most critics, including W. Christ (<title>Metrik</title>, § 443). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἵδ_222">. The MSS. have </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴδεἴδ_222">. The hiatus would be justified by the slight pause after <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴδε</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 832"> <title>Ph.</title>832</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴθι ἴθι μοι παιών</quote> (n.). But most edd. agree with Dindorf in omitting <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἴδε</quote>: and they are probably right, since it disturbs the otherwise regular metre of vv. 221—224. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάδ_223">: the good tidings (180 ff.) of which their minds are full.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐναργῆ_223"> (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 11">cp. 11</ref> n.) is strengthened by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντίπρῳρα_223">. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδέ_παρῇλθε_225">: the acc. of the part (=<quote xml:lang="grc">φρουροῦν ὄμμα</quote>) follows the pers. pron.; cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1301"> <title>Ph.</title>1301</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέθες με...χεῖρα</quote>. The subject to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρῆλθε_225"> (‘this sight’) is easily supplied from <quote xml:lang="grc">τάδ’</quote> in 223. For the phrase, cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 151"> <title>Ph.</title>151</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φρουρεῖν ὄμμ’</quote> (‘that the eye should watch’). The MS. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φρουρὰ_225"> is possible: the sense would then be, ‘nor has <emph>the task of watching</emph> with the eye <emph>escaped my care.</emph>’ But the phrase appears somewhat less natural; and the nom. <quote xml:lang="grc"> φρουρὰ</quote> may have been generated by <quote xml:lang="grc"> παρῆλθε.— <emph>μὴ λεύσσειν</emph> </quote>: it is unnecessary to insert <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 90">cp. 90</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προὐννέπω_227">, <emph>primum iubeo</emph>, with acc. and inf., like <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγω</quote> in 137 (n.), and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐννέπω</quote> in <ref n="Soph. OT 350."> <title>O. T.</title>350.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φέρεις_227">: for the change to direct address, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1353"> <title>O. C.</title>1353</ref> f. (<quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦδ’</quote> followed by <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ κάκιστε</quote>). For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_227"> emphasizing the verb, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 851"> <title>O. T.</title>851</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δ’ οὖν τι κἀκτρέποιτο</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_229"> replies to the doubt implied in <quote xml:lang="grc">χαρτὸν εἴ τι καὶ φέρεις</quote>. Hence the two co-ordinated clauses are here equally important: this is not a case in which the main stress is on the clause with <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote> (as in <ref n="Soph. OC 1536"> <title>O. C.</title>1536</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 503"> <title>Ph.</title>503</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατ_κτῆσιν_230">. As <quote xml:lang="grc">νίκη</quote> can be called a <quote xml:lang="grc"> κτῆμα</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 81"> <title>Ph.</title>81</ref>), and as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔργον</quote> itself often=‘a <emph>notable</emph> deed’ ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 730"> <title>Ant.</title>730</ref> n.), so <quote xml:lang="grc">κεκτῆσθαι ἔργον</quote> might well mean, ‘to have made an achievement one's own.’ The phrase in the text, then, seems sound, as meaning, ‘the achievement of the deed’ (=‘the deed achieved’). We cannot understand, ‘the acquisition (booty) <emph>made by</emph> the deed.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄνδρα_γὰρ_230"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> : <quote xml:lang="grc">γάρ</quote> refers to <quote xml:lang="grc">κατ’ ἔργου κτῆσιν</quote>: the welcome befits the deed, <emph>for</emph> the deed has prospered.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καλῶς_πράσσοντ_230"> <foreign xml:lang="grc"> κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: the sensitive Greek was quick to see his good or bad fortune mirrored in the behaviour of his neighbours: cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1353."> <title>Ph.</title>1353.</ref>We must not, then, alter </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καλῶς_230"> to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καλά_230">,—a change which Nauck thinks ‘necessary.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἃ_βούλομαι_232">, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">διδάσκεσθαι</quote>. For the plur., <ref n="Soph. Trach. 64">cp. 64</ref>.—<quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>Ἡρακλέα</emph>, ¯˘˘¯</quote>: it is needless to assume synizesis, since a proper name excuses an anapaest in any foot except the 6th. Another choriambic name (<quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀντιγόνη</quote>) holds the same place in <ref n="Soph. OC 507."> <title>O. C.</title>507.</ref>Below, in v. 476—the only other place where Soph. has the acc. of <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἡρακλῆς</quote>—most editors write <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἡρακλῆ</quote> (L having <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡρακλεῖ</quote>), since <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἡρακλέα</quote>, at the end of a verse, must be a trisyllable, and the synizesis of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εα_232">, in that position, would be awkward. So, too, in <ref n="Aristoph. Thes. 26">Ar. <title>Th.</title>26</ref>, where the Ravenna MS. has <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἡρακλέα</quote> at the end of the v., Dind. writes <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἡρακλῆ</quote>. In Attic inscriptions the acc. of proper names in <quote xml:lang="grc"> ‐κλῆς</quote> is regularly <quote xml:lang="grc">‐κλέα</quote> down to <emph> c.</emph> 300 B.C.; later it is <quote xml:lang="grc">‐κλῆν</quote>: while <quote xml:lang="grc">‐κλῆ</quote> does not occur. The <quote xml:lang="grc">‐κλῆ</quote> form is frequent, however, in nonAttic inscr. (as those of Delos, Delphi, Sparta), though not before 228 B.C. (Meisterhans, p. 58). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔγωγέ_τοι_234">: for <quote xml:lang="grc">γέ τοι</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1324."> <title>O. C.</title>1324.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔλειπον_234">: 76 n.—In the sequel, Deianeira dies before Heracles is brought home. The answer of Lichas is unconsciously evasive; it is also undesignedly suggestive of a contrast between the hero's present state, and that in which he is to arrive, <quote xml:lang="grc">νόσῳ βαρύς.—<emph>ἰσχύοντά τε καὶ ζῶντα</emph> </quote>. The word </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ζῶντα_234"> was prompted by the form of D. 's question; and the double copula, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τε_καί_234">, links </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰσχύοντα_234"> with it more closely than the following words are linked by the simple </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καί_234">. Hence the whole phrase, ‘strong as well as alive,’=‘not only alive, but strong.’— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θάλλοντα_234"> is more than <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰσχύοντα</quote>, as implying radiant health: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 952">Soph. <title>El.</title>952</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βίῳ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">θάλλοντ’</quote>: <title>I. A.</title> 1223 ff. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆρά σ’ ... ὄψομαι</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ζῶσάν τε καὶ θάλλουσαν<emph>;</emph>—<emph>κοὐ νόσῳ βαρύν</emph> </quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 58"> <title>O. T.</title>58</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γνωτὰ κοὐκ ἄγνωτα</quote> (n.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποῦ_γῆς_236"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἔλειπες</quote>), (<quote xml:lang="grc">εἴτε</quote>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατρῴας_εἴτεβαρβάρου_236"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἔλειπες</quote>)<emph>;</emph> where did you leave him,—whether it was in Greece or abroad that you left him? </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴτε_236">, either doubled or single, is thus used, with ellipse of the verb, when a statement or a question is to include two alternative suppositions: cp. <title>Legg.</title> 844 D <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃς ἂν ἀγροίκου ὀπώρας γεύσηται, βοτρύων εἵτε καὶ σύκων</quote> [<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγεύσατο</quote> ],...<quote xml:lang="grc">εἴτ’ ἐν τοῖς αὑτοῦ χωρίοις εἴτε καὶ ἐν ἄλλων,...πεντήκοντα ὀφειλέτω δραχμάς</quote>. So, with a single <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴτε</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OT 517"> <title>O. T.</title>517</ref>: with doubled <quote xml:lang="grc"> εἴτε</quote>, <emph>ib.</emph> 194<ref n="Soph. OT 1049."> O. T., 1049.</ref>In such sentences </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴτε_236_2"> becomes practically equivalent to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤ</quote>. But it cannot, of course, replace <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤ</quote> in a <emph>direct</emph> statement or question: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘This is either good or bad,’ could not be rendered, <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτο εἴτ’ ἀγαθὸν εἴτε κακόν ἐστι</quote>. Hence the following punctuation, adopted by Dindorf, is impossible:</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποῦ_λέγε_236">. This would require <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ</quote> instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴτε</quote>. (Paley, who follows Dindorf, seems to have felt a misgiving; ‘The use of <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴτε</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ</quote>,’ he says, ‘is remarkable.’)—Ellendt prefers a third way of pointing, which L (see cr. n.) also suggests:</gloss>
lemma: ποῦ γῆς; πατρῴας εἴτε βαρβάρου; λέγε

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποῦ_λέγε_236_2"> (‘say <emph> whether</emph>...’). The objection to this is that it throws too much emphasis on the distinction between Greek and foreign soil.</gloss>
lemma: ποῦ γῆς; πατρῴας εἴτε βαρβάρου, λέγε

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατρῴας_236"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">γῆς</quote> )=<quote xml:lang="grc">πατρίδος</quote> (as in <ref n="Soph. OC 1292"> <title>O. C.</title>1292</ref> etc.), <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> Hellas, as the land of his ancestors (not as ‘the land of his father Zeus’). <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1060">Cp. 1060</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔθ’ Ἑλλὰς οὔτ’ ἄγλωσσος</quote>. The rumours reported by Hyllus spoke of Heracles as freed from Omphalè, but left it doubtful whether he was yet in Euboea (69—75). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Εὐβοίς_237">: <ref target="cl74">74 n.</ref> The northwestern extremity of Euboea is a small peninsula, which runs out westward just opposite the mouth of the Malian Gulf. It ends in the promontory once called <quote xml:lang="grc">Κήναιον</quote>, and now Cape Litháda. Zeus <quote xml:lang="grc">Κήναιος</quote> was worshipped on the neighbouring hill-tops (upwards of 2800 ft. in height), as on so many other summits (cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1040"> <title>Ph.</title>1040</ref> n.): Aesch. fr. 29 <quote xml:lang="grc">Εὐβοῖδα κάμπτων ἀμφὶ Κηναίου Διὸς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκτήν</quote>. The legendary Oechalia, which Heracles sacked, was not near Cenaeum, but some 50 miles S. E. of it, in the territory of Eretria (Hecataeus <emph>ap.</emph> <ref n="Paus. 4. 2. 3">Paus.4. 2. 3</ref>: <ref n="Strab. 10">Strabo 10</ref> p. 448). Sophocles shows his knowledge of this tradition by his reference to the hero's <emph> march</emph> from Oechalia to Cenaeum (750 <quote xml:lang="grc">εἷρπε</quote> ).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁρίζεται_βωμοὺς_237">: the verb denotes properly the act of tracing the temenos in which the altars were to stand. Cp. <ref n="Hdt. 3. 142">Her.3. 142</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Διὸς...βωμὸν ἱδρύσατο καὶ τέμενος περὶ αὐτὸν οὔρισε</quote>. In v. 754 the act. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁρίζει</quote> is used: the midd. occurs in <ref n="Xen. Anab. 7. 5. 13">Xen. <title>An.</title>7. 5. 13</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">στήλας ὁρισάμενοι</quote>. The plur. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βωμοὺς_237"> (as in <ref n="Soph. Trach. 754">754</ref> , <ref n="Soph. Trach. 993">993</ref>) might be used of a single altar (cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1006"> <title>Ant.</title>1006</ref>), but here prob. denotes several,—the sacrifice being on so great a scale (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 760">760 ff.</ref> ).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τέλη_ἔγκαρπα_237">, tributes (or dues) of fruits; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, the revenues derived from a temenos containing fruit-trees or capable of yielding crops. The poet can say, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ ρίζεται τέλη ἔγκαρπα</quote>, because he is thinking of the temenos itself (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 754">cp. 754</ref>). Such lands were sometimes cultivated under the direct control of the priests; sometimes they were let to tenants: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 759e">Plat. <title>Legg</title> 759 E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταμίας...καὶ τεμενῶν καὶ καρπ<*> τούτων καὶ μισθώσεων κυρίους</quote> </cit>. <ref> <title>C. I. G.</title> 4474</ref> (a <quote xml:lang="grc">κώμη</quote> is attached to a shrine of Zeus), <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως ἡ ἀπὸ ταύτης πρόσοδος ἀναλ<*> σκηται εἰς τὰς κατὰ μῆνας συντελουμέν<*> θυσίας καὶ τἄλλα τὰ πρὸς αὔξησιν τοῦ ἱεροί συντείνοντα κ.τ.λ.</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
ταμίας...καὶ τεμενῶν καὶ καρπ<*> τούτων
							καὶ μισθώσεων κυρίους

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐκταῖα_μαντείας_239">: <*> may have vowed them <emph>before</emph> the event: or, <emph>after</emph> it, an oracle may have demanded them. Thus, after the battle of Salamis, the Delphian Apollo claimed a thank-offering from the Aeginetans (<ref n="Hdt. 8.122">Her. 8.122</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φαίνων_239">, presenting them, in fulfillment of the promise. This is a rare sense of the verb, but somewhat like that in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 721"> <title>O. C.</title> 721</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν σὸν τὰ λαμπρὰ ταῦτα δὴ φαίνειν ἔπη</quote> </cit> (n.), ‘to make those bright praise<*> seen in deeds.’ </gloss>

data.quote: νῦν σὸν τὰ λαμπρὰ ταῦτα δὴ φαίνειν ἔπη

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐχαῖς_240">, causal dat.: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1127">cp. 1127</ref><*> <ref n="Soph. OC 332"> <title>O. C.</title>332</ref> f.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνάστατον_240">, proleptic: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 106">cp. 106</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀδακρύτων</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧν_241">: for the attract., cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 35"> <title>O. C.</title>35</ref><*></gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_ὄμμασιν_241">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 764"> <title>Ant.</title>764</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν <*> ὁρῶν</quote> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_εἰσὶ_242">: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc"><*> εἰσὶ δεσπότου</quote>. Their appearance in charge of the herald shows that they are captives, and consequently slaves (302): she asks, then, who is their captor. Hence a slight emphasis falls on </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκεῖνος_242"> (244). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἰκτραὶ_γάρ_243">: ‘(I ask this,) <emph>for</emph> they deserve pity, unless their present plight deceives me,’—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, unless it excites greater pity than I should feel if I knew more.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυμφοραὶ_243"> is much better than the <emph> v.l.</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυμφορᾷ_243">, which would easily arise from a wish to have the same subject in both clauses. When a common word for fraud, such as <quote xml:lang="grc">κλέπτειν</quote>, is used in the figurative sense, ‘to produce an illusion,’ it is evidently fitter that the subject to the verb should <emph>not</emph> be a human being. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 681"> <title>Ant.</title>681</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ μὴ τῷ χρόνῳ κεκλέμμεθα</quote>: <emph>ib.</emph> 1218 <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοῖσι κλέπτομαι</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξείλεθ_245">: the midd. here suits </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὑτῷ_245">: but in ref. to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεοῖς_245"> the act. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξεῖλεν</quote> would have been more usual, cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1431"> <title>Ph.</title>1431</ref> n.: [Eur. ] <title>Rhes.</title> 470 <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοῖσί τ’ ἀκροθίνἰ ἐξέλῃς</quote>: <ref n="Thuc. 3. 50">Thuc.3. 50</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κλήρους ... τῆς γῆς ... τοῖς θεοῖς ἱεροὺς ἐξεῖλον.—<emph>κριτόν</emph> </quote>: so in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1302">Soph. <title>Ai.</title>1302</ref>Hesionè is the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔκκριτον δώρημα</quote> given to Telamon as a prize of valour. Prisoners of war often became <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἱερόδουλοι</quote> in temples (cp. <ref n="Hdt. 6. 134">Her.6. 134</ref> : <ref n="Paus. 3. 18. 3">Paus.3. 18. 3</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_κἀπὶ_246">: for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ καί</quote> in a question, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 368."> <title>O. T.</title>368.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄσκοπον_246">, ‘not to be looked for,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, here, longer than could possibly have been expected. Cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 864"> <title>El.</title>864</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄσκοπος ἁ λώβα</quote> : <ref n="Soph. Phil. 111"> <title>Ph.</title>111</ref>1n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡμερῶν_246"> goes with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνήριθμον_246">, not with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρόνον_246">: ‘without number of days,’=‘extending to countless days.’ Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 601"> <title>Ai.</title>601</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">μηνῶν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνήριθμος</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OC 677"> <title>O. C.</title>677</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_χρόνον_248">: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, twelve of the fifteen months (44).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_αὐτός_248">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 253">cp. 253</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς αὐτὸς λέγει</quote>. It would have seemed incredible without such testimony. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμποληθείς_250"> may be freely rendered, ‘sold into bondage,’ but its literal sense is rather, ‘made merchandise of,’ or, ‘bought.’ Hesychius, indeed, explains <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἠμπόλησεν</quote> by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπέδοτο</quote>: but, though <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξεμπολᾶν</quote>=‘to sell off,’ <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμπολᾶν</quote> as=‘to sell’ lacks classical evidence. (Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 417"> <title>Ph.</title>417</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμπολητός</quote>, ‘bought.’) <quote xml:lang="grc">πραθῆναι</quote> (252), <quote xml:lang="grc">πεπρᾶσθαι</quote> were the proper terms for ‘to be sold’ (as a slave).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_φθόνον_250">, dislike (felt by the hearer for the narrator) on account of the telling.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσεῖναι_250">, abs., to be an attendant circumstance ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1252"> <title>Ant.</title>1252</ref> n.),—to attend upon the act (of telling).</gloss>
