<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅτου_250">, neut.: (in the case of anything) of which Zeus is seen to be the doer. Cp. <ref n="Thuc. 6. 14">Thuc.6. 14</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ καλῶς ἄρξαι τοῦτ’ εἶναι, ὃς ἂν τὴν πατρίδα ὠφελήσῃ</quote>. We cannot make </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅτου_250_2"> masc. (relat. to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_λόγου_250">), because <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ λόγος</quote> here denotes, not the reported deed, but the (mere) act of reporting it, as contrasted with the causing of it.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πράκτωρ_φανῇ_250">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 862">cp. 862</ref>: for <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτου</quote> without <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1231"> <title>O. T.</title>1231</ref> n. The agency of Zeus is explained below<ref n="Soph. OT 274"> O. T., 274</ref> ff.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κεῖνος_δὲ_252">: <quote xml:lang="grc">δὲ</quote> resumes the story after the parenthetic apology (<quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ λόγου... φανῇ</quote>): cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">κεῖνοι δ’</quote> in 281. Wunder rejects these two verses, (1) because they repeat the substance of vv. 248—250, and (2) because Lichas ought not to pain his mistress by mentioning Omphalè. But (1) these vv. explain </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_Λυδοῖς_252">, and define </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρόνον_252">: (2) the herald's motive for silence concerning Iolè does not apply to the case of Omphalè. Hyllus had already mentioned ‘the Lydian woman’ to his mother (70).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πραθεὶς_Ὀμφάλῃ_252">. Hermes, by command of Zeus, took Heracles to Lydia, and sold him in the slave-market to Omphalè: the price was paid to Eurytus, as a <quote xml:lang="grc">ποινή</quote> for the murder of Iphitus ( <ref n="Apollod. 2.6.2">Apollod.2. 6. 2</ref> ).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐνιαυτὸν_253">. The popular version spoke of three years. This was the term assigned by the mythographer Herodorus (<emph> c.</emph> 430 B.C.?), acc. to the schol.; as it is by Apollodorus (<ref n="Apollod. 2.6.2">2. 6. 2</ref>). If this change was due to Sophocles, we can see the artistic motive. Three months or so, after the bondage, were required for the war in Euboea. If the poet had made Heracles go to Lydia 39, instead of 15, months before his death, there would have been less room for those hopes which contend with fears in the opening scene of the <title>Trachiniae.</title> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λαβὼν_254">, having incurred: cp. fr. 742 <quote xml:lang="grc">ζημίαν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">λαβεῖν</quote>: ( <ref n="Soph. OT 1494"> <title>O. T.</title>1494</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀνείδη λαμβάνων</quote> is not exactly similar:) <ref n="Thuc. 2. 18">Thuc.2. 18</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰτίαν...ἔλαβεν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅρκον_προσβαλὼν_255">: cp. <ref n="Hdt. 1. 146">Her.1. 146</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σφίσι αὐτῇσι ὅρκους ἐπήλασαν</quote>: id. 6. 74 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρκους προσάγων σφι ἦ μὲν ἕψεσθαι κ.τ.λ.—<emph>διώμοσεν</emph> </quote>. With the exception of the perf. ( Lycurg. § 127), the act. voice is rarer than the midd. (378, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1233">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 1233</ref>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_μὴν_255">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 593."> <title>Ph.</title>593.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_πάθους_255">, the man who had <emph>brought</emph> this calamity <emph>near</emph> to him,—brought it upon him: since Eurytus, by insulting him, had provoked him to slay Iphitus,—the crime for which this <quote xml:lang="grc"> πάθος</quote> was the penalty. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγχιστήρ</quote>, in this sense, presupposes a trans. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγχίζειν</quote>. That verb does not occur, but would be analogous to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγγίζειν</quote>: and the latter, though usu. intrans., is trans. in Polyb. 8. 6 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγγίσαντες τῇ γῇ τὰς ναῦς</quote>. Compare, too, the phrases of converse form: <ref n="Hom. Il. 5.766"> <title>Il.</title>5. 766</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥ ἑ μάλιστ’ εἴωθε κακῇς ὀδύνῃσι πελάζειν</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. PB 155">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title>155</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεσμοῖς...πελάσας</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">με</quote>).—Others understand :—‘the man <emph>most nearly concerned in</emph> this calamity.’ This is the general sense intended by the schol.: <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν αἴτιον καὶ σχεδὸν αὐτὸν ποιησαντα τοῦ πάθους</quote> (where the words <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ... ποιήσαντα</quote> are parenthetical): <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘the (ultimate) cause, and <emph> almost</emph> the actual author, of the calamity.’ But, though <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγχιστήρ</quote> might naturally mean, ‘nearest kinsman’ (= <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγχιστεύς</quote>), it would be strange to say, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγχιστὴρ τοῦ πάθους</quote>, as= ‘the person who had most to do with’ that <quote xml:lang="grc">πάθος</quote> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτόχειρα_255"> would be possible only if Eurytus had himself sent Heracles into slavery. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξὺν_γυναικὶ_257">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, with his whole family. Eurytus had several children (266), but the prosaic conjecture </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παισὶ_257"> would only weaken the phrase. Schneidewin cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 9.199"> <title>Od.</title>9. 199</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὕνεκά μιν σὺν παιδὶ περισχόμεθ’ ἠδὲ γυναικί</quote> </cit>: where <quote xml:lang="grc">παισὶ</quote> is a <emph>v.l.</emph> This <emph> may</emph> be parallel: there is nothing, however, to show that the sing. cannot there be taken literally. For the collective sing., cp. <ref n="Aesch. Seven 197">Aesch. <title>Theb.</title>197</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνὴρ γυνή τε χὥ τι τῶν μεταίχμιον.— <emph>δουλώσειν</emph> </quote>: the prose word, in ref. to prisoners of war, was not <quote xml:lang="grc">δουλοῦν</quote>, but <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀνδραποδίζειν</quote>: hence <ref n="Thuc. 8. 28">Thuc.8. 28</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ ἀνδράποδα πάντα καὶ δοῦλα καὶ ἐλεύθερα</quote> (referring to their previous condition).</gloss>

data.quote: οὕνεκά μιν σὺν παιδὶ περισχόμεθ’ ἠδὲ γυναικί

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔτι_257">, as oft. in threats: <ref n="Soph. El. 66"> <title>El.</title>66</ref>: <ref n="Aesch. PB 908">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title>908</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ μὴν ἔτι Ζεύς, καίπερ αὐθάδη φρονῶν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ταπεινὸς ἔσται</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοὐχ_τοὔπος_258">: modelled on the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ἁλίωσε βέλος</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 16.737"> <title>Il.</title>16. 737</ref>), with a reminiscence also of <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ἅλιον ἔπος ἔσσεται</quote> (<emph>ib.</emph> 24. 92).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅθ_ἦν_258">. The Homeric poems know nothing of a ritual for purification from homicide: the blood-shedder either flies into exile ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 24.480"> <title>Il.</title>24. 480</ref> ff.), or prevails on the kinsfolk of the slain to accept a <quote xml:lang="grc">ποινή</quote> (<emph>ib.</emph> 9. 632 ff.), and stays at home. Here the schol. supposes that, by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἁγνός_258">, Sophocles alludes simply to the year of exile having expired. This may be so; but it is more probable that Heracles is conceived as <emph>also</emph> undergoing a formal <quote xml:lang="grc">κάθαρσις</quote>. According to other writers, he received this from Deiphobus at Amyclae, after vainly seeking it from Neleus at Pylos ( <ref n="Apollod. 2.6.2">Apollod.2. 6. 2</ref>: <ref n="Diod. 4. 31">Diod.4. 31</ref>). So Aesch. makes the exile Orestes receive the <quote xml:lang="grc">καθαρμοὶ χοιροκτόνοι</quote> ( <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 283">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title>283</ref> <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 449">Aesch. Eum., 449</ref>). The homicide who withdrew into banishment was said <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπενιαυτίζειν</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπενιαυτεῖν</quote>),—a word not always restricted to <emph>one</emph> year: <title>Legg.</title> 868 D <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνιαυτοὺς τρεῖς ἀπενιαυτεῖν</quote>. Cp. <emph>ib.</emph> E <quote xml:lang="grc">καθαίρεσθαι μὲν τοὺς αὐτοὺς καθαρμούς, τριετεῖς δὲ ἀπενιαυτήσεις διατελεῖν</quote>. The rites of <quote xml:lang="grc"> κάθαρσις</quote> for homicide are fully described by Apoll. Rhod., 4. 693—717. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στρατὸν_ἐπακτὸν_259">: the adj. here merely=<quote xml:lang="grc">ξένον</quote>, ‘alien,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, not belonging to his own home. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπακτός</quote> is prop. said of allies, or mercenaries, whom a foreign state calls in (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπάγεται</quote>) to its aid: cp. <title>O.C.</title> 1525 n. But here it denotes the allies of an <emph>exile</emph>,—just as Polyneices is said to bring a <quote xml:lang="grc">στράτευμ’ ἐπακτὸν</quote> against his country ( <ref n="Aesch. Seven 583">Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 583</ref>).— <ref n="Apollod. 2.7.7">Apollod.2. 7. 7</ref> describes this army as composed of Arcadians, Malians, and Epicnemidian Lo crians: but those who cite him here have not observed that he supposes Heracles to make the war from Trachis.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔρχεται_259"> with acc. of place: <ref n="Soph. OC 89"> <title>O. C.</title>89</ref>, <ref n="Soph. El. 893."> <title>El.</title>893.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόλιν_Εὐρυτείάν_259">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 267"> <title>O. T.</title>267</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ Λαβδακείῳ παιδὶ</quote> (n.): <emph>ib.</emph> 450 <quote xml:lang="grc">φόνον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν Λαΐειον.—<emph>τόνδε</emph> </quote>, as if <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν Εὐρύτου</quote> had gone before: so in <ref n="Hom. Il. 5.640"> <title>Il.</title>5. 640</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅς</quote> refers to <quote xml:lang="grc">βίην Ἡρακληείην</quote>: in <ref n="Soph. OC 942"> <title>O. C.</title>942</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοὺς</quote> to <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλιν</quote> in 939: in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1364"> <title>Ph.</title>1364</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἵ γε</quote> to <quote xml:lang="grc">Τροίαν</quote> in 1363: in <ref n="Soph. El. 963"> <title>El.</title>963</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶνδε</quote> to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλεκτρα ἀνυμέναιά τε.—<emph>μεταίτιον</emph> </quote>: Zeus was primarily <quote xml:lang="grc">αἴτιος</quote>, but Eurytus was the only <emph>mortal</emph> who had a part in it. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐφέστιον_262">, added to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐςδόμους_262">, marks how the hospitality of the hearth had been violated. Cp. <ref n="Eur. Med. 713">Eur. <title>Med.</title>713</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δέξαι δὲ χώρᾳ καὶ δόμοις ἐφέστιον</quote>. The stranger who had been received as an inmate was under the protection of <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζεύς</quote>, both <quote xml:lang="grc">Ξένιος</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἐφέστιος</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 492"> Soph. <title>Ai.</title>492</ref>). For the phrase </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐλθόντ_ἐφέστιον_262">, cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 23.55">Hom. <title>Od.</title>23. 55</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦλθε μὲν αὐτὸς ζωὸς ἐφέστιος</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξένον_ὄντα_263">: Eurytus was said to have taught Heracles the use of the bow (Theocr. 24. 106 f.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπερρόθησε_264">. This verb can denote the blended sound of many voices ( <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 458">Aesch. <title> Ch.</title>458</ref>); esp., the responsive shout of a crowd ( <ref n="Eur. Hec. 553">Eur. <title>Hec.</title>553</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Orest. 901">Eur. <title>Or.</title>901</ref>). Here it refers to loud and vehement railing: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 259">Soph. <title> Ant.</title>259</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγοι...ἐρρόθουν κακοί</quote> (n.), and <emph>ib.</emph> 413 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιρρόθοις</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">κακοῖσιν</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀτηρᾷ_φρενί_264">. (For the sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀτηρᾷ</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1272"> <title>Ph.</title>1272</ref>: Paley wrongly takes it as= ‘deluded.’) This is the easier, since the antithesis between </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γόγοις_264"> and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φρενί_264"> at once suggests a distinction between affronts expressed <emph>in speech</emph> and those which showed the malicious <emph>intention</emph> in another way, viz., by acts: two examples of the verbal insults are given, and then one of the other kind (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δείπνοις_δ_264"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>). Instances of zeugma quite as bold occur elsewhere in poetry; for the Greek mind was quick to seize the hint of a contrast, and did not always require full expression of it: <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>, <ref n="Hom. Od. 15.374"> <title>Od.</title>15. 374</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ δ’ ἄρα δεσποίνης οὐ μείλιχον ἔστιν ἀκοῦσαι</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτ’ ἔπος οὔτε τι ἔργον</quote> (<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">παθεῖν</quote> ): <emph>ib.</emph> 20. 312 f. <quote xml:lang="grc">μήλων σφαζομένων οἴνοιό τε πινομένοιο</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ σίτου</quote> (<emph> sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐσθιομένου</quote>). In these examples, just as here, the antithesis of nouns supersedes an explicit antithesis of verbs.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγοις_264"> (such as <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰσχροῖς</quote>), to balance </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀτηρᾷ_264">. (2) The formula </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολλὰ_πολλὰδέ_264"> would be out of place, unless two classes of verbal taunts were distinguished by the presence or absence of a spiteful intent; but the context excludes such a distinction.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολλὰ_μὲν_264"> (see cr. n.). Again, it is improbable that a verse has been lost after v. 264. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέγων_μὲν_265">: metre has influenced the place of <quote xml:lang="grc">μέν</quote>, which answers to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δὲ_265"> in 267, and ought to come next after </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέγων_265">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 919"> <title>Ph.</title>919</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σῶσαι κακοῦ μὲν πρῶτα</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦδ’, ἔπειτα δὲ</quote> | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">πορθῆσαι.—<emph>ἄφυκτα … βέλη</emph> </quote>, those which Heracles had received from Apollo, and which he bequeathed to Philoctetes ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 198"> <title>Ph.</title>198</ref> n.: <emph>ib.</emph> 105).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_τέκνων_265">: the sons were four in number, acc. to Hesiod (fr. 70, <emph>ap.</emph> schol. here),— <quote xml:lang="grc">Δηίων, Κλύτιος, Τοξεύς, Ἴφιτος</quote>. An ancient vase from Caere, which depicts the reception of Heracles by Eurytus and his family, gives the names of three sons as <quote xml:lang="grc">Κλύτιος, Τόξος, Διδαίvων</quote> (Preller II. 226 n. 3). Creophylus, the author of the <quote xml:lang="grc">Οἰχαλίας ἅλωσις</quote>, named only two sons (schol.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λείποιτο_265">: cp. <ref n="Thuc. 6. 72">Thuc.6. 72</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνὴρ καὶ ἐς τἄλλα ξύνεσιν οὐδενὸς λειπόμενος.—<emph>πρὸς τόξου κρίσιν</emph> </quote>: for the prep., cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1306">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 1306</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κακοὺς</quote> | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς αἰχμήν</quote>: <ref n="Hdt. 1. 99">Her.1. 99</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς ἀνδραγαθίην λειπόμενοι</quote>. By <quote xml:lang="grc">τόξου κρίσις</quote> is meant a trial (of the competitors) which the bow decides. <quote xml:lang="grc">κρίσις</quote> thus almost =<quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀγών</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1050">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 1050</ref> n. There was a legend that Eurytus offered the hand of his daughter Iolè as a prize for the man who should surpass him and his sons in archery. Heracles conquered, but Eurytus broke his promise (schol.: Apoll. 2. 6. 1).—Eurytus, like his father Melaneus, was a great archer. In <ref n="Hom. Od. 8.226">Hom. <title>Od.</title>8. 226</ref> ff. he challenges Apollo, and is slain by him. The bow of Eurytus, inherited by Iphitus, was given by the latter to Odysseus (<emph>ib.</emph> 21. 31 ff.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φωνεῖ_δὲ_267">, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">φωνῶν δὲ</quote> (answering to <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγων...μὲν</quote>): cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 190"> <title>El.</title>190</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκονομῶ θαλάμους πατρός, ὧδε μὲν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀεικεῖ σὺν στολᾷ</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">κεναῖς δ’ ἀμφίσταμαι τραπέζαις</quote> (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφισταμένη</quote>): <ref n="Soph. OC 351"> <title>O. C.</title>351</ref> n. This very trait confirms the soundness of the MS. text. For the historic pres., standing between <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπερρόθησε</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρριψεν</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 269"> <title>Ant.</title>269</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγει</quote>, between <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦν</quote> (268) and <quote xml:lang="grc"> προὔτρεψεν</quote> (270). The optat. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ῥαίοιτο_267"> is admissible (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥαίεται</quote>), because the historic present counts as a secondary tense: cp. <ref n="Hdt. 1. 63">Her.1. 63</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βουλὴν... ἐπιτεχνᾶται, ὅκως μήτε ἁλισθεῖεν κ.τ.λ.</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 12.12">Lys. or. 12 § 12</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρωτῶσιν ὅπῃ βαδίζοιμεν· ὁ δ’ ἔφασκεν</quote> </cit> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> </gloss>

data.quote: ἐρωτῶσιν ὅπῃ βαδίζοιμεν· ὁ δ’ ἔφασκεν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνδρὸς_ἐλευθέρου_267"> with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δοῦλος_267"> (not with <quote xml:lang="grc"> ῥαίοιτο</quote>, as gen. of agent, like <quote xml:lang="grc">πληγεὶς θυγατρὸς τῆς ἐμῆς</quote>, <ref n="Eur. Orest. 497">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 497</ref>): <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλευθέρου</quote> serves merely to emphasise <quote xml:lang="grc">δοῦλος</quote> by contrast.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ῥαίοιτο_267_2">: <ref n="Hom. Od. 6.325"> <title>Od.</title>6. 325</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεὶ πάρος οὔ ποτ’ ἄκουσας</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥαιομένου, ὅτε μ’ ἔρραιεν κλυτὸς ἐννοσίγαιος</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δείπνοις_267">, dat. of the occasion: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Sym. 174a">Plat. <title>Symp.</title> 174 A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς ἐπινικίοις</quote> </cit>: for the plur., cp. <title>O.T.</title> 779, <ref n="Soph. El. 203."> <title>El.</title>203.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ᾠνωμένος_267">, Heracles. Cp. <ref n="Eur. Alc. 756">Eur. <title>Alc.</title>756</ref>(of Heracles) <quote xml:lang="grc">ποτῆρα δ’ ἐν χείρεσσι κίσσινον λαβὼν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πίνει μελαίνης μητρὸς εὔζωρον μέθυ</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕως ἐθέρμην’ αὐτὸν ἀμφιβᾶσα φλὸξ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴνου· στέφει δὲ κρᾶτα μυρσίνης κλάδοις</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄμουσ’ ὑλακτῶν</quote>. The <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀτηρὰ φρήν</quote> (264) of Eurytus seized this opportunity of inflicting an insult.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔρριψεν_αὐτόν_267">. A Greek vase, found in Sicily, quaintly illustrates some such incident. The inebriated Heracles is lying on his back outside a closed door, from above which an old woman is pouring cold water upon him. Satyrs and maenads appear at each side of the group. (Benndorf, <title>Gr. und Sicilische Vasenbilder</title> pl. 44.) I am indebted for this reference to Mr MurrayS. , of the British Museum.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧν_χόλον_267">: for the causal gen. cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 327"> <title>Ph.</title>327</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὖθις_270">, at a later time: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1204"> <title>Ant.</title>1204</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Τιρυνθίαν_κλιτύν_270">. We cannot be sure that Sophocles had any clear picture of the place before his mind; but his phrase, at least, is not unsuitable. <quote xml:lang="grc">κλιτύς</quote>, ‘slope,’ does not necessarily imply great elevation. The site of Tiryns is a ridge of limestone rock on the Argolic Gulf (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 1151">cp. 1151</ref>), in which, at some prehistoric time, it formed an island. The length of this ridge, from N. to S. , is about 328 yards: its width about 109. The upper citadel of Tiryns was at the southern end, where the rock attains a height of about 72 feet above sea-level, and of 59 feet above the present surface of the plain. North of this was the lower citadel; and the whole was surrounded by those massive ‘Cyclopean’ walls from which Tiryns derived its Homeric epithet ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 2.559"> <title>Il.</title>2. 559</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">τειχιόεσσα</quote>. See Schliemann's <title>Tiryns</title>, p. 177. Such a site, though not steep or lofty, might correctly be described as the <quote xml:lang="grc">Τιρυνθία κλιτύς</quote>.—For the </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="υ_270"> in </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλιτύν_270">, cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1144"> <title>Ant.</title>1144</ref> n., and <emph>ib.</emph> 1127 <quote xml:lang="grc">λιγνύς</quote>: so <quote xml:lang="grc">νηδύς</quote> ( <ref n="Eur. Andr. 356">Eur. <title>Andr.</title>356</ref> etc.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἵππους_νομάδας_270">: acc. to <ref n="Hom. Od. 21.22"> <title>Od.</title>21. 22</ref>, Iphitus came, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵππους διζήμενος, αἵ οἱ ὄλοντο</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">δώδεκα θήλειαι, ὑπὸ δ’ ἡμίονοι ταλαεργοί</quote>: but <ref n="Apollod. 2.6.2">Apollod.2. 6. 2</ref> says, <quote xml:lang="grc">κλαπεισῶν ἐξ Εὐβοίας ὑπὸ Αὐτολύκου βοῶν</quote>. For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νομάδας_270">, ‘wandering,’ cp. <title>O.T.</title> 1350 n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλλοσ_ἔχοντ_272">: he was gazing forth from the high place, in the hope of descrying his horses; and, as he could not see them, his <emph>thought</emph> was wandering to <emph>other</emph> places where they might perhaps be. Cp. <ref n="Diod. 4.31">Diod. Sic. 4. 31</ref>: Heracles commands Iphitus, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφορᾶν, μή που νεμόμεναι τυγχάνουσιν· οὐ δυναμένου δὲ κατανοῆσαι τοῦ Ἰφίτου κ.τ.λ.</quote> Thus </gloss>
lemma: ἄλλοσ’ … ὄμμα, θατέρᾳ δὲ νοῦν ἔχοντ’

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θατέρᾳ_272"> does not merely repeat </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλλοσε_272">, but is opposed to it: as in <ref n="Hdt. 1. 32">Her.1. 32</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλο μὲν ἔχει</quote> to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑτέρου δὲ ἐπιδέεται</quote>. Cp. <title>Theages</title> 129 C <quote xml:lang="grc">βουλόμενός με λαθεῖν ἀνέστη,...ἐπιτηρήσας ἄλλοσε τὸν νοῦν ἔχοντα</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πυργώδους_πλακός_272">. The current version spoke of Heracles as hurling Iphitus from a <emph>wall</emph> or <emph> tower.</emph> Pherecydes the logographer (5th cent. B.C.) is quoted to this effect (schol. <ref n="Hom. Od. 21.23"> <title>Od.</title>21. 23</ref>): <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν δὲ Ἡρακλέα μηχανῇ τινι καὶ στρατηγίᾳ συνεφελκυσάμενον αὐτὸν ἄγειν εἰς ἐπίκρημνον τεῖχος</quote>. <ref n="Apollod. 2.6.2">Apollod.2. 6. 2</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μανεὶς δὲ αὖθις</quote> (Heracles) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπὸ τῶν Τιρυνθίων ἔρριψεν αὐτὸν τειχῶν</quote>. <ref n="Diod. 4.31">Diod. Sic. 4. 31</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτον μὲν ἀναβιβάσας ὁ Ἡρακλῆς ἐπί τινα πύργον ὑψηλὸν ἐκέλευσεν ἀφορᾶν</quote>. The word <quote xml:lang="grc">πύργος</quote> oft.=a city-wall with its towers ( <ref n="Soph. OT 56"> <title>O. T.</title>56</ref> n.). Thus it would satisfy all these statements to suppose that Iphitus was thrown from some high part of the walls which encompassed Tiryns <quote xml:lang="grc">τειχιόεσσα</quote>. And by <quote xml:lang="grc">πυργώδης πλάξ</quote> Sophocles may well have meant ‘the summit of a tower-like <emph>building.</emph>’ Modern critics have usually held that he meant ‘the top of a towering rock or cliff’: and so the schol. here explains, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑψηλοῦ ὄρους</quote>. We need not press the argument that it is not well-suited to the locality. But it may be doubted whether a Greek poet would have compared a rock or cliff to a <quote xml:lang="grc">πύργος</quote> merely because it was high and steep. On the other hand, where <quote xml:lang="grc">πυργοειδής</quote> occurs elsewhere, it refers to a building. Josephus <title>Bell. Iud.</title> 5. 5. 8 (the <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀντωνία</quote>, or citadel of Jerusalem) <quote xml:lang="grc">πυργοειδὴς...οὖσα τὸ πᾶν σχῆμα</quote>. Dion Cassius 74. 5 <quote xml:lang="grc">πυρὰ πυργοειδής</quote>. And it is consonant with the style of Tragedy that, in regard to such a detail, the vaguer phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">πυργώδης πλάξ</quote> should be preferred to <quote xml:lang="grc">πλὰξ πύργου</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_ἁπάντων_275"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> The emphasis of this verse is designed (like the comment in 250 f.) to bring out the higher and more soothing aspect of the doom suffered by Heracles.—For the strengthening art. with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁπάντων</quote>, cp. <ref n="Aesch. PB 483">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title>483</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰς ἁπάσας...νόσους.—<emph>πατὴρ Ὀλύμπιος</emph> </quote>, not <quote xml:lang="grc">οὑλύμπιος</quote> (cr. n.), since the words form a single notion ( <ref n="Soph. OT 1199">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 1199</ref> n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρατόν_ἐξέπεμψεν_276">, sent him out of the country to be sold (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 252">cp. 252</ref> n.): the adj. is proleptic (106 n.).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδ_ἠνέσχετο_276">, ‘and did not tolerate’ (his deed): the verb is really absol. (as in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 75"> <title>Ai.</title>75</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ σῖγ’ ἀνέξει</quote> <emph> ;</emph>), though it is easy to supply an acc.: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁθούνεκ_276">=‘because,’ as in 571.—Others understand, ‘brooked not <emph> that</emph> (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 813">cp. 813</ref>) he had slain him.’— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοῦνον_276">: <ref n="Soph. OT 1418"> <title>O. T.</title>1418</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἠμύνατο_278">, avenged himself: <title>O.C.</title> 873 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔργοις πεπονθὼς ῥήμασίν σ’ ἀμύνομαι</quote>. The <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕβρις</quote> of Eurytus would have justified Heracles in challenging Iphitus to open combat. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χειρουμένῳ_279"> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ Ἡρακλεῖ τὸν Ἴφιτον</quote>: for this midd., <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1109">cp. 1109</ref> : <title>O.C.</title> 950, 1009: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 92."> <title>Ph.</title>92.</ref>Of the pass., Sophocles has only <quote xml:lang="grc">χειρωθείς</quote> (below<ref n="Soph. Phil. 1057"> Ph., 1057</ref>, and <ref n="Soph. OC 903"> <title>O. C.</title>903</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδὲ_δαίμονες_279">, <foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign>, they like it as little as mortals do: for the adverbial <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέ</quote> after <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 287"> <title>O. T.</title>287</ref>, <ref n="Soph. El. 595"> <title>El.</title>595</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1242."> <title>Ai.</title>1242.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κεῖνοι_δ_281">: for the resumptive <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 252">cp. 252</ref> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπερχλίοντες_281">: this form, attested by the first hand in L and by the schol. (cr. n.), is confirmed by the fact that Aesch. twice uses <quote xml:lang="grc">χλίειν</quote> with ref. to insolent triumph. <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 137">Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 137</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν τοῖσι σοῖς πόνοισι χλίουσιν μέγα</quote> : <ref n="Aesch. Supp. 914">Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title>914</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κάρβανος ὢν δ’ Ἕλλησιν ἐγχλίεις ἄγαν</quote>. The compound with <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπέρ</quote> does not occur elsewhere.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_κακῆς_281">: here <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</quote> is virtually ‘with’: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 875">cp. 875</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OT 528"> <title>O. T.</title>528</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ὀμμάτων ὀρθῶν κ.τ.λ.</quote> (n.): <ref n="Soph. OC 486"> <title>O. C.</title>486</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ εὐμενῶν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">στέρνων δέχεσθαι</quote>. This is better than to take <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</quote> as=‘in consequence of.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτοὶ_μὲν_281"> after </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κεῖνοι_281"> and before </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόλιςδὲ_281">, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1008"> <title>O. C.</title>1008</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κλέψας... ἐμὲ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτόν τ’ ἐχειροῦ τὰς κόρας τ’ οἴχει λαβών</quote>: and <emph>ib.</emph> 462 (n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἰκήτορες_281">: 1161: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 517"> <title>Ai.</title>517</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἅιδου θανασίμους οἰκήτορας</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάσδε_283">, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc"> αἵδε</quote>, by attract. to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἅσπερ_283">: see on <ref n="Soph. OT 449."> <title>O. T.</title>449.</ref>— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξ_ὀλβίων_283"> : <ref n="Soph. OT 454"> <title>O. T.</title>454</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τυφλὸς...ἐκ δεδορκότος</quote>: so below<ref n="Soph. OT 619"> O. T., 619</ref> <ref n="Soph. OT 1075."> O. T., 1075.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χωροῦσι_σέ_285">. The accentuation <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς σέ</quote>, which is L's, seems right, as implying, ‘to thee, their new mistress.’ If we wrote <quote xml:lang="grc">πρός σε</quote>, the emphasis would fall wholly on v. 284: ‘these, who are coming to thee, are now slaves.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόσις_δὲ_285">: the antithesis between </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐφεῖτο_285"> and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τελῶ_285"> seems to warrant us in keeping <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote> here (instead of changing it to <quote xml:lang="grc">τε</quote>): <ref n="Soph. Trach. 143">cp. 143</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἁγνὰ_θύματα_287">: cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 21.258"> <title>Od.</title>21. 258</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑορτὴ τοῖο θεοῖο</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁγνή</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Ion 243">Eur. <title>Ion</title>243</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁγνὰ Λοξίου χρηστήρια.—<emph>πατρῴῳ Ζηνὶ</emph> </quote>, Zeus as the god of his fathers, the protector of his race, rather than with ref. to the personal relationship: so again in 753.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆς_ἁλώσεως_287">, <emph>for</emph> it, causal gen. (here akin to the gen. of price), with the whole phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">θύματα ῥέξῃ</quote>: cp. <title>O.T.</title> 47 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς σὲ νῦν μὲν ἥδε γῆ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">σωτῆρα κλῄζει τῆς πάρος προθυμίας</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φρόνει_ἥξοντα_289">: for the redundant </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νιν_289"> (after <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτὸν δ’ ἐκεῖνον</quote>), cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 248"> <title>O. T.</title>248</ref>(n.). For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_289"> prefixed to the partic., after an imperative verb of thinking or knowing, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 253"> <title>Ph.</title>253</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 848."> <title>O. T.</title>848.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καλῶς_290"> <quote xml:lang="grc">λεχθέντος</quote>, ‘auspiciously,’ ‘happily,’ told; since the news is good. <quote xml:lang="grc">καλῶς λέγειν</quote> more usually means to speak (1) sensibly, or (2) speciously, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1047."> <title>Ant.</title>1047.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νῦν_κυρεῖ_291">, now thy joy is manifest, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, is assured beyond all doubt (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 223">cp. 223</ref> f.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_μὲν_291">, the herald and the captives: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_δὲ_291">, the news that Heracles will soon return. For the gen. absol. co-ordinated with a partic. in another case, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 737"> <title>O. C.</title>737</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πανδίκῳ_φρενί_294">, ‘with a thoroughly justified feeling’ (not, ‘with my whole heart’): <ref n="Soph. Trach. 611">cp. 611</ref> n. The adj. occurs only here. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῇδε_295">, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῇ πράξει</quote>: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτο_295"> , <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ <*> χαίρειν.—<emph> συντρέχειν</emph> </quote>, coincide with, be combined with: for this sense of the zerb, cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. OC 158"> <title>O. C.</title>158</ref> ff.—Not (as <*>ood), ‘Such joy must needs accompany [the event] <emph>in this way</emph> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τῇδε</quote>).’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖσιν_σκοπουμένοις_296">, absol., for those who take just views,—who prudently consider human affairs. For the midd., cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 964"> <title>O. T.</title>964</ref>: it was common also in good prose.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταρβεῖν_πράσσοντα_296">, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ταρβεῖν ὑπὲρ τοῦ εὖ πράσσοντος</quote> (<ref n="Plat. Rep. 387c">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 387 C</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπὲρ τῶν φυλάκων φοβούμεθα, μὴ...γένωνται</quote> etc.). Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 493"> <title>Ph.</title>493</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃν δὴ παλαιὸν ἐξότου δέδοικ’ <*>γὼ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μή μοι βεβήκῃ</quote> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμοὶ_εἰσέβη_299">: for the dat., cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 372"> <title>O. C.</title>372</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεινὸς_299">, of strong feeling: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 476">cp. 476</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπάτορας_300">, since their fathers are supposed to be dead; for, when a city is sacked, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνδρας μὲν κτείνουσι, πόλιν δέ τε πῦρ ἀμαθύνει</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">τέκνα δέ τ’ ἄλλοι ἄγουσι βαθυζώνους τε γυναῖκας</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 9.593"> <title>Il.</title>9. 593</ref>). Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1506"> <title>O. T.</title>1506</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πτωχὰς ἀνάνδρους ἐγγενεῖς ἀλωμένας</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴσως_300"> in a like conjecture, cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 180"> <title>Ph.</title>180</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὗτος πρωτογόνων ἴσως</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴκων οὐδενὸς ὕστερος.—<emph>πρὶν μὲν ἦσαν</emph> </quote>, not <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰσίν</quote>, because </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξἐλευθέρων_ἀνδρῶν_300"> implies <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλεύθεραι</quote>. The word </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνδρῶν_300"> (which some would change to <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴκων</quote>) has been suggested by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπάτορας_300_2"> (300).—Nauck's grounds for rejecting these two vv. are: (1) that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσως</quote> is out of place, since they must have been free-born; but cp. <ref n="Thuc. 8. 28">Thuc.8. 28</ref>, quoted on v. 257: (2) that <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὶν μὲν ἦσαν</quote> is wrong, because their origin remains the same. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_τροπαῖε_303">. Zeus who turns foemen to flight: see on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 143."> <title>Ant.</title>143.</ref>He is fitly invoked by her, since it was he who had brought the captives to this plight. Not, ‘Averter of evil,’ for <quote xml:lang="grc">τροπαῖος</quote> is not a classical equiv. for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποτρόπαιος</quote>. Plutarch indeed, supplies an in stance in later Greek, <title>Mor.</title>p. 149 D <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν καθαρμὸν...κινεῖν διανοεῖ καὶ παρέχειν πράγματα τοῖς τροπαίοις</quote> (alluding to the exclamation <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀλεξίκακε</quote> just before); unless <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποτροπαίοις</quote> should be read there.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὐμὸν_σπέρμα_303">, though it is the mother who speaks (so <ref n="Aesch. Supp. 275">Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title>275</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σπέρματ’ εὐτέκνου βοός</quote>, and oft.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χωρήσαντα_303">. The notion of hostile advance was associated with this verb in such phrases as <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁμόσε χωρεῖν</quote>. But when it is followed by <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς, ἐπί</quote>, or <quote xml:lang="grc">πρός τινα</quote>, the poetical usage varies somewhat from that of good prose. (1) The sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">χωρεῖν εἴς τινα</quote> is usu. friendly in prose, as <ref n="Thuc. 5. 40">Thuc.5. 40</ref>; more rarely hostile, as id. 4. 95, and <ref n="Soph. Phil. 396.">Soph. <title>Ph.</title>396.</ref> (2) <quote xml:lang="grc">χωρεῖν ἐπί τινα</quote> is hostile in prose, as <ref n="Thuc. 1. 62">Thuc.1. 62</ref>, but friendly in <ref n="Pind. N. 10">Pind. <title>N.</title>10. 73.</ref>(3) <quote xml:lang="grc">χωρεῖν πρός τινα</quote> is friendly in prose, as <ref n="Thuc. 5. 43">Thuc.5. 43</ref>, and above<ref n="Thuc. v. 285">Thuc., v. 285</ref>; but hostile here. Sophocles would possibly have preferred </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰς_303"> to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_303"> here, if v. 303 had not ended with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σε_303">.—The aor. part., not the pres., because she thinks of the onset in its ruinous result: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1113">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 1113</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰδοίμαν δέ νιν</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμὰς λαχόντ’ ἀνίας.—<emph>ποι</emph> </quote>, in any direction,—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, in any of their homes, or in any point of their fortunes. The conject. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="που_303"> seems unnecessary. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηδ_δράσεις_305">, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">δράσειας</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 1434"> <title>El.</title>1434</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν, τὰ πρὶν εὖ θέμενοι, τάδ’ ὡς πάλιν</quote> (<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὖ θῆσθε</quote>). Remark, as evidence that this suspected verse is genuine, the thoroughly idiomatic use of the fut. indic. with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_305">, in connection with the prayer: ‘if thou <emph> must</emph> do it, at least do it thus.’ So <ref n="Soph. OC 166"> <title>O. C.</title>166</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγον εἴ τιν’ οἴσεις</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς ἐμὰν λέσχαν, ἀβάτων ἀποβὰς</quote> | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">φώνει.—<emph>ἔτι</emph> </quote> after </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ζώσης_305"> is here almost pleonastic: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 3"> <title>Ant.</title>3</ref>(n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μή_εἰσίδοιμι_305">: ‘may I never <emph>see</emph> it; nay, if it is ever to happen, may it not happen while I <emph>live.</emph>’ Her words unconsciously foreshadow the troubles which, after her death, were brought upon her children by Eurystheus ( Eur. <title>Heracl.</title>). Such an allusion is quite in the poet's manner (cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1080">Soph. <title> Ant.</title>1080</ref> ff.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηδέ_305"> means ‘or,’ and that, therefore, the wish ‘not to see’ the woe is <emph> distinguished</emph> from a wish which it includes,—viz., that the woe may not come while she lives. The answer is simply that the <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote> in <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδέ</quote> means ‘and.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τεκνοῦσσα_308">: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">τέκνα ἔχουσα ὅπερ Καλλίμαχός φησι παιδοῦσα</quote> [<quote xml:lang="grc"> παιδοῦσσα</quote>: though Schneider <title>Callim.</title> fr. 431 defends <quote xml:lang="grc">τεκνοῦσα</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc"> παιδοῦσα</quote>]. No part of <quote xml:lang="grc">τεκνόεις</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">παιδόεις</quote> occurs elsewhere. But the adj. is decidedly fitter here than </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τεκοῦσα_308"> (esp. in view of v. 311): nor is there sufficient ground for the assumption that <quote xml:lang="grc">τεκνοῦσσα</quote> would imply <emph>several</emph> children.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_φύσιν_308">, judging by it: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 885"> <title>Ph.</title>885</ref> n.: <quote xml:lang="grc">φύσις</quote> of physical aspect, as <ref n="Soph. OT 740"> <title>O. T.</title>740</ref>: but otherwise below<ref n="Soph. OT 379."> O. T., 379.</ref>— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάντων_τῶνδε_308">, schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ἐκ τοῦ γάμου προσγινομένων</quote>. The different surmise in 536 agrees better with 1225 f.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γενναία_308"> =<quote xml:lang="grc">εὐγενής</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OC 76"> <title>O. C.</title>76</ref> etc.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_πατήρ_311">: the same phrase in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1296"> <title>Ai.</title>1296</ref>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 793"> <title>O. T.</title>793</ref> <ref n="Soph. OT 1482."> O. T., 1482.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ᾤκτισα_311">: for the aor., referring to the recent moment at which the feeling began, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 464">cp. 464</ref>, 1044, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1307"> <title>Ant.</title>1307</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅσῳπερ_311"> is used as if <quote xml:lang="grc"> πλεῖστον</quote>, instead of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόνη_311">, followed: see <ref n="Soph. OC 743"> <title>O. C.</title>743</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φρονεῖν_οἶδεν_311">, like <quote xml:lang="grc">σωφρονεῖν ἐπίσταται</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OT 589"> <title>O. T.</title>589</ref>). Iolè (whose actual relation to Heracles appears from 1225 f.) is feeling not only bitter grief (326), but the new shame and embarrassment caused by the presence in which she stands. While the other captives are comparatively callous, <emph>she</emph> appears to Deianeira as one whose sense of the calamity is such as might be looked for in a maiden of noble birth and spirit. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φρονεῖν_311"> here denotes that fine intelligence which is formed by gentle breeding, and which contributes to delicate propriety of behaviour. So, in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1250"> <title>Ant.</title>1250</ref>, it is conjectured of Eurydicè that, in her grief, she has sought privacy: <quote xml:lang="grc">γνώμης γὰρ οὐκ ἄπειρος, ὥσθ’ ἁμαρτάνειν</quote>: and cp. the account of Panthea's <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρετὴ καὶ εὐσχημοσύνη</quote> in <ref n="Xen. Cyrop. 5. 1">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title>5. 1</ref>§ 5.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_311"> emphasising the verb, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 490">cp. 490</ref>, 600, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 772"> <title>Ant.</title>772</ref> n.: for </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρίνοις_311">, above<ref n="Soph. Ant. 195."> Ant., 195.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γέννημα_ἐκεῖθεν_311">, an offspring of the folk there (at Oechalia). Others make the gen. partitive (supplying <quote xml:lang="grc">γεννημάτων</quote>); but this seems less natural here. For <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ἐκεῖθεν</quote> as = <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ἐκεῖ</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 601">cp. 601</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῖς ἔσωθεν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1070"> <title>Ant.</title>1070</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν κάτωθεν</quote>. (In 632 <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀκεῖθεν</quote> in not quite similar.)— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_ὑστάτοις_311"> goes with <quote xml:lang="grc">γέννημα</quote>, not with <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ἐκ</quote>., as the schol. saw: <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἐν ταῖς ἀπερριμμέναις καὶ εὐτελέσι τεταγμένη ἀλλὰ δῆλον ὅτι προὔχουσα ἐν εὐγενείᾳ</quote>. For the litotes cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 15.11"> <title>Il.</title>15. 11</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεὶ οὔ νιν ἀφαυρότατος βάλ’ Ἀχαιῶν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_τυράννων_316"> <emph>; sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">γέννημα ἦν</quote>: for the interrogative <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1502."> <title>O. C.</title>1502.</ref>The plur. (like <quote xml:lang="grc">βασιλέων</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1172"> <title>Ant.</title>1172</ref>) denotes ‘the royal house’: so, in <ref n="Soph. OC 851"> <title>O. C.</title>851</ref>, Creon calls himself <quote xml:lang="grc">τύραννος</quote>, though Eteocles is reigning.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Εὐρύτου_ἦν_316"> <emph>;</emph> The only natural sense is, ‘had Eurytus any issue?’ She had heard that he had sons (266). But we may suppose, either that she forgets this, or, better, that her question is qualified by its context, meaning, ‘had Eurytus any <emph>daughter?</emph>’ The other version, ‘was she a child of Eurytus?’ would make </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τις_316"> strangely weak. Further, in a mere surmise, such as this, the less direct inquiry seems the fitter. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνιστόρουν_317">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 253"> <title>Ph.</title>253</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς μηδὲν εἰδότ’ ἴσθι μ’ ὧν ἀνιστορεῖς</quote>: the simple <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱστορεῖν</quote> below<ref n="Soph. Phil. 382"> Ph., 382</ref> <ref n="Soph. Phil. 397"> Ph., 397</ref> <ref n="Soph. Phil. 404."> Ph., 404.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μακράν_317">: <ref n="Soph. OT 220"> <title>O. T.</title>220</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ ἂν μακρὰν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴχνευον</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυνεμπόρων_318">: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc"> ξυναιχμαλωτίδων.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχεις_318">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">compertum habes</foreign>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 9"> <title>Ant.</title> 9</ref> (n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἤνυτον_318">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 231"> <title>Ant.</title>231</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_ἡμὶν_320">, ‘to <emph>me</emph> at least’: since Lichas has not questioned thee. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1276"> <title>O. C.</title>1276</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πειράσατ’ ἀλλ’ ὑμεῖς γε</quote>: and <emph>ib.</emph> 241 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἐμέ.—<emph>ἐκ σαυτῆς</emph> </quote>, here=‘from thine own <emph>mouth</emph>’ (since Lichas cannot tell me), rather than, ‘of thine own <emph>accord.</emph>’ In <ref n="Soph. El. 343"> <title>El.</title>343</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅπαντα γάρ σοι τἀμὰ νουθετήματα <*>κείνης διδακτά, κοὐδὲν ἐκ σαυτῆς λέγεις</quote>, the sense is, ‘from thine own <emph>mind.</emph>’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_321"> closely with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυμφορά_321">, a very misfortune. Deianeira is deeply interested by the captive, and feels drawn towards her. She is anxious to know the stranger's story, in order to offer her <emph>personal</emph> sympathy. These words express the pain and regret which she would feel at <emph> not</emph> being able to do so. The subtle art of the poet's language here depends on the different shades of meaning possible for <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυμφορά</quote>. When Deianeira at last learns all, that knowledge is to her a <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυμφορά</quote> in the gravest sense: she knows that, in Iolè, she has received a <quote xml:lang="grc">πημονὴν ὑπόστεγον</quote> (376). But here she is courteously using <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυμφορά</quote> in the milder sense which it could also bear,—‘a matter of deep regret.’ Cp. <ref n="Hdt. 1. 216">Her.1. 216</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">συμφορὴν ποιεύμενοι ὅτι οὐκ ἵκετο ἐς τὸ τυθῆναι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔ_τἄρα_322"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>: lit., ‘It will be in a manner very unlike the past that she will utter a word’: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, if she does speak, it will be very unlike her conduct hitherto. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδὲν_ἐξἴσου_322"> must be taken together: for </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῷ_χρόνῳ_322"> depending on </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξ_ἴσου_322">, cp. <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 302">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title>302</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσον δ’ ἄπεσμεν τῷ πρίν. <emph>διήσει</emph> </quote> is a certain correction of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διοίσει_322">: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γλῶσσαν_322"> here is fig., ‘speech,’ precisely as in fr. 844. 3 <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὴν γλῶσσαν ἐκχέας μάτην</quote>, and <ref n="Soph. El. 596">Soph. <title>El.</title>596</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἣ πᾶσαν ἵης γλῶσσαν</quote>: for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱέναι γλῶσσαν</quote> could not mean, ‘to unloose’ the tongue: it means ‘to send forth’ an utterance, being a poetical equiv. for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱέναι φωνήν</quote>: cp. <title>Legg.</title> 890 D <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶσαν, τὸ λεγόμενον, φωνὴν ἱέντα</quote>. The use of <quote xml:lang="grc">διιέναι</quote>, as meaning to <emph> send</emph> speech <emph>through</emph> the lips, is thus the same here as in <ref n="Soph. OC 963"> <title>O. C.</title>963</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">φόνους</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">μοι</quote>...) <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ σοῦ διῆκας στόματος</quote>. The attempted interpretations of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διοίσει_322_2">, and some other conjectures, will be found in the Appendix.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἤτις_322">, causal ( <ref n="Soph. OT 1184"> <title>O. T.</title>1184</ref>), justifying v. 322.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδαμὰ_322">, adv.: <ref n="Soph. OC 1104"> <title>O. C.</title>1104</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτε_ἐλάσσονα_324">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1245"> <title>Ant.</title>1245</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὶν εἰπεῖν ἐσθλὸν ἢ κακὸν λόγον</quote>: <ref n="Hom. Od. 10.93"> <title>Od.</title>10. 93</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ μὲν γάρ ποτ’ ἀέξετο κῦμά γ’ ἐν αὐτῷ</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε μέγ’ οὔτ’ ὀλίγον</quote>: <ref n="Hdt. 3. 62">Her.3. 62</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἔστι...ὅκως τι...νεῖκός τοι ἔσται ἢ μέγα ἢ σμικρόν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὠδίνουσα_βάρος_325"> (cognate acc.): cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 790"> <title>Ai.</title>790</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρᾶξιν ἣν ἤλγησ’ ἐγώ</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Her. 990">Eur. <title>Her.</title>990</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἥρα με κάμνειν τήνδ’ ἔθηκε τὴν νόσον</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διήνεμον_327"> simply = <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἠνεμόεσσαν</quote>: so Oechalia is called <quote xml:lang="grc"> ὑψίπυργος</quote> (354) and <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰπεινή</quote> (858). The word does not occur elsewhere in classical Greek, but Hermann quotes it from Philo Byzant. <title>De septem mirabilibus</title> 1, where it means ‘fanned by breezes.’ Hermann prefers the first of the two explanations (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρημον, ὑψηλήν</quote>) given by the schol.: thinking that the epithet describes the <emph>ruins</emph> of Oechalia as <emph>patulum ventis iter praebentes.</emph> This seems very far-fetched; the more so, as the noun is </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάτραν_327"> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡ_τὐχη_327">, not the doom of captivity, but rather her present condition of mute and inconsolable grief.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτῇ_γ_327"> is emphatic; sad for <emph>her</emph>, but to be condoned by <emph>us:</emph> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γ_327"> is therefore in place.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συγγνώμην_ἔχει_327">: <ref n="Thuc. 3. 44">Thuc.3. 44</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχοντάς τι ξυγγνώμης</quote> (some claim to it). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡ_οὖν_329">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 669"> <title>O. T.</title>669</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ δ’ οὖν ἴτω</quote> : <ref n="Soph. Aj. 961"> <title>Ai.</title>961</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ δ’ οὖν γελώντων</quote> : <ref n="Aristoph. Ach. 186">Ar. <title>Ach.</title>186</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ δ’ οὖν βοώντων</quote>. Idiom thus favours </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡ_δ_329">; and </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥδ_329"> would here be too emphatic.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὕτως_ἥδιστα_329">: <foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign>, in silence.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλλην_λύπην_329">, Triclinius: and (2) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λύπην_διπλῆν_329">, F. W. Schmidt. In favour of (2), it might perhaps be said that the second <quote xml:lang="grc"> λύπην</quote> is more likely to be corrupt than the first. But it is also conceivable that the error should have arisen through the transcriber glancing forward. And, in close connection with <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς κακοῖς τοῖς οὖσιν, <emph>ἄλλην</emph> </quote> seems the fittest word. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διπλῆν_329"> would be less clear (meaning the former <quote xml:lang="grc">κακά</quote> <emph> plus</emph> a new <quote xml:lang="grc">λύπη</quote>): it would also be too emphatic for this context.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λάβοι_329">, which is possible (‘Heaven forbid that she should receive...!’). But, as this clause is linked with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐάσθω καὶ πορευέσθω</quote>,—being, in fact, merely a repetition of the command in a negative form,—I feel sure that Blaydes is right in reading </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λάβῃ_329"> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἷ_σπεύδῃς_333">, back to Cenaeum, so as to be in time for the sacrifice (287): <ref n="Soph. Trach. 599">cp. 599</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγὼ_δὲ_333">, after </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σύ_θ_333">, is warranted by the antithesis, as in 143 (n.), 286.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξαρκῆ_τιθῶ_333">, make them such as they ought to be,—set them in satisfactory order. The word is used in <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 237">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title>237</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">πλοῦτος ἐξαρκής</quote>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτοῦ_ἀμμείνας_335">, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">χώρει</quote> (from <quote xml:lang="grc">χωρῶμεν</quote> in 333). Where </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_335"> is thus used in reply, without an expressed verb, the verb can usu. be supplied <emph>directly</emph> from what immediately precedes (as in 399 <quote xml:lang="grc">νεμῶ</quote> from <quote xml:lang="grc">νεμεῖς</quote>). Here we may compare <ref n="Soph. OT 678"> <title>O. T.</title>678</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΧΟ....τί μέλλεις κομίζειν δόμων τόνδ’ ἔσω</quote> <emph>;</emph> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ΙΟ. μαθοῦσά γ’ ἥτις ἡ τύχη</quote> (<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc"> κομιῶ</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμμείνας_335">: Sophocles has the form <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμμένειν</quote> in four lyric passages (527, 648, <ref n="Soph. El. 1389"> <title>El.</title>1389</ref> <ref n="Soph. El. 1397"> El., 1397</ref>); but there is no other instance of it in tragic iambics. The apocopè of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνά</quote>, so frequent in tragic lyrics, is comparatively rare in dialogue; the examples in iambics are chiefly nouns, as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμβάτης, προσάμβασις, ἀμβολή, ἀμπνοή, ἀμπτυχή</quote>: more rarely verbs; though <ref n="Soph. Trach. 396">cp. 396</ref> (n.); <ref n="Eur. Hec. 1263">Eur. <title>Hec.</title>1263</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμβήσει</quote>. In <ref n="Eur. Tro. 1277">Eur. <title> Tro.</title>1277</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμπνέουσ’</quote> is only a <emph>v. l.</emph> for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμπνέουσ’</quote>, as in <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 1410">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title>1410</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφέρει</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναφέρει</quote>. An example in Attic prose is <ref n="Xen. Cyrop. 7. 5. 12">Xen. <title> Cyr.</title>7. 5. 12</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμβολάδος γῆς</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄνευ_336"> here = <quote xml:lang="grc">χωρίς</quote>, as in <ref n="Soph. OT 1464"> <title>O. T.</title>1464</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνευ τοῦδ’ ἀνδρός.—<emph>οὕστινάς τ’</emph> </quote>: the </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τ_336"> is placed as if </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάθῃς_336"> were to serve for both clauses: but, in the form which the sentence actually takes, this <quote xml:lang="grc"> τε</quote> properly belongs to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάθῃς_336_2">, and the second <quote xml:lang="grc">τε</quote> to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκμάθῃς_336">. (Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1415"> <title>Ph.</title>1415</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ Διός τε φράσων βουλεύματά σοι</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">κατερητύσων θ’ ὁδὸν ἣν στέλλει</quote>.) For the simple verb followed by the compound, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 566"> <title>O. T.</title>566</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔσχετε —παρέσχομεν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 249"> <title>Ph.</title>249</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶσθα—κάτοιδ’</quote>. —The masc. plur. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὕστινας_336"> alludes to Iolè: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 366"> <title>O. T.</title>366</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν τοῖς φιλτάτοις</quote> (Iocasta).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧν_εἰσήκουσας_336">: her relations with Heracles. The first clause corresponds with the information which the speaker gives in vv. 379 ff.; the second, with that which he gives in vv. 351 ff. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάντ_338">, adv., ‘in all respects’; to be taken, not with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιστήμην</quote> alone (as if =‘complete knowledge’), but with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχω ἐπιστήμην</quote>.—Nauck, who pronounces the text corrupt, contends that we can say, (1) <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτων ἐπιστήμην ἔχω</quote>, or (2) <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντα ἐπιστήμην ἔχω</quote>: but that we cannot ‘combine <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτων πάντα</quote>.’ He compares <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντα ἐπιστήμην ἔχω</quote> with <ref n="Soph. OC 583"> <title>O. C.</title>583</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ δ’ ἐν μέσῳ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ λῆστιν ἴσχεις κ.τ.λ.</quote>, where <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ δ’ ἐν μέσῳ</quote> depends on <quote xml:lang="grc">λῆστιν ἴσχεις</quote> as = <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐπιλανθάνει</quote>. Hence it appears that he takes <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντα</quote> for an acc. depending on <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιστήμην ἔχω</quote> as=<quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐπίσταμαι</quote>. But <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντα</quote> in our verse is an adverb. This adverb is used by Sophocles, not only ‘to strengthen adjectives’ (Nauck on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 721"> <title>Ant.</title>721</ref>), as in <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ πάντ’ ἄναλκις</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. El. 301"> <title>El.</title>301</ref>), but also with verbs and participles: as <ref n="Soph. Ant. 640"> <title>Ant.</title>640</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γνώμης πατρῴας πάντ’ ὄπισθεν ἑστάναι</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 99"> <title>Ph.</title>99</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάνθ’ ἡγουμένην</quote>. The adverbial use of <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντα</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιστήμην ἔχω</quote> is none the less correct because a gen., <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτων</quote>, happens to be joined with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιστήμην</quote>. In <ref n="Soph. Ant. 721"> <title>Ant.</title>721</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">φῦναι τὸν ἄνδρα πάντ’ ἐπιστήμης πλέων</quote>, the adverb certainly goes with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιστήμης πλέων</quote>: but that proves nothing against the phrase used here. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_ἔστι_339"> <emph>;</emph> Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1144"> <title>O. T.</title>1144</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί δ’ ἔστι<emph> ;</emph> πρὸς τί τοῦτο τοὔπος ἱστορεῖς</quote> <emph>;</emph> (n.). Here, as there, a mark of interrogation must follow <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔστι</quote>, since <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς</quote> can stand for <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅστις</quote> only in an indirect question.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_339">, causal gen.; so <quote xml:lang="grc"> τίνος</quote> <ref n="Soph. Phil. 327"> <title>Ph.</title>327</ref>(n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐφίστασαί_με_339">, makest me to <*>lt, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τήνδε_βάσιν_339"> (acc. of respect), in this movement (towards the house). For the second acc., cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1242"> <title>Ph.</title>1242</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς ἔσται μ’ οὑπικωλύσων τάδε</quote> <emph>;</emph> (n.): <emph>ib.</emph> 1301 <quote xml:lang="grc">μέθες με...χεῖρα</quote>. Schol.: <quote xml:lang="grc">τίνος ἕνεκεν τὴν πορείαν καὶ τὴν εἴσοδον ἱστᾷς καὶ κωλύεις</quote> <emph>;</emph> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_ἐφίστασαι_339">, ‘why hast thou come close up to me, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τήνδε_βάσιν_339_2"> (cogn. acc.), with this (hurried) step?’ But: (1) instead of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="με_339">, we should then expect </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοι_339">: which Madvig, indeed (<ref> <title>Adv.</title> 1. 227</ref>), proposed, though with the further (and needless) change of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_339_2"> to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐφ_οὗ_339">. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 776"> <title>O. T.</title>776</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρίν μοι τύχη</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιάδ’ ἐπέστη</quote>. The acc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="με_339_2"> is not adequately defended by fr. 155, <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς γάρ με μόχθος οὐκ ἐπεστάτει</quote> <emph>;</emph> where the acc. is like that which can follow <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐπιβαίνειν</quote> as = ‘to assail’ ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 138"> <title>Ai.</title>138</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὲ δ’ ὅταν πληγὴ Διὸς</quote>... | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιβῇ</quote>): ‘what trouble was not ever <emph>coming upon</emph> me?’ (2) <quote xml:lang="grc">τήνδε... βάσιν</quote> here refers more naturally to the movement in which Deianeira is stopped than to a movement which the <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἄγγελος</quote> makes towards her. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδὲ_νῦν_340">. The double </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδὲ_340"> here must not be confused with a double </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτε_340">: this is not a case of parataxis,—‘<emph> as</emph> my first story was worth hearing, <emph>so</emph> will this one be.’ The first <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲ</quote>=‘<emph>not even</emph>,’ and the second, ‘<emph>no</emph>, <emph>nor</emph>...’: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1402"> <title>O. C.</title>1402</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιοῦτον οἷον οὐδὲ φωνῆσαί τινι</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔξεσθ’ ἑταίρων, οὐδ’ ἀποστρέψαι πάλιν.—<emph>τὸν πάρος μῦθον</emph> </quote>: vv. 180—199.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάτην_340">: not <quote xml:lang="grc">ψευδῶς</quote>, as the schol. explains (a sense which must refer to the speaker, not to the hearer), but simply, ‘in vain.’ His promise of good news proved true.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δοκῶ_340">, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">μάτην σε ἀκούσεσθαι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκείνους_342">, Lichas and the captives: who are moving away into the house, but have not yet disappeared. Their movement, which would begin at v. 335, ends only at 345. As the space to be traversed by them would not be great, we may suppose that Lichas, though he does not overhear the words of the <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἄγγελος</quote>, has paused near the door of exit, in uncertainty, on seeing Deianeira stop, and is finally dismissed by a gesture of hers, after the words </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τούτους_ἔα_342"> (344). Here she forgets the </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄνευ_τῶνδ_342"> of v. 336.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_τ_342"> (the Chorus) —<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">μόναις</quote>: an addition which the emphasis on the pronouns renders needless. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοὶ_εἴργεται_344">: the verb is clearly passive: the midd. <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴργομαι</quote> occurs only as=‘to keep oneself off’ from something ( <ref n="Soph. OT 890"> <title>O. T.</title>890</ref> n.). And it is simpler to take </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδὲν_344"> as nom. than as adv. with an impers. verb. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοὶ_τ_344"> might be a dat. of interest: ‘<emph>for</emph> thee and these, nothing is excluded’: but it is truer (I think) to carry on <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξειπεῖν</quote>. Thus the strict sense is:—‘for the purpose of telling to thee and these, nothing is excluded.’ The ordinary <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν εἴργει</quote> occurs at 1257. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_δὴ_345">: <ref n="Soph. OC 31"> <title>O. C.</title>31</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χὡ_σημαινέτω_345">. Blaydes writes <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅ τι λέγεις σήμαινέ μοι</quote>: Nauck too (cr. n.) thinks the text corrupt, chiefly because <quote xml:lang="grc">σός</quote> is absent. But cp. <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 341">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title>341</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">ΦΑ. τρίτη δ’ ἐγὼ δύστηνος ὡς ἀπόλλυμαι</quote>. | <quote xml:lang="grc">ΤΡ. ἔκ τοι πέπληγμαι· ποῖ προβήσεται λόγος</quote> <emph> ;</emph> For the verb, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 598">cp. 598</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σήμαινε</quote>. Apitz cites <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Gorg. 511b">Plat. <title>Gorg.</title> 511 B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ὁ λόγος σημαίνει</quote> </cit>: but the sense there is different (‘as our argument indicates’). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δίκης_ὀρθόν_347">, conformably with the straight rule of honesty: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 853"> <title>O. T.</title>853</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δικαίως ὀρθόν</quote>, truly right (for the prophecy). For the implied metaphor, cp. <ref n="Eur. Hec. 602">Eur. <title>Hec.</title>602</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ο<*>δεν τό γ’ α<*>σχρὸν κανόνι τοῦ καλοῦ μαθών</quote>: for <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς</quote>, fr. 555 <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς ὀρθὸν φρονεῖν</quote> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">καλῶς φρονεῖν</quote>): <ref n="Thuc. 6. 82">Thuc.6. 82</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς τὸ ἀκριβὲς εἰπεῖν</quote> (so the MSS.: <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote> Krüger). The gen. <quote xml:lang="grc"> δίκης</quote> can be joined to <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρθόν</quote> (though without art.), since the latter is felt as a subst.: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1144">Soph. <title>Ai.</title>1144</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν κακῷ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">χειμῶνος</quote> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_παρῆν_347">: since <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν</quote> can mean ‘just now,’ it is not necessary to supply <quote xml:lang="grc">πάρεστιν</quote>: but it is easy to do so: cp. <ref n="Xen. Anab. 3. 3. 2">Xen. <title>An.</title>3. 3. 2</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ...καὶ Κύρῳ πιστὸς ἦν,...καὶ νῦν ὑμῖν εὔνους</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">εἰμί</quote>).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακός_347"> here=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἄπιστος</quote>, as at 468 it is opposed to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀψευδεῖν.—<emph>οὐ δίκαιος</emph> </quote> is merely a synonym for <quote xml:lang="grc">κακός</quote> (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 457">cp. 457</ref>), ‘not <emph>honest</emph>’: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 411">cp. 411</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 671"> <title>Ant.</title>671</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δίκαιον κἀγαθὸν παραστάτην</quote>. The antithesis, which is only between <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσθεν</quote>, is thus somewhat blurred. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἃ_ἐξείρηκας_350">, standing where it does, is most simply taken as an acc. of respect; though <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτων</quote> might be supplied.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀγνοία_350">: for the <quote xml:lang="grc">α—</quote>, see on <ref n="Soph. Phil. 129"> <title>Ph.</title>129</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγνοία προσῇ</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μαρτύρων_352">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 188">cp. 188</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Εὔρυτόν_Οἰχαλίαν_352">: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, <emph>slew</emph> him, and <emph>took</emph> the town. Just so in <ref n="Hom. Il. 11.328"> <title>Il.</title>11. 328</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑλέτην δίφρον τε καὶ ἀνέρε</quote> (the men are slain). This is usu. called a case of ‘zeugma’: but it is not really of the same kind as (<foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>) <ref n="Hdt. 4. 106">Her.4. 106</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐσθῆτά τε φορέουσι τῇ Σκυθικῇ ὁμοίην, γλῶσσαν δὲ ἰδίην</quote>: where the verb properly suits the first clause only, and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχουσι</quote> would naturally have been added to the second clause. The poetical use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑλεῖν</quote>, in regard to contests, included the senses, (1) ‘to overcome,’ often <emph> connoting</emph> ‘to slay’; and (2) ‘to gain by overcoming.’ Cp. <ref n="Pind. O. 1">Pind. <title>O.</title>1. 88</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕλεν δ’ Οἰνομάου βίαν, παρθένον τε σύνευνον</quote> (<emph> overcame</emph> the father in a race, and <emph>won</emph> the daughter). So we can say, ‘they conquered their oppressors,’ and ‘they conquered freedom.’ The difference is that we should not say, in one sentence, ‘they conquered their oppressors, and freedom.’ Schneidewin compares <ref n="Pind. N. 10">Pind. <title>N.</title>10. 25</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκράτησε...στρατὸν</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ... στέφανον, Μοίσαισί τ’ ἔδωκ’ ἀρόσαι</quote>. But there is much probability in Heyne's correction of the MS. <quote xml:lang="grc">Μοίσαισί τ’</quote> to <quote xml:lang="grc">Μοίσαισιν</quote> (with no comma after <quote xml:lang="grc">στέφανον</quote>).</gloss>
lemma: Εὔρυτόν θ’ ἕλοι τήν θ’ ὑψίπ. Οἰχαλίαν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὴν_ὑψίπυργον_352">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 327">cp. 327</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Οἰχαλίαν_352">: for the anapaest, excused by the proper name, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 233">cp. 233</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόνος_θεῶν_355">: whereas Lichas had represented Zeus as <quote xml:lang="grc">πράκτωρ</quote> of all (251).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θέλξειεν_355">, with irony; that gentle spell produced these exploits. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1142">Cp. 1142</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιῷδε φίλτρῳ.—<emph>αἰχμάσαι τάδε</emph> </quote> (cogn. acc.), to do these warlike deeds. The verb has here a general sense, as in <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 756">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title>756</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔνδον αἰχμάζειν</quote>, to play the warrior at home: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 97.">Soph. <title>Ai.</title>97.</ref>In <ref n="Hom. Il. 4.324"> <title>Il.</title>4. 324</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰχμὰς δ’ αἰχμάσσουσι</quote> means, ‘shall wield spears’: whence Ellendt understands here, ‘<emph>intorquere</emph> hoc excidium.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_Λυδοῖς_356">. Here <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ Λυδοῖς</quote>=‘in their country’ (248 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν Λυδοῖς</quote> ): <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1100">cp. 1100</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γῆς ἐπ’ ἐσχάτοις τόποις</quote>: <ref n="Hdt. 5. 77">Her.5. 77</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ...τῇ χώρῃ</quote>.—<emph>*<quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπ’ Ὀμφάλῃ</quote> </emph>: in subjection to her,—a common sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπό</quote> with dat. (as <ref n="Thuc. 1. 32">Thuc.1. 32</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ ἐσόμεθα ὑπ’ αὐτοῖς</quote>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπ_356"> is an almost certain correction of the MS. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπ_356"> (see cr. n.), which </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἀπὶ_356"> may have generated. If </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπ_356_2"> were retained, it could mean only ‘in the power of’: for, in reference to <emph>one person</emph>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ</quote> could not possibly mean merely ‘with.’ After <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ Λυδοῖς</quote>, however, the repetition of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπί</quote> in a different sense would here be awkward.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόνων_λατρεύματ_356">, service consisting in toils (defining gen.): <ref n="Soph. Trach. 505">cp. 505</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄεθλ’ ἀγώνων</quote>. ( <ref n="Soph. OC 105"> <title>O. C.</title>105</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μόχθοις λατρεύων</quote>, ‘thrall <emph>to</emph> woes,’ is not parallel.)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_μόρος_356">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 36"> <title>Ant.</title>36</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φόνον... δημόλευστον</quote>. A sarcastic allusion to the vivid detail with which Lichas had told the story (270 ff.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὃν_358"> refers, not to <quote xml:lang="grc"> μόρος</quote>, but back to <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἔρως</quote> (354), verses 356 f. being parenthetical; just as in 997 <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἣν</quote> refers to <quote xml:lang="grc">κρηπὶς</quote> in 992, and not to <quote xml:lang="grc">λώβαν</quote> in 996. The conjecture </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὃ_358"> would enfeeble the passage. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὃν_παρώσας_358"> expresses that the divine agent, who should have been placed in the foreground of the story (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 862">cp. 862</ref>), has been thrust out of sight. Cp. <ref n="Eur. Andr. 29">Eur. <title>Andr.</title>29</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἑρμιόνην γαμεῖ</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὐμὸν παρώσας δεσπότης δοῦλον λέχος.—<emph>ἔμπαλιν λέγει</emph> </quote>, speaks in a contrary sense. <ref n="Hdt. 1. 207">Her.1. 207</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχω γνώμην(...τὰ ἔμπαλιν ἢ οὗτοι</quote>. <ref n="Hom. Il. 9.56"> <title>Il.</title>9. 56</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲ πάλιν ἐρέει</quote> (‘gainsay’). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_ἡνίκ_359">: here <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀλλά</quote> merely serves to resume the story, after the parenthesis: cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote> in 252, 281.
