Others make it strictly adversative: ‘(It was <emph>not</emph> on account of his enslavement); <emph>rather</emph> it was when he could not persuade,’ etc.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_ἔπειθε_359">: the <emph>imperf.</emph> is regularly used with ref. to such failure (<foreign xml:lang="lat"> e.g.</foreign>, <ref n="Thuc. 3. 3">Thuc.3. 3</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπειδὴ...οὐκ ἔπειθον</quote>: id. 4. 4 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς οὐκ ἔπειθεν</quote>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_φυτοσπόρον_359">: not yet identified with Eurytus. That disclosure forms the climax, at 380. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρύφιον_λέχος_360">: cp. <ref n="Hdt. 3. 1">Her.3. 1</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ὡς γυναῖκά μιν ἔμελλε... ἕξειν, ἀλλ’ ὡς παλλακήν.—<emph>ἔγκλημα</emph> </quote> is properly the matter of the complaint: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰτίαν_360">, the imputation of blame for it. For <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰτία</quote> in this sense, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 656">Soph. <title>O. T.</title>656</ref> , <ref n="Soph. Aj. 28.">Soph. <title>Ai.</title>28.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑτοιμάσας_360">: cp. Isae. or. 11 § 14 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγῶνας παρασκευάζειν</quote> (‘to get up’ lawsuits against one).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπιστρατεύει_πατέρα_364">. I keep the traditional text, only with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τόνδ_364"> (B) instead of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶνδ_364"> (L) in 363: in the poet's time either would have been written <quote xml:lang="grc">ΤΟΝΔ</quote>. If the text be sound, it means:—‘he makes war upon her country, that in which (Lichas) said that this Eurytus was master of the throne.’ But there are three difficulties:</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τόνδ_364_2">, it may be noted, suits this view of him. And </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶνδ_364_2"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">θρόνων</quote>) would be very awkward.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπιστρατεύει_364"> and </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κτείνει_364">: but Lichas to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἶπε_364">. (Heracles cannot be the subject to <quote xml:lang="grc"> εἶπε</quote>: he needed not to tell his warriors that Eurytus <emph> reigned</emph> there; and, on the other hand, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεσπόζειν_364"> could not mean, ‘<emph>usurped.</emph>’) Such a change of subject is very harsh: still, it is not impossible; and, as the <emph> narrative</emph> of Lichas has been the foremost topic so far, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἶπε_364_2"> would at once suggest him. An example almost as bold occurs in <ref n="Thuc. 2. 3">Thuc.2. 3</ref>: <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ δὲ Πλαταιῆς... λόγους δεξάμενοι ἡσύχαζον, ἄλλως τε καὶ ἐπειδὴ ἐς οὐδένα οὐδὲν ἐνεωτέριζον. πράσσοντες δέ πως ταῦτα κατενόησαν κ.τ.λ.</quote>: where the Plataeans are the subject of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡσύχαζον</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">κατενόησαν</quote>, but the Thebans of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνεωτέριζον</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄνακτα_364"> in 364 to mean, not ‘the king,’ but some (minor) ‘prince’ or ‘chief.’ Yet, even so, her question at v. 377 is strange. (At v. 342 we saw that she ignored a hint given in v. 336: but on <emph>this</emph> question—the girl's birth—we should have expected her to be attentive.)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὴν_πατέρα_364">, so that three verses shrink into one,— <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιστρατεύει πατρίδα τῆσδε, καὶ πόλιν κ.τ.λ.</quote> This would certainly improve the passage. And it is conceivable that the interpolation should have been due to actors.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸνἐργάτην_364"> (for <quote xml:lang="grc">Εὔρυτον</quote>) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶνδ_364_3">: ‘where Heracles said (to his warriors) that the author of these wrongs was king.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥκει_365">, Heracles: he is not, indeed, yet at Trachis (and the words <quote xml:lang="grc">δόμους ὡς τούσδε</quote> go with <quote xml:lang="grc">πέμπων</quote>); but, after his distant wanderings, he may be said to ‘have arrived,’ since in his march from Oechalia he has already reached the point of Euboea nearest to his home (237). Heracles being the subject to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔπερσε_365"> and to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐντεθέρμανται_365"> (368), there would be an exceptional harshness in making Lichas the subject to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥκει_365_2">: nor would this suit the sense so well.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δόμους_τούσδε_365">: see n. on <ref n="Soph. OT 1481"> <title>O. T.</title>1481</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς τὰς ἀδελφὰς τάσδε τὰς ἐμὰς χέρας</quote>. The case for reading <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐς</quote> is stronger here than there. Yet I refrain from altering, since the house so easily suggests the household. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδ_εἰκός_368">: <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέ</quote> here=‘nor,’ rather than, ‘not even.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐντεθέρμανται_368">. This compound is not found elsewhere, while <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκθερμαίνω</quote> is frequent. But <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔνθερμος</quote> was common, and is applied by Arist. to a ‘fervid’ temperament (<title>Physiogn.</title> 2, p. 806 <emph> b</emph> 26: <quote xml:lang="grc">διάνοιαν...ἔνθερμον</quote>: 3 p. 808 <emph> a</emph> 37 <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐφυεῖς καὶ ἔνθερμοι</quote>). Here </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_368">, suggesting the inward, <emph>hidden</emph> flame, seems better than the more prosaic </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_368">. In fr. 430. 3 the corrupt <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶθ’ ἅλλεται</quote> is corrected by Valckenaer (after Ruhnken) to <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐνθάλπεται</quote>, but by Ellendt to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκθάλπεται</quote>: and the latter is confirmed by Bekker <title>Anecd.</title> p. 40. 20. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_ἀγορᾷ_371">: <quote xml:lang="grc">μέσῃ</quote> here implies, ‘open,’ ‘public,’ as in <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔδειξ’...ἐς μέσον</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 609"> <title>Ph.</title>609</ref> n.): <quote xml:lang="grc">πρός</quote>, lit. ‘close to’; the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγγελος</quote> had been one of those who stood in the outer part of the crowd, while inner circles were thronging round Lichas; he had thus been able to get away quickly (188—195). In 423 the prep. is the vaguer </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_371"> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀγορᾷ_371">, not ‘market-place,’ but ‘gathering’ (the place was a <quote xml:lang="grc">λειμών</quote>, 188); a sense not rare even in Attic prose: cp. <ref n="Xen. Anab. 5. 7. 3">Xen. <title> An.</title>5. 7. 3</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">συναγαγεῖν αὐτῶν ἀγοράν</quote>: Aeschin. or. 3 § 27 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγορὰν ποιῆσαι τῶν φυλῶν</quote> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡσαύτως_ἐμοί_371">: cp. <ref n="Hdt. 2. 67">Her.2. 67</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὣς δὲ αὕτως τῇσι κυσὶ οἱ ἰχνευταὶ θάπτονται</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_ὀρθὸν_374">: cp. the words of the messenger to Eurydicè in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1194"> <title>Ant.</title>1194</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">τί γάρ σε μαλθάσσοιμ’ ἂν ὦν ἐς ὕστερον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ψεῦσται φανούμεθ); ὀρθὸν ἁλήθεἰ ἀεί</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποῦ_πράγματος_375"> <emph>; </emph> <ref n="Soph. Aj. 102"> Ai.102</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῦ σοι τύχης ἕστηκεν</quote> <emph>: ib.</emph> 314 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν τῷ πράγματος</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπόστεγον_375"> with <quote xml:lang="grc"> εἰσδέδεγμαι</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 1386"> <title>El.</title>1386</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βεβᾶσιν ἄρτι δωμάτων ὑπόστεγοι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_δὐστηνος_377">, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγώ</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1143">cp. 1143</ref>, 1243. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 744"> <title>Ph.</title>744</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δύστηνος, ὦ τάλας ἐγώ.— <emph>ἀνώνυμος</emph> </quote>: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄσημος καὶ δυσγενής</quote> (schol.). The reference to origin is brought out by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέφυκεν_377">.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κτείνειτ_τῆσδε_377"> in 364,—which Deianeira can hardly be supposed to have forgotten. (See n. on 362 ff.) If those words be genuine, we might perhaps regard the question here as merely continuing her own bitter thought,— not as really asking for information:— ‘Wretched that I am! Is this the nameless maiden of whom he spoke?’ (Cp. the bitter self-communing of Oedipus, <ref n="Soph. OT 822"> <title>O. T.</title>822</ref>: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆρ’ ἔφυν κακός</quote> <emph> ;</emph> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆρ’ οὐχὶ πᾶς ἄναγνος</quote> <emph>;</emph>) It is not decisive against this view that the matter-of-fact <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγγελος</quote> takes the question literally.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διώμνυτο_377"> (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 255">cp. 255</ref> n.): Lichas had merely declared that he knew nothing (314—319). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_κάρτα_379">: these words begin the reply to a question in <ref n="Soph. El. 312"> <title>El.</title>312</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. Supp. 452">Aesch. <title> Suppl.</title>452</ref>: they are the first words of a speaker also in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1359">Soph. <title>Ai.</title>1359</ref>, <ref n="Soph. El. 1279.">Soph. <title>El.</title>1279.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄνομα_379"> for </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄμμα_379"> not only removes a difficulty, but is made almost certain by the question, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἆρ_ἀνώνυμοςπέφυκεν_379"> <emph>;</emph> The words were easily confused: thus in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 447"> <title>Ai.</title>447</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄμμα</quote> has been made in L from <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄνομα</quote>. By </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄνομα_379_2">, as dist. from </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φύσιν_379">, is meant partly the nobleness of the name itself (akin to Iolaüs, etc.), partly her personal renown for beauty. On the other hand, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατ_ὄμμα_379">, ‘in regard to her appearance,’ is a phrase for which there is no real parallel: it cannot be justified by the use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄψις</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 24.632"> <title>Il.</title>24. 632</ref>) in that sense. In <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1004"> <title>Ai.</title>1004</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσθέατον ὄμμα</quote> is not similar.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φύσιν_379_2"> birth, as <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1301"> <title>Ai.</title>1301</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φύσει μὲν ἦν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">βασίλεια</quote> (and <emph>ib.</emph> 1259).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_κάρτα_379_2"> are a speaker's first words. —Some, indeed, of the MSS. (as B, K, T), which give v. 379 to Deianeira, have </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_379"> instead of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_379">, with the mark of interrogation after </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φύσιν_379_3">, and only a comma after </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διώμνυτο_379">. Thus D. asks, ‘Is she obscure, <emph>or</emph> illustrious?’ But this is weak. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατρὸς_οὖσα_380"> <quote xml:lang="grc"> κ.τ.λ.</quote> The simplest account of the <quote xml:lang="grc">μέν</quote> is that <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἰόλη δὲ καλουμένη</quote> ought to have followed, but, owing to the fact that her <emph>name</emph> is primarily in question, the second clause be came </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἰόληκαλεῖτο_380">. The </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποτέ_380"> belongs in sense to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καλεῖτο_380">, not to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὖσα_380">: the imperf. refers to her former condition in her own home: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 301">cp. 301</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦσαν</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἰόλη_380">: <quote xml:lang="grc">vιόλα</quote> on the vase from Caere mentioned above (265 f. n.). Cp. Hes. fr. 45 (<emph> ap.</emph> schol. on 266 above), <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς δὲ μεθ’ ὁπλοτάτην τέκετο ξανθὴν Ἰόλειαν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀντιόχη</quote>. Curtius (<title>Etym.</title> § 590) accepts the connection with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴον</quote>. Some mythologists regard Iolè as ‘the violet dawn,’ who is wedded to the rising sun (Hyllus) after his precursor (Heracles) has passed away in fiery glory (Paley, <title>Introd. to Tr.</title>, p. 204). The poet, at least, is innocent of such symbolism.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆς_380">: 47 n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βλάστας_382">: the plur., as <ref n="Soph. OT 717"> <title>O. T.</title>717</ref>, <title>O.C.</title> 972.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδὲν_ἱστορῶν_382">, compared with the words of Lichas himself (317 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ἀνιστόρουν μακράν</quote>), seems better taken as=‘because he had not been <emph>inquiring</emph>’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτι οὐδὲν ἱστόρει</quote>), than, ‘because he did not <emph> know</emph>’ (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1484"> <title>O. T.</title>1484</ref> f.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐφώνει_382">. This implies that </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δῆθεν_382"> could stand as the first word of a sentence or a clause. Now there are certainly instances in which the special point of the irony conveyed by <quote xml:lang="grc">δῆθεν</quote> lies in words which follow it: yet, even then, <quote xml:lang="grc">δῆθεν</quote> is <emph>also</emph> retrospective. <ref n="Aesch. PB 986">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title>986</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκερτόμησας δῆθεν ὡς παῖδ’ ὄντα με</quote>: here <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς παῖδ’ ὄντα</quote> is the point: but that is no reason why <quote xml:lang="grc">δῆθεν</quote> should not refer to the whole sentence: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘thou hast mocked me, forsooth, as though I were a child,’ represents the sense no less well than, ‘thou hast mocked me as if, forsooth, I were a child.’ The same remark applies to <ref n="Thuc. 1. 127">Thuc.1. 127</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτο δὴ τὸ ἄγος οἱ Λακεδαιμόνιοι ἐκέλευον ἐλαύνειν δῆθεν τοῖς θεοῖς πρῶτον τιμωροῦντες</quote>. <ref n="Thuc. 4. 99">Id. 4. 99</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ αὖ ἐσπένδοντο δῆθεν ὑπὲρ τῆς ἐκείνων</quote>. This last example would really show <quote xml:lang="grc">δῆθεν</quote> as the first word of a clause, <emph>if</emph> it were necessary to take it exclusively with <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπὲρ τῆς ἐκείνων</quote>: ‘nor, again, were they (the Boeotians) going to make a truce about ground which, forsooth, was Athenian.’ But the sense is rather: ‘nor, again, were they going to make a truce, forsooth, [<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, as the Athenian proposal implied,] about ground which was Athenian.’ And so, here also, the irony of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δῆθεν_382_2"> affects the whole sentence, and not only the words </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδὲν_ἱστορῶν_382_2">. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄλοιντο_383"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>: a forcible way of saying, ‘Any kind of misdoing might be pardoned sooner than treachery of the kind which we see here.’ <ref n="Soph. Trach. 468">Cp. 468</ref> f., <quote xml:lang="grc">σοὶ δ’ ἐγὼ φράζω κακὸν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς ἄλλον εἶναι κ.τ.λ.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_δὲ_383">: for the place of the art., <ref n="Soph. Trach. 92">cp. 92</ref> n.: for </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δὲ_383"> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλά</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 85"> <title>Ant.</title>85</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_383"> (generic) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρέπονθαὑτῷ_383">: the treachery is aggravated by the fact of the high trust reposed in those from whom it proceeds. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1227"> <title>Ph.</title>1227</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπραξας ἔργον ποῖον ὧν οὔ σοι πρέπον</quote> <emph>;</emph> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποεῖν_385">: for the spelling, cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 120"> <title>Ph.</title>120</ref> n., and <emph>ib.</emph> p. 234. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πεύθου_387">: Nauck writes </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πυθοῦ_387">. But the change is as needless here as in <ref n="Soph. OT 604."> <title>O. T.</title>604.</ref>Where the sense is, ‘<emph>inquire</emph>,’ the pres. is right: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 993"> <title>O. C.</title>993</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πότερα πυνθάνοἰ ἂν εἰ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πατήρ σ’ ὁ καίνων</quote>: <emph> ib.</emph> 1155 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς μὴ εἰδότ’ αὐτὸν μηδὲν ὧν σὺ πυνθάνει</quote>. On the other hand in <ref n="Soph. OT 332"> <title>O. T.</title>332</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">τί ταῦτ’</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλως ἐλέγχεις<emph>;</emph> οὐ γὰρ ἂν πύθοιό μου</quote>, the aor. is required, as the sense is, ‘<emph>learn.</emph>’ Cp. above<ref n="Soph. OT 66"> O. T., 66</ref> <ref n="Soph. OT 91"> O. T., 91</ref>; and below<ref n="Soph. OT 458."> O. T., 458.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σαφῆ_387">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀληθῆ</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 1223"> <title>El.</title>1223</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔκμαθ’ εἰ σαφῆ λέγω.—<emph>πρὸς βίαν</emph> </quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, with stringent questioning (such as the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγγελος</quote> himself applies<ref n="Soph. El. 402"> El., 402</ref> ff.). The phrase is rare, except where physical force is meant; cp., however, <ref n="Soph. OC 1185"> <title>O. C.</title>1185</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γάρ σε, θάρσει, πρὸς βίαν παρασπάσει</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">γνώμης.—<emph> κρίνειν</emph> </quote> =<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνακρίνειν</quote>: 195 n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_γνώμης_389">, not away from good judgment,—not otherwise than it prescribes: <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἄνευ συνέσεως</quote> (schol.). Cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἀπὸ καιροῦ, οὐκ ἀπὸ τρόπου</quote> (n. on <ref n="Soph. OC 900"> <title>O. C.</title>900</ref>): Plat. <title>Theaet.</title>p. 179 C <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἀπὸ σκοποῦ εἴρηκεν</quote>. Others understand, ‘not contrary to <emph>my own</emph> judgment’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτο κἀμοὶ ἀρέσκει</quote>, schol.). <ref n="Hom. Il. 10.324"> <title>Il.</title>10. 324</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σοὶ δ’ ἐγὼ οὐχ ἅλιος σκοπὸς ἔσσομαι, οὐδ’ ἀπὸ δόξης</quote> (‘belying thy hope’): <emph> ib.</emph> 1. 561 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπὸ θυμοῦ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μᾶλλον ἐμοὶ ἔσεαι</quote>. But here <quote xml:lang="grc">γνώμης</quote> seems better taken generally. Distinguish the sense in <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 674"> <title>Eum.</title>674</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπὸ γνώμης φέρειν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ψῆφον</quote> (in accordance with one's opinion).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_ἀγγέλων_391">: though it would be easy to supply <quote xml:lang="grc">κληθείς</quote> from </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτόκλητος_391"> ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 289"> <title>Ai.</title>289</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄκλητος οὐδ’ ὑπ’ ἀγγέλων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">κληθείς</quote>), it is needless to do so: cp. <ref n="Eur. Andr. 561">Eur. <title>Andr.</title>561</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ μιᾶς σε κληδόνος προθυμίᾳ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μετῆλθον, ἀλλὰ μυρίων ὑπ’ ἀγγέλων</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ὁρᾷς_394">. I receive this slight and easy correction of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰσορᾷς_394">, holding that the latter does not admit of any sound defence. Various explanations of it have been attempted. (1) Seidler: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰσορᾷς_394_2"> governs the gen. No one would now maintain this: the alleged instances are irrelevant ( <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 815">Ar. <title>Ran.</title> 815</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">παρίδῃ</quote> with a gen. <emph>absol.</emph> : <title>M.</title> 1. 1. 11 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδεὶς δὲ τώποτε Σωκράτους οὐδὲν ἀσεβὲς...οὔτε πράττοντος εἶδεν</quote>, etc., where the verb governs the acc., and the gen. is possessive). (2) Hermann: the constr. is, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_394"> (‘<emph>since</emph>’), </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἕρποντος_εἰσορᾷς_394"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτο, ἕρποντά με</quote>). But <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote> must go with the gen. absol. (3) Nauck: </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ἐμοῦ_394">, (<quote xml:lang="grc">οὕτως</quote>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰσορᾷς_394_3"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμὲ ἕρποντα</quote>). But (<emph>a</emph>) this implies a harsh asyndeton (<quote xml:lang="grc">δίδαξον —εἰσορᾷς</quote>): and (<emph>b</emph>) the constr. is not justified by <ref n="Soph. Aj. 281"> <title>Ai.</title>281</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ὧδ’ ἐχόντων τῶνδ’ ἐπίστασθαί σε χρή</quote> (cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1179"> <title>Ant.</title>1179</ref> n.). (4) Matthiae: there is a confusion between </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰσορᾷς_ἐμοῦ_394">, and <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote> (since) <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰσορᾷς ἕρποντα εμέ</quote>. (5) Dindorf: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰσορᾷς_394_4"> is parenthetic. This is the best plea: but it does not suit the idiom of tragedy. The parenthetic <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁρᾷς</quote> (interrogative) does not support it.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ὁρᾷς_394_2">, Blaydes well points out that a double </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_394_2"> occurs elsewhere: 1241 <quote xml:lang="grc">τάχ’, ὡς ἔοικας, ὡς νοσεῖς φράσεις</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 735"> <title>Ant.</title>735</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁρᾷς τάδ’ ὡς εἴρηκας ὡς ἄγαν νέος</quote> <emph>; </emph> <ref n="Soph. El. 1341"> El.1341</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤγγειλας, ὡς ἔοικεν, ὡς τεθνηκότα</quote>: and cp. the double <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 729."> <title>Ai.</title>729.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_ταχείας_395">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 727">cp. 727</ref>, and n. on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 994."> <title>Ant.</title>994.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺν_βραδεῖ_395">: <ref n="Soph. OC 1602"> <title>O. C.</title>1602</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταχεῖ...σὺν χρόνῳ</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὶν_λόγους_395">. The compound verb is confirmed, as against the <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ νεώσασθαι</quote> of the MSS. (cr. n.), by the schol.'s paraphrase, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνακαινίσασθαι</quote> [this schol. has no lemma]; and more esp. by Eustath., p. 811, 20 (on <quote xml:lang="grc">νειοῖο βαθείης</quote>, <ref n="Hom. Il. 10.353"> <title>Il.</title>10. 353</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">νεᾶν, οὗ χρῆσις παρ’ Ἡσιόδῳ ἐν τῷ, θέρεος δὲ νεωμένη οὐκ ἀπατήσει</quote> ( <ref n="Hes. WD 462"> <title>Op.</title>462</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ οὗ καὶ παρὰ Σοφοκλεῖ ἀνανεώσασθαι λόγους, τὸ ἀνακινῆσαι</quote>. On the other hand, the MS. <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote> is clearly sound: <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὶν καί</quote> here =‘before <emph>even</emph>’; see on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 280."> <title>Ant.</title>280.</ref>For the apocopè of the prep. in <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀννεώσασθαι</quote>, <ref target="cl335">see on 335</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμμείνασ’</quote>. The synizesis of <quote xml:lang="grc">εω</quote>, assumed by those who write <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀνανεώσασθαι</quote>, would be very harsh. I doubt whether Eustathius was right in his theory —suggested by <quote xml:lang="grc">νεᾶν</quote>—that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνανεοῦσθαι λόγους</quote> was a metaphor from ploughing. Had it been so, it ought to have implied, ‘going again over <emph>old</emph> ground,’ like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναπολεῖν ἔπη</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1238"> <title>Ph.</title>1238</ref> n.). Here the sense is simply, ‘to renew converse.’ Cp. Polyb. 5. 36 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀεὶ τὸν λόγον ἀνενεοῦτο</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_καὶ_398">: 246.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_ἀλη_398"> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θείας_398">, the faithfulness of the truth,= the honest truth.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νεμεῖς_398">, as in <quote xml:lang="grc">νέμειν μοῖράν τινι</quote>, because she claims a true account as <emph>due</emph> to her: 436 f. <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ...ἐκκλέψῃς λόγον</quote>. Cp. the pass. in <ref n="Hdt. 9. 7">Her.9. 7</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ μὲν ἀπ’ ἡμέων οὕτω ἀκίβδηλον νέμεται ἐπὶ τοὺς Ἕλληνας</quote>: so honestly do we discharge our duty towards the Greeks.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νεμεῖς_398_2"> must be read here. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νέμεις_398"> has been explained as follows:—(1) Wunder: ‘Do you give the pledge of veracity?’—<foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign>, ‘Are you prepared to swear that you will speak the truth?’ (2) Linwood, ‘colis, <emph>observas</emph>’: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘Do you respect fidelity to the truth?’ (3) Campbell takes <quote xml:lang="grc">νέμεις</quote> as ‘possess,’ ‘wield,’ ‘use’; rendering, ‘And dost thou maintain the faithful spirit of truth?’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺ_με_401"> <foreign xml:lang="grc"> κ.τ.λ.</foreign> (2) It is out of accord with Deianeira's courteous dignity that she should address Lichas with such words as </gloss>
lemma: σὺ δ’ εἰς τί δή με

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τόλμησον_φρονεῖς_401"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὗτος_ὧδε_402">: the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγγελος</quote> roughly bespeaks attention for his own question; <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧδε</quote>=<quote xml:lang="grc"> δεῦρο</quote> (<title>O.T.</title> 7 n.). Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1121"> <title>O. T.</title>1121</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὗτος σύ, πρέσβυ, δεῦρό μοι φώνει βλέπων</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1047"> <title>Ai.</title>1047</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὗτος, σὲ φωνῶ</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺ_δ_403">: a reproof of the meddling stranger. Cp. Isae. or. 8 § 24 <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺ δὲ τίς εἶ<emph>;</emph> σοὶ δὲ τί προσήκει θάπτειν<emph>;</emph> οὐ γιγνώσκω σε.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τόλμησον_404">, an ironical rejoinder: ‘bring yourself to do it,’—‘have the goodness to do it.’ (Not, ‘dare.’) Cp. <title> O.C.</title> 184, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 82"> <title>Ph.</title>82</ref> <ref n="Soph. Phil. 481."> Ph., 481.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_φρονεῖς_404">, ‘if thou comprehendest’ (the question). Not, ‘if thou art sane’—which would be too strong here.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἱστορῶ_404"> with double acc., like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρωτῶ</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 621">Soph. <title> Ph.</title>621</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί μ’ ἱστορεῖς τόδε</quote> <emph> ;</emph> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δάμαρτα_404"> were not followed by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεσπότιν_404">, the change of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θ_404"> to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δ_404"> made by Blaydes would be probable: cp. <title>O.C.</title> 1217 n. But, where <emph> three</emph> relationships of the same persons are mentioned, there is no reason for preferring </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέ_δέ_404"> to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τε_μάταια_404"> (adv., cp. <title> O.T.</title> 883 n.), suffering a delusion of the eyes. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 863">Cp. 863</ref>: <title>C.</title> 3. 27. 39 (<emph>an me</emph>) <title>Ludit imago</title> | <title>Vana?</title> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δίκαια_409">: for the plur., <ref n="Soph. Trach. 64">cp. 64</ref>.— The division of the verse between two speakers (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντιλαβή</quote>) gives vivacity: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 418">cp. 418</ref>, 876. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δίκαιος_411">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 348">cp. 348</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποικίλας_412"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ι—</quote> ): <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1121">cp. 1121</ref>: for <quote xml:lang="grc">ποικίλος</quote> with ref. to subtlety, see on <ref n="Soph. OT 130"> <title>O. T.</title>130</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ ποικιλῳδὸς Σφίγξ</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἱστορούμενος_415">=<quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐρωτώμενος</quote>, a comparatively rare use of this passive; so <ref n="Hdt. 1. 24">Her.1. 24</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κληθέντας ἱστορέεσθαι εἴ τι λέγοιεν.—<emph>οὐ σιγηλὸς εἶ</emph> </quote>: as Creon calls the <quote xml:lang="grc">φύλαξ</quote> a <quote xml:lang="grc">λάλημα</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 320."> <title>Ant.</title>320.</ref>Possibly an echo of <ref n="Aesch. Supp. 567">Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title>567</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγ’, εἴ τι βούλει· καὶ γὰρ οὐ σιγηλὸς εἶ</quote>: where the phrase alludes to the rhetoric of the herald Copreus. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κάτοισθα_418">: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, thou knowest whom I mean: <ref n="Soph. OT 1048"> <title>O. T.</title>1048</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅστις κάτοιδε τὸν βοτῆρ’ ὂν ἐννέπει</quote>. The conject., <quote xml:lang="grc"> κάτοισθα <emph>δῆτ’</emph> <emph>;</emph>—<emph>οὔ</emph> φημί</quote>, assumed that <quote xml:lang="grc">κάτοισθα</quote>=<quote xml:lang="grc">γιγνώσκεις</quote> (‘knowest who she is’). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἣν_ὁρᾷς_419">. If these words are sound, they mean, ‘on whom you look with (affected) ignorance.’ There is little force in the objection that Iolè is not actually present: the Messenger is calling up the recent scene (314—319), which is so fresh in their minds. The real question is,—could </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπἀγνοίας_419"> be thus used,—as=‘<emph>with</emph>’ (not ‘<emph>from</emph>’) ‘ignorance’? Elsewhere <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπό</quote> denotes some external accompaniment of action, as (1) <emph>sound</emph>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπὸ συρίγγων</quote>: or <emph>silence</emph>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπ’ εὐφήμου βοῆς</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. El. 630"> <title>El.</title>630</ref>): (2) <emph>light</emph>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπὸ λαμπάδων.</quote>: (3) <emph>a pressure from without</emph>, as <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπὸ μαστίγων</quote>. There is perhaps no instance in which it refers distinctly to the mental or moral circumstances (as distinct from motives) of the agent. In <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 1299">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title>1299</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπ’ εὐκλείας θάνῃ</quote> means, ‘amid men's praises’: even in <ref n="Eur. Hec. 351">Eur. <title>Hec.</title>351</ref> , <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐθρέφθην ἐλπίδων καλῶν ὕπο</quote>, Polyxena alludes not merely to the hopes in her own breast, but to the fair auguries of those who watched her youth. Possibly the use of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπ_ἀγνοίας_419"> in this verse may have been felt to convey a certain irony which excused it; as if it implied, ‘with a look of ignorance assumed for the occasion,’—the deceiver's outward equipment for his part.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίς_μολὼν_421">: <ref n="Hom. Od. 1.170"> <title>Od.</title>1. 170</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς πόθεν εἶς ἀνδρῶν</quote> <emph>;</emph> <ref n="Soph. El. 779">Soph. <title>El.</title> 779</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίνες</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πόθεν πορεύεσθ)<emph>;</emph>—<emph>πάρα</emph> </quote> is much better here than <quote xml:lang="grc">παρών</quote>, a corruption which may have been induced by <quote xml:lang="grc">μολὼν</quote> above. In 431, on the other hand, the emphasis of <quote xml:lang="grc"> παρὼν</quote> is fitting. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολλοῖσιν_ἀστῶν_423"> answers </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποίοις_ἀνθρώποισι_423">; The conject. <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>πολλοῖς ἐν</emph> ἀστῶν</quote> is admissible (<title>O.T.</title> 178 n.), but unnecessary.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀγορᾷ_423">: 372 n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταῦτά_423"> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γ_423">: Iolè's name and birth: </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_423"> hints that more is in reserve.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ναί_423"> be genuine here, it stands, of course, <emph>exira metrum</emph>, as in <title>I. T.</title> 742 <quote xml:lang="grc">ναί</quote>. | <quote xml:lang="grc">πείσω σφε κ.τ.λ.</quote> There, too, it has good MS. authority, but is omitted by Dindorf. Here, perhaps, it might indicate a moment of embarrassment on the part of the herald, who now sees that he is detected. The </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_423_2"> after </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλύειν_423"> makes </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ναί_423_2"> unnecessary, but proves nothing against it.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταὐτὸ_423">: other places where tragic metre proves this form are <title>O.T.</title> 734; fr. 771 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γάρ τι βουλῆς ταὐτὸ καὶ δρόμου τέλος</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Med. 564">Eur. <title>Med.</title>564</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς ταὐτὸ θείην</quote>. But metre proves <quote xml:lang="grc">ταὐτόν</quote> in five places of Soph. (<title>O.T.</title> 325: <title>O.C.</title> 612, 1419: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 546"> <title>Ph.</title>546</ref> <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1256"> Ph., 1256</ref>), as in <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 625">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title>625</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Orest. 1280">Eur. <title>Or.</title>1280</ref>, etc. Aristophanes uses both forms (<title>Nub.</title> 663 <quote xml:lang="grc">ταὐτό</quote>, <ref n="Aristoph. Kn. 319"> <title>Eq.</title>319</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταὐτόν</quote>, etc.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δόκησιν_423"> being the important word in the first, and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξακριβῶσαι_423"> in the second. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξακρ. λόγον</quote> means here, ‘to render a statement <emph>precise</emph>,’ by bringing definite evidence in support of it. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποίαν_δόκησιν_427">; This idiom, so common in colloquial Attic ( <ref n="Aristoph. Ach. 61">Ar. <title>Ach.</title>61</ref> f., etc.), is alien from tragedy; but Schneidewin and others quote <title> Helen.</title> 566 <quote xml:lang="grc">ΕΛ. ὦ χρόνιος ἐ<*>θὼν σῆς δάμαρτος ἐς χέρας</quote>. | <quote xml:lang="grc">ΜΕ. ποίας δάμαρτος</quote> <emph> ;</emph> (‘how? “<emph>wife</emph>”?’). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγὼ_δάμαρτα_429">; Another colloquialism: cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Lys. 529">Ar. <title>Lys.</title>529</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπανορθώσαιμεν ἂν ὑμᾶς</quote>. | —<quote xml:lang="grc">ὑμεῖς ἡμᾶς<emph>;</emph> δεινόν γε λέγεις κοὐ τλητὸν ἔμοιγε.—<emph>τόνδε τίς</emph> κ.τ.λ.</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 444"> Soph. <title>Ph.</title>444</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτον οἶσθ’ εἰ ζῶν κυρεῖ</quote> <emph>;</emph> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοῦ_429"> seems better here than </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σου_429">. It may be noted that, instead of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἤκουσεν_429">, we should usu. have </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἤκουσα_429">: see on <ref n="Soph. OC 6."> <title>O. C.</title>6.</ref>Shilleto (<emph>ap.</emph> Pretor here) was disposed, on this ground, to think that vv. 431—433 should be given to Deianeira: but they are not in her spirit. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡ_Λυδία_432"> (<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">γυνή</quote>)=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ Λυδή</quote> (70), Omphalè. The adj. <quote xml:lang="grc">Λύδιος</quote> (frequent in poetry) is used by Soph. in fr. 728 <quote xml:lang="grc">Λυδία λίθος</quote>. And as in fr. 49 he has <quote xml:lang="grc">Λυδῆς</quote> (for <quote xml:lang="grc">Λυδίας</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">κερκίδος</quote>, so here he admits the converse licence. Bothe and others take </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡ_Λυδία_432_2"> as ‘Lydia,’—a bold equiv. for <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀφὶ Λυδοῖς λατρεύματα</quote> (356). This is tenable, but seems less natural.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_ἔρωςφανείς_432">, the love for her, as it was manifested,—<quote xml:lang="grc">φανείς</quote> implying that this manifestation was sudden and violent,—like a fire blazing forth: cp. <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 353">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title>353</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦρξεν μέν, ὦ δέσποινα, τοῦ παντὸς κακοῦ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">φανεὶς ἀλάστωρ ἢ κακὸς δαίμων ποθέν</quote>.—For this third clause, reiterating the sense of the first (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡςταύτης_πόθῳ_432"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>), see on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 465">Soph. <title>Ant.</title>465</ref>—468. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀποστήτω_434">: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 912"> <title>El.</title>912</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆσδ’ ἀποστῆναι στέγης</quote>: <ref n="Thuc. 7. 28">Thuc.7. 28</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποστῆναι ἐκ Σικελίας</quote>. Here a prose-write<*> would have said rather <quote xml:lang="grc"> μεταστήτω.—<emph>νοσοῦντι ληρεῖν</emph> </quote>: the dat. is bold, but does not warrant suspicion; it follows the analogy, partly of <quote xml:lang="grc">διαλέγεσθαί τινι</quote>, but more especially of <quote xml:lang="grc">φιλονεικεῖν τινι</quote> (<ref n="Plat. Laws 731a">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 731 A</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">στασιάζειν τινί</quote> (<ref n="Plat. Rep. 556e"> id. <title>Rep.</title> 556 E</ref>): the notion is, ‘to hold a silly <emph> controversy</emph> with a madman.’ Cp. the schol., <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ φιλονεικήσω πρὸς αὐτόν</quote>. (For other examples of bold datives, cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1232"> <title>Ant.</title>1232</ref> n.) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρός_Διός_436">: <ref n="Soph. OC 250"> <title>O. C.</title>250</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νάπος_436">: the <quote xml:lang="grc">ὔψιστος πάγος</quote> of Oeta (1191), as conceived in this play, is well-wooded (1195 f.). It was sacred to Zeus (200 n.). In an oracle of the Clarian Apollo, <emph>ap.</emph> Euseb. <title>Praep. Ev.</title> 5. 214, it symbolises the blest place which is reached by the rugged path of virtue: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔστιν ἐν Τρηχῖνος αἴῃ κῆπος Ἡρακλήϊος</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντ’ ἔχων θάλλοντα, πᾶσι δρεπόμενος πανημαδόν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ὀλιζοῦται, βέβριθε δ’ ὑδάτεσιν διηνεκές.—<emph> καταστράπτοντος</emph> </quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 729"> <title>Ph.</title>729</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θείῳ πυρὶ παμφαής, Οἴτας ὑπὲρ ὄχθων</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκκλέψῃς_λόγον_436">, ‘steal the story away,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘keep back from me that which ought to be told.’ Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 449c">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 449 C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δοκεῖς...εἶδος ὅλον οὐ τὸ ἐλάχιστον ἐκκλέπτειν τοῦ λόγου, ἵνα μὴ διέλθῃς</quote> </cit>: ‘you seem to be cheating us out of a whole chapter which is a very important part of the story’ (Jowett).—Not, ‘<emph>falsify</emph> your story.’—Distinguish the use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκκλέπτειν</quote> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξαπατᾶν</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 55."> <title>Ph.</title>55.</ref> </gloss>

data.quote:
δοκεῖς...εἶδος ὅλον οὐ τὸ ἐλάχιστον ἐκκλέπτειν
							τοῦ λόγου, ἵνα μὴ διέλθῃς

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακῇ_438">, here opp. to <quote xml:lang="grc"> χρηστῇ</quote>: one who is capable of rancour. (Not, ‘cowardly.’)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἀνθρώπων_438">: human nature generally; not <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀνδρῶν</quote>: the latter would be at once less delicate and less pathetic.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέφυκεν_438">: Nauck's </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πεφύκας_438"> is neither better nor worse than the vulgate. If <quote xml:lang="grc">πεφύκας</quote> had been traditional, doubtless some one would have conjectured <quote xml:lang="grc">πέφυκεν.—<emph>χαίρειν … τοῖς αὐτοῖς</emph> </quote>, to delight in the same things: <quote xml:lang="grc">μεταβολὴ πάντων γλυκύ</quote>.— Wunder and others understand: ‘joy is not always given by nature to the same persons.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἔρωτι_νυν_441">, like <ref n="Soph. OT 31"> <title>O. T.</title>31</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοῖσι μέν νυν</quote>: so in Ionic prose, as <ref n="Hdt. 4. 145">Her.4. 145</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὗτος μέν νυν ταῦτα ἔπρησσε.— <emph>ἀντανίσταται</emph> </quote>: like the athlete who rises, when called by the herald, and presents himself for the contest: <ref n="Hdt. 8. 59">Her.8. 59</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν τοῖσι ἀγῶσι οἱ προεξανιστάμενοι</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, <emph>before</emph> they are thus summoned) <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥαπίζονται</quote>. So <ref n="Plut. Sull. 7">Plut. <title>Sull.</title>7</ref>(with ref. to a contest for the consulship) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντανίστατο δ’ αὐτῷ Μάριος.—<emph>ἐς χεῖρας</emph> </quote>, with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντανίσταται</quote>: a terse way of saying, ‘so as to come to close quarters’: <ref n="Soph. OC 835">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 835</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τάχ’ εἰς βάσανον εἶ χερῶν</quote> (n.); <emph> ib.</emph> 975 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς χεῖρας ἦλθον</quote>. <ref n="Plut. Thes. 5">Plut. <title>Thes.</title>5</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγχέμαχοι καὶ μάλιστα δὴ πάντων εἰς χεῖρας ὠθεῖσθαι</quote> (to push forward to close quarters) <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς ἐναντίοις μεμαθηκότες</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πύκτης_ὅπως_441">. No one can parry the adroit and rapid blows of Erôs. His antagonist fares like the barbarian opposed to the skilled pugilist (<ref n="Dem. 4.40">Dem. or. 4 § 40</ref>),—<quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ πληγεὶς ἀεὶ τῆς πληγῆς ἔχεται, κἂν ἑτέρωσε πατάξῃς, ἐκεῖσέ εἰσιν αἱ χεῖρες· προβάλλεσθαι δ’ η— βλέπειν ἐναντίον οὔτ οἶδεν οὔτ’ ἐθέλει</quote>. Schneidewin cp. Anacreon fr. 63. 3 <quote xml:lang="grc">στεφάνους ἔνεικον, ὡς δὴ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς Ἔρωτα πυκταλίζω</quote>: but the resemblance is only verbal; the reveller does not wish to <emph>resist</emph> Love, but to <emph>make trial</emph> of his might.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_φρονεῖ_441">: cp. Eur. fr. 271 <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἔρωτα δ’ ὅστις μὴ θεὸν κρίνει μέγαν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ τῶν ἁπάντων δαιμόνων ὑπέρτατον</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ σκαιός ἐστιν, ἢ καλῶν ἄπειρος ὢν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ οἶδε τὸν μέγιστον ἀνθρώποις θεόν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄρχει_θεῶν_443">: so of <quote xml:lang="grc">Κύπρις</quote>, fr. 856. 13 <quote xml:lang="grc">τίν οὐ παλαίουσ’ ἐς τρὶς ἐκβάλλει θεῶν</quote> <emph>; ib.</emph> 15 <quote xml:lang="grc">Διὸς τυραννεῖ πλευμόνων</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀμοῦ_γε_444">: instead of saying <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ βροτῶν</quote>, she touchingly refers to her own experience: she, certainly, (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_444">,) can attest the Love-god's power.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πῶς_οὐ_444"> clearly goes with what follows; it would be weak as a parenthesis (<quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς δ’ οὔ</quote> <emph> ;</emph>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἵας_ἐμοῦ_444">, by assimilation to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑτέρας</quote>, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">οἵα γ’ ἐγώ</quote>: <ref n="Thuc. 7. 21">Thuc.7. 21</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς ἄνδρας τολμηροὺς οἵους καὶ Ἀθηναίους</quote>. The </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_444_2"> means, ‘a poor mortal like myself.’ It should not be transposed and placed after <quote xml:lang="grc"> χἁτέρας</quote> (‘and another <emph>too</emph>’).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὠμῷ_ἀνδρὶ_445">: for </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τε_445"> irregularly followed by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_445"> (447), cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Ion 535d">Plat. <title>Ion</title> 535 D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃς ἂν...κλαίῃ τ’ ἐν θυσίαις καὶ ἑορταῖς, μηδὲν ἀπολωλεκὼς τούτων, ἢ φοβῆται.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ὃς ἂν...κλαίῃ τ’ ἐν θυσίαις καὶ ἑορταῖς, μηδὲν
							ἀπολωλεκὼς τούτων, ἢ φοβῆται.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῇδε_νόσῳ_445">, the violent passion of love: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 544">cp. 544</ref>: this was prob. the sense in fr. 615 (from the <title>Phaedra</title>), <quote xml:lang="grc">νόσους δ’ ἀνάγκη τὰς θεηλάτους φέρειν</quote>. But in 491 the meaning is different.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μεμπτός_445">: for the active sense, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 969"> <title>O. T.</title>969</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_αἰσχροῦ_447">, that which is in no way of a shameful kind (generic <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote>): cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 494"> <title>Ant.</title>494</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν μηδὲν ὀρθῶς... τεχνωμένων</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 409"> <title>Ph.</title>409</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδὲν δίκαιον</quote> (n.).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηδ_τινος_447">. As we could say, <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ μὴ ἐμοὶ κακόν τι</quote> (‘what is not any ill to me’), so here it seems simplest to carry on the </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_447">: though it is not necessary to do so.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_ἀλλ_449"> <quote xml:lang="grc"> κ.τ.λ.</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘it is impossible that I should have bitter feelings towards Heracles or Iolè (and so the danger which you may fear is imaginary); <emph>but</emph> you, on the other hand, if you are withholding the truth, are doing wrong.’ For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_ταῦτα_449">, finally dismissing a supposition, cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 470"> <title>Ai.</title>470</ref>: <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα</quote> is sometimes omitted, as in 709 below, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 289"> <title>Ant.</title>289</ref>, <ref n="Soph. El. 448."> <title>El.</title>448.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μαθὼν_ἐκμανθάνεις_449">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 336">cp. 336</ref> f. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὑτὸν_451">=<quote xml:lang="grc">σεαυτόν</quote> : <ref n="Soph. OC 929"> <title>O. C.</title>929</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰσχύνεις πόλιν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν αὐτὸς αὑτοῦ.—<emph>ὅταν θέλῃς</emph> κ.τ.λ.</quote>: the form is general, but the reference is to this particular case: when you wish to prove <emph>kind</emph> (by sparing pain), you will be found the reverse (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 458">cp. 458</ref>). —For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀφθήσει_451">, cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 709."> <title>Ant.</title>709.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κὴρ_454">, a deadly thing ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 42"> <title>Ph.</title>42</ref> <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1166"> Ph., 1166</ref> ): </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρόσεστιν_454">, said of a quality or a repute which attaches to a man: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1079"> <title>Ai.</title>1079</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δέος γὰρ ᾧ πρόσεστιν αἰσχύνη θ’ ὁμοῦ</quote>: cp. <emph>ib.</emph> 521. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπως_λήσεις_455"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>: and as for the hope of your escaping detection, not even that comes to pass (as a result of reticence). Cp. <title>O.T.</title> 1058 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἂν γένοιτο τοῦθ’, ὅπως</quote>... | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ φανῶ τοὐμὸν γένος</quote>. Instead of saying, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ αὖ τοῦτο γίγνεται, ὅπως λήσεις</quote>, the speaker puts </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπως_λήσεις_455_2"> first, to mark the fresh hypothesis. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέδοικας_ταρβεῖς_457">: for the substituted synonym, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 347">cp. 347</ref> f.: <ref n="Soph. OT 54"> <title>O. T.</title>54</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ εἴπερ ἄρξεις τῆσδε γῆς, ὥσπερ κρατεῖς</quote> (n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτο_457">, emphatic: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 912"> <title>Ph.</title>912</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλείστας_εἷς_460">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 563"> <title>O. C.</title>563</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔγημε_460"> does not necessarily denote wedlock: <ref n="Eur. Tro. 44">Eur. <title>Tro.</title>44</ref>(of Cassandra) <quote xml:lang="grc">γαμεῖ βιαίως σκότιον Ἀγαμέμνων λέχος.— <emph>δή</emph> </quote> =<quote xml:lang="grc">ἤδη</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OT 968">Soph. <title>O. T.</title>968</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἠνέγκατ_462">: so <title>Legg.</title> 762 A (quoted by Campbell) <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῖς μὲν θωπείαις ὑπείκοντες ὀνείδη φερέσθωσαν ἐν πάσῃ τῇ πόλει</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘have reproaches for their reward.’ (For the normal use, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phaedrus 245b"> <title>Phaedr.</title> 245 B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φερέσθω τὰ νικητήρια</quote> </cit>.) The irony of <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρεσθαι ὀνείδη</quote> is less open than in such phrases as <quote xml:lang="grc">πένθη καρποῦσθαι</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">δάκρυα κερδαίνειν</quote>: we might rather compare our own phrase, ‘to <emph>come off</emph> secondbest’ (instead of ‘victorious’). For <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠνέγκατο...ἐκ</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 968"> <title>El.</title>968</ref> f.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥδε_ἐντακείῃ_462">, (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνέγκαιτ’</quote>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἂν_462"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὄνειδος</quote>). For the ellipse of the optat., cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 364"> <title>El.</title>364</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς σῆς δ’ οὐκ ἐρῶ τιμῆς τυχεῖν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτ’ ἂν σύ, σώφρων γ’ οὖσα</quote> (<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρῴης</quote>): <ref n="Soph. Phil. 115"> <title>Ph.</title>115</ref> n. Though </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδ_462"> (‘not even’) goes closely with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_462">, yet </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἂν_462_2"> is placed between them: cp. <title>O.C.</title> 272 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ἂν ὧδ’ ἐγιγνόμην κακός</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐντακείη_φιλεῖν_462">; the subject to the verb is surely Iolè. To make Heracles the subject is not impossible (Greek could be bold in such transitions),— but it would be excessively harsh. Deianeira has already implied that she believed Iolè to be enamoured of Heracles (444). Such a belief would mitigate, rather than increase, the wife's pain. The opposite supposition would be still more humiliating; for it would imply more persistent ardour on the part of Heracles. And it is pathetically natural that Deianeira should assume Iolè's passion as a matter of course.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐντακείη_462"> admits of two explanations: I prefer the first. (1) ‘Though she be utterly <emph>absorbed in</emph> her love’: lit., melted into it,—with her whole soul irrevocably steeped in it. The metaphor is from pouring molten wax or metal into a mould, to which it cleaves. Extant examples of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐντήκεσθαι</quote> show only the converse way of speaking, as if here we had <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ φιλεῖν ἐντακείη αὐτῇ</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 1311"> <title>El.</title>1311</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μῖσός τε γὰρ παλαιὸν ἐντέτηκέ μοι</quote>: fr. 856. 7 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐντήκεται γὰρ</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρως</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">πλευμόνων ὅσοις ἔνι</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ψυχή</quote>. But cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1311"> <title>Ant.</title>1311</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">συγκέκραμαι δύᾳ</quote> : <ref n="Aesch. Supp. 1029">Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title>1029</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γαμέτας</quote> | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">συντηχθεὶς ἀλόχῳ</quote> (‘husband <emph>made one with</emph> wife’): Plut. <title> Mor.</title>p. 342 C <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῖς ἐλπίσιν ἤδη...ἐμπεφυκώς</quote> (‘absorbed in’ his hopes). (2) The other possible sense is, ‘be melted,’ ‘languish,’ <emph>with</emph> love; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῷ_φιλεῖν_462"> being then instrum. dat. This is, however, a weaker meaning, and less appropriate. For: (<emph>a</emph>) it would imply an <emph>unsatisfied</emph> longing; and (<emph>b</emph>) Deianeira's thought is rather this:—‘I will not be harsh to her, even though she be resolved <emph>never to renounce</emph> his love.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκτακείη_462"> is no improvement: it would mean ‘waste away’: <ref n="Eur. Orest. 860">Eur. <title>Or.</title> 860</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξετηκόμην γόοις</quote>. In Lycophron 498 (<quote xml:lang="grc">θρήνοισιν</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐκτακεῖσα</quote> is a <emph>v. l.</emph> for <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐντακεῖσα</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ᾤκτιρα_464">: for the spelling, cp. <title>O.T.</title> 13n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δὴ_464"> here emphasises the whole phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">ᾤκτιρα...μάλιστα</quote>: we cannot hold (with Blaydes) that <quote xml:lang="grc">δὴ μάλιστα</quote> is for <quote xml:lang="grc">μάλιστα δή</quote>, any more than that <quote xml:lang="grc">δὴ πόλλ’</quote> (153) is for <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὰ δή</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ῥείτω_οὖρον_468">, go down the stream before the wind. Nothing is gained by changing <quote xml:lang="grc"> ῥείτω</quote> to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴτω_468"> ( <ref n="Aesch. Seven 690">Aesch. <title>Theb.</title>690</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴτω κατ’ οὖρον</quote> ... | ... <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶν τὸ Λαΐου γένος</quote>). For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὖρον_468">, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 815">cp. 815</ref>. She means, ‘it is idle to dwell upon what cannot be undone.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακὸν_468"> = <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄπιστον, ψευδῆ</quote> (347).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_ἄλλον_468"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> : <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘if thou must use deceit, use it towards anyone rather than me’: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1119"> <title>Ph.</title>1119</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">στυγερὰν ἔχε</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">δύσποτμον ἀρὰν ἐπ’ ἄλλοις</quote> (n.). The parataxis makes the precept </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακὸν_468_2"> | </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_εἶναι_468"> absolute in form, though it is only relative in sense: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 383">cp. 383</ref> f.: <ref n="Isoc. 6.54">Isocr. or. 6 § 54</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς οὐκ αἰσχρὸν,...τὴν μὲν Εὐρώπην...μεστὴν πεποιηκέναι τροπαίων,...ὑπὲρ δὲ τῆς πατρίδος...μηδὲ μίαν μάχην φαίνεσθαι μεμαχημένους</quote> <emph>;</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πιθοῦ_λεγούσῃ_470">: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 1207"> <title>El.</title>1207</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πιθοῦ λέγοντι, κοὐχ ἁμαρτήσει ποτέ</quote>. The aor. imper.=‘obey her,’—‘do the thing which she urges’: while </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πείθου_470"> would mean rather, ‘be persuaded’: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1181"> <title>O. C.</title>1181</ref> n. Here the context seems slightly in favour of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πιθοῦ_470">, though the pres. is also quite admissible. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γυναικὶ_τῇδε_470">: this simple dat. of the pers. with <quote xml:lang="grc">μέμφομαι</quote>, though not very rare, is less frequent than either (1) <quote xml:lang="grc">μέμφομαί τινα</quote>, <ref n="Soph. El. 383"> <title>El.</title>383</ref> f., or (2) <quote xml:lang="grc">μέμφομαί τινί τι</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_472">, ‘Nay, then’: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 524."> <title>Ph.</title>524.</ref>— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θνητὴνφρονοῦσαν_θνητὰ_472">: Eur. fr. 796 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥσπερ δὲ θνητὸν καὶ τὸ σῶμ’ ἡμῶν ἔφυ</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">οὕτω προσήκει μηδὲ τὴν ὀργὴν ἔχειν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀθάνατον, ὅστις σωφρονεῖν ἐπίσταται</quote>. <ref n="Aristot. Rh. 2.21">Arist. <title> Rhet.</title>2. 21</ref>§ 6 quotes from an unknown poet, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀθάνατον ὀργὴν μὴ φύλασσε θνητὸς ὤν</quote>: also (perh. from Epicharmus, as Bentley thought), <quote xml:lang="grc">θνατὰ χρὴ τὸν θνατόν, οὐκ ἀθάνατα τὸν θνατὸν φρονεῖν</quote>. Cp. <title>Eth. Nic.</title> 10. 7 § 8 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ χρὴ δὲ κατὰ τοὺς παραινοῦντας ἀνθρώπινα φρονεῖν ἄνθρωπον ὄντα οὐδὲ θνητὰ τὸν θνητόν, ἀλλ’ ἐφ’ ὅσον ἐνδέχεται ἀθανατίζειν.—<emph>ἀγνώμονα</emph> </quote> seems best taken as acc. neut. plur. It is true that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγνώμων</quote> is usu. said of persons: but (<emph>a</emph>) analogous compounds are often neut., as <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1236"> <title>Ai.</title>1236</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κέκραγας... ὑπέρφονα</quote>, <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 88">Aesch. <title>Cho.</title>88</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς εὔφρον’ εἴπω</quote> <emph>;</emph> and (<emph>b</emph>) in later Greek, at least, we find (<foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>) Lucian <title>Abdic.</title> 24 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγνωμον ποιεῖς</quote> : <ref n="Diod. 13. 23">Diod.13. 23</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ δυνατὸν... πράξαντας δεινὰ παθεῖν εὐγνώμονα</quote> (to receive considerate treatment). If </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀγνώμονα_472"> were acc. fem. sing., <quote xml:lang="grc">οὖσαν</quote> could be understood. For <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγνώμων</quote>, ‘inconsiderate,’ ‘not making fair allowance,’ cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 86">Soph. <title>O. C.</title>86</ref> n.: and below<ref n="Soph. OC 1266.">Soph. O. C., 1266.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρύψομαι_474">: midd., ‘keep my knowledge to myself’: but it is not practically different from <quote xml:lang="grc"> κρύψω</quote> here. A midd. force is better marked in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 647"> <title>Ai.</title>647</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φύει τ’ ἄδηλα καὶ φανέντα κρύπτεται</quote> (hides in itself), and <emph>ib.</emph> 246 <quote xml:lang="grc">κάρα...κρυψάμενον</quote>. The only other examples of the simple midd. <quote xml:lang="grc">κρύπτομαι</quote> are late, though the midd. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποκρύπτομαι</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπικρύπτομαι</quote> were frequent. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔστιν_οὕτως_475">: this is not the <quote xml:lang="grc">γάρ</quote> which merely prefaces a statement ( <ref n="Soph. OT 277"> <title>O. T.</title>277</ref> n.): rather it refers to v. 474: ‘the truth,—<emph>for</emph> it has still to be told by me.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταύτης_ἵμερος_476">: the article is explained by the preceding verse:—‘It is as he says: <emph>she</emph> inspired <emph>that</emph> strong passion (of which he has spoken, 431 f.).’ This is a compressed way of making two admissions,—‘love was the real motive, and she was the object of that love.’—Some commentators hold that </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_δεινὸς_476"> here means simply, ‘<emph> very</emph>’ (or ‘<emph>most</emph>’) ‘potent.’ The evidence for this supposed use of the article is examined in the Appendix.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποθ_476">: referring to a time before the death of Iphitus: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 359">cp. 359</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διῆλθε_476">: cp. <ref n="Aesch. Supp. 288"> Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title>288</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀμὲ γὰρ διῆλθέ τι</quote> (‘a pang shot through my heart also’).— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆσδ_476"> after </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταύτης_476"> : <ref n="Soph. Ant. 296">Soph. <title>Ant.</title>296</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡ_πολύφθορος_476">: the adj., though proleptic, takes the art.: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1088"> <title>O. C.</title>1088</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν εὔαγρον τελειῶσαι λόχον</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατρῷος_476">: a somewhat rare fem., used either (1) for metre's sake, as here, and <ref n="Eur. Supp. 1146">Eur. <title> Suppl.</title>1146</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δίκα</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρῷος</quote>: or (2) for euphony, as <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 210">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title>210</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρῴους χέρας</quote>, <ref n="Eur. Her. 810">Eur. <title> Her.</title>810</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τιμὰς πατρῴους</quote>. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 533">Cp. 533</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OC 751"> <title>O. C.</title>751</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_κείνου_479">, what is on <emph>his</emph> side (in his favour) too: <ref n="Soph. OT 1434"> <title>O. T.</title>1434</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς σοῦ γὰρ οὐδ’ ἐμοῦ φράσω</quote> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴ_ἁμαρτ_483">, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ τι τόδ’ ἁμάρτ.</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OC 88"> <title>O. C.</title>88</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταύτην</quote> (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτ’</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔλεξε παῦλαν</quote>, n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νέμεις_483">=<quote xml:lang="grc">νομίζεις</quote> , <ref n="Soph. OC 879"> <title>O. C.</title>879</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_δὴ_484">: as <ref n="Soph. El. 1243"> <title>El.</title>1243</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρα γε μὲν δὴ κ.τ.λ.</quote> Blaydes remarks that these particles do not elsewhere follow </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπεὶ_484">, and therefore alters the text (cr. n.). But their combination with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεί</quote> here is quite correct: ‘since, <emph>however</emph>, you <emph>do</emph> know all,’ etc. Just so they follow a participle in <title>Helen.</title> 1259 <quote xml:lang="grc">διδούς γε μὲν δὴ</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, when you <emph>do</emph> give anything) <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσγενὲς μηδὲν δίδου.—<emph>κείνου τε καὶ σὴν</emph> κ.τ.λ.</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 762">Soph. <title> Ph.</title>762</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σοῦ τε τήν τ’ ἐμὴν χάριν</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στέργε_486">: cp. <ref n="Eur. Andr. 213">Eur. <title> Andr.</title>213</ref>, where Andromachè is giving Hermionè the same kind of advice;—<quote xml:lang="grc">χρὴ γὰρ γυναῖκα, κἂν κακῷ πόσει δοθῇ</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">στέργειν, ἅμιλλάν τ’ οὐκ ἔχειν φρονήματος.—<emph>λόγους οὓς εἶπας ἐς τήνδ’</emph> </quote>, alluding to the assurance given in 462 ff.: for </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐς_486">, ‘with regard to,’ cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1053.">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 1053.</ref>The reference is not to Deianeira's reception of Iolè in 310— 334.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμπέδως_486">, unalterably,—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, so that the promise shall be kept: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 827">cp. 827</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1197"> <title>Ph.</title>1197</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσθι τόδ’ ἔμπεδον</quote>. The conjecture </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμπέδους_486"> is plausible, but not necessary.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἄλλ_486"> or </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χεροῖν_486"> ought logically to be absent. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_490"> emphasises </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φρονοῦμεν_490"> (‘I <emph>do</emph> think thus’): <ref n="Soph. Trach. 314">cp. 314</ref>, 600. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νόσον_ἐξαρούμεθα_491">, lit., ‘<emph>take up</emph> (the burden of) a trouble which I should <emph>bring upon myself</emph>,’—viz., the anguish of a vain fight against all-conquering <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἔρως</quote>. The schol. rightly explains </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπακτὸν_491"> by <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐθαίρετον</quote>: as does the schol. on <ref n="Soph. Phil. 343">Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 343</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γάμων ἐπακτὸν ἄταν</quote>. This view is confirmed by the presence of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_491">, meaning, ‘at any rate I shall not add to my own woes.’ For a parallel use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπάγεσθαι</quote>, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 4.19">Lys. or. 4 § 19</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολὺ μείζω συμφορὰν ἐμαυτῷ...ἐπαγαγέσθαι</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 19.259">Dem. or. 19 § 259</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐθαίρετον αὑτοῖς ἐπάγονται δουλείαν</quote> </cit>. For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξαρούμεθα_491"> cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 10.84"> <title>Od.</title>10. 84</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔνθα κ’ ἄϋπνος ἀνὴρ δοιοὺς ἐξήρατο μισθούς</quote> (‘take up,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign>, ‘win’). The simple <quote xml:lang="grc">αἴρομαι</quote> is often so used, with ref. either to ‘winning’ a prize, or ‘taking up’ a burden ( <ref n="Soph. OT 1225"> <title>O. T.</title>1225</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρεῖσθε πένθος</quote> , <ref n="Soph. Ant. 907"> <title>Ant.</title>907</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠρόμην πόνον</quote>). This <quote xml:lang="grc">αἴρομαι</quote> can be replaced by the rarer <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξαίρομαι</quote> just as <quote xml:lang="grc"> φέρομαι</quote>, in a like sense (462 n.), by the rarer <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκφέρομαι</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 60"> <title>El.</title>60</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀξενέγκωμαι κλέος</quote> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξαρούμεθα_491_2"> is strange: and </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_491_2"> is then weak; hence Nauck read </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νόσημ_491">. (2) ‘I shall not try to <emph> shift</emph> that trouble <emph>from myself</emph>’: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘I shall not try to <emph>put away</emph> the grief of these tidings by vain complaints against the gods.’ This last version seems impossible.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεοῖσι_δυσμαχοῦντες_491">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1106"> <title>Ant.</title>1106</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνάγκῃ δ’ οὐχὶ δυσμαχητέον</quote>. The compound means, ‘to wage an up-hill fight’: cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">δυστοκεῖν, δυσθανατεῖν</quote>. For the masc. plur., used by a woman with ref. to herself, cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 399"> <title>El.</title>399</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Hec. 511.">Eur. <title> Hec.</title>511.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγων_τ_493">: the elision gives a quasi-caesura: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 101"> <title>Ph.</title>101</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγων_ἐπιστολὰς_493">=‘mandates consisting in words’ (defining gen.), <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, her (verbal) messages to Heracles, as distinguished from the <quote xml:lang="grc">δῶρα</quote>. Sophocles, like Aesch. , uses <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιστολή</quote> only in the general sense of ‘mandate’ ( <ref n="Soph. OC 1601">Soph. <title>O. C.</title>1601</ref> , <ref n="Soph. Aj. 781">Soph. <title>Ai.</title>781</ref>): Eur. uses it also with ref. to a written letter (<title>I. A.</title> 111 etc.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσαρμόσαι_494">, lit., ‘adjust’; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘give in fitting recompense.’ But Deianeira's choice of the word has been influenced by her secret thought,—already turned towards the philtre which she would <emph>apply</emph> to Heracles: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 687">cp. 687</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔως νιν ἀρτίχριστον ἁρμόσαιμί που</quote>. And at the same time the word is unconsciously ominous (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 767">cp. 767</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">προσπτύσσεται</quote>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄγῃς_494"> is not meant to be in contrast with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φέρῃς_494"> (493): it is here a mere synonym for it (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 457">cp. 457</ref>). <quote xml:lang="grc"> φέρειν</quote> is used with ref. to the robe in 602, 622, 758, 774. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 354"> <title>O. C.</title>354</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μαντεἶ ἄγουσα πάντα</quote> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">φέρουσα</quote>). On the other hand in 183 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγοντ’ ἀπαρχάς</quote>, and 751 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγων τροπαῖα κ.τ.λ.</quote>, an attendant train is implied.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δίκαια_494">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 409">cp. 409</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧδε_494"> is explained by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺνπ_στόλῳ_494">: it should not be taken with <quote xml:lang="grc"> πολλῷ</quote>, nor as=<quote xml:lang="grc">δεῦρο</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OT 7"> <title>O. T.</title>7</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέγα_νίκας_497">=<quote xml:lang="grc">μεγασθενῆ τινα νίκην</quote>: the victory which she carries off is the glorious proof of her might. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1088"> <title>O. C.</title>1088</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σθένει )πινικείῳ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκφέρεται_497">: <ref target="cl491">see on 491</ref>.—Others explain: (1) ‘carries a great strength <emph>out of</emph> victory’—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, wins with much to spare: (2) ‘<emph>advances in</emph> mighty conquering force’ (cogn. acc.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεῶν_498">, a monosyll.: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 183">cp. 183</ref>.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρέβαν_498">: for the aor., cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1289"> <title>Ph.</title>1289</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπώμοσ’</quote> (n.). It is better not to dwell on stories which ascribe weakness to the gods: <ref n="Pind. O. 1">Pind. <title>O.</title>1. 35</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔστι δ’ ἀνδρὶ φάμεν ἐοικὸς ἀμφὶ δαιμόνων καλά</quote> : <emph>ib.</emph> 9. 40 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεὶ τό γε λοιδορῆσαι θεοὺς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐχθρὰ σοφία</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Κρονίδαν_498">: Zeus is <quote xml:lang="grc">κατ’ ἐξοχήν</quote> so called, though his two brothers had the same father. Cp. the Homeric hymn to Aphroditè (4. 38), <quote xml:lang="grc">καί τε τοῦ</quote> (Zeus), <quote xml:lang="grc">εὖτε θέλοι, πυκινὰς φρένας ἐξαπαφοῦσα</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥηιδίως συνέμιξε καταθνητῇσι γυναιξίν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἥρης ἐκλελαθοῦσα.—<emph>οὐ λέγω</emph> </quote>: implying religious <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐφημία</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 1467"> <title>El.</title>1467</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δ’ ἔπεστι νέμεσις, οὐ λέγω</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_Ἅιδαν_501">: <ref n="Soph. OC 1559"> <title>O. C.</title>1559</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐννυχίων ἄναξ</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 15.187"> <title>Il.</title>15. 187</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τρεῖς γάρ τ’ ἐκ Κρόνου εἰμὲν ἀδελφεοί, οὓς τέκε Ῥείη</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζεὺς καὶ ἐγώ, τρίτατος δ’ Ἀΐδης ἐνέροισιν ἀνάσσων</quote>. The allusion is to Pluto carrying off Persephonè. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ποσειδάωνα_502">, the regular Homeric form: L, which has <quote xml:lang="grc">ποσειδῶνα</quote> here, conversely gives <quote xml:lang="grc">ποσειδαωνίῳ</quote> (where <quote xml:lang="grc"> Ποσειδωνίῳ</quote> is most probable) in <ref n="Soph. OC 1494."> <title>O. C.</title>1494.</ref>Poseidon was as little true to Amphitritè as Zeus to Hera: Propert. 2. 26. 46 <title>Neptunus fratri par in amore Iovi.</title> Thus Tyro (the subject of a play by Sophocles) bore Pelias and Neleus to the sea-god. At Aegina the cult of Poseidon was combined with that of Aphroditè ( <title>Quaest. Gr.</title> 44: Athen. XIII. p. 590 F).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τινάκτορα_γαίας_502">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐννοσίγαιον, ἐνοσίχθονα</quote>: <title>Hom. hymn.</title> 22. 2 <quote xml:lang="grc">γαίης κινητῆρα καὶ ἀτρυγέτοιο θαλάσσης</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπὶ_ἄκοιτιν_503">, ‘to win Deianeira as bride’ (predicate): for the prep., cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 591"> <title>Ph.</title>591</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ τοῦτον</quote>... | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">πλέουσιν</quote>: <ref n="Xen. Cyrop. 1. 2. 9">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title>1. 2. 9</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅταν...ἐξίῃ... ἐπὶ θήραν.—<emph>ἀμφίγυοι</emph> </quote>: the prep. expresses the idea, ‘two’; the second part of the compound suggests that of ‘stalwart,’ ‘vigorous.’ Thus the epithet is of the same class as <quote xml:lang="grc">δίστολοι</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OC 1055">Soph. <title>O. C.</title>1055</ref>), said of <emph> two</emph> persons who are <emph>travelling.</emph> It seems more likely that Sophocles here used <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφίγυος</quote> with an original boldness, than that he was directly thinking of the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔγχεσιν ἀμφιγύοισιν</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 13.147"> <title>Il.</title>13. 147</ref>): where the adj. has been explained as (<emph>a</emph>) ‘having a <quote xml:lang="grc">γυῖον</quote>, a limb (of iron), at each end,’—the <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγχη</quote>, and the <quote xml:lang="grc">σαυρωτήρ</quote>: or (<emph>b</emph>) ‘having a <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγχη</quote> curved (<quote xml:lang="grc">γυ</quote>) on both sides’: but Leaf <emph>ad loc.</emph> suggests (<emph>c</emph>) ‘bending to either side,’ ‘elastic.’ The primary notion of <quote xml:lang="grc">γυῖον</quote> is, ‘a <emph> flexible</emph> limb.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατέβαν_503">, <emph>in certamen descenderunt.</emph> — <ref n="Xen. Anab. 4. 8. 27">Xen. <title>An.</title>4. 8. 27</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠγωνίζοντο δὲ παῖδες κ.τ.λ....καὶ καλὴ θέα ἐγένετο· πολλοὶ γὰρ κατέβησαν.—<emph>πρὸ γάμων</emph> </quote>, ‘<emph>for</emph> it,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, to win it (=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπέρ</quote>): not, ‘<emph>before</emph> it.’ In <quote xml:lang="grc">πρό</quote>, just as in ‘for,’ the two notions are closely linked. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 134">Soph. <title>O. T.</title>134</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸ τοῦ θανόντος</quote> (on his behalf): <ref n="Soph. El. 495">Soph. <title>El.</title>495</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸ τῶνδε</quote> (on this account). </gloss>
