<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάμπληκτα_506">: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">πληγῶν μεστά</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 50">cp. 50</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πανδάκρυτ’</quote>. (It is overrefining to explain it as referring to the various <emph>kinds</emph> of blow, 517 f.)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παγκόνιτά_τ_506">: the dust raised by the comba. is the proverbial symbol of violent effort: neither can win <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκονιτί</quote>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Ov. Am. 1.15">Ov. <title>Am.</title> 1. 15. 4</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">Praemia militiae pulverulenta sequi.</quote> </cit> Ovid may have had <quote xml:lang="grc">παγκόνιτα</quote> in mind when he described Heracles and Acheloüs as throwing handfuls of dust at each other <cit> <bibl n="Ov. Met. 9.35">(<title>Met.</title> 9. 35 f.):</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">Ille cavis hausto spargit me pulvere palmis, | Inque vicem fulvae iactu flavescit arenae.</quote> </cit> For the accumulated compounds of <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶς</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 660">cp. 660</ref> f.</gloss>

data.quote:
Ille cavis hausto spargit me pulvere palmis, |
							Inque vicem fulvae iactu flavescit arenae.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξῆλθον_ἄεθλ_506">: <ref target="cl159">159 n.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄεθλα_506"> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἄθλους</quote> ) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀγώνων_506">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 507"> <title>Ph.</title>507</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσοίστων πόνων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆθλ’</quote> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποταμοῦ_σθένος_507">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 38">cp. 38</ref>: <ref n="Hom. Il. 13.248"> <title>Il.</title>13. 248</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σθένος Ἰδομενῆος.—<emph>τετραόρου</emph> </quote>= <quote xml:lang="grc">τετρασκελοῦς</quote> (schol.): lit., ‘<emph>erect</emph> upon four legs’: elsewhere always epithet of four horses yoked abreast, or of the chariot drawn by them.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φάσμα_ταύρου_507">, a periphrasis expressing his dread aspect: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1568"> <title>O. C.</title>1568</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σῶμά τ’</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνικάτου κυνός</quote>: <ref n="Verg. A. 6. 289">Verg. <title>Aen.</title>6. 289</ref> <emph>et forma tricorporis umbrae</emph> (Geryon).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπ_Οἰνιαδᾶν_509">: a town in Acarnania, on the west bank of the Acheloüs. It was about ten miles from the mouth of that river, which is described by <ref n="Thuc. 2.102">(2. 102)</ref> as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς θάλασσαν .. ἐξιεὶς παρ’ Οἰνιάδας καὶ τὴν πόλιν αὐτοῖς περιλιμνάζων</quote>. Marshes, due partly to the lake Melitè, insulated the hill on which the town stood, and made the site a strong one. The name was familiar to Athenians in the poet's time. Oeniadae was long a centre of anti-Athenian influence in western Greece. It was unsuccessfully besieged by Pericles (<ref n="Thuc. 1.111">Th. 1. 111</ref> , <date when="-0454">454 B.C.</date>); but, under pressure from the other Acarnanian towns, was received into the Athenian alliance by Demosthenes in <date when="-0424">424 B.C.</date> <ref n="Thuc. 4.77">(Th. 4. 77)</ref>. The site (now Tricardo) was first identified by Leake.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Βακχίας_510">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1122"> <title>Ant.</title>1122</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Βακχεῦ, Βακχᾶν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ ματρόπολιν Θήβαν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ναιετῶν</quote> (n.). Heracles was born at. Thebes (116 n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπὸ_510">: L's accent, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄπο_510">, represents the doctrine of some grammarians that the accent of the prep. suffered anastrophè when it stood between adj. and subst. (or subst. and adj.): see Ellendt <title>Lex.</title>p. 78 <emph>b.</emph> There is more reason for this when the subst. precedes; <foreign xml:lang="lat"> e.g.</foreign>, <quote xml:lang="grc">Ξάνθου ἄπο δινήεντος</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 2.877"> <title>Il.</title>2. 877</ref>) is more natural than <quote xml:lang="grc">Βακχίας ἄπο Θήβας</quote>. Modern editors differ: nor is their practice always consistent. But in all such cases it seems better to regard adj. and subst. as forming a single expression, and therefore to keep the normal accent. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παλίντονα_511">, ‘back-bent,’ is a general epithet, referring to the shape of the bow; not to its being ‘drawn back’ in shooting, nor to its ‘springing back’ after the shot. It seems properly to have denoted a bow of which the curvature was in a direction contrary to that in which the archer bent the bow when drawing it. See Appendix.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγχας_511">, two spears, in Homeric fashion: cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 3.17"> <title>Il.</title>3. 17</ref> f., where Paris is armed with <quote xml:lang="grc">κάμπυλα τόξα, ξίφος</quote>, and <quote xml:lang="grc">δύο δοῦρε</quote>. (Not, ‘pointed arrows,’ as Paley renders.)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ῥόπαλον_511">, the club, made from a wild-olive tree which Heracles had plucked up by the roots on Mount Helicon: cp. Theocr. 25. 206 ff., where he carries this <quote xml:lang="grc">βάκτρον</quote> in his right hand, and his bow in the left: <ref n="Apollon. 2.34">Apoll. Rh. 2. 34</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καλαύροπά τε τρηχεῖαν</quote> | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρειτρεφέος κοτίνοιο.—<emph>τινάσσων</emph> </quote> would suit </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγχας_511_2"> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 12.298"> <title>Il.</title>12. 298</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δύο δοῦρε τινάσσων</quote>) as well as </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ῥόπαλον_511_2">, but not </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τόξα_511">, for which a word such as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχων</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">νωμῶν</quote> must be supplied: cp. n. on 353.—The picture is not distinct; his right hand must wield the club; his left may hold either bow or spears,—the other weapon being slung about him. As to the archer type of Heracles, here partly blended with the hoplite, cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 727"> <title>Ph.</title>727</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀολλεῖς_513"> here simply=<quote xml:lang="grc"> ὁμοῦ</quote>: Hermann compares <ref n="Mosch. 2.48">Mosch.2. 48</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δοιοὶ δ’ ἕστασαν ὑψοῦ ἐπ’ ὀφρύος αἰγιαλοῖο</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">φῶτες ἀολλήδην.—<emph>ἴσαν</emph> </quote> (epic=<quote xml:lang="grc">ᾐέσαν</quote>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐςμέσον_513">: so Theocr. 22. 183 (of a fight) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ δ’ εἰς μέσον ἤλυθε Λυγκεύς.—<emph>λεχέων</emph> </quote>: the plur., as <ref n="Soph. Ant. 630">Soph. <title>Ant.</title>630</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπάτας λεχέων</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OT 821">Soph. <title>O. T.</title>821</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λέχη...τοῦ θανόντος</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόνα_δ_515">: whereas in an ordinary <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγών</quote> there were several <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥαβδοῦχοι. ...<emph>εὔλεκτρος</emph> </quote>: in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 795"> <title>Ant.</title>795</ref> the epithet of a bride: here, of the goddess who gives fair brides to men.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_μέσῳ_515"> here refers to the umpire as an impartial judge between two competitors.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ῥαβδονόμει_515"> ( = <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐρραβδονόμει</quote>). The officials who maintained order in the contests at the great festivals were called <quote xml:lang="grc"> ῥαβδοῦχοι</quote>: <ref n="Thuc. 5. 50">Thuc.5. 50</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν τῷ ἀγῶνι ὑπὸ τῶν ῥαβδούχων πληγὰς ἔλαβεν</quote>. The term included the notion of ‘umpire’: <ref n="Plat. Prot. 338A"> Plat. <title>Prot.</title> 338A</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πείθεσθέ μοι ῥαβδοῦχον καὶ ἐπιστάτην καὶ πρύτανιν ἑλέσθαι, ὃς ὑμῖν φυλάξει τὸ μέτριον μῆκος τῶν λόγων ἑκατέρου</quote>. The verb <quote xml:lang="grc"> ῥαβδονομεῖν</quote> occurs only here, and <quote xml:lang="grc"> ῥαβδονόμος</quote> itself is postclassical: but cp. Hesych. (<emph>s. v.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥάβδοι</quote>), <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ὁ βραβευτὴς ῥαβδονόμος</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυνοῦσα_515">): Deianeira views the fight from afar. But the scene was not always so conceived. Thus the Megarian <quote xml:lang="grc">θησαυρός</quote> at Olympia contained a group of figures in gilt cedarwood, of which Paus. (6. 19. 12) says: <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζεὺς δὲ ἐνταῦθα καὶ ἡ Δηιάνειρα καὶ Ἀχελῷος καὶ Ἡρ<*>κλῆς ἐστιν Ἄρης τε τῷ Ἀχελῴῳ βοηθῶν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τότ_χερός_517"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> In this compressed description of the fight, the two combatants figure alternately. (1) Heracles deals blows with his fists (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χερὸς_πάταγος_517">),—then retires a little, and sends a shaft from his twanging bow (<quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>τόξων</emph> πάταγος</quote>). (2) Acheloüs charges, and the hero's club rattles on his horns (<quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>κεράτων</emph> πάταγος</quote>). (3) Then Heracles, <*>ning to the wrestler's arts, endeavours to grapple with Acheloüs, to spring upon his back (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμφίπλεκτοικλίμακες_517">). (4) The tauriform god butts at his adversary (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μετώπων_πλήγματα_517">). And the account fitly closes with the words, <quote xml:lang="grc">στόνος <emph>ἀμφοῖν</emph> </quote> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦν_517"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">μὲν</quote> )...</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦν_δὲ_517">, cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 806"> <title>Ant.</title>806</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνάμιγδα_518">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναμίξ</quote>, suggesting the confusion of sounds. The form is a rare one: but Nicander has <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμμίγδην</quote> ( <ref n="Nic. Alex. 570"> <title>Alex.</title>570</ref>, <ref n="Nic. Ther. 912"> <title>Ther.</title>912</ref>). <ref n="Soph. Trach. 839">Cp. 839</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄμμιγα</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κεράτων_518">. A prominent mention is given to the horns, since the story was that Heracles broke off one of them. Cp. Ovid <title>M.</title> 9. 85 <emph>rigidum fera dextera cornu</emph> | <title>Dum tenet</title>, <emph>infregit</emph>, <emph>truncaque a</emph> <emph>fronte revellit.</emph> Acheloüs ransomed it by giving his conqueror the horn of Amaltheia, or cornucopia ( <ref n="Apollod. 2.7.5">Apollod.2. 7. 5</ref>). This gift, which Heracles transferred as the bride-price to Oeneus, was explained as a symbol of the increased fertility gained by works which altered the course of the Acheloüs (<ref n="Strab. 10">Strabo 10</ref>, p. 458). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦν_520"> with plur. subj.: the so-called <emph>schema Pindaricum:</emph> <title>Theog.</title> 825 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦν ἑκατὸν κεφαλαί</quote> (cp. <emph>ib.</emph> 321): Pind. fr. 75. 15 <quote xml:lang="grc">τότε βάλλεται τότ’ ἐπ’ ἀμβρόταν χθόν’ ἐραταὶ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴων φόβαι</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Ion 1146">Eur. <title> Ion</title>1146</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνῆν δ’ ὑφανταὶ γράμμασιν τοιαίδ’ ὑφαί</quote>. In this constr., the sing. verb always precedes the plur. subject. ‘As the sing. is the general and the plur. the particular, we have not so much a want of concord as an afterthought’ (Gildersleeve, Introd. to Pindar, p. lxxxviii.). The genuine examples in Pindar are not numerous.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμφίπλεκτοι_κλίμακες_520">. Ancient writers mention the <quote xml:lang="grc">κλῖμαξ</quote> as a wrestling trick, but do not explain it (Hesych. <emph>s. v.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc"> κλίμακες</quote>: Pollux 3. 155). The schol. here had evidently no clear notion of it: <quote xml:lang="grc">κλίμακες· αἱ ἐπαναβάσεις</quote> (a literal paraphrase) <quote xml:lang="grc">παρὰ τὸ ἄνω τε καὶ κάτω αὐτοὺς στρέφεσθαι ἐν τῇ μάχῃ</quote>. Hermann explains it thus;—the wrestler turns his adversary round, seizes him from behind, and springs on his back, so as to force him down. He relies on <ref n="Ov. Met. 9. 50">Ov. <title> Met.</title>9. 50</ref>—54, where Heracles shakes off the embrace of Acheloüs, and then, with a strong push from his hand, <quote xml:lang="lat">protinus avertit, tergoque onerosus inhaesit.</quote> For the view that this is the <quote xml:lang="grc">κλῖμαξ</quote> see Appendix. The Acheloüs of Ovid, it should be remembered, is at that moment the horned man, not yet the bull (cp. n. on 507 f.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μετώπων_πλήγματα_521">, blows <emph>from</emph> the forehead; the bull is now butting (<quote xml:lang="grc">κυρίττων</quote>) at Heracles,—as shown on the gem mentioned above (507 f., n.). Schol.: <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ γὰρ ταῦροι τοῖς κέρασι καὶ μετώποις μάχονται.—<emph> στόνος</emph> </quote>, in the strenuous effort. Cp. <ref n="Cic. Tusc. 2. 23. 56">Cic. <title>Tusc.</title>2. 23. 56</ref>(quoted by Billerbeck and others) <quote xml:lang="lat">pugiles etiam cum feriunt adversarium in iactandis cestibus ingemiscunt</quote>,—not from pain or fear, he adds, but because the very utterance helps to brace up the nerves (<quote xml:lang="lat">omne corpus intenditur</quote> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τηλαυγεῖ_524">, ‘seen afar,’ ‘distant’: Theog. 550 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπὸ τηλαυγέος φαινόμενος σκοπιῆς</quote>: <title> Nub.</title> 281 <quote xml:lang="grc">τηλεφανεῖς σκοπιάς</quote>: cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">τηλωπός</quote> (n. on <ref n="Soph. Phil. 216"> <title>Ph.</title>216</ref>).—Others take it actively, ‘seeing (=giving a view) from afar’ (cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">τηλεσκόπος</quote> ). <ref n="Diod. 1. 50">Diod.1. 50</ref> has this use: <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς χώρας αὐτοῖς συνεργούσης πρὸς τὸ τηλαυγέστερον ὁρᾶν</quote>. But it seems less probable here.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄχθῳ_524">: Nonnus 43. 13 (quoted by Schneidewin) makes Deianeira watch the fight from the bank of a river. Did he, then, take <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄχθῳ</quote> here as = <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄχθῃ</quote>? (Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1132"> <title>Ant.</title>1132</ref> n.)</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_ὃν_524">, emphatic (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 266">cp. 266</ref>): the husband who was to be hers. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγὼ_φράζω_526">. The words <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ δὲ μάτηρ</quote> (if no others) are unquestionably corrupt. They have been explained to mean:—‘I speak as a mother’ (or ‘her mother’) ‘might speak’: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, with all a mother's tender sympathy. If anything could increase the strangeness of such language, it would be the fact that the young maidens of Trachis are speaking of one who is old enough to be <emph> their</emph> mother. Or: (2) ‘I tell the story as her mother told it,’—a way of explaining how they can describe what they had not seen. This needs no refutation.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγὼ_526"> has come from </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀγὼν_526">, under the influence of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φράζω_526">. The sense of the verse was, ‘And the strife goes on raging, as I describe’ (referring to vv. 517—522). <quote xml:lang="grc">ΜΑΤΗΡ</quote> may well be a corruption of <quote xml:lang="grc">ΜΑΡΓΑΙ</quote>, a loss of <quote xml:lang="grc">Ρ</quote> having been followed by a change of <quote xml:lang="grc">Γ</quote> to <quote xml:lang="grc">Τ</quote>. This may have happened after <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγὼν</quote> had become <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ</quote>, with help, perhaps, from a reminiscence of <ref n="Soph. El. 233"> <title>El.</title>233</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ οὖν εὐνοίᾳ γ’ αὐδῶ</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">μάτηρ ὡσεί τις πιστά</quote>. Cp. Hesychius <quote xml:lang="grc">μαργᾷ· μαργαίνει, ὑβρίζει, ἐνθουσιᾷ, μαίνεται</quote>. <ref n="Eur. Her. 1005">Eur. <title>H. F.</title>1005</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅς νιν φόνου μαργῶντος ἔσχε</quote>. I would read, then, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀγὼν_φράζω_526">. The next words, </gloss>
lemma: ἀγὼν δὲ μαργᾷ μέν, οἷα φράζω

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_ἀμφινείκητον_526"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>, then fiftly turn from the stubborn fight to her continuing suspense.—For other views, see Appendix. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄμμα_527">, in periphrasis, is sometimes little more than ‘form,’ ( <ref n="Eur. Ion 1261">Eur. <title>Ion</title>1261</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ ταυρόμορφον ὄμμα Κηφισοῦ πατρός</quote>:) but here it refers specially to her anxious gaze: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 139"> Soph. <title>Ai.</title>139</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πεφόβημαι</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πτηνῆς ὡς ὄμμα πελείας</quote>. Since the words </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄμμα_νύμφας_527"> form one notion, the adj. is in the nom.: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 794.">Soph. <title>Ant.</title>794.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐλεινὸν_ἀμμένει_527">: awaits (the result) with a piteous look: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1130"> <title>Ph.</title>1130</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ που ἐλεινὸν ὁρᾷς</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀπὸ_βέβακεν_529">. Sophocles does not tell us whether Heracles took his bride away from her father's home immediately after the fight. According to the common account Heracles and Deianeira remained with Oeneus for a considerable time after their marriage, while the hero did various exploits. ( <ref n="Apollod. 2.7.6">Apollod.2. 7. 6</ref>: <ref n="Diod. 4. 36">Diod.4. 36</ref>, who speaks of three years.) Then, having accidentally slain a youth in the house of Oeneus, Heracles departed with his wife and infant son (Hyllus) to Trachis.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄφαρ_529"> is not necessarily inconsistent with that version: it means merely that the result of the fight at once transferred Deianeira from the care of her mother to that of a husband. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὥστε_530"> is preferable to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὥσπερ_530"> (cr. n.) in the first foot of the glyconic verse: for this use of it, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 112">cp. 112</ref>, 367, 537, 699, 703, 768, 1071.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόρτις_ἐρήμα_530">: Schneidewin compares Anacreon fr. 52 <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷά τε νεβρὸν νεοθηλέα</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">γαλαθηνόν, ὅστ’ ἐν ὕλῃ κεροέσσης</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπολειφθεὶς ἀπὸ μητρὸς ἐπτοήθη</quote>. The image is especially suitable to one who was destined to have no secure or permanent home. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦμος_531"> (155 n.): this is the only Attic instance of the epic </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆμος_531">. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_ξένος_531">, merely as a guest from a distance; Lichas was no stranger to her (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 232">cp. 232</ref>, 310).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θροεῖ_531"> seems to imply that their voices could be heard within, and that therefore she felt safe from sudden interruption.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταῖς_παισὶν_531">: who charge him with messages to their kinsfolk and friends among the Oechalian captives with Heracles: these maidens were <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξαίρετοι</quote> (245).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ἐξόδῳ_531">: for <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote> denoting the intention, cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 58"> <title>Ph.</title>58</ref> n.: and for </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπἐξόδῳ_531">, cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 1322."> <title>El.</title>1322.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θυραῖος_531"> fem., as <ref n="Soph. El. 313"> <title>El.</title>313</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θυραῖον οἰχνεῖν</quote> : <ref n="Soph. Trach. 478">cp. 478</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρῷος</quote>, and <ref n="Soph. OC 751."> <title>O. C.</title>751.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_μὲν_534"> (adv.) refers to the recital which begins at v. 555: </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_δ_534">, to vv. 536 ff.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χερσὶν_534"> is slightly emphasised by its place (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 139"> <title>O. T.</title>139</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκεῖνον ὁ κτανών</quote>); her <emph>sense</emph> of wrong (<quote xml:lang="grc">οἷα πάσχω</quote>) has prompted <emph>action.</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συγκατοικτιουμένη_534">: some take the midd. as meaning, ‘to seek pity for myself from you.’ Rather it means simply, ‘to bewail (my woes) along with you’ (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, in your presence). So <ref n="Hdt. 2. 121">Her.2. 121</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποκλαύσαντα ἢ κατοικτισάμενον</quote>: 3. 156 <quote xml:lang="grc">κατοικτίζετο, φὰς... πεπονθέναι κ.τ.λ.</quote> The midd. <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκτίζομαι</quote> is used by Aesch. ( <ref n="Aesch. Supp. 1032">Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title>1032</ref>), by Eur. (<title>I. T.</title> 486), and even in prose ( <ref n="Thuc. 2. 51">Thuc.2. 51</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κόρην_536">, as Theocr. 27. 65 <quote xml:lang="grc">τεκέων τροφός, οὐκέτι κώρα</quote>: but it is also said of a young wife ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 6.247"> <title>Il.</title>6. 247</ref> etc.) where her <emph>father</emph> is mentioned: in <emph>loc. cit.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">κουράων</quote> refers to <quote xml:lang="grc">Πριάμοιο</quote> in the preceding line.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐζευγμένην_536">: see 1226. Deianeira's first belief (309) has been changed by learning the vehemence of her lord's new passion (359 f.: 488 f.). Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 825"> <title>O. T.</title>825</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γάμοις</quote>... | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">ζυγῆναι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρεισδέδεγμαι_537"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> Among the captives whom she has received into her house, there is one who is to be her rival. This suggests the comparison with the master of a trading vessel, who, along with the rest of his cargo, ships some merchandise which is destined to prove his ruin. The parallelism between </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φόρ_537"> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τον_537"> and <quote xml:lang="grc">λωβητὸν ἐμπόλημα</quote> marks that the <quote xml:lang="grc">φόρτος</quote>, too, is disastrous: but the way in which it is so is left indefinite. There is no explicit reference to overloading.—For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρεισδέδεγμαι_537_2"> cp. <title>De part. anim.</title> 1 (p. 662 <emph>a</emph> 9) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναγκαῖον ...παρεισδέχεσθαι τὸ ὑγρὸν ἅμα τῇ τροφῇ</quote> (=‘to receive incidentally’). So, here, the <quote xml:lang="grc">παρά</quote> seems to mean strictly, ‘have received <emph>as an incident</emph> of receiving the others’ (Iolè having come in among them); cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">παραπολλύναι</quote> etc. The objection to taking the prep. as=‘surreptitiously’ (=<quote xml:lang="grc">λαθραῖον</quote> in 377) is that Deianeira was the victim, not agent, of the fraud.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λωβητὸν_537"> in active sense, as <ref n="Soph. Phil. 607"> <title>Ph.</title>607</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λωβήτ’ ἔπη</quote>, words of contumely.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμπόλημα_537">, a thing gained by traffic; here, an ‘acquisition’ (in an ironical sense).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆς_φρενός_537"> with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λωβητὸν_537_2">: cp. the gen. after <quote xml:lang="grc">λυμαντήριος, ὀλέθριος</quote>, etc. It might also depend on the phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">λωβητὸν ἐμπόλημα</quote> as=<quote xml:lang="grc"> βλάβη</quote>. Others understand: ‘a disastrous merchandise, (bought by) my loyalty to Heracles’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">τ<*> ἐμῆς φρενός</quote> as gen. of price). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δὔ_οὖσαι_539">, both of us: <ref n="Soph. OT 1505"> <title>O. T.</title>1505</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀλώλαμεν δὔ ὄντε</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μιᾶς_χλαίνης_539">: Eur. fr. 606 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅταν δ’ ὑπ’ ἀνδρὸς χλαῖναν εὐγενοῦς πέσῃς</quote>: Theocr. 18. 19 <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζανός τοι θυγάτηρ ὑπὸ τὰν μίαν ᾤχετο χλαῖναν</quote> (shared the bed of Menelaus). For <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπό</quote> with gen. in this sense, cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 65">Soph. <title>Ant.</title>65</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπαγκάλισμα_539">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 650"> <title>Ant.</title>650</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_καλούμενος_541">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὃν ἡμεῖς... ἐκαλοῦμεν</quote>: though in <ref n="Soph. OT 8"> <title>O. T.</title>8</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ πᾶσι κλεινὸς...καλούμενος</quote> the dat. goes with the adj.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πιστός_541"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 385"> <title>O. T.</title>385</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Κρέων ὁ πιστός</quote> , <ref n="Soph. Ant. 31"> <title>Ant.</title>31</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ἀγαθὸν Κρέοντα</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἰκούρια_542">, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">δῶρα</quote>: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_χρόνου_542"> (gen. of price), for the long time during which she has been his true wife. Cp. <ref n="Eur. Her. 1371">Eur. <title>H. F.</title>1371</ref>(Heracles bewailing Megara) <quote xml:lang="grc">σέ τ’ οὐχ ὁμοίως, ὦ τάλαιν’, ἀπώλεσα</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥσπερ σὺ τἀμὰ λέκτρ’ ἔσῳζες ἀσφαλῶς</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">μακρὰς διαντλοῦσ’ ἐν δόμοις οἰκουρίας</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_ἐπίσταμαι_543">, am incapable of it: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 582">cp. 582</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 686"> <title>Ant.</title>686</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῇδε_νόσῳ_543">, a very rare substitute for the cogn. accus. It is justified by the fact that <quote xml:lang="grc"> νοσεῖν</quote> is a word of such wide meaning; while <quote xml:lang="grc">τῇδε τῇ νόσῳ</quote> here signifies, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρωτι</quote>. So in <ref n="Aesch. PB 384">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title>384</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">τῇδε τῇ νόσῳ νοσεῖν</quote>, the dat. really means, ‘to be distempered in this special way’ (viz., by good sense). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_ξυνοικεῖν_545">: the inf. with art., placed at the beginning, gives an indignant tone: ‘but as to living...’ etc. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 78"> <title>Ant.</title>78</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ δὲ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">βίᾳ πολιτῶν δρᾶν ἔφυν ἀμήχανος</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧν_547">, fem., refers to the two phases of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥβη</quote> just mentioned. The gen. is partitive: ‘of (out of) these <quote xml:lang="grc">ἧβαι</quote>, the eye delights in the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνθος</quote>.’ Here <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἄνθος</quote> is a shorter way of expressing <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν ἀνθοῦσαν</quote>,—the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥβη</quote> which is in its early bloom. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧν_547_2"> could not, surely, refer to <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν μὲν ἕρπουσαν πρόσω</quote> <emph>only</emph>, as if it meant <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν νέων γυναικῶν</quote> (schol.): it must refer to <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν δὲ φθίνουσαν</quote> also. Nor, again, could <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦν</quote> stand for <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧν τῆς μέν</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_πόδα_547">. Here <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν δ’</quote> ought in strictness to have been <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς δ’</quote>, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς φθινούσης ἥβης</quote>. But, in the poet's thought, <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν δ’</quote> means, ‘<emph>the other kind</emph>,’— <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, the women who represent the <quote xml:lang="grc">φθίνουσα ἥβη</quote>. The subject to <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπεκτρέπει</quote> is not <quote xml:lang="grc"> ὀφθαλμός</quote>, but the <emph>man</emph> implied by it (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ ὁρῶν</quote>). The eye, as being here the guide of the choice, might, indeed, be said to ‘turn the foot aside,’ in the sense of <emph>causing</emph> that movement; but this would be awkward. For the transition of thought from <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀφθαλμός</quote> to the <emph>person</emph>, cp. <ref n="Eur. Med. 1244">Eur. <title> Med.</title>1244</ref>(quoted by Wecklein), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγ’, ὦ τάλαινα χεὶρ ἐμή, λαβὲ ξίφος</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">λάβ’, ἕρπε πρὸς βαλβῖδα λυπηρὰν βίου</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀφαρπάζειν_547">, said of the eye, means, to <emph>seize eagerly upon</emph> the beautiful sight (cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hor. S. 2.5.53">Hor. <title>Sat.</title> 2. 5. 53</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">Sic tamen ut limis rapias</quote> </cit> etc.). So we can speak of ‘snatching’ a glance, or of the eyes ‘drinking in’ beauty. There is no allusion to the idea expressed by <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Supp. 663">Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title> 663</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥβας δ’ ἄνθος ἄδρεπτον ἔστω</quote> </cit> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀφθαλμὸς_547">: the swift and ardent <emph> glance</emph> of the lover is often mentioned in Greek poetry: see esp. fr. 431 <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιάνδ’ ἐν ὄψει λίγγα θηρατηρίαν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρωτος, ἀστραπήν τιν’ ὀμμάτων, ἔχει</quote>. <ref n="Aesch. Supp. 1003">Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title>1003</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ παρθένων χλιδαῖσιν εὐμόρφοις ἔπι</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶς τις παρελθὼν <*>μματος θελκτήριον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τόξευμ’ ἔπεμψεν ἱμέρου νικώμενος</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταῦτ_οὖν_550">, for this reason, then: the pron. is adverbial: cp. Aesch <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 159"> <title>Pers.</title>159</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα δὴ λιποῦσ’ ἱκάνω χρυσεοστόλμους δόμους</quote>: <title>Vesp.</title> 1358 <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτ’ οὖν περί μου δέδοικε μὴ διαφθαρῶ</quote>. This seems better than to govern <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτ’</quote> by <quote xml:lang="grc">φοβοῦμαι. —<emph>πόσις</emph> </quote> was in Attic mainly a poetical word; but Arist. uses it, as <ref n="Aristot. Pol. 7.1335b">Arist. <title>Pol.</title>7. 16. 18</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅταν ᾖ καὶ προσαγορευθῇ πόσις</quote>: where, as here, it denotes the recognised or legal status.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καλῆται_550"> is right here, because there is a real anxiety: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καλεῖται_550"> (which would be fut., like <quote xml:lang="grc">καλεῖ</quote> in <ref n="Soph. El. 971"> <title>El.</title>971</ref>) would imply too much certainty. The subjunctive is similarly preferable to the indic. in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 30"> <title>Ph.</title>30</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">κυρῇ</quote>) and <emph> ib.</emph> 494 (<quote xml:lang="grc">βεβήκῃ</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καλῆται_550_2"> suits <quote xml:lang="grc">πόσις</quote> (‘bear the name of husband’); <ref n="Soph. Trach. 149">cp. 149</ref>: but <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνήρ</quote>, denoting here a clandestine relationship, requires <quote xml:lang="grc">ᾖ</quote> to be supplied. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 561">Cp. 561</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνήρ_550">: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, paramour. Cp. the sarcasm of the comic poet Pherecrates (fr. incert. 5) on Alcibiades:—<quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ὢν ἀνὴρ γὰρ</quote> [<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔτι νέος ὤν</quote> ] <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀλκιβιάδης, ὡς δοκεῖ</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνὴρ ἁπασῶν τῶν γυναικῶν ἐστι νῦν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_γάρ_552">: elliptical: <ref n="Soph. OC 755"> <title>O. C.</title>755</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ᾗ_ἔχω_552"> | </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λυτήριον_λύπημα_552">. For the adj., cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 635"> <title>El.</title>635</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λυτηρίους</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐχὰς...δειμάτων</quote>: <emph>ib.</emph> 1490 <quote xml:lang="grc">τόδ’ ἂν κακῶν μόνον γένοιτο τῶν πάλαι λυτήριον</quote>: fr. 687 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ μεθύειν πημονῆς λυτήριον</quote>. Clearly, then, <quote xml:lang="grc">λυτήριον</quote> is sound: and it must mean, as everywhere else, ‘giving deliverance.’ The corrupt word is </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λύπημα_552">: it has displaced some word of which <quote xml:lang="grc">λυτήριον</quote> could be the epithet. I believe that Sophocles wrote </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λώφημα_552">, ‘a means of <emph> relief.</emph>’ Hesychius shows that this noun was not only current, but tolerably familiar; for he has <quote xml:lang="grc">λῶφαρ· λώφημα</quote>,—using it to explain the rarer form. The corruption into <quote xml:lang="grc"> λύπημα</quote> probably arose through a marginal gloss, <quote xml:lang="grc">λύπης</quote>, on <quote xml:lang="grc">λυτήριον</quote> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχω_552"> to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχει_552">, renders, ‘in what way my grief has <emph>a remedy</emph>’— making <quote xml:lang="grc">λυτήριον</quote> a subst. This is clearly untenable. He cites <ref n="Pind. P. 5">Pind. <title>P.</title>5. 106</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ καλλίνικον λυτήριον δαπανᾶν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μέλος χαρίεν</quote>: but there <quote xml:lang="grc"> λυτήριον</quote> is a second epithet of <quote xml:lang="grc">μέλος</quote>. Nor is the case helped by Hesychius, <quote xml:lang="grc">λυτήριον· φυλακτήριον.— <emph>τῇδ’ ὑμῖν φράσω</emph> </quote>: the words mean strictly that the story will follow the course— <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, will exhibit the line of thought—by which the remedy has been found. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦν_μοι_555">: the imperf., because she has now used it.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποτὲ_555"> belongs in sense to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δῶρον_555">, as though we had <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐδόθη ποτέ</quote>. If the comma after </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θηρός_555"> were omitted, and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦν</quote> joined with <quote xml:lang="grc">κεκρυμμένον</quote> (as=<quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐκέκρυπτο</quote>), then <quote xml:lang="grc">ποτὲ</quote> would go with the verb: but <quote xml:lang="grc">κεκρυμμένον</quote> seems to be an afterthought.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παλαιὸν_555">, because she has had it long; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρχαίου_555">, because he lived long ago. This emphasis on the past is natural in one who is looking back sadly to the days of her youth, and speaking to young maidens for whom Nessus is only a legendary name.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θηρός_555_2">: so <ref n="Hom. Il. 1.268"> <title>Il.</title>1. 268</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φηρσὶν ὀρεσκῴοισι</quote>. They are called <quote xml:lang="grc">κένταυροι</quote> in <ref n="Hom. Il. 11.832"> <title>Il.</title>11. 832</ref>, as in the <title>Odyssey</title> (21. 295). Cp. below, 680.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέβητι_555">: properly a deep basin; also a kind of kettle used in cooking: but the poets can use the word to describe a cinerary urn (as <ref n="Soph. El. 1401"> <title>El.</title>1401</ref>). Here it means some kind of urn or jar. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δασυστέρνου_557">: shagginess is a regular attribute of the Centaurs in Greek poetry and art: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 837">cp. 837</ref>: <ref n="Hom. Il. 2.743"> <title>Il.</title>2. 743</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φῆρας ...λαχνήεντας</quote>: <title>Hom. hymn</title> 3. 224 <quote xml:lang="grc">κένταυρον λασιαύχενα</quote>. In <ref n="Ov. Met. 12. 284">Ov. <title>Met.</title>12. 284</ref>Cometes is the name of a Centaur.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρὰ_Νέσσου_557">, because it was his <quote xml:lang="grc">δῶρον</quote> (555): he invited her to take it, and told her how to use it: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_φονῶν_557">, gathered up from his wounds, as he lay dying. <quote xml:lang="grc">φονῶν</quote>, Bergk's correction of <quote xml:lang="grc">φόνων</quote>, seems right. The plur. <quote xml:lang="grc">φόνοι</quote> elsewhere (1) denotes separate acts of slaughter, <ref n="Soph. OC 1234"> <title>O. C.</title>1234</ref>: or (2) is a tragic expression for one such act (like <quote xml:lang="grc">θάνατοι</quote>): as <ref n="Soph. El. 11"> <title>El.</title>11</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρὸς ἐκ φόνων</quote> : <emph>ib.</emph> 779 <quote xml:lang="grc">φόνους πατρῳους</quote>. But here we expect rather a word which shall directly suggest the <emph> wounds:</emph> <ref n="Soph. Trach. 573">cp. 573</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σφαγῶν</quote>. And <quote xml:lang="grc">φονῶν</quote> can do so, since the phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν φοναῖς</quote> so often refers to carnage in battle. The schol. has </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φόνων_557"> in the lemma, but explains by <quote xml:lang="grc"> αἵματος</quote>, and quotes <ref n="Hom. Il. 10.521"> <title>Il.</title>10. 521</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνδρας τ’ ἀσπαίροντας</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ἀργαλέῃσι φονῇσιν</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 696"> <title>Ant.</title>696</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν φοναῖς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πεπτῶτ’</quote> (n.). If metre had allowed <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ φόνου</quote>, there would then have been no reason for change.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Νέσσος_557"> symbolises the <emph>roar</emph> of the angry torrent: the Sanskrit is <emph>nad</emph>, loud sound, whence <emph>nadá-s</emph>, ‘bellower’ (bull), or river: <emph>nad-i</emph>, flood: Curtius <title>Etym.</title> § 287 <emph>b.</emph> Hence the Thracian river <quote xml:lang="grc">Νέστος</quote> (also <quote xml:lang="grc">Νέσσος</quote>, <ref n="Hes. Th. 341">Hes. <title> Th.</title>341</ref>), and the Arcadian <quote xml:lang="grc">Νέδα</quote>, described by Strabo as <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥεῦμα λαβρὸν ἐκ τοῦ Λυκαίου κατιόν</quote> (8, p. 348). Among the Centaurs whom Heracles met at Mount Pholoè were <quote xml:lang="grc">Δούπων</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">Ὅμαδος</quote>,—names likewise denoting <emph>noise</emph>; and it is noteworthy that Homados, like Nessus, was a ravisher: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν Ἀρκαδίᾳ τὴν Εὐρυσθέως ἀδελφὴν Ἀλκυόνην βιασάμενος ἀνῃρέθη</quote> ( <ref n="Diod. 4. 12">Diod.4. 12</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_Εὔηνον_559">. The Evenus (<title> Fidhari</title>) rises on the high western slopes of Oeta; in its lower course, it passes through Aetolia, and enters the Corinthian Gulf at a point about 12 miles w. of Antirrhion. Calydon was on its w. bank; Pleuron, some 10 or 12 miles to the w. It is ‘one of the fiercest and most treacherous torrents in Greece’ (Tozer, <title>Geo. of Greece</title>, p. 96). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Ov. Met. 9.104">Ov. <title>Met.</title> 9. 104</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">Venerat Eveni rapidas Iove natus ad undas.</quote> </cit> The older name of the river, <quote xml:lang="grc">Λυκόρμας</quote> (<ref n="Strab. 7.327">Strabo7. 327</ref>), expressed the ‘wolf-like’ rush of its waters.</gloss>

data.quote: Venerat Eveni rapidas Iove natus ad undas.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποταμὸν_πόρευε_559">: for the double acc., cp. <ref n="Eur. Alc. 442">Eur. <title>Alc.</title>442</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γυναῖκ’ ἀρίσταν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">λίμναν Ἀχεροντίαν πορεύσας ἐλάτᾳ δικώπῳ</quote>. Here the second acc. denotes the space traversed; it would more usually denote the place <emph>to</emph> which, as in <ref n="Eur. Tro. 1085">Eur. <title>Tro.</title>1085</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμὲ...σκάφος</quote> | ...<quote xml:lang="grc"> πορεύσει</quote> | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">Ἄργος</quote>.—For the prodelision of the augment in </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόρευε_559">, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1602">Soph. <title>O. C.</title>1602</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταχεῖ )πόρευσαν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 360"> Soph. <title>Ph.</title>360</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεὶ )δάκρυσα.—<emph> μις‐</emph> </quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θοῦ_559">: acc. to Apollodorus (2. 7. 6) Nessus pretended divine authority for levying this toll,—<quote xml:lang="grc">λέγων παρὰ θεῶν τὴν πορθμείαν εἰληφέναι διὰ δικαιοσύνην.—<emph>πομπίμοις</emph> </quote>: cp. <title>I. A.</title> 1319 <quote xml:lang="grc">ναῶν</quote>... | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλάταν πομπαίαν.—<emph>οὔτε λαίφεσιν νεώς</emph> </quote> might be an instrum. dat. construed directly with <quote xml:lang="grc"> )πόρευε</quote>: but it is perhaps truer to say that the notion of <quote xml:lang="grc">πέμπων</quote> is evolved from the preceding clause. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 512">Cp. 512</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τινάσσων</quote> ). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_στόλον_562">, ‘by my father's sending,’ cogn. acc. to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑσπόμην_562">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 159">cp. 159</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγῶνας ἐξιών</quote> (n.). The peculiarity is that <quote xml:lang="grc">στόλος</quote> here=<quote xml:lang="grc">πέμψις</quote>, corresponding to the sense of the act. <quote xml:lang="grc">στέλλω</quote>: whereas it usu. means ‘expedition,’ ‘journey,’ from midd. <quote xml:lang="grc">στέλλομαι</quote>. It may be noted, however, that the boldness is softened by the fact that <quote xml:lang="grc">στόλος</quote> sometimes meant a journey with ref. to its purpose, ‘a <emph>mission</emph>’: <ref n="Soph. OC 358"> <title>O. C.</title>358</ref> n. Certainly <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρῷος στόλος</quote> would ordinarily mean, ‘a journey of my father<*>s,’ or, ‘an expedition despatched by’ him; yet the sense required here seems possible for poetry. The phrase cannot well mean, (1) ‘the <emph>journey</emph> prescribed for me by my father’; nor (2) ‘under my father's <emph>escort</emph>,’—as if Oeneus had accompanied them for some distance.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὖνις_562">=<quote xml:lang="grc">εὐνέτις</quote>, as in <ref n="Eur. Orest. 929">Eur. <title>Or.</title>929</ref> etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φέρων_ὤμοις_564">. Nessus is here imagined as a Centaur of the older form known to Greek art,—viz., a complete man, with the barrel and hinder parts of a horse attached to the middle of his back. A Centaur on the <quote xml:lang="grc">λάρναξ</quote> of Cypselus at Olympia is described by Paus. (5. 19. 7) as <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ τοὺς πάντας ἵππου πόδας, τοὺς δὲ ἔμπροσθεν αὐτῶν ἔχων ἀνδρός</quote>. This form may be called the <emph>andro-centaur.</emph> The more familiar <emph>hippo-centaur</emph>—a complete horse, only with a human chest and head substituted for the equine neck and head—was of later origin. In <title>Journ. Hellen. Stud.</title> I. 130 Mr Sidney Colvin gives a wood-cut of an early gem (in the British Museum), representing an androcentaur carrying off a woman, who is grasped in his right arm. Similar subjects occur on coins of Eastern Macedonia. Violence of this kind was part of the <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕβρις</quote> (1096) ascribed to the savage Centaurs, and appears in numerous legends (<title>J. H. S.</title>, <emph>l.c.</emph>, p. 140).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_564">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1123"> <title>O. T.</title>1123</ref> n. The third person, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦν_564">, would be less fitting: she speaks of her own helplessness at the moment.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέσῳ_πόρῳ_564">: for the dat., <ref n="Soph. Trach. 172">cp. 172</ref>: <ref n="Soph. El. 313"> <title>El.</title>313</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν δ’ ἀγροῖσι τυγχάνει.—<emph>ματαίαις</emph> </quote>, implying rash folly ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1339"> <title>Ant.</title>1339</ref> n.), here =‘wanton.’ The schol. wrongly took it to mean that the attempt was baffled by Heracles.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_ἐγώ_564">: Sophocles has avoided the error of Archilochus, who had described Deianeira as making a prolix appeal to her husband (<quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς τὸν Ἡρακλέα ῥαψῳδοῦσαν</quote>: Dion Chrysost. or. 60). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπιστρέψας_566">, intrans., as in <ref n="Hdt. 2. 103">Her.2. 103</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιστρέψας ὀπίσω ἤιε</quote>: <title>Vesp.</title> 422 <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐπίστρεφε</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῦρο</quote>. The poet, though he has called the river <quote xml:lang="grc">βαθύρρουν</quote> (559), seems to imagine Heracles as wading across it, in front of Nessus. If Heracles had been carried over first, he would naturally have been facing the river. Ovid makes him <emph>swim</emph> across, and shoot the Centaur from the bank ( <ref n="Ov. Met. 9. 110"> <title>Met.</title>9. 110</ref> ff.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κομήτην_566">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 711"> <title>Ph.</title>711</ref> n. According to Dion (or. 60), Sophocles was criticised on the ground that the hero's act might have been fatal to Deianeira; Nessus might have dropped her in the river. It would be enough to suppose that Heracles could not pause to think; but the context also suggests, as we have seen, that he was near enough to rescue her at need.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλεύμονας_566">=<quote xml:lang="grc">πνεύμονας</quote> (rt <quote xml:lang="grc">πνυ</quote>). Curtius (<title>Etym.</title> § 370) explains the change of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ν_566"> to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λ_566"> by ‘the rarity of the sound-group <emph>pn</emph> and the frequency of <emph>pl</emph>’; comparing the kindred words for ‘lungs,’ Lat. <emph>pulmo</emph>, Church-Slavonic <emph>plušta</emph>, Lithuanian <emph>plaúczei.</emph> The form with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λ_566_2"> is attested as Attic by schol. <ref n="Aristoph. Peace 1069">Ar. <title> Pax</title>1069</ref>, Eustath. p. 483. 8. In 1054, as here, L gives the </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λ_566_3"> form, though with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ν_566_2"> written above by the first hand: but in 1054 <quote xml:lang="grc">πνευμόνων</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκθνῄσκων_566">, as the <emph>faintness</emph> of approaching death began to come over him. The regular sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκθνῄσκειν</quote> is ‘to swoon away’: cp. <title>Hist. Anim.</title> 3. 19 (p. 521 <emph>a</emph> 11) <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀφιεμένου</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">αἵματος</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔξω πλείονος μὲν ἐκθνῄσκουσι, πολλοῦ δ’ ἄγαν ἀποθνῄσκουσιν</quote>. So <title>Legg.</title> 959 A distinguishes a person in a swoon, <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ἐκτεθνεῶτα</quote>, from <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ὄντως τεθνηκότα</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοσοῦτον_566">, referring to what follows, and associated with <quote xml:lang="grc"> τοσόνδε</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 679"> <title>Ai.</title>679</ref> ff. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς τοσόνδ’...τοσαῦθ’.—<emph>τοσόνδ’</emph> </quote> is explained by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐὰν <emph>γὰρ</emph> </quote> etc.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_πορθμῶν_566">: <quote xml:lang="grc"> πορθμός</quote> usu. means (1) a ferry, or (2) the act of crossing water; here the second sense passes into that of <quote xml:lang="grc"> πορθμεία</quote>, ‘my services as ferryman’: for the plur., <ref n="Soph. Trach. 628">cp. 628</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐὰν_ὕδρας_572">. The gen. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_σφαγῶν_572"> depends on the prep. in </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμφίθρεπτον_572">, ‘coagulated around the wound.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐνέγκῃ_χερσίν_572">: for the midd., <ref n="Soph. Trach. 558">cp. 558</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνειλόμην</quote> (n.). The phrase seems to imply a careful collecting of the blood with a cioth. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ᾗ_μελαγχόλους_572"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: ‘at the part (of the wound) where the monstrous hydra has tinged the arrow with black gall’: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘where the hydra's gall, with which the arrow is tinged, can be traced,’—by a darker tint in that portion of the blood with which it has mixed. When the arrow was withdrawn from the wound, there would be a gush of blood, but some parts of the blood would have been more affected by the venom than others; and Nessus wishes her to take the most envenomed parts. The stress is on </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μελαγχόλους_572">, and the whole phrase is a compressed way of saying, <quote xml:lang="grc">ᾗ μέλας χόλος ἐστίν, <*> ἰοὺς ὕδρα ἔβαψεν</quote> (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1451"> <title>O. T.</title>1451</ref> n.). For the proleptic adj., cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 475"> <title>Ant.</title>475</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀπτὸν...περισκελῆ</quote>: for the plur. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰούς_572"> (referring to the single arrow of 567), <ref n="Soph. Aj. 231"> <title>Ai.</title>231</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξίφεσιν</quote>, <ref n="Soph. El. 196"> <title>El.</title>196</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γενύων</quote>. The double barb makes such a plur. intelligible. Heracles cannot have shot twice.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θρέμμα_ὕδρας_572">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 508">cp. 508</ref> n.: so <quote xml:lang="grc">θρέμμα</quote> refers to a monster in 1093 and 1099. For the periphrasis, cp. <title>Legg.</title> 790 D <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ νεογενῆ παίδων θρέμματα.—<emph>Λερναίας</emph> </quote>: dwelling in the marsh of Lerna, on the Argolic coast, s. of Argos. <ref n="Apollod. 2.5.2">Apollod.2. 5. 2</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ δὲ σῶμα τῆς ὕδρας ἀνασχίσας τῇ χολῇ τοὺς ὀϊστοὺς ἔβαψεν</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐνέγκῃ_χερσίν_572_2"> here favours the same view.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μελάγχολος_ἰός_572">, the latter word would mean ‘venom,’ and </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θρέμμα_572"> ‘issue’ (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 834">cp. 834</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔτρεφε δ’ αἰόλος δράκων</quote>): the object of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔβαψεν_572"> would be <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτάς</quote> (the wound), The objection is that the wily monster does not wish to draw Deianeira's attention to the <emph>venom</emph>;—as he would then do very pointedly. He is content to speak of the <emph>dark colour</emph> (<quote xml:lang="grc"> μελαγχόλους</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὥστε_στέρξει_576">. After <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε</quote>, the negative of the infin. is <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote>, but of the indic., <quote xml:lang="grc"> οὐ</quote>. Here the <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote> must be due to the final sense: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, the notion of result is merged in that of aim; as if it were <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως μή</quote>. I have not found any real parallel. <ref n="Dem. 19.218">Dem. or. 19 § 218</ref> writes, <quote xml:lang="grc">τοσαύτης ἀνανδρίας...ὁμολογεῖτε εἶναι μεστοί, ὥστε μήτ’ ἐν τῇ χώρᾳ πολεμίων ὄντων μήτ’ ἐκ θαλάττης πολιουρκούμενοι...ε<*>τα τὴν εἰρήνην ἐποιήσασθε</quote>. But there the <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote> seems clearly ‘generic’: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, the sense is: ‘you are so weak as to have made peace at <emph>a time when</emph> there was no enemy in the country,’ etc. [Prof. Goodwin, <title>Moods and Tenses</title>, new ed., § 606, suggests that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐποιήσασθε</quote> virtually depends on an <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ</quote> further back, and that the force of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε</quote> is lost. This would be conceivable if the <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote> came after <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐποιήσασθε</quote>: but it immediately follows <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε</quote>.] —The opposite anomaly occurs in <ref n="Soph. El. 780"> <title>El.</title>780</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">ωστ’ οὔτε</quote>... | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">στεγάζειν</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντὶ_σοῦ_576">, instead of the gen. after the comparat.: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 182"> <title>Ant.</title>182</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐννοήσας_578">: Sophocles prefers the act. voice of this compound: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1440"> <title>Ph.</title>1440</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δόμοις_578">, simply ‘in the house’: </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγκεκλῃμένον_578"> adds the notion expressed in 686 by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν μυχοῖς σῴζειν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χιτῶνα_τόνδ_580">: a handmaid carries the casket (622) containing the robe.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔβαψα_580">. The notion of a <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλτρον</quote> in the form of an <emph>unguent</emph> was a familiar one: thus in <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 516">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 516</ref>Phaedra asks the Nurse, <quote xml:lang="grc">πότερα δὲ χριστὸν ἢ ποτὸν τὸ φάρμακον</quote> <emph>;</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσβαλοῦς_580">, ‘with application of everything that he directed,’—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, applying the philtre to the robe with attention to all the prescribed details. From vv. 680—687 it appears that Nessus had said more than she repeats in 572—577. He gave <quote xml:lang="grc">θεσμοί</quote> (682), precise instructions.—This is better than to understand, ‘with all <emph>the additions</emph> that he enjoined’ (schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ἄλλα τινὰ συμμίξασα</quote>): though it may be supposed that the coagulated blood, before being applied to a large surface, was diluted with water. (Schneidewin explains the word by <ref n="Apollod. 2.7.6">Apollod.2. 7. 6</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τόν τε γόνον ὃν ἀφῆκε κατὰ τῆς γῆς καὶ τὸ...αἷμα συμμίξαι</quote>.)— Not, ‘with <emph>observance of</emph> all that he said’<*> as if <quote xml:lang="grc">νῷ</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">νοῦν</quote>) were understood. <ref target="cl844">See on 844</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">προσέβαλε</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πεπείρανται_581"> (3rd sing.), from the epic <quote xml:lang="grc">πειραίνω</quote>: <ref n="Hom. Od. 12.37"> <title>Od.</title>12. 37</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα μὲν οὕτω πάντα πεπείρανται</quote>: <ref n="Pind. I. 7">Pind. <title>I.</title>7. 24</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δίκας ἐπείραινε</quote>. The Attic form <quote xml:lang="grc">πεπέρανται</quote> occurs in <ref n="Plat. Rep. 502"> Plat. <title>Rep.</title>502</ref> The E. words have a tragic significance. Her remedial measures are now complete. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακὰς_τόλμας_582">. She wishes to assure them that she intends no harm to Heracles, and has no reason to fear evil. The results of so-called ‘lovecharms’ were often disastrous. Antiphon's first oration is against a woman charged with the wilful murder of her husband by causing a love-potion to be administered to him: she pleaded, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἐπὶ θανάτῳ...διδόναι, ἀλλ’ ἐπὶ φίλτροις</quote> (§ 9). <title>Magn. Mor.</title> 1. 16 (p. 1188 <emph>b</emph> 31) notices a case in which a woman was tried under like circumstances by the Areiopagus, but acquitted, because there was no proof of <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόνοια</quote>. <ref n="Plut. Conjug. 139a">Plut. <title> Mor.</title>139</ref>A compares lovers won by such means to fish captured by baits which spoil them; <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱ φίλτρα τινὰ καὶ γοητείας ἐπιτεχνώμεναι τοῖς ἀνδράσι, καὶ χειρούμεναι διὰ ἡδονῆς αὐτούς, ἐμπλήκτοις καὶ ἀνοήτοις καὶ διεφθαρμένοις συμβιοῦσι</quote>. Alciphron 1. 37 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφιβάλλειν</quote> (to have dubious effects) <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴωθε τὰ φίλτρα, καὶ ἀποσκήπτειν εἰς ὄλεθρον</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μήτ_ἐκμάθοιμι_582">: an emphatic way of protesting how utterly foreign such thoughts are to her nature: —‘may I never be capable of them (543 n.), or be led to learn anything about them.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στυγῶ_582">: for the indic. coordinated with the opt., <ref n="Soph. Trach. 143">cp. 143</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φίλτροις_πως_584">. The use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐάν πως</quote> is the same here as when it serves for the elliptical expression of a hope or aim ( <ref n="Soph. OC 1769"> <title>O. C.</title>1769</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Θήβας δ’ ἡμᾶς</quote> | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">πέμψον, ἐάν πως</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">διακωλύσωμεν ἰόντα φόνον</quote>. But </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μεμηχάνηται_τοὔργον_584">, since it <emph>follows</emph> the clause with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐάν πως</quote>, is not really analogous to the verb which usually <emph>precedes</emph> such a clause; as <quote xml:lang="grc">πέμψον</quote> in <ref n="Soph. OC 1770."> <title>O. C.</title>1770.</ref>The constr. is not, <quote xml:lang="grc">μεμηχάνηται τοὔργον, ἐάν πως ὑπερβαλώμεθα</quote>, ‘the deed has been devised, in the hope that,’ etc. Rather the sense is: ‘But <emph>as to the possibility</emph> of prevailing by love-charms,— the means for <emph>that</emph> attempt have been devised.’ <quote xml:lang="grc">μεμηχάνηται τοὔργον</quote> is an abrupt substitute for <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτο πειρᾶσθαι βούλομαι</quote> or the like, and is prompted by her nervous sense that she <emph>has taken</emph> a bold step. The peculiar form of the sentence arises from the wish to emphasise <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλτροις</quote> as opposed to <quote xml:lang="grc">κακὰς τόλμας</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπερβαλώμεθα_584">: the midd., in this sense, is more freq. than the act.; but the dat. usu. denotes the point of excellence ( <ref n="Aristoph. Kn. 409">Ar. <title>Eq.</title>409</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔ τοί μ’ ὑπερβαλεῖσθ’ ἀναιδείᾳ</quote>), and not, as here, the means.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὴν_παῖδα_584">, —strong in the charms of youth (547).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θέλκτροισι_584">, a reiteration that the means are to be <emph>gentle:</emph> cp. <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 509">Eur. <title> Hipp.</title>509</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔστιν κατ’ οἴκους φίλτρα μοι θελκτήρια</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρωτος. —<emph>τοῖς ἐφ’ Ἡρακλεῖ</emph> </quote>, aimed at him, as the person whose love was to be won: cp. <ref n="Apollod. 2.7.6">Apollod.2. 7. 6</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ θέλοι φίλτρον πρὸς Ἡρακλέα ἔχειν</quote> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴ_μὴ_584">: <quote xml:lang="grc">τι</quote> (adv.)=‘perchance’: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 712">cp. 712</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OT 969"> <title>O. T.</title>969</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OC 1450."> <title>O. C.</title>1450.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάταιον_584">, culpably rash (cp. n. on 565).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_μή_584">: ‘otherwise,’ after a negative: cp. <title>Vesp.</title> 434 <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ μεθῆσθε μηδενί·</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δὲ μή, )ν πέδαις παχείαις οὐδὲν ἀριστήσετε</quote>. So <ref n="Thuc. 1. 28">Thuc.1. 28</ref>, <ref n="Plat. Phaedrus 63d"> <title>Phaed.</title>63D</ref>, etc. This rather clumsy formula was recommended by brevity: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, in <emph>Ar. l. c.</emph>, the alternative was <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δὲ μεθήσεσθε</quote>, as here <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δὲ δοκῶ.— <emph>πεπαύσομαι</emph> </quote> : <ref n="Soph. Ant. 91">Soph. <title>Ant.</title>91</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πίστις_588">, in an objective sense, a ground of confidence, a warranty: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 623">cp. 623</ref>: <ref n="Soph. El. 887"> <title>El.</title>887</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίν’, ὦ τάλαιν)<ref n="Soph. El. i"> ελ., ι</ref>)δοῦσα πίστιν<emph>;</emph>—<emph>δοκεῖς παῤ ἡμῖν</emph> </quote>: <ref n="Eur. Med. 762">Eur. <title>Med.</title>762</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γενναῖος ἀνήρ</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">Αἰγεῦ, παρ’ ἐμοὶ δεδόκησαι</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὕτως_ἔχει_588"> is slightly emphasised by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_588">, and limits the affirmative implied by the art. before </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πίστις_588_2">: ‘The <emph>present state</emph> of the warranty (given by <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ δρώμενα</quote>) is this,’ etc. It seems needless to suppose that the literal sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">πίστις</quote> here is different from that in 588.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_588">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε</quote>, answering to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὕτως_588">: cp. <ref n="Hdt. 2. 135">Her.2. 135</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτω δή τι κλεινὴ ἐγένετο ὡς καὶ οἱ πάντες...τὸ οὔνομα ἐξέμαθον</quote>. (When <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote> stands for <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε</quote>, it is more often with the infin.) It is possible, but less fitting, to take <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote> as=‘since,’ introducing the explanation (like <quote xml:lang="grc">γάρ</quote>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_δοκεῖν_588"> is ‘the expecting’ to succeed (rather than ‘the seeming likely’ to do so).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πείρᾳ_προσωμίλησα_588">, have not come to close quarters with an experiment, —have not actually essayed it: cp. <ref n="Plat. Tim. 88C">Plat. <title>Tim.</title> 88C</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γυμναστικῇ προσομιλοῦντα</quote>: <ref n="Thuc. 6. 70">Thuc.6. 70</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς...ἐλάχιστα πολέμῳ ὡμιληκόσι</quote>, opp. to <quote xml:lang="grc"> τοῖς...ἐμπειροτέροις</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δρῶσαν_592">: the partic. expresses the leading idea (‘if thou wouldest know, thou must <emph>act</emph>’): cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1038"> <title>O. C.</title>1038</ref> n.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γνῶμα_592">, a means of judging, a test: <ref n="Hdt. 7. 52">Her.7. 52</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἴωνας...τῶν ἔχομεν γνῶμα μέγιστον</quote>. (In poetry the word sometimes means merely ‘judgment,’ ‘opinion’: <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1352">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title>1352</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Her. 407.">Eur. <title>Her.</title>407.</ref>)—The Chorus do not say that she ought to make the experiment; but only that, until she does so, she can have no certainty. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_αὐτίκ_594">: for the repetition of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλά</quote> (after 592), cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 645"> <title>Ph.</title>645</ref> <ref n="Soph. Phil. 647."> Ph., 647.</ref> — </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐλεύσεται_594">, will depart (to Heracles); and so the result will soon be known. For this sense of the verb, cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 48"> <title>Ph.</title>48</ref> n.: for the form, <ref n="Soph. OC 1206"> <title>O. C.</title>1206</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόνον_596">, <emph>modo:</emph> <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1109">cp. 1109</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 528."> <title>Ph.</title>528.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρ_ὑμῶν_596">, from your side, on your part: the gen. is probably right, though the <emph>v. l.</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρ_ὑμῖν_596"> is specious. Silence is their contribution to her plan. Cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 469"> <title>El.</title>469</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σιγὴ παρ’ ὑμῶν πρὸς θεῶν ἔστω, φίλαι. —<emph>στεγοίμεθ’</emph> </quote>, have my action kept secret. Cp. fr. 614 <quote xml:lang="grc">σύγγνωτε κἀνάσχεσθε σιγῶσαι· τὸ γὰρ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">γυναιξὶν αἰσχρὸν σὺν γυναῖκα δεῖ στέγειν</quote> (so I amend <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν γυναικὶ</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 85"> <title>Ant.</title>85</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κρυφῇ δὲ κεῦθε, σὺν δ’ αὔτως ἐγώ</quote>).— Blaydes may be right in reading </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στεγώμεθ_596"> (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 49"> <title>O. T.</title>49</ref> n.). But the opt. is defensible, as expressing an ardent hope, rather than a mere injunction (‘Heaven grant that ye keep my secret!’).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰσχύνῃ_πεσεῖ_596">: the dat. is one of manner (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 51"> <title>O. T.</title>51</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀσφαλείᾳ...ἀνόρθωσον</quote>, n.), not of cause, as <ref n="Soph. El. 429"> <title>El.</title>429</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀβουλίᾳ πεσεῖν</quote>. Thus the phrase=<quote xml:lang="grc">πεσεῖ αἰσχρὸν πτῶμα</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1045"> <title>Ant.</title>1045</ref>). The simple <quote xml:lang="grc"> πεσεῖ</quote> could not stand for <quote xml:lang="grc">περιπεσεῖ</quote> (‘fall <emph>into</emph> shame’). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῷ_χρόνῳ_599">, by reason of the long time (spent at Trachis). He was ready to depart when he entered at v. 393: but Deianeira, after learning all, brought him back into the house (492). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτὰ_ταῦτα_600">: the commands for which he asks (598). Instead of saying, ‘I have been preparing this robe, in order that you may take it,’ she says: ‘I have been busied about the charge to be given to you,—so that you may take this robe.’—For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_600"> before </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πράσσω_600"> , <ref n="Soph. Trach. 314">cp. 314</ref>.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἠγορῶ_600">: the only part of the epic <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγοράομαι</quote> found in Trag.: <ref n="Hdt. 6. 11">Her.6. 11</ref> has <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠγορόωντο</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταναϋφῆ_600">, woven long, <quote xml:lang="grc"> ποδήρη</quote>. Wunder's restoration of this word, in place of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τόνδε_εὐϋφῆ_600">, is confirmed by two facts: (1) there was a mysterious variant </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀϋφῆ_600">, explained by <quote xml:lang="grc">λεπτοϋφῆ</quote>: (2) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταναϋφῆ_600_2">, explained by <quote xml:lang="grc"> λεπτοϋφῆ</quote>, occurs in Hesychius, Suidas, and Photius. It may be added that the </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_600"> of the vulgate, if not impossible, is at least suspicious.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέπλον_600">: Eustath. p. 599.44 refers to this passage as one in which <quote xml:lang="grc">πέπλος</quote> is part of a man's dress, alluding also to <title>I. A.</title> 1550, where a <quote xml:lang="grc">πέπλος</quote> is worn by Agamemnon. The Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">πέπλος</quote> belongs to women only; hence the schol. here objects to the word. <quote xml:lang="grc">χιτών</quote> is, in fact, the proper term for the long robe sent to Heracles: <quote xml:lang="grc">πέπλος</quote>, when used with ref. to it (674, 758, 774), is rather a general word for a stately garment.—She now hands to Lichas the casket (622) containing the robe. Hence the repeated </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τόνδε_600"> (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτόν</quote>) in 604 is natural. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φράζ_ἀμφιδύσεται_604">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 567"> <title>Ai.</title>567</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κείνῳ τ’ ἐμὴν ἀγγείλατ’ ἐντολήν, ὅπως</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">δείξει</quote>). The more usual infin. is often thus replaced after verbs of <emph>asking</emph> or <emph>commanding</emph>; but it is specially suitable, as here, after a verb of <emph>warning.</emph> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἕρκος_ἱερὸν_607">, a sacred temenos, where there might chance to be a blazing altar (she is thinking of vv. 237 f.): </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐφέστιον_σέλας_607">, any fire kindled in the warrior's quarters at Cenaeum. She is repeating the substance of the Centaur's precepts: see 685 f. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φανερὸς_608">, ‘conspicuous,’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμφανῶς_608">, ‘publicly,’ ‘before all eyes’: both words go with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σταθεὶς_608">, picturing the moment when Heracles shall stand forth in front of the altar. The order of words and the rhythm are against taking <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμφανῶς</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">δείξῃ.—<emph>ταυροσφάγῳ</emph> </quote>: a day of solemn rejoicing, when the greater victims are slain: <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 261">Aesch. <title>Cho.</title>261</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βουθύτοις ἐν ἤμασι</quote>. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 760">Cp. 760</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ηὔγμην_610">, the only instance of this plpf. (for <quote xml:lang="grc">εὖκτο</quote> in Hom. fr. 2. 15 is rather an a<*>.): but <ref n="Plat. Phaedrus 279c"> <title>Phaedr.</title> 279 C</ref> has the pf. <quote xml:lang="grc">ηὖκται</quote> as pass. impers. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1512"> <title>O. T.</title>1512</ref> cr. n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πανδίκως_611"> (with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στελεῖν_611">) ‘as in duty bound,’—by the vow, and by a wife's natural sympathy. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 294">Cp. 294</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πανδίκῳ φρενί</quote> (n.).—Others join the adv. with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σωθέντ_611">, as=‘completely’ (schol. <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀνενδοιάστως</quote>, ‘indubitably’). But there is no evidence for </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πανδίκως_611_2"> as merely= <quote xml:lang="grc"> παντελῶς</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1247">cp. 1247</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OC 1306"> <title>O. C.</title>1306</ref>: [Eur. ] <title>Rhes.</title> 720 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄλοιτο πανδίκως</quote> (‘as he deserves’). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καινῷ_καινὸν_613">: the epithet, strictly applicable to the <quote xml:lang="grc">πέπλωμα</quote> only, is given to the <quote xml:lang="grc">θυτήρ</quote> also, expressing the new radiance with which the robe shall invest its wearer. This common idiom ( <ref n="Hdt. 2. 173">Her.2. 173</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν θρόνῳ σεμνῷ σεμνὸν θωκέοντα</quote>) occurs elsewhere also under a similar condition,— viz., where the repeated adj. serves for collective emphasis rather than for separate characterisation: <ref n="Soph. El. 742">Soph. <title> El.</title>742</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρθὸς ἐξ ὀρθῶν δίφρων</quote> : <ref n="Soph. Aj. 267">Soph. <title>Ai.</title>267</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κοινὸς ἐν κοινοῖσι λυπεῖσθαι</quote>: <emph>ib.</emph> 467 <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυμπεσὼν μόνος μόνοις</quote>. But it should be observed that, in this passage, the repetition has a further motive. It is a touch of tragic irony, like the unconscious ambiguity of <quote xml:lang="grc">προσαρμόσαι</quote> in 494. For <quote xml:lang="grc">θυτὴρ καινός</quote> could mean, ‘a sacrificer of a novel kind’: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1542">Soph. <title>O. C.</title>1542</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ γὰρ ἡγεμὼν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">σφῷν αὖ πέφασμαι καινός</quote>. For the sinister sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">καινός</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 867">cp. 867</ref> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_ἀποίσεις_614">. Deianeira has sealed the casket (622) with her own signet. <quote xml:lang="grc">σφραγίς</quote> here is not the signetring itself, but the impression in wax, the seal: cp. <title>I. A.</title> 155 <quote xml:lang="grc">σφραγῖδα φύλασς’, <*>ν ἐπὶ δέλτῳ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τῇδε κομίζεις</quote>. The word <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕρκος</quote>, ‘enclosure,’ means the part of the ring which bears the device, <quote xml:lang="grc">σῆμα</quote>. This part was called <quote xml:lang="grc">σφενδόνη</quote> ( <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 862">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title>862</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τύποι...σφενδόνης</quote>), Lat. <emph>funda</emph>, be cause the gem in its setting was like a stone in a sling: the English term is <emph>bezel</emph>; the French, <emph>chaton.</emph> That <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕρκος</quote> here denotes the whole bezel, and not merely the rim, is shown by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπόν</quote>, which would otherwise be <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνόν</quote>. The use of the word <quote xml:lang="grc">σῆμα</quote> in this context is illustrated by an inscription on a scarab of the 6th cent. B.C., published by Rossbach, <title>Archäol. Zeit.</title> (1883, pp. 311 ff., pl. 16, No. 19): <quote xml:lang="grc">Θέρσιώς εἰμι σᾶμα· μή με ἄνοιγε</quote>. Cp. Prof. H. MiddletonJ. , <title>Engraved Gems of Classical Times</title> (1891), p. 67.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπὸν_μαθήσεται_614"> is Billerbeck's certain correction of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπ_θήσεται_614">, a corruption caused by the easy change of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ν_614"> (before <quote xml:lang="grc">μαθήσεται</quote>) into </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μ_614">; just as in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1266"> <title>Ant.</title>1266</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ξὺν μόρῳ</quote>, L has <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυμμόρωι</quote>. As to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐμαθὲς_μαθήσεται_614">, cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 502"> <title>Ant.</title>502</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κλέος... εὐκλεέστερον</quote>. Those who keep the vulgate govern </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὃ_614"> by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπιθήσεται_ὄμμα_614"> as= <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄψεται</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νόμον_616">, ‘rule’; <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1177">cp. 1177</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_ὢν_616">: not <quote xml:lang="grc">πομπὸν ὄντα</quote>. Even when the inf. has the art., and represents an oblique case, its subject stands in the nom., if identical with the subject of the principal verb; <ref n="Plat. Rep. 598D">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 598D</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξηπατήθη διὰ τὸ αὐτὸς μὴ οἷός τ’ ε<*>ναι ἐπιστήμην...ἐξετάσαι</quote>: cp. <emph>ib.</emph> 526 B: <ref n="Thuc. 4. 18">Thuc.4. 18</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλάχιστ’ ἂν...διὰ τὸ μὴ τῷ ὀρθουμένῳ αὐτοῦ πιστεύοντες ἐπαίρεσθαι...καταλύοιντο</quote>: Andoc. or. 3. § 30 <quote xml:lang="grc">εἱλόμεθα...στρατεύεσθαι..., ἀντὶ τοῦ μένοντες οἴκοι ξυμμάχους ἔχειν Συρακοσίους.—<emph>περισσὰ δρᾶν</emph> </quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 88">Soph. <title>Ant.</title>88</ref>) glances at the instance in which he had exceeded his master's orders (481 ff.); it is more particularly a hint, as the schol. remarks, that he is to respect the <quote xml:lang="grc">σφραγίς</quote> on the casket: verse 622 indicates this. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπως_ἂν_618"> depends on the notion of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιμελοῦ</quote> contained in <quote xml:lang="grc">φύλασσε νόμον</quote>.— If Lichas acts in the interest of his master only (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 286">cp. 286</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πιστὸς ὢν κείνῳ</quote>), the </gloss>
