<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χάρις_618"> won by him will be </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἁπλῆ_618">: if he regards the welfare of his mistress also, it will become </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διπλῆ_618">. The genitives </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κείνου_κἀμοῦ_618"> (of which the second has the chief stress)=‘<emph>from</emph> him and me’: hence </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυνελθοῦς_618">. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴπερ_Ἑρμοῦ_620"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>: for <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἑρμῆς ὁ πέμπων</quote>, the patron of <quote xml:lang="grc">κήρυκες</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 133"> <title>Ph.</title>133</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πομπεύω_620">, absol., act as <quote xml:lang="grc">πομπός</quote>: </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τέχνην_620">, cogn. acc., like <quote xml:lang="grc">πομπήν</quote>, which ‘the art of Hermes’ implies. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βέβαιον_620">, predicate, with the sureness of experience and good faith.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔ_μὴ_620"> is more usual than </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔ_μὴ_620_2"> (L's re<*>ding), and seems slightly more suitable here. Yet note <ref n="Soph. Aj. 560"> <title>Ai.</title>560</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔ τοι σ’ Ἀχαιῶν, ο<*>δα, μή τις ὑβρίσῃ</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OC 176"> <title>O. C.</title>176</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔ τοι μήποτέ ς’...ἄκοντά τις ἄξει.—<emph>ἔν σοι</emph> </quote>, in thy case, in what concerns thee: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1092"> <title>Ai.</title>1092</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν θανοῦσιν ὑβριστής.—<emph>σφαλῶ γ’</emph> </quote>, trip, commit a fault: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 727">cp. 727</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_οὐ_620">: <ref n="Soph. OT 1232."> <title>O. T.</title>1232.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄγγος_620">, a word applicable to ‘vessels’ of various kinds, here means a coffer or casket, the <quote xml:lang="grc"> ζύγαστρον</quote> of 692. Similarly in <ref n="Eur. Ion 32">Eur. <title> Ion</title>32</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγγος</quote> is the general term, defined by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντίπηξ</quote>, ‘cradle’ (19<ref n="Eur. Ion 1337">Eur. Ion, 1337</ref> f.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ἔχει_620">: with the seal unbroken (614). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγων_πίστιν_623">: the <emph>pledge</emph> (588) of thy words,—referring esp. to the mention of the <emph>vow</emph> (610 ff.), which explains the gift.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐφαρμόσαι_623">=‘fitly (or ‘duly’) add,)—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> ‘add in attestation of the gift.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέγεις_623"> is the best correction of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχεις_623">, which doubtless arose from </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχει_623"> in the line above. (In <ref n="Soph. El. 934"> <title>El.</title>934</ref> f., <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν χαρᾷ λόγους</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιούσδ’ ἔχουσ’ ἔσπευδον</quote>, the sense is ‘<emph>bearing</emph> news,’ not ‘<emph>speaking</emph> words.’) Otto's </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐφεῖς_623"> (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 286">cp. 286</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφεῖτ’</quote>) is ingenious, though the 2nd pers. of this aor. does not seem to occur elsewhere: but, before <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφαρμόσαι</quote>, the sound would be unpleasing. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στείχοις_ἤδη_624">: a courteous form: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 674"> <title>Ph.</title>674</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χωροῖς ἂν εἴσω</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σεσωσμένα_626">: all has been kept safe during the master's long absence: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 542">cp. 542</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκούρια</quote> (n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλὰ_δὴ_626">, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 523"> <title>O. T.</title>523</ref>,—I read </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτὴν_626"> (with A), not </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτήν_θ_626"> (with I), for these reasons. (1) It is clear that <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτὴν</quote> means merely <emph>eam</emph>, not <emph>ipsam.</emph> We cannot distinguish </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_προσδέγματα_626">, as meaning the welcome of Iolè <emph>along with the other captives</emph>, from a special welcome given to Iolè personally. (2) <quote xml:lang="grc"> αὐτὴν</quote>, although unemphatic, has a position which would usually give emphasis. But this is excused by the fact that the whole clause, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτὴν_φίλως_626">, depends on </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἶσθα_626">, being merely epexegetic of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_προσδέγματα_626_2"> (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷα ἐγένετο</quote> or the like). The chief stress falls on </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φίλως_626">. (3) If, however, we had </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτήν_θ_626_2">, then the sentence would lose that compact unity which justifies the place of the pronoun. And so <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτήν θ’</quote> would naturally seem to mean <emph> ipsam</emph>,—raising the objection noticed above (1). The insertion of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θ_626"> may easily have arisen from a notion that the second clause required a link with the first. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκπλαγῆναι_629">, of joy (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 24">cp. 24</ref>), as in <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 233"> Aesch. <title>Ch.</title>233</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χαρᾷ δὲ μὴ )κπλαγῇς φρένας</quote>.—Cp. <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 541">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 541</ref>KH. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστ’ ἐνδακρύειν γ’ ὄμμασιν χαρᾶς ὕπο</quote>. For the absence of <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote> here, cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 105">Soph. <title>Ph.</title>105</ref> <ref n="Soph. Phil. 985.">Soph. Ph., 985.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὼ_631">, ‘early,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign>, ‘too soon<*> ( <ref n="Aesch. PB 696">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title>696</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρῴ γε στενάζεις</quote>). <quote xml:lang="grc">πρω‐ί</quote> is prob. for <quote xml:lang="grc"> προv‐ι</quote> (Brugman, <title>Stud.</title> IV. 154).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_ἂν_631">. After a verb of fearing (whether the tense be primary or secondary) the potential opt. with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote> retains its ordinary sense. As <quote xml:lang="grc">γένοιτο <*></quote>=‘it might possibly happen,’ so <quote xml:lang="grc">δέδοικα μὴ γένοιτο ἄν</quote>=‘I fear that it might possibly happen.’ This is the mildest mode of expressing a fear, as <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote> with the fut. indic. is the most vivid. Hence it suits the misgiving, and the reserve, of Deianeira:—‘I fear that you might happen to be premature in speaking on that subject.’ Cp. <ref n="Xen. Anab. 6. 1. 28">Xen. <title>An.</title>6. 1. 28</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκεῖνο ἐννοῶ μὴ λίαν ἂν ταχὺ σωφρονισθείην</quote>: id. <title>De Vect.</title> 4. 41 <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δέ τινες αὖ φοβοῦνται μὴ ματαία <*>ν γένοιτο αὕτη ἡ κατασκευή</quote>. In <ref n="Lys. 13.51">Lys. or. 13 § 51</ref>, where the MSS. give <quote xml:lang="grc">δεδιότες μὴ καταλυθείησαν ὁ δῆμος</quote>, Markland conjectured <quote xml:lang="grc">καταλυθείη ἂν</quote>: and the mild phrase suits the irony of the passage. (Bekker reads <quote xml:lang="grc">καταλυθείη</quote> without <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote> .)</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_ἐμοῦ_631">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 95"> <title>Ant.</title>95</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν ἐξ ἐμο<*> δυσβουλίαν</quote> (n.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἀκεῖθεν_632">, acc. with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰδέναι_632">, explained by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_ποθούμεθα_632">. Here <quote xml:lang="grc"><*>ὰ ἐκεῖθεν</quote> is not merely <quote xml:lang="grc">τ<*> ἐκεῖ</quote> (315 n.): rather it means, ‘the feeling <emph>from</emph> (or <quote xml:lang="grc"><*></quote>) his side,’ as opp. to <quote xml:lang="grc">πόθον τὸν ἐξ ἐμοῦ.—<emph> <*>θούμεθα</emph> </quote>: for the plur., following </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέδοικα_632"> and </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμοῦ_632">, cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 734."> <title>Ant.</title>734.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="_ναύλοχα_639"> <quote xml:lang="grc"> κ.τ.λ.</quote> Wishing to call up a general picture of the whole region, the poet takes Thermopylae as his central point. From the cliffs of Oeta, which overhang that pass, his thought passes to the highlands (<quote xml:lang="grc"><*>ους Οἴτας</quote>) of Malis, and thence descends to the seaboard,—bringing us back to Thermopylae by the mention of the Amphictyons.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θερμὰ_λουτρὰ_639">: the wa<*>m springs at Thermopylae are situated between the Malian gulf on the N. (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ναλοχα_639">), and the cliffs of Oeta on the south (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πετραῖα_639">). They gush from the foot of the mountain,—that spur of Oeta which Livy and Strabo call Callidromus,—a little west of the point where the pass is narrowest. They were locally called <quote xml:lang="grc">χ<*>ροι</quote>, from the natural basins of rock; and near them was an altar of Heracles, for whose use Athena was said to have called them forth ( <ref n="Hdt. 7. 176">Her.7. 176</ref>: Peisander <emph>ap.</emph> schol. <title> Nub.</title> 1050).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ναύλοχα_639">: <ref n="Strab. 9.428">Strabo 9. 428</ref> speaks of a <quote xml:lang="grc">λιμὴν μέγας</quote> near Thermopylae. Here the word suggests the expected landing of Heracles.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάγους_Οἴτας_639">: the <quote xml:lang="grc">Τρηχινιαι πέτραι</quote> of <ref n="Hdt. 7. 198">Her.7. 198</ref>, spurs from the main range of Oeta, which enclose the plain of Malis on s. and w.: see Introd. to the <title>Philoctetes</title>, pp. ix. f.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παραναιετάοντες_639"> with acc.: cp. <ref n="Isoc. 4.162">Isocr. or. 4 § 162</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἕλληνες τὴν Ἀσίαν παροικοῦσιν</quote>, dwell <emph>along</emph> its coasts.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἵ_639"> (art.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τε_639"> , <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ναιετάονρες</quote>, following the direct vocative (633): cp. <ref n="Xen. Cyrop. 3. 3. 20">Xen. <title> Cyr.</title>3. 3. 20</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ Κῦρε καὶ οἱ ἄλλοι <*>ρσαι.—<emph>μέσσαν Μηλίδα … λί<*>ναν</emph> </quote>, the part of the gulf between the two extremities, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, the innermost part of the deep <*> which it forms. It is in this part that the shores of the gulf belonged to Malis,—viz., from the neighbourhood of Thermopylae to that of Anticyra.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρυσαλακάτου_κόρας_639">: merely another way of describing the same seaboard: they live <emph>by</emph> the <quote xml:lang="grc">λίμνη</quote>, and <emph>on</emph> the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκτή</quote>. Artemis, one of whose attributes was <quote xml:lang="grc">λιμενοσκόπος</quote>, was worshipped all along these eastern coasts, since the whole maritime life of Greece Proper looked mainly towards the Aegaean. Apoll. Rhod. 1. 571 (referring to the coasts about Pagasae and Iolcus in Magnesia), <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἄρτεμιν, ἣ κείνας σκοπιὰς ἁλὸς ἀμφιέπεσκεν</quote>. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 212">Cp. 212</ref> f.—The schol. wrongly takes <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκτὰν</quote> here as meaning that <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰγιαλός</quote> in the N.W. of Euboea which was called <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀρτεμίσιον</quote> ( <ref n="Hdt. 7. 176">Her.7. 176</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρυσαλακάτου_639">: this Homeric epithet of the goddess ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 16.183"> <title>Il.</title>16. 183</ref>) is rightly explained by Hesych. as=<quote xml:lang="grc">καλλίτοξος· ἠλακάτη γὰρ ὁ τοξικὸς κάλαμος</quote>. Cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄτρακτος</quote>=<quote xml:lang="grc">οἰστός</quote>. Artemis had nothing to do with a distaff. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔνθ_Ἑλλάνων_638"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> Meetings of the Amphictyonic Council were held at the town of Anthela, close to Thermopylae on the west,—where was the sacred precinct of <quote xml:lang="grc">Δημήτηρ Ἀμφικτυονίς</quote>, containing seats (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἕδραι</quote>) for the Council, and also a shrine of its legendary founder, Amphictyon ( <ref n="Hdt. 7. 200">Her.7. 200</ref>). Anachronisms were tolerated by Attic Tragedy, but this is hardly one, from the Greek point of view. The Thessalian and Dorian nucleus of what became the ‘Delphic’ Amphictyony was of immemorial age; Amphictyon was called the son of Deucalion; Acrisius of Argos figured in tradition as an early organiser of the league ( <ref n="Strab. 9.420">Strabo 9.420</ref> ).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἑλλάνων_638">, implying a Panhellenic character, reflects the regular phraseology of the poet's day. The Delphic Amphictyony never actually represented the whole even of Greece Proper; thus it never included the Acarnanians, Arcadians, or Eleans. Yet <ref n="Hdt. 7. 214">Her.7. 214</ref> speaks of <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ τῶν Ἑλλάνων Πυλαγόροι</quote>: an Argive inscr., older than 416 B.C., calls the Council <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ συνέδριον τῶν Ἑλλάνων</quote> (Lebas, <title>Revue Archéol.</title> XI. 577): and Hypereides <title> Epitaph.</title> c. 8. 25 describes those attending it as <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ Ἕλληνες ἅπαντες</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀγοραὶ_Πυλάτιδες_638">: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγορὰ πυλᾶτις</quote>= <quote xml:lang="grc">πυλαία</quote> (<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">σύνοδος</quote>), the name for a meeting of the Amphictyons, whether at Pylae or at Delphi,—one of several proofs that the former place was the League's older centre.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλέονται_638">, not ‘are called together,’ but, ‘are famous’: cp. <title>O.T.</title> 1451 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔνθα κλῄζεται</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">οὑμὸς Κιθαιρών</quote> (n.).—See Appendix. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀναρσίαν_641">, ‘unkindly’ (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 853">cp. 853</ref>), ‘harsh,’—referring to the use of the flute in wild or mournful music. Cp. Sextus Empiricus <title>Adv. Math.</title> 6. 22 <quote xml:lang="grc">διὸ καὶ τοῖς πενθοῦσιν αὐλοὶ μελῳδοῦσιν οἱ τὴν λύπην αὐτῶν ἐπικουφίζοντες</quote>. Lucian <title>De Luct.</title> § 19 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ πρὸς τὸν αὐλὸν αὕτη στερνοτυπία</quote>. Plato <title>Legg.</title> 800 E, <quote xml:lang="grc">Καρικῇ τινι μούσῃ προπέμπουσι τοὺς τελευτήσαντας</quote>, alludes to <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐλῳδοί</quote> (cp. Pollux 4. 75).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπάνεισιν_641">, ‘return,’ but also with the notion of sound rising.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θείας_μούσας_641">, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">καναχάν</quote>, a sound of music made to the gods (in thanksgiving): </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντίλυρον_641">, <emph>like</emph> that of the lyre: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰσόλυρον</quote>. This is simpler than to understand, ‘<emph>responsive</emph>’ to it (as if both instruments were used). The lyre, ‘common treasure of Apollo and the Muses’ ( <ref n="Pind. P. 1">Pind. <title>P.</title>1. 1</ref>), was peculiarly associated with joyous worship. Cp. <title>O.C.</title> 1222 n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀχῶν_641">, as a correction of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰάχων_641">, is hardly doubtful, since a resolution of the long syllable would impair the rhythm (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 635">cp. 635</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Οἴτας</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_κόρος_644">: objection has been taken to the double gen., but needlessly: the second gen. practically forms a single notion with <quote xml:lang="grc">κόρος</quote>,—‘Zeus's Alcmena-son,’=the son <emph>of</emph> Zeus <emph>by</emph> Alcmena. To a Greek ear the effect would be nearly the same as when the first gen. is replaced by an adj.; <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>, <ref n="Aesch. Supp. 313">Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title>313</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ Δῖος πόρτις...βοός</quote>, <ref n="Pind. O. 2"> Pind. <title>O.</title>2. 13</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"><*> Κρόνιε παῖ Ῥέας</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοῦται_645">, instead of the MS. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σεῦται_645">, is now generally read here. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1414"> <title>Ai.</title>1414</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σούσθω</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 25">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title>25</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σοῦν<*> ται</quote>: <title>Vesp.</title> 209 <quote xml:lang="grc">σοῦ</quote>. On the other hand, <quote xml:lang="grc"> σεῦται</quote> has no nearer <*>arallel than the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">στεῦται</quote>: which some, indeed, regard as syncopated (Curtius, <title>Gk. Verb</title>, ch. iv. I. C), but others as a genuine non-thematic form (Leaf, <ref n="Hom. Il. 18.191"> <title>Il.</title>18. 191</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάσας_645">, complete: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 436"> <title>Ai.</title>436</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶσαν εὔκλειαν.—<emph>ἀρετᾶς λάφυρα</emph> </quote> (like <quote xml:lang="grc"><*>θλα</quote>), ‘things won by’ <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρετή</quote> (root <quote xml:lang="grc">λαβ</quote> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὃν_εἴχομεν_647">: ‘whom we had absent’=‘whose absence we had to endure’: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃν ἐκτὸς εἴχομεν τῆς πόλεως</quote>. (Paley would join <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴχομεν ἀμμένουσαι</quote>, ‘whom we had been waiting for’: this seems inadmissible.)</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παντᾷ_647">, ‘utterly,’ goes with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπόπτολιν_647">: it implies a contrast between this long <emph>unbroken</emph> absence and his former expeditions. Cp. Eur. fr. 966 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκόλαστα πάντῃ</quote>. (The Doric form was written <quote xml:lang="grc">παντᾷ</quote>.) The adv. could mean also, ‘in all directions,’ but that sense is less fitting.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δυοκαιδεκάμηνον_χρόνον_647">: more exactly, fifteen months (44 f.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πελάγιον_647">: they imagined him as wandering on the sea, before or after his Lydian bondage: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 100">cp. 100</ref> ff., <ref n="Soph. Ant. 785"> <title>Ant.</title>785</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φοιτᾷς δ’ ὑπερπόντιος</quote> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἁ_δάμαρ_650">. The art. is here a pron., in apposition with <quote xml:lang="grc">δάμαρ</quote>, and serves to contrast the wife with the husband: the dat. <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ</quote> is equiv. to a possessive pron.: cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 13.616"> <title>Il.</title>13. 616</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λάκε δ’ ὀστέα, τὼ δέ οἱ ὄσσε κ.τ.λ.</quote> Note also the epic hiatus before </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἱ_650"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">vοἱ</quote>): cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 196"> <title>El.</title>196</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτε οἱ</quote> (Herm. , for <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτε σοι</quote>): where Dindorf cp. <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1147">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title>1147</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">περιεβάλοντό οἱ</quote>, and Cratinus <emph>ap.</emph> <ref n="Plut. Per. 24">Plut. <title>Per.</title>24</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἥραν τε οἱ</quote>. In <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 637">Eur. <title> Phoen.</title>637</ref>, however, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔθετό σοι</quote> (not <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ</quote>) is clearly right. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάλαιναν_651"> (for the MS. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάλαινα_651">) is required by the metre (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 659">cp. 659</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμείψας</quote> ).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὤλλυτο_651">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐτήκετο</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 140"> <title>El.</title>140</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἀπὸ τῶν μετρίων ἐπ’ ἀμήχανον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλγος ἀεὶ στενάχουσα διόλλυσαι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἄρης_653">. The first syll. might be long (as it is in some other lyric passages, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 139"> <title>Ant.</title>139</ref>, <ref n="Soph. El. 96"> <title>El.</title>96</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 254"> <title>Ai.</title>254</ref> <ref n="Soph. Aj. 614"> Ai., 614</ref>): but more probably it is short, and the first syll. of <quote xml:lang="grc">πειθοῦς</quote> in 661 is ‘irrational’ (see Anal.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἰστρηθεὶς_653">: the sudden rage of Ares symbolises the furious impulse which sent Heracles against Oechalia. Cp. <ref n="Eur. Ba. 119">Eur. <title>Bacch.</title>119</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰστρηθε<*>ς Διονύσῳ</quote>. The conject. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὖ_653"> (or </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἱ_653">) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στρωθεὶς_653"> would suggest the subsidence of a storm ( <ref n="Hdt. 7. 193">Her.7. 193</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ κῦμα ἔστρωτο</quote>). This is less suitable, when, as here, Ares is a personified deliverer.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξέλυσ_ἐπίπονονἁμέραν_653">, has ‘resolved,’ ‘cleared away,’ the day of sorrow. The notion of untying a knot passes into that of dissipating a trouble. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 35"> <title>O. T.</title>35</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξέλυσας...δασμὸν</quote> (n.). The image is more clearly developed in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 706"> <title>Ai.</title>706</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔλυσεν αἰνὸν ἄχος ἀπ’ ὀμμάτων Ἄρης</quote>.—For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπίπονον_ἁμέραν_653">, cp. <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 540">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title>540</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐχθρᾶς... ἡμέρας</quote>, <ref n="Eur. Hec. 364"> Eur. <title>Hec.</title>364</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λυπρὰν...ἡμέραν</quote>. In the <title>Athamas</title> Sophocles had <quote xml:lang="grc">λευκὴν ἡμέραν</quote> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν ἀγαθήν</quote> (Bekk. <title> Anecd.</title> p. 106. 33). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄχημα_ναὸς_656">: it may be doubted whether this means more than ‘the ship which conveys him.’ <title>I. T.</title> 410 <quote xml:lang="grc">νάϊον ὄχημα</quote> is similar. Cp. <title>Hipp. ma.</title> 295 D <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ ὀχήματα, τά τ<*> πεζὰ καὶ τὰ ἐν τῇ θαλάττῃ πλοῖα</quote>. But in <quote xml:lang="grc">ναυτίλων ὀχήματα</quote> ( <title>P.V.</title> 468) the metaphor of the chariot is distinct, as in <ref n="Eur. Med. 1122">Eur. <title>Med.</title>1122</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ναΐαν</quote> | ... <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπήνην.—<emph> πολύκωπον</emph> </quote> agrees with the compound phrase: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 794">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 794</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὶν_ἀνύσειε_657">: the opt. is due to the preceding opt., <quote xml:lang="grc">σταίη</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 961"> <title>Ph.</title>961</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄλοιο μήπω, πρὶν μάθοιμ’</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑστίαν_658">, altar: <ref n="Soph. OC 1495"> <title>O. C.</title>1495</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βούθυτον ἑστίαν</quote> (n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμείψας_658">, having quitted: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1262"> <title>Ph.</title>1262</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλῄζεται_θυτήρ_658">, is said by rumour to be sacrificing: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 237">cp. 237</ref>, 287: and for the verb, 1268. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πανίμερος_660">: <title>Anthol.</title> 2. 169 <quote xml:lang="grc">χαλκῷ κόσμον ἔδωκε πανίμερον</quote>. Here, however, the act. sense, ‘full of love’ (for Deianeira), is fitter than the pass., ‘alldesired.’ This is Mudge's correction of the MS. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πανάμερος_660">, which admits of no satisfactory explanation. It has been interpreted: (1) ‘travelling all day’: ‘Thence may he come, | A long day's journey without pause’ (Whitelaw). (2) ‘Today.’ One schol. paraphrases it, <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν σήμερον ἡμέραν</quote>: another, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν τῇ αὐτῇ ἡμέρᾳ</quote>. How this sense was extorted from the word, I do not know. (3) Taking it with what follows: ‘re-united to Deianeira for all his days to come.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τᾶς_παγχρίστῳ_661"> <quote xml:lang="grc"> κ.τ.λ.</quote> The corresponding verses of the strophe (653 f.) appear sound: <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν δ’ Ἄρης οἰστρηθεὶς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξέλυσ’ ἐπίπονον ἁμέραν</quote>. The traditional text here, <quote xml:lang="grc">τᾶς πειθοῦς παγχρίστῳ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">συγκραθεὶς ἐπὶ προφάσει θηρός</quote>, makes a long syllable answer to the second of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξέλυσ’</quote>, and to the second of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁμέραν</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παγχρίστῳ_661"> does not look like a gloss. Dindorf, who thinks it one, can only suggest that it arose from <quote xml:lang="grc">πάγχριστος</quote>, a gloss upon <quote xml:lang="grc">συγκραθείς</quote>. This is hardly probable. Sophocles is fond of intensive compounds with <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶς</quote> : <ref n="Soph. Trach. 505">cp. 505</ref>, 652: <ref n="Soph. El. 851"> <title>El.</title>851</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πανσύρτῳ, παμμήνῳ</quote> : <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1282"> <title>Ant.</title>1282</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">παμμήτωρ</quote>: fr. 347 <quote xml:lang="grc">πάγξενος</quote>, etc. As an epithet for the robe, <quote xml:lang="grc">παγχρίστῳ</quote>, ‘thoroughly anointed,’ is suitable. (Cp. Deianeira's words in 580.) But it cannot be a subst., as the schol. on 663 would make it (<quote xml:lang="grc">λείπει τῷ πέπλῳ</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπὶ_θηρός_661">= <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπίπονον ἁμέραν</quote> in 654. Besides being unmetrical, <quote xml:lang="grc">θηρός</quote> requires an unexampled sense for <quote xml:lang="grc">προφάσει</quote>, viz., ‘precept.’ Hence Dindorf reads <quote xml:lang="grc">προφάνσει</quote> (a word which is not extant), and in 654 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιπόνων ἁμερᾶν</quote>. For <quote xml:lang="grc">θηρός</quote>, M. Haupt conjectured </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φάρους_661"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ΘΗΡΟΣ, ΦΑΡΟΣ</quote>): for the <quote xml:lang="grc">α^</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 916">cp. 916</ref>. The words <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ προφάσει φάρους</quote> then mean, ‘on the pretext of the robe.’ The robe was the <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόφασις</quote> for using the love-charm. <quote xml:lang="grc">παγχρίστῳ</quote> can be retained in the dat., agreeing with <quote xml:lang="grc">προφάσει</quote> (cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 794"> <title>Ant.</title>794</ref> n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συγκραθεὶς_661">, cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1311"> <title>Ant.</title>1311</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δειλαίᾳ δὲ συγκέκραμαι δύᾳ</quote> (‘steeped in’ it): <ref n="Soph. Aj. 895"> <title>Ai.</title>895</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴκτῳ τῷδε συγκεκραμένην</quote> (‘steeped in this lament,’—<foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign>, in the anguish of it). <title>Plut.</title> 853 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὕτω πολυφόρῳ συγκέκραμαι δαίμονι</quote>. Thus the primary sense of <emph>mixture</emph>, or <emph>fusion</emph>, led to that of intimate union. If we accept <quote xml:lang="grc">φάρους</quote>, then <quote xml:lang="grc">συγκραθεὶς</quote> would be most naturally taken in the sense which <quote xml:lang="grc">πανίμερος</quote>, just before it, could suggest,— ‘made one in heart’ with Deianeira.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συγκραθεὶς_661_2"> and </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξέλυς_661"> in 654. Hermann read </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξείλυς_661"> (‘unrolled,’ meaning, ‘disentangled,’ ‘brought to a smooth close’). But <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξέλυσ’</quote> seems genuine. It is possible, however, that <quote xml:lang="grc">συγκραθεὶς</quote> was an explanation of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συντακεὶς_661"> (suggested by Paley), which would give the same sense. Cp. <ref n="Aesch. Supp. 1029"> Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title>1029</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γαμέτας</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">συντηχθεὶς αὔραις ἀδόλοις</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">γενναίας ἀλόχῳ ψυχᾶς</quote>, ‘husband <emph>made one with</emph> wife in the sincere spirit of a loyal soul.’ So <quote xml:lang="grc">συντακείς τινι</quote>, of close attachment, <ref n="Plat. Sym. 183E">Plat. <title>Symp.</title> 183E</ref> <ref n="Plat. Sym. 192">Plat. Symp., 192</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="περαιτέρω_663">, absol., implying <quote xml:lang="grc">περαιτέρω τοῦ δέοντος</quote> ( <ref n="Plat. Gorg. 484C">Plat. <title>Gorg.</title> 484C</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Δῃάνειρα_Οἰνέως_665">: the form of address implies earnest sympathy with the terror which they perceive in her: cp. the <quote xml:lang="grc">παῖ Μενοικέως</quote> of the Chorus in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1098."> <title>Ant.</title>1098.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀθυμῶ_φανήσομαι_666">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 176">cp. 176</ref> n.: <ref n="Eur. Andr. 61">Eur. <title> Andr.</title>61</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φόβῳ μέν, εἴ τις δεσποτῶν αἰσθήσεται.—<emph>ἀπ’ ἐλπίδος καλῆς</emph> </quote>, as the outcome of it: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 695">Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 695</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κάκιστ’ ἀπ’ ἔργων εὐκλεεστάτων φθίνει</quote>. (Not, ‘contrary to it.’) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_δή_668"> in a question, as <ref n="Soph. Phil. 900"> <title>Ph.</title>900</ref>: more often, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ δή που</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OT 1472"> <title>O. T.</title>1472</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 381"> <title>Ant.</title>381</ref>), or <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ δή ποτε</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Trach. 876"> <title>Tr.</title>876</ref>, <ref n="Soph. El. 1108"> <title>El.</title>1108</ref>).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τι_668"> is acc. of respect, and the gen. depends on it: ‘Surely thou art not anxious as to aught <emph>belonging to</emph>, <emph>concerning</emph> thy gift?’ This is simpler than to take the gen. as depending on </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐλπίδος_668">, and </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τι_668_2"> as adv.: ‘Not, perchance, (the hope concerning) thy gift?’—For the plur. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δωρημάτων_668">, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 494">cp. 494</ref>: for the dat. <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἡρακλεῖ</quote>, depending on the verbal notion, <ref n="Plat. Euthyph. 15A">Plat. <title>Euthyph.</title> 15A</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ παρ’ ἡμῶν δῶρα τοῖς θεοῖς</quote>: and <ref n="Soph. OC 1026">Soph. <title>O. C.</title>1026</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὥστε_παραινέσαι_669">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε οὔποτ’ ἂν παραινέσαιμι</quote>. The words </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προθυμίαν_ἔργου_669"> form a single notion, the sense being the same as if </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄδηλον_669"> were <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀδήλου</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 794"> <title>Ant.</title>794</ref> n. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄδηλον</quote> here means, ‘untried by previous test’: cp. her own words in 590 f. So in <ref n="Thuc. 5. 103">Thuc.5. 103</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφανεῖς ἐλπίδες</quote> (as opp. to <quote xml:lang="grc">φανεραί</quote>) are those which rest on no solid ground. Cp. id. 1. 78 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ἀδήλῳ κινδυνεύεται</quote>.—For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λαβεῖν_669">, ‘conceive,’ cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1078">Soph. <title>Ph.</title>1078</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φρόνησιν...λάβοι</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 345"> Soph. <title>Ai.</title>345</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰδῶ...λάβοι</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_διδακτόν_671">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 64">cp. 64</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἷον_φράσω_672"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> The choice here seems to lie between two courses.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑμῖν_672">, but with a comma after it, and to supply from it <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑμᾶς</quote> as subject to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μαθεῖν_672">: ‘such that, if I tell it to you, ye will learn an unexpected marvel.’ Cp. <title>Plut.</title> 349 <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖός τις</quote> (<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">χρησμός</quote>)<emph>;</emph>—<quote xml:lang="grc"> οἷος</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢν μὲν κατορθώσωμεν, εὖ πράττειν ἀεί</quote>: where the subject to the inf. is <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμᾶς</quote>, supplied from the preceding verb.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑμᾶς_672"> (subject to <quote xml:lang="grc">μαθεῖν</quote>) instead of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑμῖν_672_2">, which may well have arisen from </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φράσω_672">. This course is recommended by the lucid construction, and by the better rhythm. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1295"> <title>O. T.</title>1295</ref> f. <quote xml:lang="grc">θέαμα δ’ εἰσόψει τάχα</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιοῦτον οἷον καὶ στυγοῦντ’ ἐποικτίσαι</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μαθεῖν_672_2"> with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θαῦμ_ἀνέλπιστον_672"> only, suppose an ellipse of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔσται</quote> (or an equiv. word): ‘Such that, if I tell it, (it will be) an unexpected marvel for you to hear.’ But such an ellipse is extremely harsh. Wunder's </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἂν_φράσαι_672"> (with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμέ</quote> understood as subject) is possible, but loses the emphasis prepared by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢν φράσω</quote>, and gives an unpleasing rhythm. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_πέπλον_674">. The word <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνδυτήρ</quote> (found only here) expresses that the <quote xml:lang="grc">πέπλος</quote> was not for ordinary use, but was one which Heracles was to assume for the solemn rite. Thus it is equivalent to ‘stately,’ or ‘festal.’ Cp. <ref n="Eur. Tro. 258">Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 258</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνδυτῶν στεφέων ἱεροὺς στολμούς</quote>, ‘the sacred apparel of wreaths with which <emph>thou hast been invested</emph>’ (because they mark Cassandra's prophetic character).—The <quote xml:lang="grc">χιτών</quote> belonged to the class of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνδύματα</quote>, garments ‘put on’ (and not merely wrapped round the body); while the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">πέπλος</quote> must be classed with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιβλήματα</quote> (cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 5.734"> <title>Il.</title>5. 734</ref> ff.). But we cannot well regard <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνδυτήρ</quote> here as a qualifying epithet, meaning that <emph>this</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">πέπλος</quote> was of the <quote xml:lang="grc">χιτών</quote> class (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 602">cp. 602</ref> n.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀργὴς_πόκος_675">: I follow Wunder and Lobeck in thus amending </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀργῆτ_πόκῳ_675">. The latter reading, if sound, presents a dilemma. (1) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀργῆτ_675"> =<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀργῆτι</quote>. But it is now generally recognised that the epic license of eliding datival <quote xml:lang="grc">ι</quote> was foreign to Attic Tragedy: the supposed examples all admit of easy remedy: see <title>O. C.</title>, Appendix on v. 1436. (2) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀργῆτα_675">, agreeing with <quote xml:lang="grc">πέπλον</quote>: ‘white, glistering.’ This is possible; for, though </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_πέπλον_675"> precedes, a second epithet might follow: cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. OT 1199."> <title>O. T.</title>1199.</ref>But the effect of such an epithet, added at the end of the clause, would here be very weak. And if <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀργῆτα</quote> is taken as the proleptic predicate (‘anointed so as to make it shine,’ Wecklein), this does not suit the sense. On the other hand, the connection of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀργής</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">πόκος</quote> is confirmed by <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 45">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title>45</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀργῆτα μαλλόν</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀργὴς_πόκος_675_2"> into </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀργῆτ_675_2"> (meant for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀργῆτι</quote>)...</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόκῳ_675"> would easily have been induced by the preceding </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ᾧ_675">.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐέρου_675">, the Attic form, acc. to the schol. on <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 121">Ar. <title> Av.</title>121</ref>(where metre requires <quote xml:lang="grc">εὔερον</quote> ). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_οὐδενὸς_676"> (neut.) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶνἔνδον_676">, by nothing in the house (such as fire, or a corrosive substance). The conj. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_ἐκτός_676"> is ingenious, but seems unnecessary.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φθίνει_676">, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">φθίνον</quote>: see n. on <ref n="Soph. OT 1134."> <title>O. T.</title>1134.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ψῇ_678"> is not elsewhere intrans. (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 698">cp. 698</ref>), and hence has been suspected here: but <ref n="Soph. Trach. 128">cp. 128</ref>, n. on <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ...κυκλοῦσιν.—<emph>κατ’ ἄκρας σπιλάδος</emph> </quote>, down from the top of a flat stone, or slab, in the <quote xml:lang="grc"> αὐλή</quote> of the house. Schol.: <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς οὖν ἐπὶ λίθου θεμένη αὐτὸ τοῦτό φησι</quote>. On coming into the court-yard from the room in which she had secretly anointed the robe, she had carelessly thrown the tuft of wool down upon this stone.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατ_σπόδιον_678">, ‘utterly pulverised,’ and </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σπιλάδος_678"> arose, when the letters after </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σπ_678"> had been partly effaced, through the wish to find a subst. which could agree with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄκρας</quote>. Cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">συιδ. κατ’ ἄκρας· δἰ ὅλου, παντελῶς</quote>: and <ref n="Soph. OC 1242.">Soph. <title>O. C.</title>1242.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκτενῶ_679">: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1040"> <title>Ai.</title>1040</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ τεῖνε μακράν</quote> : <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 201">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title>201</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοσοῦτο μῆκος ἔκτεινον λόγουν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γὰρ_680">, prefatory.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧν_680">, for <quote xml:lang="grc">οὒς</quote>, by attract. to the subsequent </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεσμῶν_680">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 20.87">Dem. or. 20 § 87</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧν ἔργῳ πεποίηκεν ἕκαστος..., τούτων ἐκ λόγου κρίσις γίγνεται.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ὧν ἔργῳ πεποίηκεν ἕκαστος..., τούτων ἐκ λόγου
							κρίσις γίγνεται.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_Κένταυρος_680">, as in <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1162">1162</ref>: <quote xml:lang="grc">θήρ</quote> may here be rendered by an adj., but is strictly a subst., the general term in apposition with the special.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πονῶν_πλευρὰν_680">: Hense quotes Ennius <ref n="Enn. Ann. 601"> <title>Ann.</title>601</ref> <emph>tum laterali' dolor</emph>, <emph>certissimu' nuntiu' mortis.</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πικρᾷ_680">, cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1024"> <title>Ai.</title>1024</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πικροῦ</quote> | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">κνώδοντος</quote>: <ref n="Hom. Il. 5.278"> <title>Il.</title>5. 278</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πικρὸς ὀϊστός.—<emph> γλωχῖνι</emph> </quote>. This sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">γλωχίν</quote> (which means ‘the end of a strap’ in <ref n="Hom. Il. 24.274"> <title>Il.</title>24. 274</ref>) does not occur elsewhere in poetry of the classical age, but is attested by <ref n="Hom. Il. 5.393"> <title>Il.</title>5. 393</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀϊστῷ τριγλώχινι</quote> (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 11">cp. 11</ref>. 507), a three-barbed arrow: which shows that the schol. here is right in identifying <quote xml:lang="grc">γλωχίν</quote> with the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄγκος</quote> (<emph>uncus</emph>), ‘barb’ of an arrow ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 4.151"> <title>Il.</title>4. 151</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προὐδιδάξατο_680">, ‘had taught me <emph> beforehand</emph>’: distinguish the other sense of this compound, ‘to teach gradually’ ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 163"> <title>Ai.</title>163</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1015"> <title>Ph.</title>1015</ref>). The midd. voice here suits the fact of the Centaur's hidden design. In <title>Plut.</title> 687, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ γὰρ ἱερεὺς αὐτοῦ με προὐδιδάξατο</quote>, the verb might be causal (‘had me instructed beforehand’). Sometimes, however, the midd. <quote xml:lang="grc">διδάσκομαι</quote> hardly differs from <quote xml:lang="grc">διδάσκω</quote>, unless by emphasising the te cher's effort (cp. <ref n="Pind. O. 8">Pind. <title>O.</title>8. 59</ref>: <title> Nub.</title> 783). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεσμῶν_οὐδέν_682">. The regular plur. was <quote xml:lang="grc">θεσμοί</quote>, though in fr. 90 we find <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γάρ τι θεσμὰ τοῖσιν ἀστίταις πρέπει</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 24"> <title>Ph.</title>24</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀπίλοιπα τῶν λόγων</quote> (n.).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐσῳζόμην_682">, remembered: <ref n="Plat. Rep. 455B">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 455B</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἂ ἔμαθε, σῴζεται</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 318"> Soph. <title>O. T.</title>318</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χαλκῆς_δέλτου_683">. This image is peculiarly forcible in connection with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεσμῶν_683">,—the word which expresses her religious reverence for the Centaur's precepts. Laws, rituals, oracles, etc., were often engraved upon bronze tables. An example is afforded by the ‘Eugubinae Tabulae’ preserved at Gubbio, which concern sacred rites. <ref n="Diod. 12.26">Diod. Sic. 12. 26</ref> (referring to the Lawsof the XII. Tables) <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς δώδεκα χαλκοῦς πίνακας χαράξαντες οἱ ὕπατοι</quote>. <ref n="Plut. Alex. 17">Plut. <title>Alex.</title>17</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δέλτον...χαλκῆν, τύπους ἔχουσαν ἀρχαίων γραμμάτων</quote> (an oracle): where, for <quote xml:lang="grc">τύπους</quote> (graven characters), cp. Pollux 8. 128 <quote xml:lang="grc">δέλτοις χαλκαῖς ἦσαν πάλαι ἐντετυπωμένοι οἱ νόμοι οἱ περὶ τῶν ἱερῶν καὶ τῶν πατρίων</quote> . <cit> <bibl n="Cic. Phil. 1.26">Cic. <title>Phil.</title> 1. § 26</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">Quod ita erit gestum, id lex erit? et in aes incidi iubebitis...?</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
Quod ita erit gestum, id lex erit? et in aes
							incidi iubebitis...?

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καίμοι_684"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> Those who agree with Wunder in rejecting this verse hold that </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σῴζειν_684"> depends on </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προὐδιδάξατο_684"> (681), and urge that </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἄδρωντοιαῦτα_684"> in 688 condemns </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοιαῦτ_ἔδρων_684"> here. The question must be considered in the light of the whole context. She begins by saying that the Centaur's dying precepts remained in her memory as if graven on bronze (680—683). That passage is much more impressive if a full stop follows 683. She then says, ‘Now <emph>these</emph> were his precepts,’— verse 684 marking her anxiety to explain that she had simply obeyed him to the letter. The reiteration, <quote xml:lang="grc">κἄδρων τοιαῦτα</quote>, in 688, is unnecessary; but, in her actual state of mind, it is full of dramatic truth. The scholiast read this verse. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄπυρον_685"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> : <ref n="Soph. Trach. 606">cp. 606</ref> f.—For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄθικτον_685"> with gen., <ref n="Soph. Trach. 23">cp. 23</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἕως_νιν_687">. The solecism in the MSS., <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕως ἂν</quote>, is like their <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὶν ἂν θάνοι</quote> in 2 f., and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡνίκ’ ἃν...ἀπείη</quote> in 164 f.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="που_687">: neither Nessus (575) nor she herself had ever thought of the charm being used on any one except Heracles. But, as she shrank from naming Eurystheus (35), so, at this moment, she shrinks from naming the man whom she loves. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχρισα_μὲν_689"> corresponds with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἄθηκα_689"> in 691, not with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴσωδ_689"> in 693. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1"> <title>Ai.</title>1</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀεὶ μέν</quote>,...3 <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ νῦν</quote>: <ref n="Thuc. 2. 65">Thuc.2. 65</ref>§ 12 <quote xml:lang="grc">δέκα μὲν ἔτη ἀντεῖχον...καὶ οὐ πρότερον ἐνέδοσαν</quote>. So <quote xml:lang="grc">μέν...τε</quote>, below<ref n="Thuc. 1012.">Thuc., 1012.</ref>— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατοἶκον_689">, in the house (and not outside the gates): </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_δόμοις_689">, in the <quote xml:lang="grc"> γυναικωνῖτις</quote>, and not in the open <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐλή</quote>. For this limited sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">δόμοι</quote>, cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 6.303">Hom. <title>Od.</title>6. 303</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ὁπότ’ ἄν σε δόμοι κεκύθωσι καὶ αὐλή</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κτησίου_690">, ‘belonging to the household,’—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, to a flock kept near the house, to supply food, and victims for sacrifice. <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζεὺς Κτήσιος</quote> was more especially the tutelar god of <emph>household</emph> property; hence domestic slaves stand <quote xml:lang="grc">κτησίου βωμοῦ πέλας</quote> ( <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1038">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1038</ref>). The epithet here does not, however, directly allude to him, as if meaning, ‘devoted to the <quote xml:lang="grc">Κτήσιος</quote> .’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλαμπὲς_ἡλίου_691">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 677"> <title>O. C.</title>677</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνήνεμον</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">χειμώνων</quote> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ζυγάστρῳ_692">: for the dat., <ref n="Soph. Trach. 564">cp. 564</ref>. <ref n="Xen. Cyrop. 7. 3. 1"> Xen. <title>Cyr.</title>7. 3. 1</ref> uses <quote xml:lang="grc"> ζύγαστρα</quote> of boxes in which treasure is packed up for removal. The word meant simply, ‘something fitted together,’ ‘compacted’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">ζυγ‐</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοίλῳ_692">: the addition of this adj. is sometimes, esp. in poetry, merely picturesque: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1593"> <title>O. C.</title>1593</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1165"> <title>Ai.</title>1165</ref>: <ref n="Eur. Ion 19">Eur. <title>Ion</title> 19</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κοίλης...ἀντίπηγος</quote>: <ref n="Hdt. 4. 2">Her.4. 2</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγγήϊα κοῖλα.—<emph>ὥσπερ εἴδετε</emph> </quote>. They saw the <quote xml:lang="grc">ζύγαστρον</quote>, but not the act of placing the robe in it: this (as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλαμπὲς ἡλίου</quote> shows) had been done in the house. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴσω_ἀποστείχουσα_693">, returning into the house (632). The words <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥσπερ εἴδετε</quote> indicate the previous dialogue in front of it (531—632).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φάτιν_693"> is boldly used here, but appears sound. The harshness is modified by the context: <foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign>, the antithesis between <emph>uttering</emph> and <emph> comprehending</emph> has led the poet to strengthen </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄφραστον_693"> by a noun specially suited to it, as he strengthens </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀξύμβλητον_693"> by the addition of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνθρώπῳ_μαθεῖν_693">. In <ref n="Soph. El. 1466"> <title>El.</title>1466</ref>, on the other hand, <quote xml:lang="grc">δέδορκα φάσμα</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc"> πεπτωκός</quote> (the corpse), the subst. is accommodated to the <emph> verb.</emph>—For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀξύμβλητον_693_2"> cp. Aelian <title>Nat. An.</title> 6. 60 <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ἀτεκμάρτων τε καὶ ἀσυμβλήτων</quote> . <ref n="Eur. Med. 675">Eur. <title>Med.</title>675</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σοφώτερ’ ἢ κατ’ ἄνδρα συμβαλεῖν ἔπη</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κάταγμα_695"> properly meant the ball of wool on the distaff, from which the thread is <emph>drawn down</emph> (<quote xml:lang="grc">κατάγεται</quote>, <emph>deducitur</emph>) by the spinner. ( <title>Polit.</title> 282 E: <title>Soph.</title>226B. ) Here it is merely a synonym for <quote xml:lang="grc">μαλλός</quote> (690). In <ref n="Aristoph. Lys. 583">Ar. <title>Lys.</title>583</ref> ff. <quote xml:lang="grc">κάταγμα</quote> is the thread itself.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προὔχριον_695">, <emph>before</emph> putting it into the casket (692). Lucian <ref n="Lucian Alex. 21"> <title>Alex.</title>21</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σιάλῳ τὴν σφραγῖδα προχρίσας.—<emph>φλόγα</emph> </quote> comes first, since <emph>heat</emph> is the main point; then, to avoid ambiguity, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκτῖν_ἡλιῶτιν_695"> is added. Seneca <title>Herc. Oet.</title> 725 translates these words: <emph>medios in ignes solis et claram facem.</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐς_695">. His words are merely: ‘Suspectus ob constructionem; <emph>nisi legas</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">φλόγα ἀκτῖνος ἡλιῶτιν</quote>’: II. <title>Adv.</title>38. But the prep. is often thus repeated when a second noun stands in apposition with the first, serving to explain it: <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>, <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 6.14">Lysias or. 6 § 14</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ἐν Ἀρείῳ πάγῳ, ἐν τῷ σεμνοτάτῳ...δικαστηρίῳ</quote> </cit>: <title> Laches</title> 183 C <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ τούτων οἱ ὀνομαστοὶ γίγνονται, ἐκ τῶν ἐπιτηδευσάντων ἕκαστα</quote>: <ref n="Plat. Prot. 358"> <title>Prot.</title>358</ref>B <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱ ἐπὶ τούτου πράξεις ἅπασαι, ἐπὶ τοῦ ἀλύπως ζῆν</quote>. </gloss>

data.quote:
καὶ ἐν Ἀρείῳ πάγῳ, ἐν τῷ
							σεμνοτάτῳ...δικαστηρίῳ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ῥεῖ_698"> is equiv. to <quote xml:lang="grc">ψῇ</quote> in 678. The wool shrivels away; and presently there is nothing to be seen but a powder, like saw-dust, on the ground. For <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥεῖν</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 259"> <title>O. C.</title>259</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατέψηκται_698">: the perf. of instant result: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 76"> <title>Ph.</title>76</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄλωλα</quote> (n.). <cit> <bibl n="Sen. Her. O. 736">Seneca <title>Herc. Oet.</title> 736</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">Dumque ipsa miror, causa mirandi perit.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote: Dumque ipsa miror, causa mirandi perit.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μορφῇ_699">, dat. of respect: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάλιστ_εἰκαστὸν_699">, lit., ‘most nearly comparable’; but, instead of a simple dat., <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐκβρώμασι</quote>, we have a clause with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὥστε_699"> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote>), as if (<foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">οὕτως ἔχον</quote> had preceded.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρίονος_ἐκβρώματ_699"> = <quote xml:lang="grc"> πρίσματα</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">παραπρίσματα</quote>): Schneidewin cp. Nicander <ref n="Nic. Ther. 52"> <title>Ther.</title>52</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ἡ πρίονεσσι τομαίη</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">κέδρος, πουλυόδουσι καταψηχθεῖσα γενείοις.—<emph>ἐν τομῇ ξύλου</emph> </quote> =<quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτε ξύλον τέμνεται</quote>: for the form of the phrase, cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 24"> <title>Ant.</title>24</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προπετές_701">, where it was thrown down: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρριμμένον</quote> (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 695">cp. 695</ref>) <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ προπεσὸν χαμαί.—<emph>ὅθεν</emph> </quote>, by attract. to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ γῆς</quote>, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπου</quote>: cp. <ref n="Thuc. 1. 89">Thuc.1. 89</ref>§ 3 <quote xml:lang="grc">διεκομίζοντο εὐθὺς ὅθεν</quote> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐντεῦθεν ὅποι</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπεξέθεντο παῖδας καὶ γυναῖκας</quote>. In <ref n="Soph. OC 1226">Soph. <title>O. C.</title>1226</ref> the doubtful <quote xml:lang="grc">κεῖθεν</quote> is not similar.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προὔκειτ_ἀναζέουσι_701">: past tense combined with historic pres. ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 254">Soph. <title> Ant.</title>254</ref> n.).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θρομβώδεις_ἀφροί_701">, foam, thickened into clots (<quote xml:lang="grc">θρόμβοι, τρέφω</quote>): hence the plur., which Seneca imitates, <title>Herc. Oet.</title> 737 <title>Quin ipsa tellus spumeos motus agit.</title> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πίονος_ἀμπέλου_703">: the rich juice of the blue fruit (obtained) from the vine: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, the <quote xml:lang="grc">γλεῦκος</quote>, or ‘must,’ newly pressed from the ripe grapes; which foams when poured on the ground, since it has not yet passed through the stage of fermentation. Schol.: <quote xml:lang="grc">μάλιστα δὲ ὁ νέος οἶνος, θερμὸς ὤν, εἰ πέσοι χαμαί, ἀφρίζει</quote>. The simile is suggested chiefly by the foam, but partly also by the purple tinge which the poison gives to the blood.</gloss>
lemma: πίονος ποτοῦ γλ. ὀπώρας ἀπὸ Β. ἀμπέλου

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χυθέντος_703"> should not be taken with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Β_ἀμπέλου_703">, since the grapes have already been gathered.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀπώρας_703"> as=‘fruit,’ cp. fr. 234. 7 <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶσα τέμνεται βλαστουμένη</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">καλῶς ὀπώρα, κἀνακίρναται ποτόν</quote>.—For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Βακχίας_703">, <emph>ib.</emph> 2 <quote xml:lang="grc">Βάκχειος βότρυς</quote>: <title>Geo.</title> 2. 5 <emph>tibi pampineo gravidus autumno</emph> | <title>Floret ager</title>, <emph>spumat plenis vindemia labris.</emph> For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πίονος_703"> cp. Tibull. 1. 1. 10 <emph>pleno pinguia musta lacu.</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποῖ_πέσω_705">: she knows not to what counsels she can have recourse: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, she can think of no remedy. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 170"> <title>O. C.</title>170</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖ τις φροντίδος ἔλθῃ</quote> <emph>;</emph> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁρῶ_ἐξειργασμένην_706">. The constr. with the acc. can be used whenever the speaker looks at his own case from without. But the examples are of two kinds. (1) Most often there is a contrast of persons; <ref n="Soph. El. 65"> <title>El.</title>65</ref>: Andoc. or. 1 § 30 <quote xml:lang="grc">φημὶ δεῖν ἐκείνους μὲν ἀπολέσθαι..., ἐμὲ δὲ σῴζεσθαι</quote>. (2) Sometimes, as here, there is no such contrast, and the effect is merely to give a certain objectivity: <ref n="Soph. El. 470">Soph. <title>El.</title>470</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πικρὰν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">δοκῶ με πεῖραν τήνδε τολμήσεὶν ἔτι</quote>: so <ref n="Soph. Aj. 606">Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 606</ref> f.: <ref n="Plat. Rep. 400B">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 400B</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶμαι δέ με ἀκηκοέναι</quote>. This is esp. fitting when the speaker is in an evil plight, and means that he can see himself as others see him: so <ref n="Xen. Anab. 5. 6">Xen. <title>An.</title>5. 6</ref>§ 20 <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν μὲν ὁρῶμεν ἡμᾶς ἀπόρους ὄντας κ.τ.λ.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόθεν_707">, ‘from what motive?’: </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντὶ_τοῦ_707">, ‘in gratitude for what?’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἂν_παρέσχ_707">: (if he had shown kindness,— as he did <emph>not</emph>), why would he have done so?</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἧς_ὕπερ_707">, <emph>on account of</emph> whom,=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἧς ἕνεκα</quote>, or <quote xml:lang="grc">δἰ ἥν</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 932"> <title>Ant.</title>932</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βραδυτῆτος ὕπερ</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀποφθίσαι_709">: <quote xml:lang="grc">φθίσω</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔφθισα</quote> have <quote xml:lang="grc">ι—</quote> in epic poetry, but <quote xml:lang="grc">ι^</quote> in Attic: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1043">cp. 1043</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OT 202"> <title>O. T.</title>202</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1027."> <title>Ai.</title>1027.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρκεῖ_711">, ‘<emph>anails</emph>’: not, ‘<emph> suffices.</emph>’ In the latter sense, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρκεῖ</quote> is usu. impers., but in the former, personal, as it is here (<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ μάθησις</quote>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄρνυμαι_711">: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 903"> <title>Ant.</title>903</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 838."> <title>Ph.</title>838.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴ_μὴ_712">: 586 n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ψευσθήσομαι_γνώμης_712">: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1382"> <title>Ai.</title>1382</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καί μ’ ἔψευσας ἐλπίδος πολύ</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄτρακτον_714">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 290"> <title>Ph.</title>290</ref> n.—</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεὸν_Χείρωνα_714">. Cheiron, as the son of the god Cronus by the nymph Philyra, was of a different origin from the other Centaurs, the descendants of Ixion and Nephelè. So Pindar distinguishes him as <quote xml:lang="grc">Κρονίδαν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">Κένταυρον</quote> (<title>N.</title> 3. 47), <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐρανίδα γόνον εὐρυμέδοντα Κρόνου</quote> (<title>P.</title> 3. 4): and Apollonius Rhodius as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλα μὲν ἵππῳ</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλα θεῷ ἀτάλαντον</quote> (2. 1240). He was still more separated from the rest of the Centaur tribe by his just and gentle character ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 11.832"> <title>Il.</title>11. 832</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δικαιότατος Κενταύρων</quote>). Hence Greek art, after the in vention of the hippo-centaur (564 n.), continued to portray Cheiron under the more humane type of the andro-centaur. This is his form on Greek vases, down at least to 400 B.C. Later art neglected this distinction. (See Colvin S. in <title>Journ. Hellen. Stud.</title>, vol. 1. pp. 133— 137.)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πημήναντα_714">. Other Centaurs, routed by Heracles in Arcadia, fled to Cheiron near Cape Malea; and the hero, in shooting at them, accidentally wounded his friend. Cheiron could not be healed; and, being a god, could not die. At last Zeus allowed him to exchange fates, the immortal for the mortal, with Prometheus; and so he found rest ( <ref n="Apollod. 2.5.4">Apollod.2. 5. 4</ref>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χὧνπερ_714"> seems a certain correction of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χὥσπερ_714">. The latter has been explained thus:—(1) ‘Even as it may touch, (so surely) does it destroy.’ This is possible, but somewhat forced; certainly less probable than <quote xml:lang="grc">χὧνπερ</quote>. (2) ‘<title>If only</title> it touch them.’ This view—that <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥσπερ <*>ν</quote>=<emph> dummodo</emph>—rests on passages in which <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote> should be corrected to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕως</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OC 1361"> <title>O. C.</title>1361</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1330"> <title>Ph.</title>1330</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1117"> <title>Ai.</title>1117</ref>).—Few will defend <quote xml:lang="grc">χὥσπερ</quote> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ὅσπερ</quote> (‘whoever touches the arrow’); or accept, with Wunder, <quote xml:lang="grc">χὥσαπερ</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_διελθὼν_716">, having passed out from the wounds of Nessus.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰὸς_αἵματος_716">, a poison consisting (or contained) in blood, because the poison from the arrow had become mixed with the blood; and it was in the form of blood (572 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφίθρεπτον α<*>μα</quote>) that the poison had been applied. For the ‘defining’ gen., cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 682"> <title>El.</title>682</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσχημ’ ἀγῶνος</quote> ,=<quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσχ. ἀγωνιστικόν</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τόνδε_716">, Heracles. <quote xml:lang="grc"> τοῦδε—ὅδε—τόνδε</quote>: this repetition of the pron., in different relations, has been thought strange. Yet cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 948"> <title>O. T.</title>948</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ νῦν ὅδε</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς τῆς τύχης ὄλωλεν, οὐδὲ τοῦδ’ ὕπο</quote>: where <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅδε</quote> is Polybus, and <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦδ’</quote>, Oedipus. She reasons from past to present:—‘<emph>the same</emph> poison, coming from <emph>this</emph> source, will kill <emph>this</emph> man.’ The reiterated pronoun really marks the stress of the inductive argument.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_τοῦδε_716"> as=‘from this arrow’: then <quote xml:lang="grc">σφαγῶν</quote> must go either with <quote xml:lang="grc">διελθών</quote>, ‘having come through (from) the wounds’; or with <quote xml:lang="grc">αἵματος</quote>, ‘poison contained in the blood of the wounds.’ But the point is that the poison, though it comes to Heracles from the wound of Nessus, and not (as to its former victims) <emph>directly</emph> from the arrow, is still the same. And, since </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅδε_716"> expresses this, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦδ_716">, if it referred to the arrow, would be superfluous.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δόξῃ_ἐμῇ_716">: cp. <ref n="Plat. Phaedo 68"> <title>Phaed.</title>68B</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἄσμενος εἶσιν αὐτόσε<emph>;</emph> οἴεσθαί γε χρή</quote>. For <quote xml:lang="grc">γοῦν</quote>, cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 45"> <title>Ant.</title>45</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν γοῦν ἐμόν</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σφαλήσεται_719">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 297">cp. 297</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">σφαλῇ. —<emph>ταὐτῇ σὺν ὁρμῇ</emph> </quote>, lit., ‘borne along by <emph>the same impetus.</emph>’ The <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁρμή</quote>, impetus, is that of the fate which brings Heracles low: she means, ‘at the same moment I, too, shall be <emph>swept out of life.</emph>’ Cp. <ref n="Xen. Ages. 2. 29">Xen. <title>Ages.</title>2. 29</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνόμιζε γὰρ τῇ αὐτῇ ὁρμῇ τῷ μὲν Αἰγυπτίῳ χάριν ἀποδώσειν..., τοὺς δ’ ἐν τῇ Ἀσίᾳ Ἕλληνας πάλιν ἐλευθερώσειν, τῷ δὲ Πέρσῃ δίκην ἐπιθήσειν</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘by the same <emph>effort.</emph>’ The phrase was perhaps a familiar one, to which the poet has here given a new turn.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺν_719">, cp. Theocr. 25. 251 <quote xml:lang="grc">τηλοῦ δὲ μιῇ πήδησε σὺν ὁρμῇ</quote> (‘at one bound’: though <quote xml:lang="grc">μιᾷ ὁρμῇ</quote> usu.=‘by a common impulse,’ <ref n="Xen. Anab. 3. 2. 9">Xen. <title>An.</title>3. 2. 9</ref> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προτιμᾷ_722"> with inf.; cp. <ref n="Hdt. 3. 21">Her.3. 21</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">προτιμῶν πολλοῦ ἐμοὶ ξεῖνος γενέσθαι. —<emph>μὴ</emph> </quote> (‘generic’) with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακὴ_722">, rather than with the inf.: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 725">cp. 725</ref>: fr. 154. 2 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχοιμ’ ἀν αὐτὸ μὴ κακῶς ἀπεικάσαι</quote>. The adj. is not here a mere synonym for <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσγενής</quote> (as in <ref n="Soph. OT 1063"> <title>O. T.</title>1063</ref>), but rather blends the ideas of birth and character. For the sentiment, cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 989"> <title>El.</title>989</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ζῆν αἰσχρὸν αἰσχρῶς τοῖς καλῶς πεφυκόσιν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 479."> <title>Ai.</title>479.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔργα_δείν_723">: the ‘deeds’ are the dire results which Deianeira has so frankly presaged (718 <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς οὐκ ὀλεῖ καὶ τόνδε</quote> <emph>;</emph>). Hence <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινά</quote> is not too strong.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐλπίδ_723"> has properly a neutral sense (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 125">cp. 125</ref> n.): ‘we must not <emph>decide</emph> our <emph>foreboding</emph> before the issue,’—<foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign>, assume that the worst will certainly happen: hence we may more freely render, ‘doom our hope.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_β_725">: for the generic <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 494"> <title>Ant.</title>494</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν μηδὲν ὀρθῶς ἐν σκότῳ τεχνωμένων.—<emph>ἐλπίς, ἥτις καὶ θρ. τι προξενεῖ</emph> </quote>, a foreboding which <emph>so much as</emph> lends courage. The measures which she has taken do not allow her to think of a good result as even possible. For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προξενεῖ_725"> cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1483"> <title>O. T.</title>1483</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμφὶ_727"> with dat., ‘in the case of,’ <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1354"> <title>Ph.</title>1354</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_ἑκουσίας_727">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 395">cp. 395</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 467"> <title>Ph.</title>467</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ )ξ ἀπόπτου</quote>. The adverbial phrase would properly precede <quote xml:lang="grc"> σφαλεῖσι</quote>: but cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 659"> <title>Ant.</title>659</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέπειρα_727"> (related to <quote xml:lang="grc"> πέπων</quote> as <quote xml:lang="grc">πίειρα</quote> to <quote xml:lang="grc">πίων</quote>) is attested by the grammarians, but extant only in Anacreon fr. 87. Hippocrates <ref n="Hp. Acut. 390"> <title>Acut.</title>390</ref> has <quote xml:lang="grc">πέπειρος νόσος</quote>, and <ref n="Aristoph. Eccl. 896">Ar. <title>Eccl.</title> 896</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ταῖς πεπείροις</quote> (Rav. <quote xml:lang="grc">πεπείραις</quote>).—For the sentiment, cp. fr. 599 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄκων δ’ ἁμαρτὼν οὔτις ἀνθρώπων κακός</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοιαῦτα_δ_729">: <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote> introduces an objection, as in <ref n="Soph. OT 379"> <title>O. T.</title>379</ref>(n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἴκοι_729">, fig.: cp. <ref n="Eur. Tro. 647"> Eur. <title>Tro.</title>647</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν δὲ νοῦν διδάσκαλον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴκοθεν ἔχουσα χρηστόν</quote>. So Lat. <foreign xml:lang="lat">domi</foreign> (<ref n="Pl. Mil. 2.2"> Plaut. <title>Miles</title> 194</ref>, with Tyrrell's note). In <ref n="Hdt. 7. 152">Her.7. 152</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ οἰκήια κακά</quote> (‘the troubles that they have <emph>at home</emph>’) is similarly figurative. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἂν_ἁρμόζοι_731">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 18.42">Dem. or. 18 § 42</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγους οὓς...ἁρμόσει λέγειν.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_λόγον_731">: the further discourse which might be expected: cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. OC 36"> <title>O. C.</title>36</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ πλείον’ ἱστορεῖν.—<emph>εἰ μή τι</emph> </quote>: the schol. seems right in taking <quote xml:lang="grc">τι</quote> as object to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέξεις_731">, rather than as an adv. (‘perchance,’ 712).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μαστὴρ_πατρὸς_731">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 455"> <title>O. C.</title>455</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοῦ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μαστῆρα</quote>. The Chorus may be supposed to know of this mission through having heard the last words of the <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόλογος</quote> as they were entering (95). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἂν_εἱλόμην_734">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1239"> <title>Ph.</title>1239</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἂν...ἐβουλόμην</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σε_734"> is subject to each of the three infinitives which follow. It is placed as if the speaker mentally supplied a word such as <quote xml:lang="grc">παθεῖν</quote>. But </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἓν_734"> is really an accus. in apposition with the whole sentence, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σε_εἶναι_734">, etc. The peculiar order has been due to the metrical difficulty of inserting </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σε_734_2"> anywhere in vv. 735—737.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_ἓν_734">. Photius and Suidas quote the proverb, <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν τριῶν κακῶν ἕν</quote>, illustrating it from Polyzelus (a poet of the later Old Comedy), <quote xml:lang="grc">τριῶν κακῶν ἕν γ’ οὖν ἑλέσθ’ αὐτῷ τι πᾶσ’ ἀνάγκη</quote>, and Menander, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἓν γάρ τι τούτων τῶν τριῶν ἔχοι κακῶν</quote>. Cp. schol. <ref n="Pind. O. 1">Pind. <title>O.</title>1. 97</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τρία λέγεται κοινῶς καὶ τὰ πρὸς τὸν θάνατον συνεργοῦντα, ξίφος, ἀγχόνη, κρημνός· Σοφοκλῆς</quote> (fr. 822)<*> <quote xml:lang="grc">λύσω γάρ, εἰ καὶ τῶν τριῶν ἓν οἴσομαι</quote>. A like prov. was <quote xml:lang="grc">τρία Θηραμένους</quote>, alluding to three alternative penalties proposed by him.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κεκλῆσθαι_734">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 149">cp. 149</ref>: <ref n="Soph. El. 366"> <title>El.</title>366</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντων ἀρίστου παῖδα κεκλῆσθαι.—<emph>ἀμείψασθαι</emph> </quote>, get in exchange: <ref n="Aesch. Seven 304">Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 304</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖον δ’ ἀμείψεσθε γαίας πέδον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τᾶσδ’ ἄρειον<emph>;</emph> <emph>ποθεν</emph> </quote> is wholly vague: the schol.'s <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ θεοῦ τινος</quote> is too definite. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_στυγού_738"> </gloss>
lemma: τί δ’ ἔστιν … πρός γ’ ἐμοῦ στυγού‐

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μενον_738">; ‘What is there, <emph>on my part</emph>, that excites hatred?’ The phrase is peculiar (since the words could mean, ‘what is hated <emph>by</emph> me?’); but, in speaking, the sense would be made clear by a very slight pause after the words </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρός_ἐμοῦ_738">, which are naturally emphatic (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 516"> <title>O. T.</title>516</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_ἐμὸν_739">: for this use of <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote> to mark a second relationship, see on <ref n="Soph. OC 1275"> <title>O. C.</title>1275</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ σπέρματ’ ἀνδρὸς τοῦδ’, ἐμαὶ δ’ ὁμαίμονες</quote>. Two modes of expression are blended here, since either </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέ_739">, or </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέγω_739">, should properly be absent: but the redundancy suits the speaker's vehemence. —For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέγω_739_2">, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 9">cp. 9</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξήνεγκας_741"> here denotes a <emph>startling</emph> utterance (like <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖόν σε ἔπος φύγεν ἕρκος ὀδόντων</quote> <emph> ;</emph>). <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκφέρω</quote>, with ref. to speech, usu. = ‘to <emph>disclose</emph>’ a secret ( <ref n="Hdt. 3. 71">Her.3. 71</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δν_τε_742"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> This <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγος</quote> is one which must needs ‘be <emph>fulfilled</emph>,’ —<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘befound to correspond with a fact,’ ‘prove <emph>true</emph>,’—since the thing has already happened. Cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 1.388"> <title>Il.</title>1. 388</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠπείλησεν μῦθον, ὃ δὴ τετελεσμένος ἐστίν</quote>.—Most edd. read </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_οὐ_742">, instead of the MS. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_742">, here: but see 90 n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_φανθὲν_742">, that which has come before men's eyes,—come to pass: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 647"> <title>Ai.</title>647</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">χρόνος</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">φύει τ’ ἄδηλα καὶ φανέντα κρύπτεται</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 457"> <title>Ant.</title>457</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κοὐδεὶς οἶδεν ἐξ ὅτου )φάνη</quote>. The word is fitting at a moment when his mind is full of the terrible sight which he has just seen (746 f.). For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_γὰρ_742"> at the end of the v., <ref n="Soph. Trach. 92">cp. 92</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίς_ἂν_742">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 21">cp. 21</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀγένητον_742">, ‘not brought to pass’ (st. <quote xml:lang="grc">γεν‐, γίγνομαι</quote>): cp. Horace's <emph>infectum</emph> (<title>C.</title> 3. 29. 47). But <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγέννητος</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">γεννα‐</quote>)=‘not begotten’ (61). Agathon fr. 5 (<emph>ap.</emph> <title>Eth. N.</title> 6. 2) <quote xml:lang="grc">μόνου γὰρ αὐτοῦ καὶ θεὸς στερίσκεται</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγένητα ποιεῖν ἅσσ’ ἂν ᾖ πεπραγμένα</quote>. <ref n="Pind.
