O. 2">Pind. <title> O.</title>2. 17</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν δὲ πεπραγμένων</quote> | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποίητον οὐδ’ ἂν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">χρόνος ὁ πάντων πατὴρ δύναιτο θέμεν ἔργων τέλος</quote>. Simonides fr. 69 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ γὰρ γεγενημένον οὐκέτ’ ἄρεκτον ἔσται</quote>. Theognis 583 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ τὰ μὲν προβέβηκεν ἀμήχανόν ἐστι γενέσθαι</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀργά</quote>. <title>H. N.</title> 2. 7 <title>Deus nullum habet in praeterita ius praeterquam oblivionis.</title> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνθρώπων_742"> added to the interrog. <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 238"> <title>El.</title>238</ref> <ref n="Soph. El. 930."> El., 930.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄζηλον_742">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 284">cp. 284</ref>.—Nauck rejects v. 745, because Deianeira ought to have one verse only (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 738">cp. 738</ref>, 741, 748). But it is unsafe to assume such a rigorous ‘symmetry’: and the larger utterance is natural here. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_ὄμμασιν_746">: 241 n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατὰ_γλῶσσαν_746">, ‘as a matter of report,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">παρ’ ἄλλων</quote>. (The similar phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπὸ γλώσσης</quote> usu. means ‘orally,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, not in writing: <ref n="Hdt. 1. 123">Her.1. 123</ref>, <ref n="Thuc. 7. 10.">Thuc.7. 10.</ref>) Cp. <title>I. T.</title> 901 <quote xml:lang="grc">τάδ’ εἶδον αὐτή, κοὐ κλύουσ’ ἀπαγγελῶ</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμπελάζεις_παρίστασαι_748">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, find him, and then stand near to him in his sufferings. The vivid historic pres. suits the tension of mind with which the question is asked: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 113"> <title>O. T.</title>113</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">συμπίπτει</quote>, and <emph>ib.</emph> 1025 <quote xml:lang="grc">δίδως</quote>.—These are Deianeira's last words on the scene. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_σε_749">. Hyllus abhors her, as the murderess of his father; there is no touch of pity in his mood. His words mean that the recital will be painful to <emph>him</emph>, and also that he shrinks from speaking to her at such length. (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 815">Cp. 815</ref> f.) According to Greek usage, it was not <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσιον</quote> for the relatives of a slain person to hold any avoidable intercourse with the slayer. (Cp. <ref n="Lys. 12.24">Lys. or. 12 § 24</ref>: <ref n="Isaeus 9.20">Isae. or. 9 § 20</ref>.) This feeling appears in <ref n="Soph. Trach. 815">815 f.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅθ_εἷρπε_750">: <ref target="cl237">see on 237</ref> f. For the absence of a prefatory <quote xml:lang="grc">γάρ</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 555">cp. 555</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 900">900</ref> . <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεί</quote> is more usual than <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτε</quote> in opening such a <quote xml:lang="grc"> ῥῆσις</quote>. The schol.'s remark here, <quote xml:lang="grc">καινοπρεπὴς ἡ φράσις</quote>, may refer partly to that, and partly to the abruptness of <quote xml:lang="grc">εἷρπε</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τροπαῖα_751">, the arms taken from the enemy (cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 143"> <title>Ant.</title>143</ref> n.), <quote xml:lang="grc">σκῦλα</quote> : </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκροθίνια_751">, the booty, <quote xml:lang="grc">λεία</quote>.—This accent for the subst. <quote xml:lang="grc">τροπαῖον</quote> is attested by the grammarians, and preserved by L in 1102: though, acc. to rule, the subst. would be <quote xml:lang="grc">τρόπαιον</quote>, and the adj. <quote xml:lang="grc"> τροπαῖος</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1459"> <title>Ph.</title>1459</ref> n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκτή_ἔστιν_752">, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦλθεν εἰς ἀκτήν κ.τ.λ.</quote>: cp. <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 1198">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title>1198</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεὶ δ’ ἔρημον χῶρον εἰσεβάλλομεν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκτή τις ἔστι κ.τ.λ.</quote> (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴδομεν ἀκτήν τινα</quote>): <title>I.T.</title> 260 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεὶ τὸν ἐσρέοντα διὰ Συμπληγάδων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">βοῦς ὑλοφορβοὺς πόντον εἰσεβάλλομεν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦν τις διαρρὼξ κυμάτων πολλῷ σάλῳ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">κοιλωπὸς ἀγμός</quote>: <emph>ib.</emph> 1449 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅταν δ’ Ἀθήνας τὰς θεοδμήτους μόλῃς</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">χῶρός τις ἔστιν</quote>. The epic fashion is to <emph> begin</emph> with a description of the place ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 2.811"> <title>Il.</title>2. 811</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔστι δέ τις...κολώνη</quote>, <ref n="Hom. Od. 3.293"> <title>Od.</title>3. 293</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔστι δέ τις πέτρη</quote>)—as above<ref n="Hom. Od. 3.237"> Od., 237.</ref>The mixed construction here comes from a wish to make the narrative at once consecutive and graphic.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Κήναιον_752">: 237 n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατρῴῳ_752">: 288 n.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βωμοὺςὁρίζει_752"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: 238 n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τεμενίαν_φυλλάδα_752">=<quote xml:lang="grc">τέμενος πολύφυλλον</quote> (schol.).— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄσμενος_πόθῳ_752">, glad, through my longing (causal dat.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολυθύτους_σφαγὰς_756">: the second part of the compound adj. is akin in sense to the subst.; cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">χοαῖσι τρισπόνδοισι</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 431"> <title>Ant.</title>431</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">νεοτόμοισι πλήγμασιν</quote> (<emph>ib.</emph> 1283). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κῆρυξ_οἰκεῖος_757">: ‘his own’ herald, returning from Trachis, was not one at whose hand he could have expected such a gift. It is needless to give </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἰκεῖος_757"> a special sense, as (<emph>a</emph>) ‘bred up in his household,’ or (<emph>b</emph>) ‘sent on a private mission,’ as Paley takes it. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προὐξεφίεσο_759">: for the imperf., cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1055"> <title>O. T.</title>1055</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφιέμεσθα</quote> : <ref n="Soph. OC 1605"> <title>O. C.</title>1605</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφίετο</quote>: so often <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκέλευον</quote>. This compound does not occur elsewhere, but is paralleled by <quote xml:lang="grc">προεξανίσταμαι, προεξαποστέλλω</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταυροκτονεῖ_760"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> The offering consisted of a hundred victims altogether, including bulls, sheep, and goats (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συμμιγῆ_βοσκήματα_760">),—a ‘hecatomb’ in the general sense ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 1.315"> <title>Il.</title>1. 315</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑκατόμβας</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ταύρων ἠδ’ αἰγῶν</quote>). The sacrifice was to begin with the slaughter of twelve oxen, animals specially selected from the spoil. Such a <quote xml:lang="grc">θυσία δώδεκα ἱερείων</quote> was called <quote xml:lang="grc">δωδεκῇς</quote> (Eustath. p. 1386. 48, etc.). In <ref n="Hom. Il. 6.93"> <title>Il.</title>6. 93</ref>Helenus directs that an offering of twelve cows shall be promised to Athena.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐντελεῖς_760">=<quote xml:lang="grc">τελείους</quote>, with no physical blemish or taint: Lucian <title>Sacrif.</title> 12 <quote xml:lang="grc">στεφανώσαντες τὸ ζῷον, καὶ πολύ γε πρότερον ἐξετάσαντες εἰ ἐντελὲς εἴη, ἵνα μηδὲ τῶν ἀχρήστων τι κατασφάττωσι, προσάγουσι τῷ βωμῷ</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βοῦς_760"> is probably masc. here (as in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 62"> <title>Ai.</title>62</ref>), though in Homer it is usu. fem., unless the contrary is specified. The word </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταυροκτονεῖ_760_2"> is not decisive, since it might express merely the antithesis between oxen and other victims (cp. such phrases as <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰνοχοεῖν νέκταρ</quote>). Seneca <title>Herc. Oet.</title> 784 <title>Ut stetit ad aras omne votivum pecus</title>, | <title>Totumque tauris gemuit auratis nemus.</title> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσῆγε_760">, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ βωμῷ</quote>: cp. Pollux 1. 29 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ δὲ προσακτέα φύματα, ἱερεῖα ἄρτια ἄτομα ὁλόκληρα ὑγιῆ ἄπηρα</quote>. The imperf. means strictly, ‘<emph>proceeded to</emph> bring’ (after donning the robe). The hist. pres. <quote xml:lang="grc"> ταυροκτονεῖ</quote>, too, represents an imperf., not an aor. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἵλεῳ_763">. While <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱλαρός</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ι^</quote>) properly refers to aspect (‘beaming,’ ‘cheerful,’ as opp. to <quote xml:lang="grc">σκυθρωπός</quote>), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵλεως</quote> denotes a placid or gracious mood of the mind: cp. Ephippus (a poet of the Middle Comedy), <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἐμπολή</quote> fr. 1 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐποίησέ θ’ ἱλαρόν, εὐθέως δ’ ἀφεῖλε πᾶν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοῦ τὸ λυποῦν, κἀπέδειξεν ἵλεων</quote> (where Meineke rashly changes <quote xml:lang="grc">θ’ ἱλαρόν</quote> to <quote xml:lang="grc">θαλερόν</quote> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κόσμῳ_στολῇ_764">=<quote xml:lang="grc">κοσμίᾳ στολῇ</quote>. So in <ref n="Eur. Med. 1165">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 1165</ref>Glauce puts on the deadly gifts of Medea, <quote xml:lang="grc">δώροις ὑπερχαίρουσα.—<emph>κατηύχετο</emph> </quote>: as Chryses <quote xml:lang="grc">μεγάλ’ εὔχετο χεῖρας ἀνασχών</quote>, at the sacrifice described in <ref n="Hom. Il. 1.447"> <title>Il.</title>1. 447</ref>—466; and as Nestor <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὰ...Ἀθήνῃ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">εὔχετ’</quote> on the like occasion in <ref n="Hom. Od. 3.430"> <title>Od.</title>3. 430</ref>—463. Those two passages explain the ritual imagined here (vv. 756—766). (1) Heracles, having put on the robe, brings his victims to the altar. (2) The next act would be to sprinkle the <quote xml:lang="grc">χέρνιψ</quote> on the sacrificers; who would then take barleymeal (<quote xml:lang="grc">οὐλοχύται</quote>) in the hands from the basket, <quote xml:lang="grc">κανοῦν</quote>. (3) Then Heracles offers his prayer to Zeus. (4) The actual immolation follows; the barley-meal is sprinkled on the heads of victims; hair, cut from one or more of them, is thrown on the altar-fire; and they are slain. (5) The <quote xml:lang="grc">μηρία</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1011"> <title>Ant.</title>1011</ref>), doubly wrapped in fat, are burnt on the altar. This act was in progress, when the agonies of Heracles began.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατηύχετο_764"> to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατήρχετο_764">. The latter, if used in its larger sense, would refer to no. 2 of the stages described above (cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 3.445"> <title>Od.</title>3. 445</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χέρνιβά τ’ οὐλοχύτας τε κατήρχετο</quote>); if in its narrower sense, to no. 4 (cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 959">Ar. <title>Av.</title>959</ref>, <ref n="Hdt. 2. 45">Her.2. 45</ref>). The larger sense would be best here.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατηύχετο_764_2"> is an immeasurably finer reading. Heracles, standing before the altar as he prays with uplifted hands to Zeus, is thrown into stronger relief than if imagined merely in the brief act denoted by <quote xml:lang="grc">κατήρχετο</quote>. The <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵλεως φρήν</quote>, too, would be more apparent in the <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐχή</quote> than in the rite. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σεμνῶν_φλὸξ_765">, ‘the flame <emph>of</emph> the solemn <emph>rites</emph>,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, the flame from the sacrifice on the altar. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄργια</quote> could not literally mean <quote xml:lang="grc">θύματα</quote> (the word by which the schol. explains it); but the sense is the same. In <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1013"> <title>Ant.</title>1013</ref>, too, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀργίων</quote> are ‘rites,’ not ‘victims.’ The flame is called <quote xml:lang="grc"> αἱματηρά</quote>, because the burning flesh was so. It is needless to take <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπό</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀργίων</quote> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πιείρας_δρυός_765">: the wood of the pine (<quote xml:lang="grc">πεύκη</quote>) is resinous (<quote xml:lang="grc"> ῥητινώδης</quote>),—a quality conducive to the bright flame which was prized as an omen ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1007"> <title>Ant.</title>1007</ref> n.). The original meaning of <quote xml:lang="grc">δρῦς</quote> was simply ‘tree’ (schol. <ref n="Hom. Il. 11.86"> <title>Il.</title>11. 86</ref>; Curt. <title>Etym.</title> § 275). In 1168 and 1195 it means ‘oak.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἱδρὼς_767">, showing that the irritant action of the poison has begun.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρτίκολλος_τέκτονος_767">, closely glued to his flesh as if by a craftsman: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ὑπὸ τέκτονος καλῶς συγκεκολλημένος</quote>. The gen. here is not so definitely equivalent to a gen. with <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπό</quote> as it is in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 807"> <title>Ai.</title>807</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φωτὸς ἠπατημένη</quote>, or <ref n="Eur. Orest. 497">Eur. <title>Or.</title>497</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πληγεὶς θυγατρός</quote>. Eur. Itexpresses a similar notion, but in a vaguer form: ‘like something <emph>from</emph> (the hand of) a <quote xml:lang="grc">τέκτων</quote>’: ‘like (a work) of his.’ Some supply <quote xml:lang="grc">κολλήσαντος</quote>: but this seems difficult, and is not warranted by such examples as <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἐμοῦ μόνης πέλας</quote> (<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔσης</quote>, <ref n="Soph. OC 83">Soph. <title>O. C.</title>83</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἅπαν_ἄρθρον_767">: the robe clings so tight as to show the contour of the body: cp. the Homeric phrase, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐντυπὰς ἐν χλαίνῃ κεκαλυμμένος</quote>, explained to mean, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε διὰ τοῦ ἱματίου τοῦ σώματος τὸν τύπον φαίνεσθαι</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 24.163"> <title>Il.</title>24. 163</ref>, with Leaf's n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀστέων_767"> might be joined with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντίσπαστος_767"> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντισπῶν τὰ ὀστᾶ</quote>), but is more simply taken with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀδαγμός_767">. The latter word means ‘a biting pain’ (rt <quote xml:lang="grc">δακ</quote>), esp. an ‘itching.’ Photius p. 7 21: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀδαγμὸς ὁ ὀδαξησμός, ὅπερ ἐστὶ κνησμός· οὕτω Σοφοκλῆς</quote>. The forms <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀδάξω</quote> (act. and midd.) and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀδαξέω</quote> (do.) seem both to have been in use; the former was perhaps chiefly Ionic. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐχίδνης_ὣς_771">: Hyllus does not know what Deianeira had applied to the robe; but, as the venom was that of the hydra, his conjecture comes near the truth.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐδαίνυτο_771">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1088">cp. 1088</ref>: Eur. fr. 790 <quote xml:lang="grc">φαγέδαιν’ ἀεί μου σάρκα θοινᾶται ποδός</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βόησε_772">: <quote xml:lang="grc">βοᾶν τινα</quote> usu.=‘to shout to (or for) one’: <ref n="Pind. P. 6">Pind. <title>P.</title>6. 36</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βόασε παῖδα ὅν</quote> (called his son to his aid): <ref n="Xen. Cyrop. 7. 2. 5">Xen. <title>Cyr.</title>7. 2. 5</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Κῦρον ἐβόα</quote>. Here it means, ‘shouted for him, (asking),’=<quote xml:lang="grc">βοῶν ἠρώτησε.—<emph>ἐνέγκοι</emph> </quote>: for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤνεγκον</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἤνεγκα</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 522.">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 522.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηχαναῖς_772"> in a bad sense, as <ref n="Soph. Aj. 181."> <title>Ai.</title>181.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔλεξεν_776">, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνεγκεῖν.—<emph>ὥσπερ ἦν ἐσταλμένον</emph> </quote>, as it had been sent; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, without tampering with it by the way. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 622">Cp. 622</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τόδ’ ἄγγος ὡς ἔχει δεῖξαι φέρων</quote>. —Not, ‘as had been prescribed’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεσταλμένον</quote>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλευμόνων_778">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1054">cp. 1054</ref>: for the form, 567 n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄρθρον_λυγίζεται_779">, ‘where the joint is supple,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, at the ankle (<quote xml:lang="grc">σφυρόν</quote>), where the ball of the joint (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀστράγαλος</quote>) plays in its socket. This mode of definition is Homeric: cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 5.305"> <title>Il.</title>5. 305</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔνθα τε μηρὸς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰσχίῳ ἐνστρέφεται</quote> (turns in the hip-joint). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_πόντου_780">, ‘in’ (‘rising from’) the sea; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, an isolated rock, not part of a promontory. This is better than to take the words with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφίκλυστον</quote>, ‘washed on all sides <emph>by</emph> the sea’: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ πόντου</quote> would then be too weak.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κόμης_ἐκραίνει_781">, he <emph>causes</emph> the brain <emph>to ooze</emph> out through his victim's hair,—at the moment when the skull is cloven. Cp. Eur. fr. 388 <quote xml:lang="grc">κάρα τε γάρ σου συγχεῶ κόμαις ὁμοῦ</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥανῶ δὲ πεδόσ’ ἐγκέφαλον</quote>: id. <ref n="Eur. Cycl. 402">Eur. <title>Cycl.</title> 402</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν δ’ αὖ, τένοντος ἁρπάσας ἄκρου ποδός</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">παίων πρὸς ὀξὺν στόνυχα πετραίου λίθου</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγκέφαλον ἐξέρρανε</quote> (where the verbal resemblance to this passage is remarkable).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μυελὸν_781">: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγκέφαλος</quote>, the proper word for ‘brain,’ is merely an adj. with which <quote xml:lang="grc">μυελός</quote> is understood. In <ref n="Plat. Tim. 73C">Plat. <title>Tim.</title> 73C</ref>, D the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγκέφαλος</quote> is described as that part of the <quote xml:lang="grc">μυελός</quote> which is to receive <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ θεῖον σπέρμα</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διασπαρέντος_781">: the skull, cleft from its centre (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέσου_781">), is scattered in fragments. Other views are:—(1) The word means merely ‘cloven,’ and has been substituted for a word like <quote xml:lang="grc">διαρραγέντος</quote> on account of the following <quote xml:lang="grc">αἵματος</quote>. This seems impossible. (2) <quote xml:lang="grc">διαρραγέντος</quote>, or the like (see cr. n.), should be read. But <quote xml:lang="grc"> διασπαρέντος</quote>, rightly understood, suits both nouns. Athenaeus (66 A) quotes vv. 781 f., as cited by Apollodorus (<emph>c.</emph> 140 B.C.), without variation from our text. The reading, then, is at least a very old one. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνηυφήμησεν_οἰμωγῇ_783">: the verb can bear this sense even without a defining addition: <ref n="Eur. Orest. 1335">Eur. <title> Or.</title>1335</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνευφημεῖ δόμος</quote> (of wailing): <ref n="Plat. Phaedo 60">Plat. <title>Phaedo</title>60 A</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνηυφήμησε</quote> (Xanthippè). The notion is that of a cry which expresses religious awe. Cp. Matthew Arnold, <title> Mycerinus</title>: ‘And one loud cry of grief and of amaze | Broke from his sorrowing people.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰύζων_787">: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰύ</quote>, a shrill sound, expressed astonishment or anguish: Aeëtes uses it in his ‘inarticulate’ vexation (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἴυξεν δ’ ἀφωνήτῳ περ ἔμπας ἄχει</quote>: <ref n="Pind. P. 4">Pind. <title>P.</title>4. 237</ref> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Λοκρῶν_πρῶνες_788">: the heights of the Cnemis range, on the Locrian coast, just s. of Cape Cenaeum in Euboea. At this point the strait is less than three miles in breadth. The </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τ_788"> after </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Λοκρῶν_788"> (cr. n.) seems genuine.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄκραι_788">, the cliffs which jut into the sea at or near Cenaeum. This fem. form is usual when, as here, the ref. is to promontories. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄκρα_788">, the reading of Diogenes Laertius (cr. n.),—meant doubtless as neut. pl.,—would be rather ‘mountain heights.’ The neut. <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἄκρον</quote> is rarely said of a foreland (as in <ref n="Hom. Od. 3.278"> <title>Od.</title>3. 278</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπεῖπε_ῥίπτων_789">: the cause of weariness is regularly expressed by a <emph>pres.</emph> part.: <ref n="Aristoph. Lys. 778">Ar. <title>Lys.</title>778</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μή νυν ἀπείπωμεν ταλαιπωρούμεναι</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 769e">Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 769 E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἄν ποτε λέγων ἀπείποι</quote> </cit> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δυσπάρευνον_791">: for the adj., compounded with a subst. akin in sense to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέκτρον_791">, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 518"> <title>O. T.</title>518</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βίου ... μακραίωνος</quote>, and above<ref n="Soph. OT 756"> O. T., 756</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐνδατούμενος_791">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 205"> <title>O. T.</title>205</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_γάμον_791">: the gen. is most simply explained by the fact that <quote xml:lang="grc">γάμος</quote> implies alliance: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 77">Soph. <title>Ph.</title>77</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κῆδος Ἀδράστου λαβών</quote>. Others understand, ‘the marriage granted to him by Oeneus,’ so that the constr, would be as with <quote xml:lang="grc">δῶρον</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσέδρου_794">, <quote xml:lang="grc"> περικεχυμένης</quote>: the cloud hung around him.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λιγνύος_794">, the smoky flame from the resinous <quote xml:lang="grc">πεύκη</quote> (766),—just as in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1126"> <title>Ant.</title>1126</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">στέροψ λιγνύς</quote> refers to pine-torches.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διάστροφον_794">, with the frenzy of pain: cp. <ref n="Eur. Her. 932">Eur. <title>H. F.</title>932</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν στροφαῖσιν ὀμμάτων ἐφθαρμένος.—<emph>στρατῷ</emph> </quote>: though the crowd must have been partly composed of warriors (259), <quote xml:lang="grc">στρατός</quote> has here the general sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">λεώς</quote> (783): cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 8">Soph. <title> Ant.</title>8</ref> n.: <ref n="Soph. El. 749.">Soph. <title>El.</title> 749.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συνθανεῖν_798">: cp. <ref n="Aesch. Supp. 1006"> Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title>1006</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅδιστος γάρ τοι θάνατος</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">συνθνῄσκειν θνῄσκουσι φίλοις</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔξω_799">, as the context shows, means ‘out of Euboea’: for </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἆρον_799">, cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 545"> <title>Ai.</title>545</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἶρ’ αὐτόν, αἶρε δεῦρο.—<emph>μάλιστα μέν</emph> </quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 327"> <title>Ant.</title>327</ref> n. His thought is: ‘Take me at once to the wilds of Mount Oeta, and leave me to perish alone; or, if you shrink from that, at least take me out of the island.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="με_θὲς_799"> to the MS. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέθες_799"> turn on these points. (1) The act. <quote xml:lang="grc">μεθιέναι</quote>, when said of <emph> persons</emph>, usu.=‘allow to escape’ ( <ref n="Soph. OC 906"> <title>O. C.</title>906</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄτρωτον οὐ μεθῆκ’ ἄν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 372"> <title>Ai.</title>372</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μεθῆκα τοὺς ἀλάστορας</quote>): or ‘leave’ to some course; as <ref n="Soph. Ant. 653"> <title>Ant.</title>653</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέθες</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν παῖδ’ ἐν Ἅιδου τήνδε νυμφεύειν τινί</quote>. But this use of the verb has no place here. The sense is not, ‘allow me to escape’ to some solitude. (2) As said of <emph>things</emph>, <quote xml:lang="grc">μεθιέναι</quote> can mean, ‘to let go,’ ‘release from one's grasp’; <ref n="Soph. El. 448"> <title>El.</title>448</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα μὲν μέθες</quote> : <emph>ib.</emph> 1205 <quote xml:lang="grc">μέθες τόδ’ ἄγγος</quote>. Hermann, who retains </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέθες_799_2">, renders it by ‘<emph>depone.</emph>’ But that is too gentle a word: <quote xml:lang="grc">μέθες</quote> here would differ from <quote xml:lang="grc">κατάθες</quote> as ‘<emph>drop</emph>’ from ‘lay down.’ <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1254">Cp. 1254</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς πυράν με θῇς</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπου_ὄψεται_799">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1410"> <title>O. T.</title>1410</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔξω μέ που</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">καλύψατ’, ἢ φονεύσατ’, ἢ θαλάσσιον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκρίψατ’, ἔνθα μήποτ’ εἰσόψεσθ’ ἔτι</quote> (n.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_ἴσχεις_801">, if thou hast no heart to do that. It would be easy, but it is needless, to read </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_ς_801"> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλά_801">, ‘at least’: 201 n.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτοῦ_801"> ‘just <emph>here</emph>’ ( <ref n="Soph. OC 78"> <title>O. C.</title>78</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνθάδ’ αὐτοῦ</quote> ).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θάνω_801">: the prohibitive subj. is rare in the 1st pers.; but cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 174"> <title>O. C.</title>174</ref>(n.): <ref n="Eur. Tro. 172">Eur. <title> Tro.</title>172</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλγυνθῶ</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοσαῦτ_803">, implying brevity.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπισκήψαντος_803">, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοῦ</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1033"> <title>Ph.</title>1033</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πλεύ‐</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">σαντος</quote> (<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐμοῦ</quote>): <ref n="Xen. Anab. 4. 8. 5">Xen. <title>An.</title>4. 8. 5</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ δ’ εἶπον, ἐρωτήσαντος, ὅτι κ.τ.λ.—<emph>ἐν μέσῳ σκάφει</emph> </quote>, recumbent in the middle of the vessel. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σφε_804"> is prob. governed by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκέλσαμεν_804"> as well as by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θέντες_804">. The acc. after <quote xml:lang="grc">κέλλω</quote> usu. denotes either the ship or the place; but cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 236"> <title>Ph.</title>236</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς ς’, ὦ τέκνον, προσέσχε κ.τ.λ.</quote> (n.).—The boat would be rowed from Cape Cenaeum to the harbour near Thermopylae (633 n.), a distance of about eighteen miles; thence Heracles would be carried some six miles to Trachis. The shortest sea-passage would be across to the Locrian coast (788 n.); but the longer land-journey would be more trying for the sufferer. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_τεθνηκότ_806">: a way of saying, ‘I cannot tell whether his life will last so long.’ The change to <quote xml:lang="grc">ζῶντ’ ἔτ’ ὄψεσθ’</quote> would weaken the verse.—<ref n="Soph. Trach. 235">Cp. 235</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βουλεύσας_807">: not only in poetry (as <ref n="Soph. Ant. 267"> <title>Ant.</title>267</ref>), but in prose also, <quote xml:lang="grc">βουλεύειν</quote> is said of ‘planning’ (as dist. from ‘executing’) a crime ( <ref n="Dem. 19.21">Dem. or. 19 § 21</ref>); and <quote xml:lang="grc">βούλευσις</quote> was a legal term in this sense. The <quote xml:lang="grc">βουλή</quote> was really that of Nessus (844 f.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δρῶς_807">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">δράσασα</quote>, since his torments continue. The dat. <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρὶ ἐμῷ</quote> can be taken with <quote xml:lang="grc">δρῶσ’</quote>, no less than with <quote xml:lang="grc">βουλεύσασ’</quote>. For the construction <quote xml:lang="grc">δρῶ ταῦτά σοι</quote> (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">σε</quote>), cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1373"> <title>O. T.</title>1373</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποίνιμος_807">, of avenging deities, as <ref n="Soph. Aj. 843."> <title>Ai.</title>843.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Δίκη_807"> is associated with <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἐρινύς</quote>, since the latter especially punishes sins against kinsfolk: cp. <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1432">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1432</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὰ τὴν τέλειον τῆς ἐμῆς παιδὸς Δίκην</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἄτην τ’, Ἐρινύν θ’, αἷσι τόνδ’ ἔσφαξ’ ἐγώ.—<emph>ὧν … τείσαιτ’</emph> </quote>: for the causal gen., cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 229">Soph. <title>O. C.</title>229</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧν προπάθῃ τὸ τίνειν</quote>: <ref n="Hdt. 4. 118">Her.4. 118</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τείσασθαι τῆς πρόσθε δουλοσύνης βουλόμεμος</quote>. For the spelling, cp. <title>O.T.</title> 810 n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπεύχομαι_807">: he deliberately gives his wish (<quote xml:lang="grc">τείσαιτ’</quote>) the solemn form of an imprecation.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_δ_807">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1035"> <title>Ph.</title>1035</ref>: fr. 856 <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ μοι θέμις· θέμις δὲ τἀληθῆ λέγειν</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Her. 141">Eur. <title>H. F.</title>141</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ χρή μ’, ἐρωτῶ· χρὴ δ’, ἐπεί γε δεσπότης κ.τ.λ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προὔβαλες_807">, hast cast from thee, spurned: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεὶ σὺ πρότερα</quote> [read <quote xml:lang="grc">προτέρα</quote>] <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν θέμιν ἀπέρριψας καὶ παρεῖδες</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 830"> <title>Ai.</title>830</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥιφθῶ κυσὶν πρόβλητος</quote>. <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 215">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 215</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Κύπρις δ’ ἄτιμος τῷδ’ ἀπέρριπται λόγῳ</quote> . <cit> <bibl n="Tac. Ann. 1.42">Tac. <title>Ann.</title> 1. 42</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">cives, quibus tam senatus proiecta auctoritas.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote: cives, quibus tam senatus proiecta auctoritas.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοι_807">, ethic dat. (=‘I have seen thee’ do so): cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 81"> <title>O. C.</title>81</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ βέβηκεν ἡμὶν ὁ ξένος</quote> <emph>;</emph> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θέμιν_807"> to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔριν_807">, explains, ‘you have challenged me to this strife.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σῖγ_813">: cp. Eurydicè's silent exit, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1244"> <title>Ant.</title>1244</ref>: and for the sentiment, cp. fr. 842 <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰδὼς γὰρ ἐν κακοῖσιν ο<*>δὲν ὠφελεῖ·</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ γὰρ σιωπὴ τῷ λαλοῦντι σύμμαχος</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀφθαλμῶν_ἐμῶν_813"> in the sentence is due to the implied thought, ‘So long as <title>I</title> see her no more, she may go where she will.’ The poet cares not that the gen. might seem to depend on </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὖρος_813">: for like cases, cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 944"> <title>Ant.</title>944</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">Δανάας</quote>), <ref n="Soph. Phil. 598"> <title>Ph.</title>598</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">τίνος δ’</quote> ).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτῇ_813"> is not emphatic (‘of her own accord’), any more than <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτὸν</quote> in 262.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καλός_813"> seems a true correction of <quote xml:lang="grc">καλῶς</quote>, and was probably read by the schol. (cr. n.). Cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 11.640"> <title>Od.</title>11. 640</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κάλλιμος οὖρος</quote>. The adv. would have a weak effect at the end of the v., and would belong to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑρπούσῃ</quote> rather than to <quote xml:lang="grc">γένοιτ’</quote>.—Cp. <ref n="Aesch. Seven 690"> Aesch. <title>Th.</title>690</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴτω κατ’ οὖρον, κῦμα Κωκυτοῦ λαχόν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">Φοίβῳ στυγηθὲν πᾶν τὸ Λαΐου γένος</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γὰρ_817"> justifies his unfilial language.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄγκον_817">, ‘importance’ ( <ref n="Soph. OC 1162"> <title>O. C.</title>1162</ref> n.), ‘pomp’ ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 129"> <title>Ai.</title>129</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδ’ ὄγκον ἄρῃ μηδέν’</quote>): here, the ‘dignity’ belonging to the name of mother. The adj. agrees with <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄγκον</quote>, since <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄγκ. ὀνόματος</quote>=‘namedignity’: cp. <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 325">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title>325</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ματρῷον ἅγνισμα...φόνου.—<emph>ἄλλως</emph> </quote>, ‘vainly’ ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 947">Soph. <title>Ph.</title>947</ref> n.), <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, when she is a mother in name only.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τρέφειν_817">, ‘keep,’ continue to enjoy.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηδὲν_817">, generic, helping the causal force of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥτις_817">. Cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 1194"> <title>El.</title>1194</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μήτηρ καλεῖται, μητρὶ δ’ οὐδὲν ἐξισοῖ</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_χαίρουσα_819">: cp. <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 921">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title>921</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χαίρων ἴθ)· οὐ γὰρ σῶν με δεῖ μαντευμάτων.—<emph>ἣν</emph> </quote> at the end of the verse: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 298">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 298</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 14">Soph. <title>O. C.</title>14</ref>, <ref n="Soph. El. 873.">Soph. <title>El.</title>873.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τήνδ_819">: <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν δ’</quote> would be too emphatic: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 23">cp. 23</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴδ_821">: though several persons are addressed, the sing. is used (as in <title>O.C.</title> 1463), since <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰδέ</quote> could be a mere interjec tion; cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 3.332"> <title>Od.</title>3. 332</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγε τάμνετε</quote> : <ref n="Aristoph. Thes. 788">Ar. <title>Th.</title>788</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρε δή νυν</quote>, | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">τί γαμεῖθ’ ἡμᾶς</quote> <emph>;</emph> id. <ref n="Aristoph. Peace 383">Ar. <title> Pax</title>383</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰπέ μοι, τί πάσχετ’, ὦνδρες</quote> <emph> ;</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσέμειξεν_821">, intrans., as in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 106"> <title>Ph.</title>106</ref>(where see n. on the spelling), ‘has come to close quarters with us,’ instead of merely threatening from a distance. As </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παλαιφάτου_821"> indicates, the poet was thinking of <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 9.507"> <title>Od.</title>9. 507</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ μάλα δή με παλαίφατα θέσφαθ’ ἱκάνει</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote: ἦ μάλα δή με παλαίφατα θέσφαθ’ ἱκάνει

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεοπρόπον_822">, ‘oracular’: usu. connected with <quote xml:lang="grc">πρέπω</quote> (intrans.), as=‘appearing from a god’; though Buttmann explains it by <quote xml:lang="grc">θεὸς πρέπει</quote> (trans.), ‘a god sends a sign.’ Acc. to another view, <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοπρόπος</quote> is ‘one who prays to a god’ (as though the rt <quote xml:lang="grc">προπ‐</quote> were akin to Lat. <emph> prec:</emph> Leaf, <ref n="Hom. Il. 1.85"> <title>Il.</title>1. 85</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τᾶς_προνοίας_822">, the (divine) prescience which was declared (which found utterance) long ago: viz., twelve years ago, at Dodona: see 44 n. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 637"> Soph. <title>Ph.</title>637</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θείᾳ προνοίᾳ</quote>, ‘with inspired foresight.’ <ref n="Soph. OC 454">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 454</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">μαντεῖα</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc"> παλαίφαθ’</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅ_τ_824">, neut. of the epic relat. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅς τε</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 151"> <title>El.</title>151</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅ τ’</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἥ τ’</quote>). There is no metrical ground for reading </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἅ_τ_824"> (fem.) here, since the syllable, forming the anacrusis of the verse, is properly short: in the antistrophic v.<ref n="Soph. El. 834"> El., 834</ref>, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὃν_824"> before <quote xml:lang="grc">τέκετο</quote> is a long substituted for a short.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔλακεν_824">, of oracular utterance, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1094."> <title>Ant.</title>1094.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκφέροι_824">, intrans., ‘come to an end’: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">παρέλθοι</quote>. Anintrans. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκφέρειν</quote> occurs elsewhere only as meaning ‘to shoot ahead’ in a race: see on <ref n="Soph. OC 1424"> <title>O. C.</title>1424</ref>(where <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκφέρει</quote> is best taken as 2nd pers. pres. midd., ‘fulfil for thyself’). But the sense found here is parallel with that of the intrans. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκδιδόναι</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξιέναι</quote>, as said of rivers, ‘to issue.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δωδέκατος_ἄροτος_824">: the twelfth year from the time when the oracle was given at Dodona: see n. on 44. Apollodorus names the same term, though, acc. to his version, the oracle was given at Delphi (2. 4. 12).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄροτος_824">, ‘year’: 69 n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀναδοχὰν_τελεῖν_824"> (fut.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόνων_824">: the subject to the inf. is <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτόν</quote> supplied from <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἄροτος</quote>: ‘that it (the twelfth year) should end the succession of toils.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τελεῖν_824"> could not be intrans., with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναδοχάν</quote> for subject. The apparently intrans. use of the verb is limited to such phrases as the following: <ref n="Soph. El. 1419"> <title>El.</title>1419</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τελοῦσ’ ἀραί</quote> (are doing their work): <ref n="Aesch. Seven 659">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 659</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰσόμεσθ’ ὅπῃ τελεῖ</quote> (‘how the god will ordain’): cp. <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 1021">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 1021</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 225.">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title>225.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτόπαιδι_826">: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">γνησίῳ παιδί</quote>: cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτανέψιος.—<emph>ὀρθῶς</emph> </quote> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, at the due moment (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 173">cp. 173</ref>): <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρθός</quote> is oft. thus said of oracles coming true: <ref n="Soph. OT 853"> <title>O. T.</title>853</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OC 1424"> <title>O. C.</title>1424</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1178."> <title>Ant.</title>1178.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔμπεδα_826">, without fail: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 487">cp. 487</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατουρίζει_826">, intrans., are coming into haven before a fair wind: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥσπερ οὐρίῳ πνεύματι προσορμεῖ ἡμῖν κατ’ ὀρθὸν ἀνυόμενα</quote>. This compound does not occur elsewhere: but cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Thes. 1226">Ar. <title> Th.</title>1226</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τρέχε νυν κατὰ τοὺς κόρακας ἐπουρίσας</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_λεύσσων_829">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ μὴ βλέπων</quote>, the dead. Though this absol. use of <quote xml:lang="grc">λεύσσειν</quote> is found only here, it does not warrant suspicion.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπίπονον_λατρείαν_829">. The insertion of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόνων_829">, due to Gleditsch, is made probable by the text of the antistrophe: <ref target="cl839">see on 839</ref> f. For the phrase, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 356">cp. 356</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πόνων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">λατρεύματ’</quote>. And for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπίπονον</quote> along with <quote xml:lang="grc">πόνων</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 502"> <title>Ant.</title>502</ref> n. Other views of this verse are given in the Appendix on 839 f. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φονίᾳ_νεφέλᾳ_831">, dat. of circumstance, ‘with a cloud of death around him’: cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 16.350"> <title>Il.</title>16. 350</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θανάτου δὲ μέλαν νέφος ἀμφεκάλυψεν</quote>. There is perhaps a reminiscence of Pindar <title>N.</title> 9. 37 <quote xml:lang="grc">φόνου</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">παρποδίου νεφέλαν</quote> (referring to battle). The image might be partly suggested by the vivid description, which the Chorus have just heard, of Heracles in his agony, with the altar-smoke hanging around him: the <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσεδρος λιγνύς</quote> (794) was indeed, for him, a <quote xml:lang="grc">φονία νεφέλη</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Κενταύρου_ἀνάγκα_831">, the Centaur's insidious constraint; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, the inevitable doom, brought upon him by the Centaur's guile. The adj. is properly active in sense, ‘contriving fraud’; cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">παιδοποιός, σιτοποιός</quote>. In <ref n="Soph. OC 698"> <title>O. C.</title>698</ref> the passive sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοποιόν</quote> (‘selfproduced’) is exceptional.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρίει_831">, irritates, torments: <ref n="Aesch. PB 567">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title>567</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χρίει τις αὖ με τὰν τάλαιναν οἶστρος</quote>. This sense, like that of ‘anointing,’ comes from the primary sense, to ‘graze,’ or ‘rub.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλευρά_831"> from <quote xml:lang="grc">πλευρόν</quote>: for the second acc., cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1301"> <title>Ph.</title>1301</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέθες με... χεῖρα</quote> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔτεκε_831"> to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔτρεφε_831"> is a certain one.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰόλος_831">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 11">cp. 11</ref> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀέλιον_835">, with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="α_835">, the rarer quantity: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 100"> <title>Ant.</title>100</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὕδρας_φάσματι_836">, monstrous hydra: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 508">cp. 508</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φάσμα ταύρου</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προστετακὼς_836">, ‘close-locked’ in the <*>eadly grip of the monster. The word came to the poet's mind through a consciousness of the literal meaning,—viz., that the hero's flesh is ‘glued’ to the robe. Thi<*> very trait, so thoroughly Sophoclean, confirms the soundness of the text. (Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 117"> <title>Ant.</title>117</ref> n.) The context (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μελαγχαίταδ_836"> etc.) further confirms it. As the Chorus picture the turments of Heracles, two dread shapes rise before their thought,—the hydra, who nursed the venom, and the Centaur, through whose blood it works.—For the proposed emendations of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φάσματι_836">, see Appendix.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μελαγχαίτα_836"> (gen.): <title>Scut.</title> 186 <quote xml:lang="grc">μελαγχαίτην τε Μίμαντα</quote>. Cp. above, 557 n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄμμιγα_838">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνάμιγα</quote> (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 519">cp. 519</ref>): <ref n="Dem. 21.52">Dem. or. 21 § 52</ref> (in a <quote xml:lang="grc">μαντεία</quote>), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱστάναι ὡραίων Βρομίῳ χάριν ἄμμιγα πάντας</quote> (‘promiscuously,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> of mixed fruits). Here the sense seems to be, ‘confusedly’; there is a tumult of pangs: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1053">cp. 1053</ref> ff. The objection of taking it as merely ‘therewith,’ or ‘at the same time,’ is that the <quote xml:lang="grc">κέντρα</quote> are only the workings of the hydra's venom. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νέσου_839"> (or </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νέσσου_839">) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θ_ὕπο_839">, found in the MSS., have arisen from a gloss,—the name of Nessus having been introduced to explain </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μελαγχαίτα_839">. But otherwise there has been little agreement. The views of various critics are given in the Appendix.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μελαγχαίτα_839_2">, ‘the blackhaired one,’ could probably stand without a substantive, or proper name,—esp. as <quote xml:lang="grc">Κενταύρου</quote> has occurred not far back; though it would be somewhat harsh.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νέσου_ὕπο_839"> were ejected, this correspondence would be obtained by reading </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπόφονα_839"> (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">φοίνια</quote>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δολόμυθα_839">. The form <quote xml:lang="grc"> ὑπόφονος</quote>, though not extant, is correct (cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπόφονος</quote> in <ref n="Eur. Orest. 163">Eur. <title> Or.</title>163</ref> <ref n="Eur. Orest. 192">Eur. Or., 192</ref>). The <quote xml:lang="grc">κέντρα</quote> would be <quote xml:lang="grc"> ὑπόφονα</quote> as being ‘secretly fraught with death.’ The hiatus in <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰκίζει</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπόφονα</quote> is not unexampled, though it has not the usual excuse of a slight pause: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 833">cp. 833</ref> f., 846 f.; <ref n="Soph. Phil. 832"> <title>Ph.</title>832</ref> n. Such a hiatus has been assumed here by Hermann and others. It might be avoided, however, by reading </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φόνια_δολιόμυθα_839">. The forms <quote xml:lang="grc">δολόμυθος</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc"> δολιόμυθος</quote> are equally correct: cp. <quote xml:lang="grc"> δολόμητις</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">δολιόμητις, δολόφρων</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">δολιόφρων</quote>. In this case, the origin of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὕπο_839"> would remain obscure.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Νέσσου_ἐπιζέσαντα_839">, and in 830 </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔτι_λατρείαν_839">. The form <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑποφόνιος</quote> occurs in the neut. pl. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑποφόνια</quote>, as a subst., meaning the <quote xml:lang="grc">ποινή</quote> paid to the kinsfolk of a slain man (Harpocr.). The great recommendation of this reading is that it fully accounts for the traditional text here,— the insertion in the MSS. of <quote xml:lang="grc">θ’</quote> before <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕπο</quote> being a trivial error of a common type; while in 830 <quote xml:lang="grc">πόνων</quote> might easily have dropped out after <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπίπονον</quote>. </gloss>
lemma:
ἔτι ποτ’ ἔτ’
							ἐπίπονον <πόνων> ἔχοι θανὼν λατρείαν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧν_ἄοκνος_841">, <emph>quorum secura</emph> (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 23">cp. 23</ref>): she had no apprehension of such results. The MS. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄοκνον_841">, an epithet of <quote xml:lang="grc">βλάβαν</quote>, is explained as ‘not shrinking,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘not delaying,’ ‘hastening on.’ But (<emph> a</emph>) such a personification is strange; and (<emph>b</emph>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧν</quote> has then to be taken, somewhat awkwardly, with <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ μὲν</quote> in 843. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσορῶσα_842"> is confirmed, as against the plausible </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προορῶσα_842"> (Blaydes), by the fact that Deianeira had seen Iolè's arrival. She did not merely ‘foresee’ evil; she had ‘beheld’ it coming. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀΐσσουσαν_843">, Nauck's correction of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀϊσσόντων_843">, is acceptable, because ‘swift coming’ should be that of the <quote xml:lang="grc">βλάβη</quote>, rather than of the <quote xml:lang="grc">γάμοι</quote>: since </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γάμων_843"> here means simply the new tie,—not a formal marriage which was in prospect: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 460">cp. 460</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔγημε</quote> (n.), <ref n="Soph. Ant. 185"> <title>Ant.</title>185</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν ἄτην ὁρῶν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">στείχουσαν ἀστοῖς. <emph>νέων ἀϊσσόντων γάμων</emph> </quote> would be a gen. absol., ‘as a new marriage was hastening on.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_προσέβαλε_844">: the <emph>remedial measures</emph> were her own; their <emph>results</emph>, due to Nessus, were not foreseen by her. <quote xml:lang="grc">προσέβαλε</quote>=‘applied,’ as a remedy to a disease: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 580">cp. 580</ref>. Others explain, ‘brought upon herself’ (<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑαυτῇ</quote>): but this would rèquire <quote xml:lang="grc">αὑτᾷ</quote> in place of <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτά</quote> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλόθρου_844"> here merely=<quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀλλοτρίας</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 540"> <title>Ph.</title>540</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀλεθρίαισι_συναλλαγαῖς_844">, causal dat., ‘through her fatal <emph>meeting</emph>, <emph>converse</emph>,’ with Nessus at the Evenus (562 ff.).—Others explain: (1) ‘by a fatal reconciliation’; either (<emph>a</emph>) between Deianeira and Nessus, or (<emph>b</emph>) between Deianeira and Heracles,—in so far as she resolved to pardon him. (2) ‘By fatal <emph>conjunctures</emph>, issues’: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 410"> <title>O. C.</title>410</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀλεθρίαισι_844"> is the simplest correction of the unmetrical </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀλεθρίαις_844">: see Analysis. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_που_846">: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1130"> <title>Ph.</title>1130</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀλοὰ_846"> is best taken here as adverbial neut. plur., ‘desperately’: though in <ref n="Soph. El. 844"> <title>El.</title>844</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀλοὰ</quote> is nom. fem.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στένει_846"> is metrically suspicious: the corresponding word in the antistr. is <quote xml:lang="grc">νύμφαν</quote> (857). But no correction is probable. Hermann, writing </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_ἀλασταίνει_846">, cites Hesychius: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλασταίνει· δυσπαθεῖ</quote>. Arndt proposes <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ που ὀλὄ <emph>ἀσταίνει</emph> </quote>,—another word which the grammarians explain by <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσπαθεῖ</quote>, but which is wholly obscure. The conject. of Blaydes, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰάζει_846">, would serve; but then </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στένει_846_2"> must be viewed as a gloss. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χλωρὰν_ἄχναν_847">, a fresh, delicate dew; the tears fall in pearly drops. <ref n="Eur. Med. 906">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 906</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπ’ ὄσσων χλωρὸν ὡρμήθη δάκρυ</quote>. <ref n="Pind. N. 8">Pind. <title>N.</title>8. 40</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χλωραῖς ἐέρσαις</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τέγγει_ἄχναν_847">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 376"> <title>Ai.</title>376</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρεμνὸν αἷμ’ ἔδευσα</quote>: <title>I. T.</title> 159 <quote xml:lang="grc">χοὰς</quote> | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">ὑδραίνειν</quote>: <ref n="Pind. N. 10"> Pind. <title>N.</title>10. 75</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θερμὰ δὴ τέγγων δάκρυα</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοῖρα_847"> is still </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐρχομένα_847">, since Heracles is not yet dead. The </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄταν_847"> is his death,</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δολίαν_847">, as wrought by the guile of Nessus.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προφαίνει_847">, ‘foreshows,’—enables us to forebode.—Hermann understood, ‘<emph>reveals</emph> the secre<*> villainy (of Nessus).’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔρρωγεν_δακρύων_852">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 919">cp. 919</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 802"> <title>Ant.</title>802</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσχειν δ’</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκέτι πηγὰς δύναμαι δακρύων</quote>. The natural sense is, ‘The <emph>stream</emph> of tears has burst forth’; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘we all weep for this calamity.’ The words could not well mean merely, ‘a <emph>source</emph> of tears has been opened’; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, a woe has befallen, which will claim tears. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κέχυται_νόσος_853">, the plague has been diffused through his whole frame: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 293"> <title>Ph.</title>293</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάγου χυθέντος</quote>, ‘spread abroad.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_πόποι_853">: <ref n="Soph. OT 167"> <title>O. T.</title>167</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀναρσίων_853">, foes: <ref n="Hom. Il. 24.365"> <title>Il.</title>24. 365</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσμενέες καὶ ἀνάρσιοι</quote>. This worst of woes has come to him from his own home: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1058">cp. 1058</ref>—1063.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόποι_853"> and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπέμολε_853">. The traditional text is, <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷον ἀναρσίων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔπω ἀγακλειτὸν Ἡρακλέους</quote>. The <emph>v. l.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἡρακλέα</quote>, found in a few of the later MSS., was apparently prompted by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγακλειτόν</quote>. In the corresponding vv. of the strophe (841 ff.), the MS. text is, <quote xml:lang="grc">μεγάλαν προσορῶσα δόμοις βλάβαν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">νέων ἀϊσσόντων γάμων, τὰ μὲν οὔ τι</quote>: where the only doubt affecting metre is between <quote xml:lang="grc">δόμοις</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">δόμοισι</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἡρακλέους_853"> was a gloss, and that <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ μὲν οὔ τι</quote> in the strophe answered metrically to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγακλειτόν</quote> here. The proposed emendations of this passage are classified in the Appendix. Those which eject <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἡρακλέους</quote> follow one of two methods. (1) To read </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δόμοις_853">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">δόμοισι</quote>, in 842: to insert a long syllable, beginning with a vowel (as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ</quote>), before <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναρσίων</quote>: and to supply something, equal to <quote xml:lang="grc">˘¯</quote> (as <quote xml:lang="grc">ποτ’ ἄνδῤ</quote>), between <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔπω</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγακλειτόν</quote>. (2) To read </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δόμοισι_853"> in 842: and to make such an addition to <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔπω</quote> as shall metrically balance <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀϊσσόντων γάμων</quote>. On this plan, I suggest </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὕπ_ἀγακλειτόν_853">. The prep. goes with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναρσίων</quote>, which, without a prep., would here be somewhat harsh (as=‘from his foes’). <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἡρακλέους</quote> would have been a gloss on <quote xml:lang="grc">το<*>ε</quote>. Sophocles is fond of the periphrasis with <quote xml:lang="grc">σῶμα</quote>, which would be fitting here: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1194">cp. 1194</ref>, 1210: <ref n="Soph. OC 355"> <title>O. C.</title>355</ref>: <ref n="Soph. El. 1233."> <title>El.</title>1233.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἰκτίσαι_853">, epexegetic, ‘for us to pity’ (rather than, ‘for him to lament’): cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 144"> <title>O. C.</title>144</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ πάνυ μοίρας εὐδαιμονίσαι πρώτης</quote> (<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰμί</quote> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κελαινὰ_856">, ‘dark,’ referring at once to the colour of the metal, and to old stains. This general character of the epithet is seen in <ref n="Eur. Ba. 628">Eur. <title>Bacch.</title>628</ref> , <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵεται ξίφος κελαινὸν ἁρπάσας</quote>: where no blood has yet been shed.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προμάχου_856">, ‘fighting in the front of battle.’ Others understand, ‘fighting on behalf of men,’ ‘champion of the oppressed’ (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 1011">cp. 1011</ref>); the sense, probably, in which Heracles was styled <quote xml:lang="grc">Πρόμαχος</quote> at Thebes ( <ref n="Paus. 9. 11. 4">Paus.9. 11. 4</ref>). But the war upon Oechalia hardly illustrated that character.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰχμᾷ_856">, in the sense of ‘prowess,’ ‘warlike might,’ can follow <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγχα προμάχου δορός</quote>, since the latter is really an image for the warrior himself. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 355">Cp. 355</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰχμάσαι</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θοὰν_856"> here is merely abverbial,=<quote xml:lang="grc">ταχέως</quote>. So <ref n="Hom. Od. 2.257"> <title>Od.</title>2. 257</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λῦσεν δ’ ἀγορὴν αἰψηρήν</quote>= <ref n="Hom. Il. 2.808"> <title>Il.</title>2. 808</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἶψα δ’ ἔλυσ’ ἀγορήν</quote>. <ref n="Hom. Od. 8.38"> <title>Od.</title>8. 38</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θοὴν ἀλεγύνετε δαῖτα</quote>, ‘quickly fall to feasting.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰπεινᾶς_856">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 327">cp. 327</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμφίπολος_860"> and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄναυδος_860">, both epithets of <quote xml:lang="grc">Κύπρις</quote>, are to be taken closely together,—‘ministering in silence,’—viz., to the purposes of the gods,—not to the desire of Heracles. Some regard </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="α_860"> as a pron., with which <quote xml:lang="grc">Κύπρις</quote> is in apposition, (‘but <emph>she</emph>,’ etc.,) like <ref n="Soph. Phil. 371"> <title>Ph.</title>371</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ δ’ εἶπ’ Ὀδυσσεύς</quote> (n.): but it is simpler to take it as an ordinary article. For the order of words, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1199"> <title>O. T.</title>1199</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰν γαμψώνυχα παρθένον χρησμῳδόν</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φανερὰπράκτωρ_860">, because that motive has now been disclosed as supreme. For <quote xml:lang="grc">πράκτωρ</quote> fem., cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 81"> <title>O. T.</title>81</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάταιος_863">, foolish, deluded: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 407">cp. 407</ref> n. For this fem., <ref n="Soph. Trach. 207">cp. 207</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κοινὸς</quote> (n.). In 565 we have <quote xml:lang="grc">ματαίαις</quote> and in 887 <quote xml:lang="grc">ματαία</quote>, but in <ref n="Soph. OC 780"> <title>O. C.</title>780</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ματαίου...ἡδονῆς</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_φημί_865">; ‘what do I say?’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘what am I to say?’ Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1471"> <title>O. T.</title>1471</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί φημί</quote> <emph> ;</emph> | <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ δὴ κλύω που</quote>...<emph>;</emph>—where, as here, it expresses perplexity at a sound suddenly heard. It is only a more vivid form of <quote xml:lang="grc">τί φῶ</quote> <emph>;</emph> ( <ref n="Soph. OC 315"> <title>O. C.</title>315</ref>). Hermann wrote </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὶφημί_865">; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> ‘do I say anything (true)?’—like <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγω τι</quote> <emph>;</emph> ( <ref n="Soph. OT 1475"> <title>O. T.</title>1475</ref>). But the pron. could not then stand first. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_ἄσημον_866">, not doubtful (between joy and woe), </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλὰ_δυστυχῆ_866">, but (clearly) woful. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 209"> <title>Ph.</title>209</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">διάσημα γὰρ θροεῖ.—<emph>εἴσω</emph> </quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 202">cp. 202</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καινίζει_866">: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔοικέ τι νεώτερον ἔχειν ὁ οἶκος</quote>: the house is <emph>experiencing</emph> something <emph>for the first time</emph>,—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, is suffering some new calamity. So <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1071">Aesch. <title> Ag.</title>1071</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καίνισον</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">ζυγόν</quote>. In Lycophron 530, <quote xml:lang="grc">καινίσει δόρυ</quote>, a <emph>v. l.</emph> is <quote xml:lang="grc">κινήσει</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀήθης_866">, cannot be right. The word means either (1) ‘unusual,’ or (2) ‘unaccustomed’ to a thing. Here it has been taken in the first sense, as meaning, ‘with strange aspect,’ ‘unlike herself,’—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, gloomy, instead of cheerful. It seems inconceivable that a classical writer should have so used <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀήθης</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀηδὴς_866"> has been generally received; but this presents almost equal difficulties. As applied to persons, it regularly means, ‘disagreeable’; <title>Eth. N.</title> 2. 7 (p. 1108 <emph>a</emph> 29) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ...ἐν πᾶσιν ἀηδὴς δύσερίς τις καὶ δύσκολος</quote>: <title>Magn. Mor.</title> 2. 3 (p. 1200 <emph>a</emph> 15) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπερόπτας καὶ ἀηδεῖς</quote>. Here it ought to mean, ‘of sad aspect’; it never occurs, however, in that sense. Hesychius has, indeed, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀηδές· στυγνὸν, λυπηρόν</quote>: but this paraphrase of the <emph>neuter</emph> proves nothing. In <ref n="Soph. OT 82"> <title>O. T.</title>82</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡδύς</quote> is not ‘joyous-looking,’ but ‘welcome.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀήθης_866_2"> was merely a corruption of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀ_866">(</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γ_866">)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ηθής_866">, which does not seem to occur, but which is as correct as <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐγηθής</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">πολυγηθής</quote>.—Cp. <ref n="Eur. Alc. 777">Eur. <title> Alc.</title>777</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">στυγνῷ προσώπῳ καὶ συνωφρυωμένῳ</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σημανοῦσα_870">, as a correction of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σημαίνουσα_870">, is not merely recommended by usage, but is necessary, unless the <quote xml:lang="grc">τροφός</quote> be supposed to make signs before she speaks. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἡρακλεῖ_πόμπιμον_872">=<quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ Ἡρ. πόμπ</quote>.: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 714"> <title>O. C.</title>714</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵπποισιν τὸν ἀκεστῆρα χαλινόν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1166"> <title>Ai.</title>1166</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βροτοῖς τὸν ἀείμνηστον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τάφον. <emph>πόμπιμον</emph> </quote> here=<quote xml:lang="grc">πεμπτόν</quote>, as in <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 579">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title>579</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πομπίμα φάτις δωμάτων</quote>=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ ἐκ δωμ. πεμφθεῖσα</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καινοποιηθὲν_873">: a verb not elsewhere found in writers of this age, but frequent later; cp. Polyb. 1. 4. 5 <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὰ γὰρ αὕτη</quote> (<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ τύχη</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">καινοποιοῦσα κ.τ.λ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξ_ποδός_875">: <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐκ</quote> expresses the condition; cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 455"> <title>El.</title>455</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ὑπερτέρας χερός</quote> : <ref n="Soph. Phil. 91"> <title>Ph.</title>91</ref> n. This is one of those proverb-like turns which a homely speaker would use in the desire to be impressive. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_ποθ_876">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 668">cp. 668</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάντ_ἀκήκοας_876">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 402"> <title>Ant.</title>402</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντ’ ἐπίστασαι</quote>, —in a similar answer.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τέθνηκεν_876">...; They are so bewildered that they repeat the question which has been answered: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 184">cp. 184</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀλεθρία_876">, ‘undone,’ ‘lost’: a rare sense; but cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1341"> <title>O. T.</title>1341</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν μέγ’ ὀλέθριον</quote>. The second syll. is short, as in 845.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σχετλιώτατα_πρᾶξιν_876">, has been variously altered (cr. n.), in order to avoid an anapaest in the 2nd foot, on the assumption that the verse is an iambic trimeter. The neatest of such corrections is Hermann's, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σχετλίῳ_πρᾶξιν_876">.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σχετλιώτατάγε_πρᾶξιν_876">. Simplicity is not the only recommendation of this course; it transfers the stress of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_876"> from <quote xml:lang="grc">πρᾶξιν</quote> to the adverb. For this sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">σχέτλιος</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 887"> <title>Ai.</title>887</ref>, and n. on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 47."> <title>Ant.</title>47.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίνι_τρόπῳ_876">, a strong emphasis on </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρᾶξιν_876"> would be, however, less natural. It might, indeed, be explained thus;—‘the <quote xml:lang="grc">τρόπος</quote> of her death was the sword; but the mode of infliction (<quote xml:lang="grc">πρᾶξις</quote>) rendered it peculiarly pitiable,’— since it was inflicted by her own hand. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυντρέχει_880">: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">τίνι θανάτῳ συνέπεσεν</quote> (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 113"> <title>O. T.</title>113</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷδε συμπίπτει φόνῳ</quote>). The verb is, in fact, a bold poetical substitute for <quote xml:lang="grc">συμπίπτει</quote>, expressing the notion of ‘suddenly encountering’ a violent death. Cp. the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc"> συνέδραμον</quote>, said of combatants ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 16.337"> <title>Il.</title>16. 337</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διηΐστωσε_881">: the compound occurs only here. For this sense, cp. <ref n="Hdt. 3. 127">Her.3. 127</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δύο ἡμέων ἠΐστωσε</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίς_θυμός_882">, what impulse of passion,</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίνες_νόσοι_882">, what pangs of frenzy ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 59"> <title>Ai.</title>59</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φοιτῶντ’ ἄνδρα μανιάσιν νόσοις</quote>). The words <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ τίνες νόσοι</quote> are really parenthetical,—suggesting that the excited mind (<quote xml:lang="grc">θυμός</quote>) may have been also deranged; hence the verb can agree with <quote xml:lang="grc">θυμός</quote>, on which the chief stress falls.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυνεῖλε_882">, <emph> corripuit</emph>, seized and carried off; cp. <ref n="Thuc. 2. 51">Thuc.2. 51</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ λοιμὸς</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντα ξυνῄρει</quote>. Not, ‘destroyed her along with Heracles.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόνα_886"> means merely that she alone is responsible for the death of Heracles as well as for her own. It does not anticipate the statement that she was unaided in her suicide (891). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στονόεντος_887">: cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 8.159"> <title>Il.</title>8. 159</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βέλεα στονόεντα χέοντο</quote> (‘dolorous darts’).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_τομᾷ_887">: the instrumental <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1003"> <title>Ant.</title>1003</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σπῶντας ἐν χηλαῖσιν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_ματαία_888"> is said with a mixture of pity and impatience; the aged <quote xml:lang="grc">τροφός</quote>, in her terror and anguish, has failed to grasp the scope of the question, <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς ἐμήσατο</quote> (884), and has replied merely, ‘with a sword.’ The leader of the Chorus now asks her if she was an <emph> eye-witness</emph> of the deed,—feeling that she will satisfy their anxiety only if she can be led on to describe what she has seen. Thus the bewilderment of the messenger becomes a preparation for the <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥῆσις</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάνδ_ὕβριν_888">, this deed of <emph>violence</emph> (done to herself). So in <ref n="Soph. El. 864"> <title>El.</title>864</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λώβα</quote> is merely a fatal accident. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_παραστάτις_889">, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὖσα</quote> (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 83"> <title>O. C.</title>83</ref> n.); here <quote xml:lang="grc">δή</quote>=‘in fact.’ Elsewhere, when <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς δή</quote> is not ironical (as it is in <ref n="Soph. OC 809"> <title>O. C.</title>809</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">δή</quote> sometimes=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἤδη</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1065"> <title>Ph.</title>1065</ref>). <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1192">Cp. 1192</ref> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίς_ἦν_890">, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ ὕβρις</quote>: what was its nature? </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πῶς_890"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγένετο</quote>), how was it executed? </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτὴ_αὑτῆς_891"> <quote xml:lang="grc"> κ.τ.λ.</quote> The verb <quote xml:lang="grc">χειροποιεῖν</quote> occurs elsewhere only in later Greek.—The exclamation which follows implies that these words add something to the disclosure made in 881, <quote xml:lang="grc">αὑτὴν διηΐστωσε</quote>. They certainly state more precisely that the blow was dealt by her own hand (and not by a slave's); also that the deed had its origin from her own mind (<quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς αὑτῆς</quote>), and not from any external influence. But it should also be recognised that, throughout this passage (871—898), the dramatic aim is to express profound horror and amazement. The messenger can hardly seize the full meaning of the questions; the hearers, on their part, find it hard to realise the answers. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔτεκεν_δὴ_893">. I read with H. H. Schmidt J. (cr. n.), thinking with him that the metre is probably <quote xml:lang="grc">˘˘˘</quote>|<quote xml:lang="grc">˘˘˘</quote>| <quote xml:lang="grc">¯˘˘</quote>| <quote xml:lang="grc">¯ Λ</quote> ||. In such a passage the text might easily have lost <quote xml:lang="grc">δή</quote>.—The firstborn of Iolè, that <quote xml:lang="grc">πημονὴ ὑπόστεγος</quote> (376), is a dire spirit which avenges the house of Eurytus on the house of Heracles. Cp. Tennyson, <title>Guinevere:</title> ‘Well is it that no child is born of thee. | The children born of thee are sword and fire’...</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νέορτος_893">, simply, ‘that has newly arisen’: not, ‘that has lately <emph>sped</emph> hither’ (schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ νεωστὶ ἐνταῦθα ὁρμήσασα</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄγαν_γε_896">, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">μεγάλαν</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 982"> <title>Ai.</title>982</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΤΕ. ὦ περισπερχὲς πάθος. | ΧΟ. ἄγαν γε, Τεῦκρε.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κάρτα_ᾤκτισας_896">, assuredly thou wouldst have felt greater pity. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_τις_898"> <quote xml:lang="grc"> κ.τ.λ.</quote> For the place of <quote xml:lang="grc">τις</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 104"> <title>Ph.</title>104</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὕτως ἔχει τι δεινὸν ἰσχύος θράσος</quote> <emph>;</emph> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κτίσαι_898"> implies that the deed was momentous: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">κατασκευάσαι καὶ ποιῆσαι· καλῶς δὲ ὡς ἐπὶ μεγάλῳ τολμήματι εἶπεν τὴν λέξιν</quote>. When <quote xml:lang="grc">κτίζειν</quote> is thus a tragic synonym for <quote xml:lang="grc">ποιεῖν</quote>, there is usu. a predicative adj., as <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 17">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title>17</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τέχνης δέ νιν Ζεὺς ἔνθεον κτίσας φρένα</quote>: cp. <ref n="Aesch. Supp. 138">Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title>138</ref>: <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 441.">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title>441.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρῆλθε_900"> is confirmed by the usage of this compound with ref. to entering a house: <ref n="Soph. OT 1241"> <title>O. T.</title>1241</ref>, <ref n="Soph. El. 1337"> <title>El.</title>1337</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Med. 1137">Eur. <title> Med.</title>1137</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 108">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 108</ref>, etc. And the asyndeton is of a kind which the poet often admits; <ref n="Soph. Trach. 555">cp. 555</ref>, 750. The conjecture </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γὰρ_ἦλθε_900">, which has been generally received, seems, then, unnecessary. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐλαῖς_901">, the <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐλή</quote> of the house; a poet. plur. like <quote xml:lang="grc">νυμφεῖα</quote> (920), <quote xml:lang="grc">παρθενῶνες</quote> ( <ref n="Aesch. PB 646">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title>646</ref>), etc.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοῖλα_901"> is not merely a general epithet (as <quote xml:lang="grc">κοίλην</quote> of <quote xml:lang="grc">κάπετον</quote> in <ref n="Soph.
