Aj. 1165"> <title>Ai.</title>1165</ref>), but means that the litter (<quote xml:lang="grc">φορεῖον</quote>) was arranged so that the sufferer could lie in it as in a hammock,—with soft bedding on each side of him as well as beneath him. His agonies made this indispensable.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στορνύνθ_901">: Attic prose rarely uses this form, except in the aor. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστόρεσα.—<emph>ἄψορρον ἀντῴη</emph> </quote>, go back to meet him on his way from the harbour (804): cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 53"> <title>El.</title>53</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄψορρον ἥξομεν πάλιν</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρύψασ_ἑαυτὴν_903">, in the women's apartments (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 686">cp. 686</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν μυχοῖς</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔνθα_εἰσίδοι_903"> would usu. mean, ‘in any place where no one beheld,’— oblique of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔνθ’ ἂν μή τις εἰσίδῃ</quote>. But here the sense is final; ‘where no one should behold.’ The normal Attic for this would be, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔνθα μή τις ὄψεται</quote> (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 800">cp. 800</ref>),—not <quote xml:lang="grc"> ὄψοιτο</quote>, since, in a final relat. clause, the fut. indic. was usu. kept even after a secondary tense.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βρυχᾶτο_904">: for the omission of the augment, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1249"> <title>O. T.</title>1249</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βωμοῖσι_904">: besides the altar of Zeus <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑρκεῖος</quote> in the <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐλή</quote>, there would be other altars of domestic gods in a large house; cp. <ref n="Eur. Alc. 170">Eur. <title>Alc.</title>170</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντας δὲ βωμούς, οἳ κατ’ Ἀδμήτου δόμους</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">προσῆλθε κἀξέστεψε καὶ προσηύξατο</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γένοιντ_ἔρημοι_904">: she said, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρημοι ἐγένοντο</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐγένεσθε</quote>). After her death, and that of Heracles, these altars were doomed to desolation. Nauck seems right in thus amending </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γένοιτ_ἐρήμη_904">, though not for the reasons which he assigns. Those words could certainly mean, ‘that she had become desolate,’— nor is the plaint less natural because death is so near. But the other reading is in truer harmony with the context, because she is saying <emph>farewell</emph> to the surroundings of happier days. Even inanimate objects move her tears at the thought of parting. Naturally the altars come first; when they were forsaken, the family life would have ceased.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀργάνων_ψαύσειεν_904">: for the optat., cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 289"> <title>Ph.</title>289</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅ μοι βάλοι</quote> (n.). <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτου</quote> here =<quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ τινος</quote>. Among the <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄργανα</quote> would be sacrificial vessels, and, as the schol. remarks, the implements which she had used in weaving the robe. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλλῃ_δωμάτων_907">: for the gen., <ref n="Soph. Trach. 375">cp. 375</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φίλων_οἰκετῶν_907">. The opening scene with the <quote xml:lang="grc">τροφός</quote> illustrates these kindly relations. Cp. <ref n="Eur. Alc. 194">Eur. <title>Alc.</title>194</ref> f., when Alcestis takes leave of her attached <quote xml:lang="grc"> οἰκέται</quote>:—<quote xml:lang="grc">κοὔτις ἦν οὕτω κακὸς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃν οὐ προσεῖπε καὶ προσερρήθη πάλιν.—<emph>εἰσορωμένη</emph> </quote>: the midd., as <ref n="Soph. El. 1060">Soph. <title>El.</title>1060</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐσορώμενοι</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνακαλουμένη_910">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1376"> <title>O. C.</title>1376</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_οὐσίας_910">, is undoubtedly corrupt. Various attempts to explain or to amend it are recorded in the Appendix.</gloss>
lemma:
καὶ τὰς ἄπαιδας ἐς
							τὸ λοιπὸν οὐσίας

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_λοιπὸνοὐσίας_910">: ‘and the fate of the property which would thenceforth be in the power of others.’ For <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπί</quote> with dat. as=<emph>penes</emph>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 66"> <title>O. C.</title>66</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1003."> <title>Ph.</title>1003.</ref>The slaves are part of the <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐσία</quote>. Euripides has <quote xml:lang="grc"> οὐσία</quote>, as=‘property,’ at least twice: <ref n="Eur. Her. 337"> <title>H. F.</title>337</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρῷον ἐς μέλαθρον, οὗ τῆς οὐσίας</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλοι κρατοῦσι</quote> : <title>Helen.</title> 1253 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἂν παρούσης οὐσίας ἕκαστος ᾖ</quote>. (See Appendix.) </gloss>
lemma:
καὶ τῆς ἐπ’ ἄλλοις ἐς τὸ λοιπὸν
							οὐσίας

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑστίας_910"> would be an easy correction of <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐσίας</quote>: but, on my view of the passage, the change is not required. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_Ἡράκλ_913">: for the adj., <ref n="Soph. Trach. 51">cp. 51</ref>, 576.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θάλαμον_913">, the nuptial chamber: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 804"> <title>Ant.</title>804</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λαθραῖον_ὄμμ_914">, acc. of respect: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπεσκιασμένη_914">, ‘overshadowed,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘shrouded from view.’ Thus the phrase means strictly, ‘shrouded as to (or in) my secret observation’: for <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄμμα</quote> here implies the act of observing. <quote xml:lang="grc">λαθραῖον</quote> expresses the result of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεσκιασμένη</quote>. She may have watched from behind a curtain, or at a partly open door.—Not, ‘with eyes shaded by my hand’ ( <ref n="Soph. OC 1650"> <title>O. C.</title>1650</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀμμάτων ἐπίσκιον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">χεῖῤ</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βάλλουσαν_916"> with dat., in the sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμ‐</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιβάλλουσαν</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 67"> <title>Ph.</title>67</ref> n.).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στρωτὰ_916"> goes closely with the partic.,— spreading them as coverings, <quote xml:lang="grc">στώματα.— <emph>φάρη</emph> </quote>: the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">φᾶρος</quote> is not thus used; but cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 4.297"> <title>Od.</title>4. 297</ref> ff., where the bed (<quote xml:lang="grc">δέμνια</quote>) is spread with <quote xml:lang="grc"> ῥήγεα</quote> (‘blankets’), <quote xml:lang="grc">τάπητες</quote> (‘rugs’), and woollen <quote xml:lang="grc">χλαῖναι</quote> as coverlets. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐνατηρίοις_918">: the form <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐναστηρίοις</quote> appears to be a later one (Dind. on <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 160">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title>160</ref> ).—Cp. <ref n="Verg. A. 4. 650">Verg. <title>Aen.</title>4. 650</ref>(Dido, about to die) <quote xml:lang="lat">Incubuitque toro dixitque novissima verba.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ῥήξασα_919">: so <ref n="Plut. Per. 36">Plut. <title> Per.</title>36</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κλαυθμόν τε ῥῆξαι καὶ πλῆθος ἐγχέαι δακρύων</quote>. <ref n="Soph. OT 1075">Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 1075</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νυμφεῖα_919">, bridalchamber ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 891"> <title>Ant.</title>891</ref>): for the plur., <ref n="Soph. Trach. 901">cp. 901</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐνάτριαν_919">: this form is rightly preferred to <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐνήτριαν</quote> by Nauck, <title>Eur. Stud.</title> II. p. 175. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συντόνῳ_923">, intense, vehement: <ref n="Eur. Ba. 1091">Eur. <title>Bacch.</title>1091</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">συντόνοις δρομήμασι</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ᾗ_924">, at the place where. The MS. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ᾧ_924"> doubtless arose from <quote xml:lang="grc"> πέπλον</quote>: it would mean, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃς περονίδα εἷχε μαστῶν προκειμένην</quote>: but this is less natural.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προὔκειτο_μαστῶν_924">: the <quote xml:lang="grc">πέπλος</quote> was fastened near the left shoulder by the <quote xml:lang="grc">περονίς</quote>, which is described as lying ‘in front of,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> ‘above,’ the (left) breast. It would not accord with Greek usage to imagine the brooch as placed at the centre of the bosom. Cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 14.180"> <title>Il.</title>14. 180</ref>(of Hera's <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἑανὸς</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">χρυσείῃς δ’ ἐνετῇσι κατὰ στῆθος περονᾶτο</quote>. <ref n="Soph. OT 1269"> <title>O. T.</title>1269</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_ἐλώπισεν_924">: for the tmesis, cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1233"> <title>Ant.</title>1233</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ δ’ ὁρμωμένου</quote>: and <emph>ib.</emph> 427. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκλωπίζω</quote> (from <quote xml:lang="grc">λώπη, λῶπος</quote>, a covering) occurs only here. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκλωπίσαι</quote> has been conjectured in Pollux 7. 44 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποδῦσαι καὶ ἀπολωπίσαι, ὡς Σοφοκλῆς</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δρομαία_927">: <ref n="Eur. Orest. 45">Eur. <title> Or.</title>45</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πηδᾷ δρομαῖος</quote>. <ref n="Thuc. 3. 29">Thuc.3. 29</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σχολαῖοι κομισθέντες</quote>.—We may render, ‘warned her son of her intent’; but the literal sense is, ‘warned the son of her who was devising these things’: the gen. depends on <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ παιδί</quote>. Others take the gen. with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φράζω_927"> (‘tell him <emph> about</emph> her’). It would then be best to govern <quote xml:lang="grc">τάδε</quote> by <quote xml:lang="grc">φράζω</quote>: for in this constr. of the gen. with verbs of saying or asking, the object is usually expressed, either by an acc. ( <ref n="Soph. El. 317"> Soph. <title>El.</title>317</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1236">Soph. <title> Ai.</title>1236</ref>), or by a relat. clause (below<ref n="Soph. Aj. 1122">Soph. Ai., 1122</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 439"> Soph. <title>Ph.</title>439</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάδε_927">, however, belongs rather to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τεχνωμένης_927">. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_τ_929">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 315">Soph. <title>Ph.</title>315</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκεῖσε καὶ τὸ δεῦρο</quote>. For the art. with the first word only, <ref n="Soph. OC 606">Soph. <title>O. C.</title>606</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀμὰ κἀκείνων</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξορμώμεθα_929"> might refer to the Nurse only, but rather includes Hyllus (as <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁρῶμεν</quote> certainly does). It suits <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῦρο</quote>, therefore, but not <quote xml:lang="grc">κεῖσε</quote>. The thought is, ‘before I could return with him.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁρῶμεν_πεπληγμένην_929">, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">πέπληκται, ὡς ὁρῶμεν.—<emph>ἀμφιπλῆγι φασγάνῳ</emph> </quote>: adjecti<*> which are properly only masc. or fem. are sometimes used in oblique cases with neuter nouns: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 19"> <title>Ph.</title>19</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφιτρῆτος αὐλίου</quote> : <ref n="Soph. Aj. 324"> <title>Ai.</title>324</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βοτοῖς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">σιδηροκμῆσιν</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑφ_φρένας_929">, lit., ‘to the liver and midriff.’ But it was her left side that she bared (926), and the fatal blow must have been nearer to the heart than to the liver. The phrase should therefore be understood in a general sense, as a poetical way of saying, ‘home to the very centre of life.’ It may have been suggested by <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 9.301"> <title>Od.</title>9. 301</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐτάμεναι πρὸς στῆθος, ὅθι φρένες ἧπαρ ἔχουσιν</quote> </cit>, ‘stab him in the breast, where the midriff holds the liver.’ Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1315"> <title>Ant.</title>1315</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">παίσασ’ ὑφ’ ἧπαρ αὐτόχειρ αὑτήν</quote>. </gloss>

data.quote: οὐτάμεναι πρὸς στῆθος, ὅθι φρένες ἧπαρ ἔχουσιν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὔργον_ἐφάψειεν_933">, that he had ‘fastened,’ ‘bound’ the deed ‘upon her,’ as a burden or doom. Cp. <ref n="Pind. O. 9">Pind. <title>O.</title>9. 64</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ καθέλοι νιν α<*>ν πότμον ἐφάψαις</quote>|<quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρφανὸν γενεᾶς</quote>, ‘having laid on him the doom of childlessness.’ <ref n="Hom. Il. 2.15"> <title>Il.</title>2. 15</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Τρώεσσι δὲ κήδἐ ἐφῆπται</quote>, ‘have been imposed’ on them.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατ_ὀργὴν_933"> must refer to the anger of Hyllus (734 ff.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_οἶκον_934">: for the simple gen. with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκδ_934">, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 117"> <title>O. T.</title>117</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτου...ἐκμα‐</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">θών.—<emph>πρὸς τοῦ θηρὸς</emph> </quote>, at his instigation. This pregnant sense of the prep. is somewhat rare: but cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 1.238"> <title>Il.</title>1. 238</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θέμιστας</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς Διὸς εἰρύαται</quote> (by his ordinance): 6. 456 <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς ἄλλης ἱστὸν ὑφαίνοις</quote> (at her bidding). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δύστηνος_936">=<quote xml:lang="grc">δύστηνος ὤν</quote>, ‘miserable as he was.’ This is better than to make it an interjection, ‘poor youth!’— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐλείπετ_οὐδέν_936"> (adv.), ‘in no wise fell short.’ The verb has here a twofold constr., viz., (1) with gen. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀδυρμάτων_936">, as <ref n="Soph. El. 474"> <title>El.</title>474</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γνώμας λειπομένα σοφᾶς</quote>: (2) with partic. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμφιπίπτων_936">: cp. <ref n="Xen. Ec. 18">Xen. <title>Oecon.</title>18</ref>§ 5 <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα μὲν τοίνυν, ἔφη, οὐδὲν ἐμοῦ λείπει γιγνώσκων</quote> (‘you understand these things just as well as I do’),—where <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοῦ</quote> is parallel, not with <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀδυρμάτων</quote> here, but with <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ὦν τέκνων</quote> in 266.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμφί_νιν_936">: the acc. with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφί</quote>, as=‘concerning,’ is somewhat rare: but cp. <ref n="Pind. P. 2">Pind. <title>P.</title>2. 15</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κελαδέοντι μὲν ἀμφὶ Κινύραν</quote>. (In <ref n="Hom. Il. 18.339"> <title>Il.</title>18. 339</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφὶ δέ σε...κλαύσονται</quote>, the sense is ‘around.’)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμφιπίπτων_στόμασιν_936">: <ref n="Eur. Alc. 404">Eur. <title>Alc.</title>404</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποτὶ σοῖσι πίτνων στόμασιν</quote> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">χείλεσι</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλευρόθεν_936">, ‘at’ (or ‘near’) ‘her side.’ The ending <quote xml:lang="grc">θεν</quote> properly denotes the point <emph>from which</emph> motion sets out. Hence a form in <quote xml:lang="grc">θεν</quote> is equivalent to a genitive expressing <emph>source</emph> or <emph> starting-point.</emph> By a stretch of that analogy, <quote xml:lang="grc">πλευρόθεν</quote> does duty here for the genitive of <emph> place</emph>, which is only a special kind of possessive genitive,—‘belonging to,’ and so, ‘in the region of’: <ref n="Soph. El. 900"> <title>El.</title>900</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐσχάτης δ’ ὁρῶ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πυρᾶς...βόστρυχον</quote>: <ref n="Hom. Il. 9.219"> <title>Il.</title>9. 219</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἷζεν</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">τοίχου τοῦ ἑτέροιο</quote>. A somewhat similar example is <ref n="Hom. Il. 15.716"> <title>Il.</title>15. 716</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρύμνηθεν ἐπεὶ λάβεν, οὐχὶ μεθίει</quote>, where the form in <quote xml:lang="grc">θεν</quote>=the gen. after a verb of seizing (‘took hold <emph>by</emph> the stern’).—Cp. <ref n="Eur. Alc. 366">Eur. <title>Alc.</title>366</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πλευρά τ’ ἐκτεῖναι πέλας</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">πλευροῖσι τοῖς σοῖς</quote>.—For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρείς_936">, cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 819.">Soph. <title> El.</title>819.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰτίᾳ_βάλοι_940">, as with a missile: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1244"> <title>Ai.</title>1244</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμᾶς...κακοῖς βαλεῖτε</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 902">Soph. <title>El.</title>902</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μή μέ τις φθόνῳ βάλῃ</quote>: <ref n="Aristoph. Thes. 895">Ar. <title>Th.</title>895</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὐμὸν σῶμα βάλλουσα ψόγῳ</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_βίον_941"> (acc. of respect), ‘orphaned as to his life,’ having his life made <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρφανός</quote>, ‘on the part of both parents at once’: cp. the lament of Eumelus for his mother Alcestis ( <ref n="Eur. Alc. 397">Eur. <title>Alc.</title>397</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">προλιποῦσα δ’ ἀμὸν βίον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὠρφάνισεν τλάμων. <emph>βίον</emph> </quote> (Wakefield) is a necessary correction of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βίου_941">, with which the sense would be either (<emph>a</emph>) ‘deprived of life,’ as in <title>Anth.</title> 7. 483 <quote xml:lang="grc">ζωᾶς νήπιον ὠρφάνισας</quote>: or (<emph>b</emph>) ‘deprived of subsistence.’ Nauck, keeping </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βίου_941_2">, changes </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_941"> to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἷς_941">, understanding, ‘bereaved of the life of both parents.’ But <quote xml:lang="grc">εἷς</quote> is clearly unsuitable here; and the phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">ὠρφ. δυοῖν βίου</quote> would be strange as well as weak. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δύο_943">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> to-day and to-morrow.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_πλείους_943"> (Dindorf) is the best correction of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_τις_943"> (L), which may have arisen from </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τι_943"> being accidentally omitted or transposed. The <emph>v. l.</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_τις_943_2"> was an attempt to reconcile that reading with metre. In lyrics we find the gen. <quote xml:lang="grc">πλέονος</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OC 1211"> <title>O. C.</title>1211</ref>; <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1100"> <title>Ph.</title>1100</ref>, if the reading of the schol. be accepted): but in the iambics of Tragedy there is no certain instance (apart from <quote xml:lang="grc">πλέον</quote>) of the shorter form. (In <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1299">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title>1299</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἔστ’ ἄλυξις, ὧ ξένοι, χρόνῳ πλέω</quote>, the text is doubtful.) A further objection to <quote xml:lang="grc">πλέους</quote> is the repeated <quote xml:lang="grc">τις</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τι_943_2">), on more days to come; but this is rash. A man can never be sure that his good fortune (<foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign> immunity from disaster) will last even to the end of to-day.’ Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 567"> <title>O. C.</title>567</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔξοιδ’ ἀνὴρ ὤν, χὤτι τῆς ἐς αὔριον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν πλέον μοι σοῦ μέτεστιν ἡμέρας</quote>. For <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ αὔριον</quote> (without <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμέρα</quote>), cp. Alexis <quote xml:lang="grc">Ὕπνος</quote> fr. 3 <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς τὴν αὔριον.—<emph>λογίζεται</emph> </quote>, ‘computes,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘sets down in his calculations,’ as something upon which he can count. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πότερα_πρότερον_947">: these words, as Schneidewin remarks, are often found in juxtaposition; <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>, <ref n="Aristoph. Eccl. 1082">Ar. <title> Eccl.</title>1082</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποτέρας προτέρας...ἀπαλλαγῶ<emph>;</emph> —<emph>δύσκριτά</emph> </quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστι</quote>), <quote xml:lang="grc"> πότεραπρότερον <emph>ἐπιστένω</emph> </quote> (delib. subjunct.), <quote xml:lang="grc">ποτέρα μέλεα περαιτέρω</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστί</quote>). For <quote xml:lang="grc">δύσκριτα</quote>, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">δύσκριτον</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 64">cp. 64</ref> n. This is better than to place a note of interrogation after <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιστένω</quote>, and another after <quote xml:lang="grc">περαιτέρω</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέλεα_947">: the MS. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τέλεα_947"> would mean, ‘which woe is the more complete’; but this is less fitting here, since the second calamity is still prospective (951): nor is <quote xml:lang="grc">τέλεἀ περαιτέρω</quote> a natural phrase. We cannot well take it adverbially (‘which woe I should mourn more completely’). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάδε_951"> is governed by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μένομεν_951">: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπ_ἐλπίσιν_951">=‘with forebodings’: cp. <ref n="Xen. Mem. 2. 1. 18">Xen. <title>Mem.</title>2. 1. 18</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ μὲν ἑκουσίως ταλαιπ<*>ρῶν ἐπ’ ἀγαθῇ ἐλπίδι πονῶν εὐφραίνεται</quote>.—Hermann's </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μελόμεν_951"> (<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστί</quote>)= ‘are cares to us’: <ref n="Soph. El. 1436">Soph. <title>El.</title>1436</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀνθάδ’ ἂν μέλοιτ’ ἐμοί</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχειν_952">, to have (troubles), </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέλλειν_952">, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕξειν</quote> (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 75">cp. 75</ref>), to be in expectation of them. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοινὰ_952">, <emph> sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστίν</quote>, are <emph>kindred</emph> things. For this sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">κοινός</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 261"> <title>O. T.</title>261</ref> n.: similarly ‘cognate’ things can be called <quote xml:lang="grc">συγγενῆ</quote>.—Others explain: (1) ‘It is all one’ whether sorrow is present or prospective. (2) ‘There are woes on both parts’ (that of Deianeira and that of Heracles), ‘for us to suffer or apprehend.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνεμόεσσα_953"> (Doric for <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἠν‐</quote>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὔρα_953">, a <emph>strong</emph> breeze: cp. <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 591">Aesch. <title>Ch.</title>591</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνεμόεντ’ ἂν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰγίδων φράσαι κότον</quote> (‘the stormy wrath of whirlwinds’). For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔπουρος_953">, ‘wafting,’ cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 194">Soph. <title>O. T.</title>194</ref> n.: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑστιῶτις_953">, ‘of the hearth,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘coming to our home’ at Trachis. The word occurs only here. Schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴθε ὡς ἕστηκα πνεύσειεν ἄνεμος οὔριος ἐπὶ τῆς οἰκίας<ref n="Soph. OT i">σοπη. ο. τ., ι</ref>(/να με λαβὼν ταύτης ἀπαγάγοι τῆς ἑστίας.—<emph>ἀποικίσειεν</emph> </quote> : <ref n="Soph. OC 1389">Soph. <title>O. C.</title>1389</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καλῶ τὸ Ταρτάρου</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">στυγνὸν πατρῷον ἔρεβος ὥς σ’ ἀποικίσῃ</quote>. The optat. in the relative clause is due to the optat. of wish in the principal clause: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 506">Soph. <title>O. T.</title>506</ref> n.—Cp. the wish of the anxious Chorus in <ref n="Soph. OC 1081">Soph. <title>O. C.</title> 1081</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴθ’ ἀελλαία ταχύρρωστος πελειὰς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰθερίας νεφέλας κύρσαιμ’</quote>. <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 732">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title>732</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλιβάτοις ὑπὸ κευθμῶσι γενοίμαν κ.τ.λ.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Διὸς_953">, instead of which we require <quote xml:lang="grc">¯˘</quote>, should be corrected to (1) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ζηνὸς_953">, or (2) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Δῖον_953">. Cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 1097."> <title>El.</title>1097.</ref>I incline to (1), because it seems unlikely that the poet should have <emph>preferred</emph> to make four consecutive words end in <quote xml:lang="grc">ον</quote>. It is also worth noticing that <quote xml:lang="grc">Δῖος</quote>, ‘belonging to Zeus,’ though used by Aesch. and Eur. , is not extant in Soph. , who has only <quote xml:lang="grc">δῖος</quote>, ‘divine’ or ‘godlike.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοῦνον_953"> (adv.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰσιδοῦσἄφαρ_953">, ‘at the mere sight of him anon.’ <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἄφαρ</quote> might be ‘suddenly,’ as in 821: but is rather ‘anon,’ ‘forthwith’ (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 135">cp. 135</ref>): his arrival is close at hand. The schol.'s words, <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ παραχρῆμα ἀποθάνω θεασαμένη τὸν Ἡρακλέα κακῶς διακείμενον</quote>, have caused a surmise that </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοῦνον_953_2"> has arisen from some word meaning ‘weak’ (see cr. n.). But there is little probability in </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μῶλυν_953"> (Hippônax fr. 60, perh. akin to <quote xml:lang="grc">μαλακός</quote>), </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καῦρον_953"> (a word which, acc. to Photius <title>Lex.</title>p. 181. 14, Sophocles used in the sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">κακός</quote>), </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μανὸν_953"> (properly opposed to <quote xml:lang="grc">πυκνόν</quote>), or </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μαῦρον_953"> (found only in grammarians). We might rather suggest <quote xml:lang="grc">θάνοιμ’, <emph>ἀ‐</emph> </quote> |</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μαυρὸν_953">, were change needful. The schol.'s <quote xml:lang="grc">κακῶς διακείμενον</quote> may, however, be a mere comment; and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοῦνον_953_3"> seems well fitted to emphasise the terror of the sight. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 536"> <title>Ph.</title>536</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶμαι γὰρ οὐδ’ ἂν ὄμμασιν μόνην θέαν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλον λαβόντα πλὴν ἐμοῦ τλῆναι τάδε</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπεὶ_959">, <quote xml:lang="grc">˘˘</quote>, with epic hiatus (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 650">cp. 650</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁ δέ οἱ</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χωρεῖν_δόμων_960">, advancing (so as to come) in front of the house. The phrase is correct, though it would more naturally suggest a movement <emph>from within</emph> the house, as in <ref n="Eur. Hec. 59">Eur. <title>Hec.</title>59</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγετ’, ὦ παῖδες, τὴν γραῦν πρὸ δόμων.— <emph>λέγουσιν</emph> </quote>: the Chorus may be supposed to overhear murmurs of astonishment and anguish from servants of the house, who are watching the approach of the litter.—As to the proposed changes in this v. (cr. n.), <ref target="cl969">see on 969</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θαῦμα_961"> has been needlessly suspected: it is often said of persons (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 1004">cp. 1004</ref>, and <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 9.190"> <title>Od.</title>9. 190</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">θαῦμ’ ἐτέτυκτο πελώριον</quote> </cit>, of the Cyclops), and is here far more forcible than </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θέαμα_961">. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀγχοῦ_ἄρα_962"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> At this moment the bearers of the litter,—first descried by the servants of the house (960),—become visible to the Trachinian Maidens; who say, in effect, ‘It seems that the woe presaged by our voice is (even) closer at hand than we knew.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀγχοῦ_προὔκλαιον_962"> is a short way of saying, ‘the subject of our boding lament is near and not distant.’ We might supply <quote xml:lang="grc">οὖσα</quote> with the verb: but it seems better to supply <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄν</quote> with the adverbs. Similar, though less bold, is <ref n="Soph. Phil. 26"> <title>Ph.</title>26</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὔργον οὐ μακρὰν λέγεις</quote>, ‘the task of which thou speakest is not distant.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀξύφωνος_ἀηδών_962"> refers to <quote xml:lang="grc">προὔκλαιον</quote> only: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> the point of comparison is merely the clear, sad note. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 105">Cp. 105</ref> n.: Theocr. 12. 6 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀηδὼν</quote> | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">λιγύφωνος</quote>. Here <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀξύφωνος</quote> well suits the context, since <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀξύς</quote> and its compounds so often refer to tones of grief: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 424"> <title>Ant.</title>424</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄρνιθος ὀξὺν φθόγγον</quote>: <emph>ib.</emph> 1316 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀξυκώκυτον</quote> : <ref n="Soph. El. 244"> <title>El.</title>244</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀξυτόνων γόων</quote>.—It would be forced to explain the simile by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀγχοῦ_962"> (because the nightingale often sings close to dwellings), or by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μακρὰν_962"> (because its note is far-reaching). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξένῳν_964"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> It should be observed how the poet has marked successive stages in the approach of the litter. When it first comes into view, the Chorus note the foreign <emph>aspect</emph> of the bearers. In another moment, they are listening for a <emph>sound</emph> (<quote xml:lang="grc">πᾷ δ’ αὖ φορεῖ νιν</quote>); and the silence dismays them.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξένων_βάσις_964">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ξένοι βαδίζοντες</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 868"> <title>Ph.</title>868</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκούρημα...ξένων</quote> (n.). The conject. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στάσις_964"> (‘company’), though specious, seems less fitting here. It would be unsafe to argue against </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βάσις_964"> from the fact that <quote xml:lang="grc">βάσιν</quote> closes v. 967. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 76"> <title>Ant.</title>76</ref>, where <quote xml:lang="grc">κείσομαι</quote> stands at the end of a clause, though it occurs also in 73.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξόμιλος_964">, living out of <emph>our</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅμιλος</quote>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘foreign.’ Cp. <title>I. A.</title> 735 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ καλὸν ἐν ὄχλῳ σ’ ἐξομιλεῖσθαι</quote> (midd.) <quote xml:lang="grc">στρατοῦ</quote> (said by Agam. to his wife), ‘to live abroad’ (out of thy proper <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁμιλία</quote>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πᾷ_αὖ_965">, ‘and then in what <emph>manner</emph> ...?’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βαρεῖαν_965">, heavy with sorrow, slow; as <quote xml:lang="grc">κούφη βάσις</quote> would be a joyously light step.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φέρει_βάσιν_965">, lit., ‘carries the step forward,’ ‘moves on its way.’ <quote xml:lang="grc">βάσις</quote> (964) is subject to <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρει</quote>, but there is little real harshness in this, since <quote xml:lang="grc">ξένων βάσις</quote> is a mere periphrasis. Schneidewin well compares <ref n="Soph. Aj. 14"> <title>Ai.</title>14</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ φθέγμ’ Ἀθάνας</quote>, followed by <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς εὐμαθές σου</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">φώνημ’ ἀκούω</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀναύδατος_968">, ‘without speech,’— either from his own lips, or from those of his bearers. Cp. the comprehensive sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ στενακτός</quote> in <ref n="Soph. OC 1663."> <title>O. C.</title>1663.</ref>In <ref n="Soph. Aj. 713"> <title>Ai.</title>713</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναύδατος</quote> has its pass. sense. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_χρή_969">, <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> In order to obtain an exact metrical agreement with 960, <quote xml:lang="grc">χωρεῖν πρὸ δόμων λέγουσιν, <emph>θανόντα</emph> </quote> has been changed (1) by Hermann, to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φθίμενον_969">: (2) by Bothe, to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θάνατον_969">. The latter seems preferable; for, though <quote xml:lang="grc">κατὰ</quote> suits <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕπνον</quote> better than <quote xml:lang="grc"> θάνατον</quote>, that turn of phrase may be regarded as an afterthought. ‘Death, is it,—or sleep?’ But I refrain from altering </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θανόντα_969">, because it is doubtful whether metre requires that the dactyl should hold the same place here as in 960: see Metrical Analysis.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρή_969">, since the constr. is, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_χρή_969_2"> (<quote xml:lang="grc"> κρῖναι</quote>)<emph>;</emph> (<quote xml:lang="grc">πότερον</quote>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θανόντα_νιν_969"> etc., as in <ref n="Soph. El. 766"> <title>El.</title>766</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί ταῦτα, πότερον εὐτυχῆ λέγω</quote> etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_μήσομαι_973">; the delib. subjunct. is combined with a fut. ind., as in <ref n="Eur. Ion 758"> Eur. <title>Ion</title>758</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴπωμεν ἢ σιγῶμεν ἢ τί δράσομεν</quote> <emph>;</emph> For <quote xml:lang="grc">μήσομαι</quote> (devise as a remedy), cp. <ref n="Aesch. Seven 1057">Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 1057</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί πάθω<emph>;</emph> τί δὲ δρῶ<emph>;</emph> τί δὲ μήσωμαι</quote> <emph>;</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὠμόφρονος_975"> does not refer to his general character, but means that he is exasperated by these torments: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1035">cp. 1035</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄχος, ᾧ μ’ ἐχόλωσεν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προπετής_976">, lying prostrate in the litter,—in a deathlike swoon. (It may be doubted whether the word here implies, ‘lying <emph>on his face</emph>,’ as the schol. explains it.) Others understand, ‘verging on death.’ But, when <quote xml:lang="grc"> προπετής</quote>=‘on the brink of,’ <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπί</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴς τι</quote>) is usu. added, as in <ref n="Eur. Alc. 908">Eur. <title>Alc.</title>908</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολιὰς ἐπὶ χαίτας</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤδη προπετής</quote>. Eur. Itseems impossible that, without such help, <quote xml:lang="grc">προπετής</quote> should express ‘moribund.’ In <ref n="Eur. Alc. 143">Eur. <title>Alc.</title>143</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤδη προνωπής ἐστι καὶ ψυχορραγεῖ</quote>, which Paley compares, the adj.=‘drooping.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δακὼν_976">, as by biting the lips,—a proverbial phrase: fr. 811 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀδόντι πρῖε τὸ στόμα</quote>: <ref n="Hom. Od. 1.381"> <title>Od.</title>1. 381</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀδὰξ ἐν χείλεσι φύντες</quote>: <title>Nub.</title> 1369 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν θυμὸν δακών</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_ξεγερεῖς_978">, a sharp prohibition: <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 462">Ar. <title>Ran.</title>462</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ μὴ διατρίψεις</quote>: cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. OC 177.">Soph. <title>O. C.</title>177.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κάτοχον_978"> with dat., as <ref n="Eur. Hec. 1090">Eur. <title>Hec.</title>1090</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἄρει κάτοχον γένος</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φοιτάδα_980">, coming at intervals: <quote xml:lang="grc">φοιτᾶν</quote> was said in this sense of intermittent diseases: see n. on <ref n="Soph. Phil. 758"> <title>Ph.</title>758</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥκει γὰρ αὕτη διὰ χρόνου, πλάνοις ἴσως</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἐξεπλήσθη</quote>. Not merely <quote xml:lang="grc">μανιώδη</quote>, as the schol. explains it. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_μοι_981">, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστί</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 806"> <title>Ph.</title>806</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀπὶ σοὶ στένων κακά</quote> (the ills which lie upon thee).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄπλετον_981">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἄπειρον</quote>, ‘immense’; a word of doubtful origin, sometimes connected with the root of <quote xml:lang="grc">πλέως</quote>, as meaning (1) ‘which cannot be filled’; or (2) ‘what exceeds measure,’ a sense which Lobeck sought through <quote xml:lang="grc">πλέθρον</quote>. The word occurs in Attic prose.—If no stop is placed after </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄπλετον_981_2">, then <quote xml:lang="grc">βάρος ἄπλετον</quote> becomes an acc. of the ‘inner object’ with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμμέμονε_981">: ‘is wild <emph> with</emph> an infinite weight of woe.’ But </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπί_981"> is then very awkward, whether we assume tmesis, or still join it with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοι_981">. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_Ζεῦ_983">: the hero's utterance begins,—as the play ends,—with his father's name.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖσι_983">=<quote xml:lang="grc">τίσι</quote>, contracted from the Ionic <quote xml:lang="grc">τέοισι</quote> ( <ref n="Hdt. 1. 37">Her.1. 37</ref>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πεπονημένος_983">: cp. Aeschin. or. 2 § 36 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν δῆμον καταπεπονημένον</quote> (‘exhausted’).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλήκτοις_983">, the regular form of this epic word: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄληκτος</quote> is very rare (<title>C. I. G.</title> 6303). For the <quote xml:lang="grc">λλ</quote>, cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 12.224"> <title>Od.</title>12. 224</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπολλήξειαν</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἴμοι_μοι_986">. The addition of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοι_986">, Brunck's remedy for the metrical defect, is better than Bergk's insertion, after </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀδύναις_986">, of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧδ_986">, which would have a weak effect there. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡ_δ_987">, the personified <quote xml:lang="grc">νόσος</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1084">cp. 1084</ref>: so <ref n="Soph. Phil. 807"> <title>Ph.</title>807</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥδε</quote>, and <emph> ib.</emph> 758 <quote xml:lang="grc">αὕτη</quote>. Blaydes writes </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἅδ_987"> (Doric), which accords with <quote xml:lang="grc">γᾶς</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">τλάμων</quote>, but not with <quote xml:lang="grc">πεπονημένος</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλήκτοις</quote>: the Doricism of tragic anapaests is not always consistent; see Appendix to <ref n="Soph. Ant. 110."> <title>Ant.</title>110.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βρύκει_987">: so <ref n="Soph. Phil. 745"> <title>Ph.</title>745</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βρύκομαι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἆρ_ἐξῄδη_988"> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσον κέρδος ἦν σε τιγῇ κεύθειν</quote> <emph>;</emph> ‘Did I not well know,’ etc., —referring to 974 <quote xml:lang="grc">σίγα, τέκνον, κ.τ.λ.</quote> Cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 1019">Ar. <title>Av.</title>1019</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΜΕ. οἴμοι κακοδαίμων. ΠΕ. οὐκ ἔλεγον ἐγὼ πάλαι<emph>;</emph> <emph> κεύθειν</emph> </quote> is really trans. in sense, ‘to hide (thy grief),’ though the object is not expressed: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 85">Soph. <title>Ant.</title>85</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κρυφῇ δὲ κεῦθε</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τοὔργον</quote>). The rare intrans. <quote xml:lang="grc">κεύθω</quote>=‘to be hidd n’ ( <ref n="Soph. OT 968">Soph. <title>O. T.</title>968</ref> n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξῄδη_ς_988"> is Wecklein's correction of the MS. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξῄδης_988">, instead of which we must at least write </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξῄδησθ_988"> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 447"> <title>Ant.</title>447</ref>). Two explanations of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξῄδησθ’</quote> have been given. (1) ‘Did you well know’ (as soon as Heracles began to speak<ref n="Soph. Ant. 983"> Ant., 983</ref>),— <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘have you now learned?’ Such is the schol.'s view: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆρα...ὅσον ἦν κέρδος τὸ σιωπᾶν ἔγνως</quote> <emph>;</emph> He classed the pluperf., then, with those aorists, referring to a moment just past, which we render by a present tense ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1289"> <title>Ph.</title>1289</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπώμοσ’</quote>, n.). This is possible, but awkward. (2) ‘Did you not well know (<emph> beforehand</emph>),— <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘had not I clearly told you?’ (Paley). The tense has then its usual force; but the words lose their special point,— which is that the <emph>result</emph> must have shown him the value of the neglected advice.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κεύθειν_σκεδάσαι_988">: for the pres. inf. (of a continued act), combined with the aor. inf. (of a momentary act), cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 95"> <title>Ph.</title>95</ref> <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1397."> Ph., 1397.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρατὸς_θ_990">: the phrase suggests a movement of the head at the moment when the sleeper opens his eyes: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 866"> <title>Ph.</title>866</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κινεῖ γὰρ ἁνὴρ <*> κἀνάγει κάρα</quote>.—Wecklein, omitting <*> takes </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βλεφάρων_ὕπνον_990"> as ‘sleep of the eyelids.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στέρξαιμι_992">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 486">cp. 486</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 538"> <title>Ph.</title>538</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">στέργειν κακά</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Κηναία_993">, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc"> Κηναίων</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 818">cp. 818</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μητρῷον</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρηπὶς_993">, the substructure, basis, of the altar; <ref n="Eur. Her. 984">Eur. <title>H. F.</title>984</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφὶ βωμίαν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπτηξε κρηπῖδ’</quote>, ‘at the altarsteps.’ The word has a picturesque force here, as recalling the moment when the altars were founded by him (237). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἱερῶν_οἵων_994">, gen. of price.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπί_μοι_994">, lit., ‘in my case’: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1384"> <title>Ph.</title>1384</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγεις δ’ Ἀτρείδαις ὄφελος ἢ )π’ ἐμοὶ τόδε</quote>; Others explain, ‘<emph>against</emph> me,’ ‘to my hurt’; but this suits the irony less well.— The MS. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἠνύσω_994"> has been altered by many recent edd. to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἤνυσας_994">: but the proper force of the midd., ‘to obtain,’ ‘win’ ( <title>Plut.</title> 196 etc.), seems fitting here, since the sacrificial altars may be said to have <emph>earned</emph> the recompense given by Zeus. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἤνυσας_994_2"> would be simply, ‘hast effected.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_Ζεῦ_994"> at the end of the sentence: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1139"> <title>Ph.</title>1139</ref> n </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔθου_λώβαν_996">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλωβήσω με</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 223"> <title>O. C.</title>223</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἣν_997">, referring back to <quote xml:lang="grc">κρηπίς</quote> (993); <ref n="Soph. Trach. 358">cp. 358</ref> (n.). Wunder needlessly placed </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἣν_ὄσσοις_997"> immediately after 993.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μή_ποτ_997"> belongs to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσιδεῖν_997">, not to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὤφελον_997">, though the latter might have come between them; cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 969"> <title>Ph.</title>969</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μή ποτ’ ὤφελον λιπεῖν</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκήλητον_997">: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνίατον, ἀκαταπράϋντον.—<emph>ἄνθος</emph> </quote>=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκμήν</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 959"> <title>Ant.</title>959</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τᾶς μανίας δεινὸν</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνθηρόν τε μένος</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καταδερχθῆναι_997">, inf. expressing result, without <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1076"> <title>Ant.</title>1076</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ληφθῆναι</quote> (n.). Though the malady is his own, he can be said ‘to look upon it,’ in the sense of experiencing it: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 832"> <title>O. T.</title>832</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσθεν ἢ τοιάνδ’ ἰδεῖν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">κηλῖδ’ ἐμαυτῷ συμφορᾶς ἀφιγμένην</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀοιδός_1000">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπῳδός</quote>, one who uses <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπῳδαί</quote>, incantations, in healing: see on <ref n="Soph. OC 1194."> <title>O. C.</title>1194.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίς_χειροτέχνης_1000">, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστίν</quote>. (There is no art. before <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀοιδός</quote>, because the insertion of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὃς_1000"> was an afterthought.) This is a climax; since, when gentle <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπῳδαί</quote> failed, the next resort was to drugs or surgery: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 581"> <title>Ai.</title>581</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ πρὸς ἰατροῦ σοφοῦ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">θρηνεῖν ἐπῳδὰς πρὸς τομῶντι πήματι. <emph>χειροτέχνης ἰατορίας</emph> </quote> does not mean definitely, ‘one who uses a skilled <emph>hand</emph> in healing,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, a <quote xml:lang="grc">χειρουργός</quote>, surgeon, <emph>as distinguished from</emph> a physician; it rather means properly, ‘a practical artist’ (as dist. from an amateur) ‘in healing’; but, at the same time, the <quote xml:lang="grc">χειρο</quote> in the compound serves to <emph>suggest</emph> the <quote xml:lang="grc">τομαί</quote> employed by the surgeon. This is quite Sophoclean. Cp. <ref n="Thuc. 6. 72">Thuc.6. 72</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰδιώτας, ὡς εἰπεῖν, χειροτέχναις ἀνταγωνισαμένους</quote>, ‘having been pitted like amateurs, as one might say, against masters of the art’ (where the dat., and not <quote xml:lang="grc">χειροτέχνας</quote>, is clearly right).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χωρὶς_Ζηνὸς_1000">=‘with the exception of Zeus’: not, ‘without the help of Zeus’ (schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ μὴ ὁ Ζεὺς βούλοιτο</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θαῦμ_ἰδοίμην_1003">: ‘I should look upon him, from afar, as a wonder,’—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘I should marvel as soon as he came within my ken.’ He means that he might scan the horizon long enough, in the vain hope of such a prodigy appearing.—Others join </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόρρωθεν_1003"> with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θμα_1003">: ‘I should behold him as a wonder from some distant region’: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, the place which contains him must be distant indeed. Hermann further supposed a question: ‘am I likely to see such a wonder coming from afar?’—The phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">τηλόθεν εἰσορῶν</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 454"> <title>Ph.</title>454</ref> is not similar: see n. there. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐᾶτέ_εὐνᾶσθαι_1005">. A restoration of this corrupt passage turns chiefly on the following points. (1) The corresponding verses of the antistrophe (1016 f.) may be taken as showing the true metre. (2) L's variant for </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐνᾶσαι_1005"> in 1005, viz. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὕστατον_1005">, may therefore be received. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐνάσαι_1005"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">α^</quote>), from <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐνάζω</quote> is impossible, since, like <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐνᾶσαι</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">εὐνάω</quote>), it could only be transitive. (3) In 1006 the MS. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δύστανον_1005"> is clearly wrong; it may have been either a gloss on </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δύσμορον_1005">, or a corruption of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὕστατον_1005_2">. (4) Hermann's reading in 1006, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐᾶθ_εὐνᾶσθαι_1005">, is strongly confirmed by the metrical correspondence with 1017, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μολὼν_φεῦ_1005">,—a verse of undoubted soundness. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πᾷ_ψαύεις_1007">: a remonstrance against being touched at all. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 817"> <title>Ph.</title>817</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπό μ’ ὀλεῖς, ἢν προσθίγῃς</quote>. Hyllus seeks to place him in a more comfortable position,—as Heracles himself soon requests (1025).—A comparison with the antistrophic verse<ref n="Soph. Phil. 1027"> Ph., 1027</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">θρῴσκει δ’ αὖ, θρῴσκει δειλαία</quote>, shows the loss of a syllable here. Hermann follows Seidler in repeating </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πᾷ_1007">, which is the simplest and most probable remedy. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνατέτροφας_1009">, from <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀνατρέπω</quote>: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅ τι ἂν ἡσυχάσῃ τοῦ κακοῦ τουτου</quote>, <quote xml:lang="grc">πάλιν κινήσας ἀνέτρεψας</quote>. Cp. <title>Hist. An.</title> 8. 24 (p. 605 <emph>a</emph> 11) <quote xml:lang="grc">κἂν ᾖ καθαρὰ</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ ὕδατα</quote> ), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνατρέπουσιν αὐτὰ οἱ ἵπποι ταῖς ὁπλαῖς</quote>, ‘trouble’ them. For the perf., cp. Andoc. or. 1 § 131 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλιτήριον αὑτῷ ἔτρεφεν, ὃς ἀνατέτροφεν ἐκείνου τὸν πλοῦτον</quote> (‘overthrown’). Aeschin. or. 1 § 190 <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλεις ἀνατετροφότας</quote>: or. 3 § 158 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν πόλιν ἄρδην ἀνατετροφότα</quote>. In <ref n="Soph. OC 186">Soph. <title>O. C.</title>186</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τέτροφεν</quote> is from <quote xml:lang="grc">τρέφω</quote>: but the classical use of that perf. is ordinarily confined to the intrans. sense ( <ref n="Hom. Od. 23.237">Hom. <title>Od.</title>23. 237</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τέτροφεν ἅλμη</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅ_μύσῃ_1009">, anything that <emph>has</emph> closed the eyes, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, any part of the pain that has been lulled to rest. This is simpler than to supply <quote xml:lang="grc">νόσον</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνατέτροφας</quote>, and to take <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅ τι</quote> as acc. of respect (‘in so far as...’). Cp. <title>Vesp.</title> 92 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢν δ’ οὖν καταμύσῃ κἂν ἄχνην</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥδ_1010">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 987">cp. 987</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόθεν_ἔστ_1010">, ‘whence are ye?’ Of what stock? Can ye be indeed of Hellenic race, and yet so heartlessly ungrateful? Cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 17.373"> <title>Od.</title>17. 373</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πόθεν γένος εὔχεται εἶναι</quote> <emph>;</emph>—Hermann explains </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόθεν_ἐστέ_1010"> as=‘whence do ye appear to aid me?’ (<emph>unde mihi auxilio adestis?</emph>):—a complaint that they do <emph> not</emph> appear. He compares <ref n="Hom. Od. 2.267"> <title>Od.</title>2. 267</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σχεδόθεν δέ οἱ ἦλθεν Ἀθήνη</quote>: but might better have cited <ref n="Hom. Il. 16.800"> <title>Il.</title>16. 800</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σχεδόθεν δέ οἱ ἦεν ὄλεθρος</quote>. The version is tenable in itself, but is not well suited to the context. Heracles is addressing the men who are actually around him,—the Greeks (some of them his own mercenaries) who have brought him from Euboea. Cp. the very similar passage in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1203"> <title>Ph.</title>1203</ref> ff.: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’, ὦ ξένοι, ἕν γέ μοι εὖχος ὀρέξατε....ξίφος, εἴ ποθεν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ γένυν, ἢ βελέων τι, προπέμψατε</quote>. He is not making a merely rhetorical appeal to the absent,—‘<emph>all</emph> those who had been benefited by him,’ as the schol. says. In that case, he would not say, <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντων <emph>Ἑλλάνων</emph> ἀδικώτατοι ἄνδρες</quote>: he had toiled for all Hellenes. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὓς_1011"> refers to <quote xml:lang="grc"> Ἑλλάνων</quote>, not to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνδρες</quote>. If the acc. be right, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καθαίρων_1011"> here=‘ridding of pests.’ In this sense, the verb is properly said of places (1061 <quote xml:lang="grc">γαῖαν καθαίρων</quote>): but the bolder use here seems possible, and is not excluded by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν πόντῳ</quote>, since the thought is of the gain to seafarers. <*> I hesitate, then, to receive the tempting </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἷς_1011"> (‘for whose good’). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_πόντῳ_1012">: cp. <ref n="Eur. Her. 222">Eur. <title>H. F.</title>222</ref> ff., where Amphitryon denounces the ingratitude of Greece towards Heracles:—<quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ Ἑλλάδ’ ᾔνες’, οὐδ’ ἀνέξομαί</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποτε</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">σιγῶν, κακίστην λαμβάνων ἐς παῖδ’ ἐμόν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἣν χρῆν νεοσσοῖς τοῖσδε πῦρ, λόγχας, ὅπλα</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρουσαν ἐλθεῖν, ποντίων καθαρμάτων</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">χέρσου τ’ ἀμοιβάς</quote>, ‘as a reward for purging sea and land.’ Cp. <emph>ib.</emph> 400: ‘he went into the uttermost parts of the deep, making peace for the oars of men.’ <ref n="Pind. N. 1">Pind. <title>N.</title>1. 63</ref>(of Heracles) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσσους μὲν ἐν χέρσῳ κτανών</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσσους δὲ πόντῳ θῆρας ἀϊδροσίκας</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δρία_1012">, from the same rt as <quote xml:lang="grc">δρῦς, δρυμός, δένδρον, δόρυ</quote> (Curt. <title> Etym.</title> § 275): the only sing. found is (<quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">δρίος.—<emph>ὠλεκόμαν</emph> </quote>, impf. of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀλέκω</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1285"> <title>Ant.</title>1285</ref>), expresses the wearing effect of continual labours: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 252"> <title>Ph.</title>252</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">διωλλύμην</quote>: <emph> ib.</emph> 686 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤλλυθ’</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_ἐπιτρέψει_1012">; ‘and now will no one <emph>turn</emph> fire or sword <emph>upon</emph> me,’— <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘come to my rescue’ with it? The repetition of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_1012"> with the verb gives a passionate emphasis: see n. on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 6"> <title>Ant.</title>6</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ὄπωπ’</quote>, after <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν γὰρ οὔτ’ ἀλγεινὸν</quote> etc.).—The reading </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀποτρέψει_1012"> has better authority (cr. n.), but seems untenable. It has been explained as, (1) ‘not-<emph> avert</emph>,’ =‘apply’: (2) ‘<emph>divert</emph> (from other uses),’ ‘turn wholly against me.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῷδε_1012">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοί</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 305">cp. 305</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πῦρ_1012">: thus Philoctetes prays to die even by fire ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 800"> <title>Ph.</title>800</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔγχος_1012"> =<quote xml:lang="grc">ξίφος</quote> : <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1032">cp. 1032</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 95"> <title>Ai.</title>95</ref> etc.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βίᾳ_1012"> ought to be substituted for <quote xml:lang="grc">βίου</quote>, which might easily have arisen from </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_στυγεροῦ_1012"> .—Cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 14.497"> <title>Il.</title>14. 497</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπήραξεν δὲ χαμᾶζε</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτῇ σὺν πήληκι κάρη</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὔργον_τόδε_1018">, the task of lifting the sufferer (who is lying <quote xml:lang="grc">προπετής</quote>, 976) into a position of greater ease (1025 <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσλαβε κουφίσας</quote> ).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μεῖζον_1018"> is proleptic with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνήκει_1018">, ‘has risen, so as to be greater’: cp. <ref n="Dem. 2.8">Dem. or. 2 § 8</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤρθη μέγας</quote>. The usu. constr. of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνήκειν</quote>, as=‘to reach’ a certain standard, <*>s with <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς</quote>, as if here we had <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς μεῖζόν τι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_ῥώμαν_1019">, with <quote xml:lang="grc">μεῖζον</quote>: <title>O.C.</title> 598 <quote xml:lang="grc">μεῖζον ἢ κατ’ ἄνθρωπον</quote> (n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοὶ_ἑτοίμα_1019">, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥώμα</quote>, ‘for strength is at thy command,’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐς_σῴζειν_1019">, ‘in too large a measure for the saving of him by my means,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> ‘so largely, that you have no need to save him by my means.’</gloss>
lemma: ἐς πλέον ἢ δἰ ἐμοῦ σῴζειν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοί_ὄμμα_1019"> | </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔμπλεον_σῴζειν_1019">. The correction of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμπλεον</quote> (‘full’) to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς πλέον</quote> is due to Meineke, and is confirmed by the following </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_1019"> with the inf.: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1293"> <title>O. T.</title>1293</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μεῖζον ἢ φέρειν</quote> : <ref n="Eur. Hec. 1107">Eur. <title>Hec.</title>1107</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κρείσσον’ ἢ φέρειν κακά</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοί_ὄμμα_1019_2">, are unquestionably corrupt. This is shown by two things: (1) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄμμα_1019"> is incongruous with the context; strength, not keenness of sight, is in question; and, even if it were suitable, it could not be reconciled with any probable emendation of the following words. (2) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τε_1019"> is unmeaning and impossible. I believe that I have found the solution by the slight change of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοί_ὄμμα_1019_3"> into </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοὶ_ἑτοίμα_1019_2">. The corruption began by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οίμα_1019"> passing into </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄμμα_1019_2">. How easy this would have been, may be judged from <ref n="Hom. Il. 21.252"> <title>Il.</title>21. 252</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰετοῦ οἴματ’ ἔχων</quote>, where Philetas read <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄμματ’</quote>, as in <ref n="Hom. Il. 8.349"> <title>Il.</title>8. 349</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">Γοργοῦς ὄμματ’ ἔχων</quote>, Aristarchus read <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴματ’</quote> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λαθίπονον_ὀδυνᾶν_1021">: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 1002"> <title>El.</title>1002</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλυπος ἄτης</quote> : <title>I. T.</title> 450 <quote xml:lang="grc">δουλείας</quote>... | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">παυσίπονος</quote>. The adj., found only here and in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 711"> <title>Ai.</title>711</ref>, recalls the epic <quote xml:lang="grc"> λα^θικηδής</quote>. It is proleptic predicate with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξανύσαι_1021">, ‘to effect that his life shall forget,’ etc. The reference of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βίοτον_1021"> to Heracles would be clearer if we could read </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔστι_οἱ_1021"> (not <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔστιν οἱ</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 650">cp. 650</ref>): but </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοι_1021"> is confirmed by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔνδοθεν_1021">, ‘from my own resources’ (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 730">cp. 730</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴκοι</quote> n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θύραθεν_1021">, by the help of others: cp. <ref n="Eur. Her. 713">Eur. <title>H. F.</title>713</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς θύραθεν εἰκάσαι</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign> without personal knowledge).—The MS. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀδύναν_βιότου_1021"> is tenable, but less probable, and much less forcible.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοιαῦτα_Ζεύς_1021">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, so grievous a doom. Not, ‘such <emph> healing</emph> is of Zeus alone.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τᾷδε_1023">: he indicates the place at which Hyllus is to take hold of him.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τᾷδέ_πρόσλαβεκουφίσας_1023">, literally, ‘lend a helping hand in raising me thus’: the pron. depends on the partic. only. For this sense of the verb, cp. <title> Legg.</title> p. 897 D <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ἐμὲ τῆς ἀποκρίσεως ὑμῖν... δίκαιον προσλαμβάνειν</quote>, ‘it is right that I, too, should <emph> help</emph> you with the answer.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρόσλαβέ_με_1023"> could not mean literally, ‘lay hold on me,’ which would be <quote xml:lang="grc">προσλαβοῦ μου</quote> (cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Lys. 202">Ar. <title>Lys.</title>202</ref>, etc.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κουφίσας_1023"> denotes the act in which, when done, the help will consist. For this quasi-proleptic use of the aor. partic., cp. <ref n="Plat. Gorg. 516B">Plat. <title>Gorg.</title> 516B</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τόδε τοίνυν μοι χάρισαι ἀποκρινάμενος</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰὼ_δαῖμον_1026">: the movement causes a new access of pain. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1186"> <title>Ph.</title>1186</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰαῖ, αἰαῖ</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">δαίμων δαίμων</quote>. <ref n="Soph. OT 1311"> <title>O. T.</title>1311</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰὼ δαῖμον</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θρῴσκει_1027"> denotes the shooting spasms: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1083">cp. 1083</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">διῇξε</quote> : <ref n="Soph. Phil. 743"> <title>Ph.</title>743</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">διέρχεται</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">διέρχεται.—<emph>δειλαία</emph> </quote>: the <quote xml:lang="grc">αι</quote> is long here, though sometimes short ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1310"> <title>Ant.</title>1310</ref> n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀποτίβατος_1027">=<quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀπρόσβατος</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1214">cp. 1214</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποτιψαύων</quote> (n.). The personified <quote xml:lang="grc">νόσος</quote> is ‘unapproachable’ in the sense that no healer can successfully cope with it. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1093">Cp. 1093</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄπλατον...κἀπροσήγορον</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Παλλάς_1031">. Athena was always a guardian goddess to her half-brother, Heracles; of whom she says in <ref n="Hom. Il. 8.363"> <title>Il.</title>8. 363</ref> ff., <quote xml:lang="grc">τειρόμενον σώεσκον ὑπ’ Εὐρυσθῆος ἀέθλων·</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ τοι ὁ μὲν κλαίεσκε πρὸς οὐρανόν, αὐτὰρ ἐμὲ Ζεὺς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ ἐπαλεξήσουσαν ἀπ’ οὐρανόθεν προΐαλλεν</quote>. She was constantly represented in Greek art as present with Heracles during his labours, or as honouring and comforting him in seasons of repose. Sometimes she gives him a flower or a wreath; sometimes a refreshing draught. And, after the close of his mortal toils, it is Athena Nikè who escorts him to Olympus. (Cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. Phil. 728."> <title>Ph.</title>728.</ref>) The art-literature of the subject will be found in Roscher, <title>Lex.</title>, p. 2215. Attic black-figure vases often illustrate this relationship,—one which was the more welcome to Athenians because Heracles was essentially a Dorian hero. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φύτοῤ_1032">, Dindorf's correction of <quote xml:lang="grc">φύσαντ’</quote>, gives an incomparably better verse than Fröhlich's </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_οἴκτιῤ_1032">. It is very improbable that </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἴκτιῤ_1032"> would have been corrupted into the aor. partic.; but a rare word, such as </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φύτοῤ_1032_2">, would easily have become </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φύσαντ_1032">. Dindorf is clearly right in holding that the <quote xml:lang="grc">ν</quote> of <quote xml:lang="grc">φύτωρ</quote> would be short, though metrical convenience might sometimes cause it to be lengthened in such compounds as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμπελοφύτορα</quote> (<title>Anth.</title> 6. 44), which could not otherwise come into a hexameter. He might have added that the verse, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀθάνατοι δὲ Πτέρωτα, διὰ πτεροφύτορ’ ἀνάγκην</quote>, is prefaced by Plato with the remark that it is <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ σφόδρα τι ἔμμετρον</quote>,— a comment which, as W. H. Thompson observes, may apply to the <quote xml:lang="grc">υ—</quote> no less than to the <quote xml:lang="grc"> δε^</quote> (<ref n="Plat. Phaedrus 252c"> <title>Phaedr.</title> p. 252 C</ref>). Hesychius has <quote xml:lang="grc">φύτορες· γεννήτορες</quote>. For the spelling <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκτίρας</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 464">cp. 464</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνεπίφθονον_1032">: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνεμέσητον, ἐφ ῳ οὐδείς σε μέμψεται ὡς πατροκτόνον</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμᾶς_κλῇδος_1035">: ‘<emph>under</emph> the collar-bone’ must denote a stab in the upper region of the breast: it cannot mean decapitation. For <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπό</quote> with gen., in a local sense, cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 65"> <title>Ant.</title>65</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐχόλωσεν_1035">, an epic word ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 18.111"> <title>Il.</title>18. 111</ref>), here partly suggested by the thought of the venom working in his veins. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1142">Cp. 1142</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκμῆναι</quote>. The <emph>v.l.</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐχόλησεν_1035"> (schol.) was intended to mean, ‘has used the hydra's gall against me’: but the Attic sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">χολᾶν</quote> was ‘to be <quote xml:lang="grc"> μελάγχολος</quote>.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὔτως_1039">: for the smooth breathing, see n. on <title> O.T.</title> 931.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γλυκὺς_1039">; cp. <title>O.C.</title> 106 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴτ’, ὦ γλυκεῖαι παῖδες ἀρχαίου Σκότου</quote> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐθαίμων_1041">: nom. for voc., like <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ τλάμων</quote> (<title>O.C.</title> 185). This adj., found only here,=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὅμαιμος, ὁμαίμων</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OC 330"> <title>O. C.</title>330</ref> n.), <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐθόμαιμος</quote> (<emph>ib.</emph> 335), as denoting the fraternal tie. But <quote xml:lang="grc">αὔθαιμος</quote>, as used in <title>O.C.</title> 1078, is merely ‘kinsman.’—It is as the son of Zeus that he invokes Hades. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὠκυπέτᾳ_1042">, an epic epith. for a horse ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 8.42"> <title>Il.</title>8. 42</ref>), or a bird ( <title>Op</title> 210). <quote xml:lang="grc">‐<emph>φθίσας</emph> </quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ι^</quote>); 709 n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἵαις_1045">, not </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἵας_1045">, is clearly right here. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλαύνειν</quote> can take a cogn. acc. denoting the <emph>course</emph> on which one is driven, as <title> Nub.</title> 29 <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλοὺς τὸν πατέρ’ ἐλαύνεις δρόμους</quote>: but when it means ‘to vex’ or ‘harass,’ the troubles inflicted are expressed by the instrum. dat., as in the examples cited by Dindorf: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 275"> <title>Ai.</title>275</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λύπῃ...ἐλήλαται</quote> : <ref n="Eur. Andr. 31">Eur. <title>Andr.</title>31</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καχοῖς ἐλαύνομαι</quote>: <ref n="Eur. Ion 1620"> Eur. <title>Ion</title>1620</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλαύνεται συμφοραῖς</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_δὴ_1046"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> This speech, down to v. 1102, is translated by Cicero in <ref n="Cic. Tusc. 2. 8"> <title>Tusc.</title>2. 8</ref>, where the fact that the poets recognise pain as an evil is illustrated by the laments of Philoctetes, Heracles and Prometheus.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θερμὰ_1046">: <quote xml:lang="grc">θερμός</quote> was said (1) of a hot or rash temperament ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 88"> <title>Ant.</title>88</ref>): (2) of a rash deed, as in <title>Plut</title> 415 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ θερμὸν ἔργον κἀνόσιον καὶ παράνομον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τολμῶντε δρᾶν</quote>. Here <quote xml:lang="grc">θερμὰ</quote> is not ‘rash,’ but expresses intense conflict with deadly peril; as we speak of ‘a hot fight.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοὐ_κακὰ_1046"> fitly follows </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θερμά_1046">, the word which recalls the moment of dire stress. His trials had been fiery, and grievous, not in report or name alone. No <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγος</quote> could express to others what the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔργα</quote> had been to the doer. In <ref n="Soph. El. 761"> <title>El.</title>761</ref> ff. a similar antithesis is implied: <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιαῦτά σοι ταῦτ’ ἐστίν, ὡς μὲν ἐν λόγῳ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλγεινά, τοῖς δ’ ἰδοῦσιν, οἵπερ εἴδομεν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">μέγιστα πάντων ὧν ὄπωπ’ ἐγὼ κακῶν</quote>: grievous enough to hear; but far worse to see. For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_λόγῳ_1046">, cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 813"> <title>Ai.</title>813</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κοὐ λόγῳ δείξω μόνον</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 1453"> <title>El.</title>1453</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀπέδειξεν οὐ λόγῳ μόνον</quote>. <ref n="Thuc. 6. 18">Thuc.6. 18</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμύνεσθαι οὐ λόγῳ ἀλλ’ ἔργῳ μᾶλλον</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_κακὰ_1046">, is certainly wrong, for two reasons. (1) When the required sense is, ‘grievous to tell,’ <quote xml:lang="grc">κακά</quote> becomes, for Greek poetical idiom, too weak; we need such a word as <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινά</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλγεινά</quote>. This objection does not apply to a phrase of ironical form, such as <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ λόγῳ κακά</quote>. (2) Idiom would require <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ λέγειν</quote> rather than <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ λόγῳ</quote>. Cicero, no doubt, read <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ λόγῳ</quote> (‘<title>O multa</title> dictu gravia, <emph>perpessu aspera</emph>’); but that proves nothing. In <ref n="Soph. Ant. 4"> <title>Ant.</title>4</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτ’ ἄτης ἄτερ</quote> was the only reading known to Didymus (<emph>c.</emph> 30 B.C.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χερσὶ_1047"> refers to deeds of prowess: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νώτοισι_1047"> to feats of sheer strength, as when he took the place of Atlas, or carried the Erymanthian boar to Mycenae.—Cicero's version, <title>Quae corpore exanclavi atque animo pertuli</title>, has caused the inference that his text contained an equivalent for <emph>animo.</emph> Wecklein suggests that he read </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στέρνοισι_1047"> (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 1090">cp. 1090</ref>), but misunderstood it. Perhaps, however, Cicero felt his rhetorical antithesis to be warranted by the idea of mental anguish implied in </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοχθήσας_1047">. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοὔπω_1048">: <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote> here=‘and nevertheless’; cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 332"> <title>Ant.</title>332</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄκοιτις_Διὸς_1048"> (for the place of the art., <ref n="Soph. Trach. 732">cp. 732</ref>), since the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀργαλέος χόλος Ἥρης</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 18.119"> <title>Il.</title>18. 119</ref>) was the prime source of all his troubles. Hera caused Eurystheus to be born at Argos a little before the birth of Heracles at Thebes ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 19.114"> <title>Il.</title>19. 114</ref> ff.), and afterwards gave the hero into his power. But she also persecuted Heracles directly, as when she sent the serpents to his cradle ( <ref n="Pind. N. 1">Pind. <title>N.</title>1. 40</ref>), or drove him by storms to Cos ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 14.253"> <title>Il.</title>14. 253</ref> ff.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προὔθηκεν_1048">, of setting a task: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 216"> <title>Ant.</title>216</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Εὐρυσθεὺς_1048"> is not elsewhere named in the play: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 35">cp. 35</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἷον_τόδ_1050">...<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφίβλ. <emph> καθῆψεν</emph> </quote>=<quote xml:lang="grc">οἷον τόδ’ ἀμφίβληστρόν ἐστιν, ὃ καθῆψεν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 184">cp. 184</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δολῶπις_1050">, ‘with deceitful face,’ smiling on him while plotting evil.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἐρινύων_ἀμφίβληστρον_1050">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1034"> <title>Ai.</title>1034</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆρ’ οὐκ Ἐρινὺς τοῦτ’ ἐχάλκευσεν ξίφος</quote> <emph>;</emph> The epithet </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑφαντὸν_1050"> marks that the ‘net’ is the robe: just as, in <ref n="Aesch.
