Ag. 1580">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title>1580</ref> , <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑφαντοῖς ἐν πέπλοις Ἐρινύων</quote>, it marks that a real robe is meant. (So an eagle is <quote xml:lang="grc">πτηνὸς κύων</quote>, <ref n="Aesch. PB 1022.">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title>1022.</ref>) A net would properly be described as <quote xml:lang="grc">πλεκτόν</quote> rather than <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑφαντόν</quote> (cp. Pollux 7. 139 <quote xml:lang="grc">δικτυοπλόκος</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσμαχθὲν_1053">, ‘plastered’ to his sides: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 768">cp. 768</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀρτίκολλος.—<emph>ἐσχάτας … σάρκας</emph> </quote> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, not only on the surface of the body, but to the inmost parts. Cicero renders the phrase by <emph> viscera.</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλεύμονός_ἀρτηρίας_1053">: ‘the suspenders of the lungs,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, <emph>the bronchial tubes</emph> which convey air to the lungs. For the sing. <quote xml:lang="grc">πλεύμων</quote> in a collective sense cp. Plat. <title> Tim.</title>p. 84 D <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ τῶν πνευμάτων τῷ σώματι ταμίας πλεύμων</quote>. As to the word <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρτηρία</quote>, see Appendix. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ῥοφεῖ_1055">, ‘drains,’ ‘empties’ (of air): his breath is arrested by the spasms: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 778">cp. 778</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σπαραγμὸς...πλευμόνων ἀνθήψατο</quote>. Though the grammatical subject is <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφίβληστρον</quote>, the agent is properly the venom itself; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυνοικοῦν_1055">, since this interruption of the breath is frequent. For this use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥοφεῖν</quote>, cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Ach. 278">Ar. <title> Ach.</title>278</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥοφήσει τρύβλιον</quote> (empty it). Cicero well renders, <title>Urgensque graviter pulmonum haurit spiritus.</title> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χλωρὸν_1055">, fresh, vigorous; cp. Theocr. 14. 70 <quote xml:lang="grc">ποιεῖν τι δεῖ, ἇς</quote> (=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἔως</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">γόνυ χλωρόν</quote> (‘youthful’).—Not ‘discoloured’ (<emph>decolorem sanguinem</emph>, Cic. ). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀφράστῳ_1057">, ‘indescribable,’ unutterably dreadful; not, ‘inexplicable,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, of unknown origin.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χειρωθεὶς_1057">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 279">cp. 279</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοὐ_οὔθ_1058">. It is unnecessary to change </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔθ_1058"> to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδ_1058">. The sequence <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ...οὔτε</quote> is foreign to Attic prose; and an Attic poet would presumably have avoided it where <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ</quote> was followed by only one negative clause: <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>, in <ref n="Soph. OC 702"> <title>O. C.</title>702</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ νεαρὸς οὐδὲ γήρᾳ</quote>, etc., <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε</quote> is improbable. In Theognis 125 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ ἂν εἰδείης ἀνδρὸς νόον οὐδὲ γυναικός</quote>, where the MSS. have <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε, οὐδὲ</quote> stands in Aristotle's quotation of the verse (<title>Eth. Eud.</title> 7. 2). But when, as here, <emph>several</emph> clauses with <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε</quote> follow <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ</quote>, an Attic poet might imitate the frequent Homeric usage: <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>, <ref n="Hom. Od. 4.566"> <title>Od.</title>4. 566</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ νιφετὸς οὔτ’ ἂρ χειμὼν πολὺς οὔτε ποτ’ ὄμβρος</quote>. So <emph>ib.</emph> 9. 136 f. <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ</quote> is followed by two clauses with <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε</quote>, and in <ref n="Hom. Il. 6.450"> <title>Il.</title>6. 450</ref> f. by three.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγχη_πεδιάς_1058">, the spear of the warrior on a battle-field; as when Heracles fought with Laomedon of Troy, with the Amazons, or with Augeas king of Elis ( <ref n="Apollod. 2.7.2">Apollod.2. 7. 2</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στρατὸς_Γιγάντων_1058">: after sacking Troy, and ravaging Cos, Heracles went to Phlegra (sometimes identified with Pallenè, the westernmost headland of the Chalcidic peninsula), and helped the gods to vanquish their Earth-born foes. In <ref n="Pind. N. 1">Pind. <title>N.</title>1. 67</ref>Teiresias predicts what Heracles shall achieve, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅταν θεοὶ ἐν πεδίῳ Φλέγρας Γιγάντεσσιν μάχαν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντιάζωσιν</quote>. In the Gigantomachia on the pediment of the Megarian Treasury at Olympia, Heracles fought at the right hand of Zeus (cp. <title> Ausgrabungen</title>, vol. IV. pl. 20 <emph>b</emph>). Early Attic vase-paintings of this subject associate him with Zeus and Athena (Roscher, <title>Lex.</title>, p. 2211).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θήρειος_βία_1058"> seems to be a general phrase, including both the Centaurs (<quote xml:lang="grc">θηρῶν</quote>, 1096) and the wild beasts (1092 ff.). Cicero understood it of the former only, <emph>non biformato impetu</emph> | <title>Centaurus.</title> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄγλωσσος_1060"> profits by the suggestion of <quote xml:lang="grc">γῆ</quote> in the adjective </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἑλλὰς_1060"> ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 256"> <title>Ph.</title>256</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἑλλάδος γῆς</quote>). The <quote xml:lang="grc">βάρβαρος</quote> has <emph>no</emph> ‘language’ properly so called: to the ear of the Hellene, he merely twitters like a bird (n. on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1002"> <title>Ant.</title>1002</ref>). Cp. <ref n="Pind. I. 6">Pind. <title> I.</title>6. 24</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἔστιν οὕτω βάρβαρος οὔτε παλίγγλωσσος πόλις</quote>, ‘barbarous or strange of speech.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔθ_ὅσην_1060">: the division of <emph>mankind</emph> into Greeks and barbarians is exhaustive; but the range of <emph>earth</emph> traversed by Heracles extended beyond the dwellings of men (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 1100">cp. 1100</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπ’ ἐσχάτοις τόποις</quote>). It seems unnecessary, then, to regard this third clause as merely a rhetorical summary of the other two.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γαῖαν_1060">: antecedent attracted into relative clause: <ref n="Soph. OC 907"> <title>O. C.</title>907</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θῆλυς_1062"> for <quote xml:lang="grc">θηλεῖα</quote>, as in the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">θῆλυς ἐέρση</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Od. 5.467"> <title>Od.</title>5. 467</ref>): <ref n="Soph. OC 751"> <title>O. C.</title>751</ref> n.—Nauck's correction of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὖσα_1062"> into </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φῦσα_1062"> is indispensable, if </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνδρὸς_1062"> be retained; the alternative would be to read </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνὴρ_1062">, which is less probable. For the cogn. acc., cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 760"> <title>Ai.</title>760</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνθρώπου φύσιν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">βλαστών.—<emph>μόνη … δὴ</emph> </quote> : <ref n="Soph. Ant. 58"> <title>Ant.</title>58</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καθεῖλε_1062">, brought low, destroyed, as in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 517"> <title>Ai.</title>517</ref>(of death).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φασγάνου_δίχα_1062">: the warrior laments that he has not fallen in combat; cp. <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 627">Aesch. <title> Eum.</title>627</ref>(of Agamemnon's death) <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ταῦτα πρὸς γυναικός, οὔ τι θουρίοις</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τόξοις ἑκηβόλοισιν ὥστ’ Ἀμαζόνος</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γενοῦ_1064">, show thyself: </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γεγώς_1064"> and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐτήτυμος_1064"> cohere, making an equiv. for <quote xml:lang="grc">γνήσιος</quote>: hence there is no awkwardness in having two forms from <quote xml:lang="grc">γίγνομαι</quote> . <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1158">Cp. 1158</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 556"> <title>Ai.</title>556</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖ σ’ ὅπως πατρὸς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">δειξεις ἐν ἐχθροῖς οἷος ἐξ οἵου )τράφης.—<emph>τὸ μητρὸς ὄνομα</emph> </quote>: she is such in name only (817).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρεσβεύσῃς_1064">, <emph>prefer</emph> in honour: <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 5">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title>5</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς μὲν σέβοντας τἀμὰ πρεσβεύω κράτη.—<emph>πλέον</emph> </quote> is, in strictness, redundant; cp. <title>Legg.</title> 887 B <quote xml:lang="grc">προτιμᾶν βραχυλογίαν μᾶλλον ἢ μῆκος</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰδῶ_1067">, which Nauck changes to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴδω_1067">, is in accord with usage (cp. <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>, 678, <ref n="Soph. OC 889"> <title>O. C.</title>889</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως εἰδῶ</quote> : <ref n="Soph. Phil. 238"> <title>Ph.</title>238</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς εἰδῶ</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_τοὐμὸν_1067"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> The constr. is, <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ μᾶλλον ἀλγεῖς, ὁρῶν τοὐμὸν λωβητον εἶδος, ἢ</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">κείνης</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">λωβητὸν εἶδος</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν δίκῃ κακούμενον</quote>. For the omission of <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ</quote> before <quote xml:lang="grc">κείνης</quote> , <ref n="Soph. Trach. 929">cp. 929</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ κεῖσε δεῦρό τ’</quote> (n.).— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακούμενον_1067"> as in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 228"> <title>Ph.</title>228</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 261."> <title>O. C.</title>261.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴθ_1070">, expressing entreaty, is similarly combined with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τόλμησον_1070"> in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 480"> <title>Ph.</title>480</ref> f.: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴθ’, ἡμέρας τοι μόχθος οὐχ ὅλης μιᾶς</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">τόλμησον, κ.τ.λ.—<emph>πολλοῖσιν οἰκτρόν</emph> </quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1296"> <title>O. T.</title>1296</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷον καὶ στυγοῦντ’ ἐποικτίσαι</quote>. Cicero: <title>Miserere! Gentes nostras flebunt miserias.</title> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὥστε_παρθένος_1070">: the schol. compares <ref n="Hom. Il. 16.7"> <title>Il.</title>16. 7</ref>, where Patroclus weeps <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠΰτε κούρη</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">νηπίη.— <emph> βέβρυχα</emph> </quote> might seem strange in such a comparison; yet <ref n="Soph. Trach. 904">cp. 904</ref>, where <quote xml:lang="grc">βρυχᾶτο</quote> is said of Deianeira. The fitness of the word is more evident in 805, as in <ref n="Soph. OT 1265"> <title>O. T.</title>1265</ref>, and <ref n="Soph. Aj. 322"> <title>Ai.</title>322</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦρος ὡς βρυχώμενος</quote>. For the perf., cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">μέμυκα</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">μυκάομαι</quote>), <quote xml:lang="grc">μέμηκα</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">μηκάομαι</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀστένακτος_1074">: as Ajax was <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀψόφητος ὀξέων κωκυμάτων</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 321"> <title>Ai.</title>321</ref>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἱπόμην_1074">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">εἵπετο</quote>, though </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τόνδ_ἄνδρα_1074"> precedes: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 6"> <title>O. C.</title>6</ref> n. The imperf., which was read here by a scholiast of the <title>Ajax</title> (cr. n.), is certainly preferable to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑσπόμην_1074">, though the aor. would also be right, if he was viewing the past as a whole. If <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἑσπόμην</quote> were read, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰὲν_1074"> would go with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀστένακτος</quote>: though <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀεί</quote> is not necessarily incompatible with an aor. ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1140"> <title>Ph.</title>1140</ref> n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἱπόμην_κακοῖς_1074">: cp. <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 408">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title>408</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς δ’ ἦλθες Ἄργος</quote> <emph>;</emph>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ οἶδ)· ὁ δαίμων μ’ ἐκάλεσεν πρὸς τὴν τύχην</quote>. So <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκολουθεῖν τῷ λόγῳ</quote> ( <ref n="Plat. Phaedo 107">Plat. <title>Phaedo</title>107</ref>B), <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς πράγμασιν</quote> ( <ref n="Dem. 4.39">Dem. or. 4 § 39</ref>), <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, to follow their lead. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_τοιούτου_1075">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 284">cp. 284</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_δ_1076">. The first clause is introduced by <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote>, the second by <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote> (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">τε</quote>), as in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 432"> <title>Ant.</title>432</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χἡμεῖς ἰδόντες ἱέμεσθα, σὺν δέ νιν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">θηρώμεθ’ εὐθύς</quote>. The effect of <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote> is to throw the second clause into relief by a slight rhetorical antithesis (as if <quote xml:lang="grc">μέν</quote> had followed <quote xml:lang="grc">στῆθι</quote>). This expressive </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δ_1076"> should not be changed to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θ_1076">. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δείξω_γὰρ_1078">: the ictus on <quote xml:lang="grc">γάρ</quote> does not spoil the rhythm, because the chief stress falls on the verb: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1540"> <title>O. C.</title>1540</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χῶρον δ’, ἐπείγει γάρ με τοὐκ θεοῦ παρόν</quote>. Below, in 1247, the case of <quote xml:lang="grc">οὖν</quote> is similar.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_καλυμμάτων_1078"> = <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐκκεκαλυμμένον</quote>, since </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_1078"> here=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἔξω</quote>, ‘outside of’: cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 15.272"> <title>Od.</title>15. 272</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὕτω τοι καὶ ἐγὼν ἐκ πατρίδος</quote> (<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰμί</quote>), ‘I am an exile.’ The sense is different in <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1178"> Aesch. <title>Ag.</title>1178</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ καλυμμάτων</quote> | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">δεδορκώς</quote>, where <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</quote>=‘forth from.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰδού_1079">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 821">cp. 821</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴδ’</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰαῖ_αἰαῖ_1079">, L's reading. Hermann and others, taking <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰαῖ ὦ τάλας</quote> as a dochmiac, read <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐέ</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἒ ἔ</quote> instead of the second <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰαῖ</quote>, placing it in a line by itself. Dindo<*>f formerly read <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰαῖ τάλας</quote> (deleting <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ</quote> and the second <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰαῖ</quote>), as an iambic dipodia: but his latest text gives <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰαῖ, ἆ τάλας</quote> (as a dochmiac). Nauck requires bacchii, and suggests <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰώ μοι, τάλας, φεῦ</quote>. Wilam. (<title>Hermes</title> XVIII. 246) says—(1) <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰαῖ, ὦ τάλας</quote>, dochmius: (2) <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰαῖ</quote>, interjection: (3) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦναξ ...παῖσον</quote>, ‘acatalectic anapaestic trimeter.’ A brief interjection of this kind could take almost any metrical form; and, in the absence of a lyric context, the metre here cannot be defined with certainty. Cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 1160"> <title>El.</title>1160</ref>—2. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔθαλψεν_1082"> is trans., <quote xml:lang="grc"> με</quote> being understood, as after <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμπρησον</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 801"> <title>Ph.</title>801</ref>(n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄτης_1082">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1104">cp. 1104</ref> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅδ_αὖ_1082"> should be taken with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔθαλψεν_1082_2">, because (1) <quote xml:lang="grc"> ὅδ’</quote> fitly stands in the first clause, and (2) <quote xml:lang="grc">διῇξε</quote> thus gains force by its abruptness: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1088">cp. 1088</ref> f. If a point were placed after <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρτίως, ὅδ’</quote> would still be better than <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ δ’. —<emph>διῇξε</emph> </quote>, a word used by medical writers, as Wakefield pointed out; <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign>, Hippocr. <ref n="Hp. Morb. 1. 5"> <title>Morb.</title>1. 5</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑφ’ ἑωυτῶν ὀδύναι διαΐσσουσιν ἄλλοτε ἄλλῄ τοῦ σώματος.—<emph>ἀγύμναστον</emph> </quote>: cp. Eur. fr. 683 <quote xml:lang="grc">μῶν κρυμὸς αὐτῆς πλευρὰ γυμνάζει χολῆς<emph>;</emph>—<emph>διαβόρος</emph> </quote>: distinguish <quote xml:lang="grc">διάβορον</quote> (pass.) in 676. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 7">Soph. <title>Ph.</title>7</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νόσῳ ...διαβόρῳ</quote> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέξαι_μ_1085">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 819"> <title>Ph.</title>819</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ γαῖα, δέξαι θανάσιμόν μ’ ὅπως ἔχω</quote>.—Dindorf is probably right in regarding these two lines as anapaestic dimeters, each short of a foot (<quote xml:lang="grc">βραχυκατάληκτοι</quote>): cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 1160"> <title>El.</title>1160</ref> ff. Hermann, writing </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἅιδη_1085"> instead of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἀΐδη_1085">, made them iambic verses with spondees in the second place (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἰσχιορρωγικοί</quote>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔνσεισον_1087"> suggests the force with which the brandished bolt leaves the Thunderer's hand; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγκατάσκηψον_1087">, its descent upon the victim's head.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δαίνυται_1087">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 771">cp. 771</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἤνθηκεν_1089">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1000">cp. 1000</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνθος</quote> (n.): <ref n="Soph. Phil. 258"> <title>Ph.</title>258</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ δ’ ἐμὴ νόσος</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀεὶ τέθηλε</quote> (n.). For this rare perf., cp. <ref n="Thuc. 2. 49">Thuc.2. 49</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐξηνθηκός</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_χέρες_1089">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1004"> <title>Ph.</title>1004</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ χεῖρες</quote>: <emph> ib.</emph> 1354 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ...κύκλοι</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_στέρν_1089">. Quoting from memory (with <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ στέρν’</quote> instead of these words), the rhetorician Apsines (<emph>c.</emph> 240 A.D.) cites this passage (<ref> Spengel <title>Rhet.</title> 1.400</ref>) as an instance of pathetic apostrophe,—subjoining it to another example from Sophocles, viz. <ref n="Soph. OT 1391.">Soph. <title>O. T.</title>1391.</ref>Dindorf notes this (ed. 1860). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑμεῖς_κεῖνοι_1091">: for <quote xml:lang="grc"> δέ</quote> cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1097"> <title>O. T.</title>1097</ref>(n.). This reading is not better than </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑμεῖς_ἐκεῖνοι_1091">, but has better authority, because the <quote xml:lang="grc">δε</quote> in L (cr. n.) was not likely to have been interpolated.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καθέσταθ_1091">, not merely ‘are,’ but ‘have come to be.’ For <quote xml:lang="grc">καθεστάναι</quote> in this sense, see on <ref n="Soph. Ant. 435."> <title>Ant.</title>435.</ref>The form of expression is due to the emphasis on </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑμεῖς_1091">: ‘<emph>yours</emph> is the plight to which those arms have come.’ It is a compressed way of saying, <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιοῦτοι ὑμεῖς καθέστατε, ἐκεῖνοι δὴ</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὄντες</quote>) etc. For <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐκεῖνος</quote> referring to the past, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1195"> <title>O. C.</title>1195</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺ δ’ εἰς ἐκεῖνα, μὴ τὰ νῦν, ἀποσκόπει</quote>: for its juxtaposition with <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑμεῖς</quote>, <emph>ib.</emph> 138 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅδ’ ἐκεῖνος ἐγώ</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Νεμέας_1092">, a valley in Argolis, about three miles S.W. of Cleonae, four and a half S. E. of Phlius, and eleven N. of Argos. The lion is described by Hesiod ( <ref n="Aesch. Seven 331"> <title>Th.</title>331</ref>) as <quote xml:lang="grc">κοιρανέων Τρητοῖο Νεμείης ἠδ’ Ἀπέσαντος</quote>, Treton and Apesas being mountains which partly enclose the valley. It was in <quote xml:lang="grc">Τρητόν</quote> (‘the caverned’) that the monster had his den. Pindar calls Nemea the <quote xml:lang="grc">χόρτοι λέοντος</quote> (<title>Ol.</title> 13. 44); also <quote xml:lang="grc">Διὸς ἄλσος</quote> (<title>N.</title> 2. 9), from its temple of Zeus, in a cypress-grove.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλάστορα_1092"> ( <ref n="Soph. OC 788"> <title>O. C.</title>788</ref> n.), as Hesiod calls him <quote xml:lang="grc">πῆμ’ ἀνθρώποις</quote> ( <ref n="Aesch. Seven 329"> <title>Th.</title>329</ref>).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄπλατον_1092"> =<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπέλαστον</quote>, unapproachable: cp. <ref n="Pind. P. 12">Pind. <title>P.</title>12. 9</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπλάτοις ὀφίων κεφαλαῖς.—<emph>ἀπροσήγορον</emph> </quote>, lit., ‘not affable,’— boldly applied to the intractable beast with which men can establish no relations. The word has here much the sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπρόσοιστον</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1277"> Soph. <title>O. C.</title>1277</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ δυσπρόσοιστον κἀπροσήγορον στόμα</quote>. Vergil's description of the Cyclops has been quoted ( <ref n="Verg. A. 3. 621"> <title>Aen.</title>3. 621</ref>), <title>Nec visu facilis nec dictu affabilis ulli;</title> but Polyphemus could speak. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βίᾳ_κατειργάσασθε_1094">: Heracles throttled the lion, which was invulnerable: <ref n="Eur. Her. 153">Eur. <title>H. F.</title> 153</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃν ἐν βρόχοις ἑλὼν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">βραχίονός φησ’ ἀγχόναισιν ἐξελεῖν</quote>. This was the first of his labours; and thus he won the lion-skin ( <ref n="Pind. I. 5">Pind. <title>I.</title>5. 47</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Λερναίαν_ὕδραν_1094">: see 574 n. <ref n="Eur. Her. 419">Eur. <title>H. F.</title>419</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τάν τε μυριόκρανον</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">πολύφονον κύνα Λέρνας</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕδραν ἐξεπύρωσεν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">βέλεσί τ’ ἀμφέβαλ’ ἰόν</quote>. This <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆθλος</quote>—usually made the second—is closely connected with the first; it is wrought in Argolis; and it completes his equipment by giving him the poison for his arrows. In both these labours, as in others, he is the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλεξίκακος</quote> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θῆρα_1094"> 1097) that haunted Mount Erymanthus in north Arcadia, Heracles passed over Pholoè, a wild upland district on the borders of Elis. Here he was entertained by the Centaur Pholos, and routed the other Centaurs who flocked to demand a share of his host's wine.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διφυᾶ_1094">: Diodorus (4. 69) applies this word to the Centaurs. Cp. <ref n="Pind. P. 2">Pind. <title>P.</title>2. 47</ref>: Ixion and Nephelè begat a son Centaurus; <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵπποισι Μαγνητίδεσσιν ἐμίγνυτ’ ἐν Παλίου</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">σφυροῖς· ἐκ δ’ ἐγένοντο στρατὸς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">θαυμαστός, ἀμφοτέροις</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁμοῖοι τοκεῦσι, τὰ ματρόθεν μὲν κάτω, τὰ δ’ ὕπερθε πατρός.—<emph>ἄμικτον</emph> </quote>, with whom it is impossible to hold humane intercourse; <ref n="Eur. Cycl. 429">Eur. <title>Cycl.</title>429</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄμικτον ἄνδρα</quote>: cp. <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀμιξία</quote> ( <ref n="Thuc. 1. 3">Thuc.1. 3</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἱπποβάμονα_1094">, usu., ‘mounted on horses,’ and so some take it here as=‘mounted on horses' legs’; but it is more simply explained as ‘<emph>moving like</emph> horses.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θηρῶν_1094">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 556">cp. 556</ref> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑβριστήν_ἄνομον_1094">: intemperance and violence were essential attributes of the Centaurs (excepting Cheiron): <ref n="Soph. Trach. 565">cp. 565</ref>. <ref n="Eur. Her. 181">Eur. <title>H. F.</title>181</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τετρασκελές θ’ ὕβρισμα, Κενταύρων γένος</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἅιδου_σκύλακ_1098">: a <emph> three-headed</emph> Cerberus seems to have been the usual type in early Ionian art; while on Attic black-figure vases of the middle and later style he is two-headed: see Roscher, <title>Lex.</title>p. 2205. Hesiod, the first poet who names Cerberus ( <ref n="Aesch. Seven 311"> <title>Th.</title>311</ref>), gives him fifty heads.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἐχίδνης_θρέμμα_1098">, as in <ref n="Hes. Th. 310">Hes. <title>Th.</title>310</ref>: but in <ref n="Soph. OC 1574">Soph. <title>O. C.</title>1574</ref> he is the offspring of Tartarus and Earth. In <ref n="Hom. Il. 8.366"> <title>Il.</title>8. 366</ref> ff. Athena saves Heracles when Eurystheus sends him <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ἐρέβευς ἄξοντα κύνα στυγεροῦ Ἀΐδαο</quote>: cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 11.623."> <title>Od.</title>11. 623.</ref>—Pluto said that Heracles might take Cerberus, if he could do so without using any weapon. The hero succeeded, and having shown his living prize to the terrified Eurystheus, restored it to the nether world. ( <ref n="Apollod. 2.5.12">Apollod.2. 5. 12</ref>§ 8.)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρυσέων_1098">: in tragic dialogue <quote xml:lang="grc">χρύσεος</quote> usu. suffers synizesis, but there are several exceptions, such as fr. 313: fr. 439: <ref n="Eur. Ion 1175."> Eur. <title>Ion</title>1175.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δράκοντα_φύλακ_1098">. The garden was in the far west, where Atlas supports the sky, beyond the stream of the Oceanus ( <ref n="Hes. Th. 215">Hes. <title>Th.</title>215</ref>). When Zeus espoused Hera there, a wondrous apple-tree (<quote xml:lang="grc">μηλέα</quote>) sprang up. This tree was committed to the care of maidens called Hesperides, daughters of Night ( <ref n="Hes. Th. 211">Hes. <title>Th.</title>211</ref>), sweet singers; and it was guarded by a terrible dragon, coiled round the stem ( <ref n="Eur. Her. 397">Eur. <title>H. F.</title>397</ref>, <ref n="Paus. 6. 19. 8">Paus.6. 19. 8</ref>). Heracles slew this dragon with poisoned arrows (Apoll. <ref n="Apollon. 4.1396">Rh. 4.1396</ref> ff., where the monster is named <quote xml:lang="grc">Λάδων</quote>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπ_τόποις_1098">: for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπί</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 356">cp. 356</ref>. So Hesiod <ref n="Aesch. Seven 518"> <title>Th.</title>518</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πείρασιν ἐν γαίης</quote> . <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 742">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title>742</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἑσπερίδων δ’ ἐπὶ μηλόσπορον ἀκτὰν ἀνύσαιμι τᾶν ἀοιδῶν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵν’ ὁ ποντομέδων πορφυρέας λίμνας</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ναύταις οὐκέθ’ ὁδὸν νέμει</quote>. The garden was sometimes placed among the Hyperboreans as by <ref n="Apollod. 2.5.11">Apollod.2. 5. 11</ref>, and prob. by Aeschylus in the <quote xml:lang="grc">Προμηθεὺς Λυόμενος</quote>, <ref n="Strab. 4">Strabo 4</ref>, p. 183: sometimes in Libya, or in Spain. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόχθων_1101">, a general word, including both the tasks done for Eurystheus (the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆθλοι</quote> proper),—such as the five just enumerated,—and other enterprises, such as the warfare against Laomedon and the Giants (1058). In the temple of Athena <quote xml:lang="grc">χαλκίοικος</quote> at Sparta Pausanias saw <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὰ μὲν τῶν ἄθλων Ἡρακλέους, πολλὰ δὲ καὶ ὦν ἐθελοντὴς κατώρθωσε</quote> (3. 17. 2). As to the cycle of the ‘twelve labours,’ and its probable origin, see Introduction, § 2.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγευσάμην_1101">: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐπειράθην</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1005"> <title>Ant.</title>1005</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμπύρων ἐγευόμην</quote> (n). <ref n="Eur. Her. 1353">Eur. <title>H. F.</title>1353</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ γὰρ πόνων δὴ μυρίων ἐγευσάμην</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χερῶν_1102">, valour (488); for the gen., cp. Andoc. or. 1 § 147 <quote xml:lang="grc">τροπαῖα τῶν πολεμίων...ἀπέδειξαν</quote>. Cicero well renders, <title>Nec quisquam e nostris spolia cepit laudibus.</title> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄναρθρος_1103">, like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκνενε<*> μένος</quote>, since his whole frame has been unhinged and unnerved; so <ref n="Eur. Orest. 227">Eur. <title>Or.</title>227</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅταν</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">μ’ ἀνῇ νό<*>ος</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μανίας, ἄναρθρός εἰμι κἀσθενῶ μέλη.—<emph>κατερρακωμένος</emph> </quote>: cp. <ref n="Aesch. PB 1023">Aesch. <title>P. V.</title>1023</ref>(the eagle rending the flesh of Prometheus) <quote xml:lang="grc">διαρταμήσει σώματος μέγα ῥάκος</quote>: Lycophron 1113 <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ πᾶν λακίζουσ’ ἐν φοναῖς ψυχρὸν δέμας.—<emph>τυφλῆς</emph> </quote> , <emph>caeca</emph>, unseen: fr. 533 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ δ’ ἐς αὔριον ἀεὶ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τυφλὸν ἕρπει</quote>. Cp. <ref n="Eur. Med. 1200">Eur. <title>Med.</title>1200</ref>(the fatal robe consuming Glaucè's flesh) <quote xml:lang="grc">σάρκες δ’ ἀπ’ ὀστέων ὥστε πεύκινον δάκρυ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">γναθμοῖς ἀδήλοις φαρμάκων ἀπέρρεον</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρίστης_1105">, since Alcmena, daughter of Electryon and Anaxo, belonged on both sides to the Perseidae, and so traced her descent from Zeus himself.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὠνομασμένος_1105">: it is not necessary to supply <quote xml:lang="grc">γόνος</quote> from 1106: cp. fr. 84 <quote xml:lang="grc">καταρκεῖ τοῦδε κεκλῆσθαι πατρός.—<emph>αὐδηθεὶς</emph> </quote> : <ref n="Soph. Trach. 736">cp. 736</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 240"> <title>Ph.</title>240</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐδῶμαι δὲ παῖς</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀχιλλέως</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_ὦ_1107">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1275"> <title>Ai.</title>1275</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤδη τὸ μηδὲν ὄντας</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 234"> <title>Ant.</title>234</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἂν_ἕρπω_1108">: the adv. is emphatic; <ref n="Soph. Trach. 773">cp. 773</ref>: <ref n="Soph. El. 1014"> <title>El.</title>1014</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σθένουσα μηδέν</quote>. Powerless as he is to seek her out; he is still able to execute his vengeance if she be brought to him. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χειρώσομαι_1109">: 279 n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀκ_τῶνδε_1109">: <ref n="Eur. Med. 458">Eur. <title> Med.</title>458</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅμως δὲ κἀκ τῶνδ’ οὐκ ἀπειρηκὼς φίλοις</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥκω</quote>: also <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἐκ τῶνδε</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 537">Soph. <title> Ai.</title>537</ref>, etc.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἵν_ἀγγέλλειν_1109">, with grim irony: see on <ref n="Soph. OC 1377."> <title>O. C.</title>1377.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακούς_γε_1109"> is far better than Cobet's <quote xml:lang="grc">κακούργους</quote>, which, indeed, would mar the point. The </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_1109"> is very expressive: it means, ‘when <emph>guilt</emph> is to be chastised, I am strong even in weakness,—even unto death.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θανὼν_1109">, since he thinks of his life as already closed: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1137">cp. 1137</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κτείνασα</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_Ἑλλάς_1112">: cp. <ref n="Eur. Her. 877">Eur. <title>H. F.</title>877</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέλεος Ἑλλάς, ἂ τὸν εὐεργέταν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποβαλεῖς</quote>: and <emph>ib.</emph> 135.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σφαλήσεται_1112">, not <quote xml:lang="grc"> σφαλήσει</quote>, since <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ τλῆμον Ἑλλάς</quote> is rather an exclamation than an address. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σφαλεῖσἔσει_1112"> would be an easy correction (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 816"> <title>O. C.</title>816</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λυπηθεὶς ἔσει</quote>), but is needless. —The poet may have preferred this verb to the more natural <quote xml:lang="grc">στερήσεται</quote> as more forcibly expressing a disaster (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 297">cp. 297</ref>, 719). Elsewhere the genitive after <quote xml:lang="grc">σφάλλομαι</quote> always denotes, not a person. but a thing (<quote xml:lang="grc">δόξης, τύχης, χρημάτων</quote>, etc.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρέσχες_1114"> followed by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρασχὼν_1114"> is somewhat inelegant, but it should not too hastily be pronounced spurious: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 967">cp. 967</ref> (<quote xml:lang="grc">βάσιν</quote>, after <quote xml:lang="grc">βάσις</quote> ): <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1219"> <title>Ph.</title>1219</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">στείχων</quote> followed in the next v. by <quote xml:lang="grc">στείχοντα</quote> (n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νοσῶν_ὅμως_1114">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 666"> <title>O. C.</title>666</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δίκαια_1114">: 409. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δός_σεαυτόν_1117">, give thyself to me, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, listen to what I would say, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_δύσοργος_1117"> (<quote xml:lang="grc"> ὢν</quote>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_θυμῷ_1117">, without being wrathful in the degree to which thou art (now) stung by passion; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, in a less wrathful mood than that to which thy present anger excites thee. <quote xml:lang="grc">δύσοργος</quote> agrees with the subject to <quote xml:lang="grc">δός</quote>, rather than with <quote xml:lang="grc">σεαυτόν</quote>, since <quote xml:lang="grc">δός μοι σεαυτόν</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 84"> <title>Ph.</title>84</ref> n.) is equivalent to <quote xml:lang="grc">πιθοῦ μοι. θυμῷ</quote> is best taken with <quote xml:lang="grc">δάκνει</quote> only, though it might go with <quote xml:lang="grc">δύσοργος</quote> also. For <quote xml:lang="grc"> δάκνει</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 254">cp. 254</ref>. —Prof. Campbell, reading the subjunct. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δάκνῃ_1117">, construes <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς μὴ τοσοῦτον δάκνῃ κ.τ.λ.</quote>, ‘that you may not be so exceedingly vexed with rage, being grievously distempered.’ But <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote> should then precede <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοιοῦτον_1117"> (masc.), the sense would be, ‘not in the mood to which thou art stung by anger.’ This would be simpler, but is unnecessary. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_γνοίης_1118">: the suppressed protasis is <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ μὴ <*>ης</quote>: cp. <title>O.C.</title> 98 n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_προθυμεῖ_1118">, in what a situation, under what circumstances, you desire a triumph,—the intended victim being already dead: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 971"> <title>Ai.</title>971</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν κενοῖς. ἐν οἷς</quote> is used as in 1122: for <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷς</quote>, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτοις</quote>, in the indirect question, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1171"> <title>O. C.</title>1171</ref> n. There is no class. example of <quote xml:lang="grc">χαίρειν ἔν τινι</quote> as=‘to rejoice <emph>in</emph> a thing,’ the regular constr. being <quote xml:lang="grc">χαίρειν τινί</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπί τινι</quote>: in <ref n="Soph. El. 1343"> <title>El.</title>1343</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὖν</quote>, not <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν</quote>, is right: and in <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 996">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title>996</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">χαίρετ’ ἐν αἰσιμίαισι πλούτου, ἐν</quote>=‘amidst.’ <quote xml:lang="grc">τρυφᾶν ἔν τινι</quote> occurs, but is not exactly similar.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀν_μάτην_1118">, and under what circumstances you are bitter without cause,—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, against one who is really innocent. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποικίλλεις_1121">, of riddling speech, as in 412 <quote xml:lang="grc">τί ποτε ποικίλας ἔχεις</quote> <emph>;</emph> He does not understand the allusions in <quote xml:lang="grc">χαίρειν</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλγεῖς.—<emph>πάλαι</emph> </quote> expresses impatience: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 589"> <title>Ph.</title>589</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆς_μητρὸς_1122">...</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φράσων_1122"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: cp. n. on 928: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 439"> <title>Ph.</title>439</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναξίου μὲν φωτὸς ἐξερήσομαι</quote>, | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">τί νῦν κυρεῖ.—<emph>νῦν ἐστιν</emph> </quote>: some edd. give </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νῦν_ἔστιν_1122">: but the verb is here merely the copula, not substantive (‘exists’).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἷς_ἥμαρτεν_1122">: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν</quote> is carried on from the first <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷς</quote> to the second, as in 423 to <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλοῖσιν</quote> from <quote xml:lang="grc">ποίοις ἐν ἀνθρώποισιν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_παρεμνήσω_1124">: <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote> gives an indignant emphasis to the verb,—‘hast thou <emph>indeed</emph>...?’ cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 726"> <title>Ant.</title>726</ref>(n.) <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ τηλικοίδε καὶ διδαξόμεσθα δὴ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">φρονεῖν</quote>...<emph> ;</emph> It could also mean, ‘even,’ ‘so much as’; but this would be hardly so forcible. —For </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γὰρ_1124"> in an angry question, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 863."> <title>O. C.</title>863.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρεμνήσω_1124">, of <emph>incidental</emph> mention; <ref n="Hdt. 7. 96">Her.7. 96</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ἐγώ, οὐ γὰρ ἀναγκαίῃ ἐξέργομαι ἐς ἱστορίης λόγον, οὐ παραμέμνημαι</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατροφόντου_1125">, fem., as the poets use <quote xml:lang="grc">σωτήρ</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OT 81"> <title>O. T.</title>81</ref> n.), <quote xml:lang="grc">φονεύς</quote> ( <title>I. T.</title> 586), <quote xml:lang="grc">χιλιοναύτης</quote> (<emph> ib.</emph> 141), <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἕλλην</quote> ( <ref n="Eur. Heraclid. 130"> <title>Heracl.</title>130</ref>), etc. The word ought to mean, ‘slayer of her own father’; but here its reference is decided by the subject of the principal verb, as in <ref n="Hom. Od. 1.299"> <title>Od.</title>1. 299</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">ἔκτανε πατροφονῆα</quote>). Cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 558"> <title>El.</title>558</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πατέρα φὴς κτεῖναι</quote> (‘<emph>my</emph> father’). A still bolder use occurs in <ref n="Eur. Orest. 193">Eur. <title>Or.</title>193</ref>, where the sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">πατροφόνου ματρός</quote> is relative to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡμᾶς</quote> in 191, while the subject of the principal verb is <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ Φοῖβος</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ἐμέ_1125">, the last person who ought to hear it. The emphasis on the pron. is, however, very slight; <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1220">cp. 1220</ref>: <ref n="Soph. OT 1045"> <title>O. T.</title>1045</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστ’ ἰδεῖν έμέ</quote> : <ref n="Soph. Phil. 299"> <title>Ph.</title>299</ref>(n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχει_οὕτως_1126">, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκείνη</quote>. This suits the context better than to make <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχει</quote> impersonal (‘the case stands thus’). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_δῆτα_1127"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">σιγᾶν πρέπει</quote>), </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_ἡμαρτ_1127">., by reason of them, in view of them: for the caus. dat., cp. <ref n="Thuc. 3. 98">Thuc.3. 98</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς πεπραγμένοις φοβούμενος τοὺς Ἀθηναίους</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_ἐρεῖς_1128"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ὄτι σιγαν πρέπει</quote>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖςγ_ἡμέραν_1128">, by reason of this day's deeds. Heracles has said, bitterly, ‘Silence is indeed unfitting, in view of her crimes.’ Hyllus replies, ‘It is so also in view of her deeds to-day,— as you will admit, when you know all.’ His father must learn that she has died, and that she was innocent.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_δὴ_1128">, rejecting an alternative, as in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 877"> <title>Ai.</title>877</ref>, <ref n="Soph. El. 913."> <title>El.</title>913.</ref>(Cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ μὲν δή</quote> in 627.)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_ἡμέραν_1128">=<quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς σήμερον πεπραγμένοις</quote>. The sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφ’ ἡμέραν</quote> is usu. ‘<emph>for</emph> the day,’ as in <ref n="Hdt. 1. 32">Her.1. 32</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ ἐπ’ ἡμέρην ἔχοντος</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 429">Soph. <title>El.</title>429</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς ἐφ’ ἡμέραν βορᾶς</quote>. Here the phrase is perhaps tinged with a sad irony,—‘this day's portion of evil.’ Cp. <title> O.C.</title> 1079, where <quote xml:lang="grc">κατ’ ἆμαρ</quote>=‘to-day,’ though <emph>ib.</emph> 682 <quote xml:lang="grc">κατ’ ἦμαρ</quote> means, as usual, ‘daily.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακὸς_1129">, by defending her; he is a true son of Heracles (1064 ff.) only i<*> he abhors his mother. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέγω_1130">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 591"> <title>Ph.</title>591</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 245."> <title>Ant.</title>245.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρτίως_νεοσφαγής_1130">: the same phrase occurs in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 898"> <title>Ai.</title>898</ref>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1283"> <title>Ant.</title>1283</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">τέθνηκε</quote> ...) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρτι νεοτόμοισι πλήγμασιν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐθέσπισας_1131">, as having announced what no human wit could have foreseen, —since Deianeira, as Heracles supposes, is happy and triumphant. So Theseus says to Oedipus ( <ref n="Soph. OC 1516"> <title>O. C.</title>1516</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὰ γάρ σε θεσπίζονθ’ ὁρῶ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">κοὐ ψευδόφημα.—<emph> τέρας</emph> </quote> implies incredulity.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διὰ_κακῶν_1131">, ‘in ill omened words,’ not because she has died, but because his vengeance has been baffled (1133). Schol. : <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄπιστον γὰρ διὰ δυσφήμων ὥσπερ ἐμαντεύσω</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδενὸς_ἐκτόπου_1132">: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, by no one coming, from without, into the place where she was: hence, ‘by no <emph>external</emph> hand.’ Hyllus knew that she had been alone in the <quote xml:lang="grc">θάλαμος</quote> when she did the deed (927—931). We need not suppose, then, that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔκτοπος</quote> means <emph>merely</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλος</quote> (which it could not do); nor, again, that it means ‘foreign to Trachis.’ These two explanations, which miss the mark on each side, have been fused in L's gloss, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπὸ ἄλλου ξένου</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξ_χερός_1132"> excuses the form of expression, (<quote xml:lang="grc">τέθνηκε</quote>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὶν_θανεῖν_1132">. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 110"> <title>Ai.</title>110</ref>, where the stress on <quote xml:lang="grc">νῶτα φοινιχθεὶς</quote> similarly excuses <quote xml:lang="grc">θάνῃ</quote> (after <quote xml:lang="grc">θανεῖν</quote> in 106). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεινοῦ_1135">, ironical, as in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1127"> <title>Ai.</title>1127</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινόν γ’ εἶπας</quote> : <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1225."> <title>Ph.</title>1225.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατῆρξας_1135">: cp. <ref n="Plat. Prot. 351E"> Plat. <title>Prot.</title> 351E</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κατάρχεις τοῦ λόγου. —ᾗ</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 553">cp. 553</ref> f. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἅπαν_χρῆμ_1136">, ‘this is the sum of the matter.’ For this use of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_χρῆμα_1136">, as meaning ‘the state of the case,’ like <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ πρᾶγμα</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 789"> <title>Ph.</title>789</ref>, cp. <title>Vesp.</title> 799 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρα τὸ χρῆμα· τὰ λόγἰ ὡς περαίνεται</quote>. The phrase is best taken as a nom., with <quote xml:lang="grc">τόδε ἐστίν</quote> understood: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1234"> <title>O. T.</title>1234</ref> f.: Eur. fr. 255 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁπλοῦς ὁ μῦθος, μὴ λέγ’ εὖ</quote>. Eur. Itmight, however, be an acc. in appos. with the sentence.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μωμένη_1136">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 836"> <title>O. C.</title>836</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κτείνασα_1137">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1111">cp. 1111</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θανὼν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1126"> <title>Ai.</title>1126</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δίκαια γὰρ τόνδ’ εὐτυχεῖν κτείναντά με</quote> <emph>;</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γὰρ_1138"> justifies <quote xml:lang="grc">χρηστὰ μωμένη. —<emph>στέργημα</emph> </quote> occurs only here. Its formation from <quote xml:lang="grc">στέργ‐ω</quote> is anomalous, since the word ought to be <quote xml:lang="grc">στέργμα</quote>: but the same may be said of <quote xml:lang="grc">στέργηθρον</quote> (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">στέρκτρον</quote>): and <quote xml:lang="grc">θέλγητρον</quote> was in use along with <quote xml:lang="grc">θέλκτρον</quote>. [Lidd. and Scott cite <quote xml:lang="grc">θέλγημα</quote> from Suidas <emph>s.v.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">βουκ<*>λήσας</quote>: but Bernhardy (ed. 1853, vol. I. p. 1017) reads <quote xml:lang="grc">θέλγητρον</quote> there, without noting a variant.] The objection to <quote xml:lang="grc"> στέργημα</quote> from the <emph>form</emph> is not, then, decisive. As to <emph>sense</emph>, <quote xml:lang="grc">στέργηθρον</quote>, like <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλτρον</quote>, is ‘an instrument for producing love’; while <quote xml:lang="grc">στέργημα</quote>, like <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλημα</quote>, ought to denote an effect. But here, again, we must allow for the freedom of poetical diction. The analogy of <quote xml:lang="grc">κήλημα</quote>, ‘a spell’ ( <ref n="Eur. Tro. 893"> Eur. <title>Tro.</title>893</ref>), by the side of <quote xml:lang="grc">κήληθρον</quote> (Bek ker <title>Anecd.</title> p. 46. 25), is not a strict one, since <quote xml:lang="grc">κήλημα</quote> is properly, ‘an effect of charming’; still, such an analogy may have influenced a poet who found <quote xml:lang="grc">στέργημα</quote> more convenient than <quote xml:lang="grc">στέργηθρον</quote>. Hyllus presently refers to this charm as <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιῷδε φίλτρῳ</quote> (1142): which rather suggests that a word in the sing. number was used here also. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 575">Cp. 575</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κηλητήριον</quote>: 685 <quote xml:lang="grc"> φάρμακον</quote>. (In 584 f. the plurals <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλτροις, θέλκτροισι</quote> describe the <emph>class</emph> of remedy: they do not directly denote the unguent.) For these reasons I refrain from changing </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στέργημα_1138">, with Nauck, to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στέργηθρα_1138">. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σέθεν_1138">, objective gen. with <quote xml:lang="grc">στέργημα</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δοκοῦσα_1138">, imperf. partic. ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 166"> <title>Ant.</title>166</ref> n.), =<quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτε ἐδόκει</quote>. The position of the clause, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς προσεῖδε τοὺς ἔνδον γάμους</quote>, which would properly precede <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀπήμπλακε</quote>, is made possible by the strong emphasis on <quote xml:lang="grc">στέργημα...δοκοῦσα προσβαλεῖν</quote>: ‘It was a love-charm that she thought to apply (though she failed), when she saw,’ etc. The leading idea of the sentence is here expressed by the participial clause (592 n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὺς_γάμους_1138">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 843">cp. 843</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> νέων...γάμων</quote>, and 460 (n. on <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔγημε</quote>). ‘The new union (=the new paramour) in the house there,’—a way of indicating Iolè, whom he abhors (1233), without naming her. Cp. the euphemistic <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς...κατ’ οἴκους</quote> in <ref n="Soph. OT 1447."> <title>O. T.</title>1447.</ref>—The new turn given to the thoughts of Heracles by vv. 1141 f. averts them wholly from Deianeira; and he speaks no word of pardon. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_1140"> gives a scornful tone to the question: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 263"> <title>O. C.</title>263</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοσοῦτος_1140">, so potent: <ref n="Plat. Sym. 177C">Plat. <title>Symp.</title> 177C</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοσοῦτος θεός</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκμῆναι_1142">: cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Eccl. 965"> Ar. <title>Eccl.</title>965</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Κύπρι, τί μ’ ἐκμαίνεις ἐπὶ ταύτῃ</quote> <emph>;</emph> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰοὺ_ἰοὺ_1143">, as in <ref n="Soph. OT 1071"> <title>O. T.</title>1071</ref>(Iocasta)<ref n="Soph. OT 1182"> O. T., 1182</ref>(Oedipus).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δύστηνος_1143">: for the nom., <ref n="Soph. Trach. 986">cp. 986</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἴχομαι_1143">. From the beginning of his torments, Heracles has felt that they could end only in death (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 802">cp. 802</ref>: 1001: 1111). Why, then, should he now speak as if he realised his state for the first time? The answer seems to be that, though the ultimate prospect is unchanged, his doom acquires a new terror in the light of its supernatural source. Hitherto he has believed himself the victim of human malice: it might leave no hope, but still it fixed no term. Now he knows that he is in the grip of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνάγκη</quote>: his moments are numbered. Henceforth he thinks only of the end. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φέγγος_μοι_1144">: cp. Theocr. 1. 102 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤδη γὰρ φράσδῃ πάνθ’ ἅλιον ἄμμι δεδυκεῖν.—<emph>ξυμφορᾶς ἵν’ ἕσταμεν</emph> </quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 375">cp. 375</ref> n.: <ref n="Soph. OT 1442"> <title>O. T.</title>1442</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵν’ ἕσταμεν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">χρείας.—<emph>ἴθ’, ὡ τέκνον</emph> </quote>: for the pause, and the absence of caesura, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 27">cp. 27</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κάλει_δὲ_1147">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 806"> <title>Ant.</title>806</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁμαιμόνων_1147">, brethren ( <ref n="Soph. OC 330"> <title>O. C.</title>330</ref> n.): Hyllus was the eldest of the family (56).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἀλκμήνην_1147">: 1105 n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάτην_1147">, since Zeus had been cruel to her son. Cp. <ref n="Eur. Her. 339">Eur. <title>H. F.</title>339</ref>(quoted by Wakefield), where her mortal husband, Amphitryon, says, with the same meaning, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ Ζεῦ, μάτην ἄρ’ ὁμόγαμόν σ’ ἐκτησάμην</quote> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τελευταίαν_θεσφάτων_1147">, my last (dying) utterance of them. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 723"> <title>O. T.</title>723</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φῆμαι μαντικαί</quote> : <emph>ib.</emph> 86 <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ θεοῦ φήμην φέρων. <emph> ἐμοῦ</emph> </quote> with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πύθησθε_1147">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 333"> <title>O. T.</title>333</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ ἂν πύθοιό μου</quote>. The schol. wrongly took <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοῦ</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">τελ. φήμην</quote>, as= <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν περὶ τῆς τελευτῆς μου φήμην</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_ἀλλ_1151">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 524"> <title>Ph.</title>524</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτε_1151"> is followed by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δὲ_1151"> (1153), as in <ref n="Aesch. Supp. 223">Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title> 223</ref> ff., <ref n="Xen. Anab. 6. 3. 16">Xen. <title>An.</title>6. 3. 16</ref>(=6. 1. 16 of some edd.), <ref n="Plat. Rep. 389A">Plat. <title> Rep.</title> 389A</ref>, etc. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 143">Cp. 143</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπακτίᾳ_Τίρυνθι_1151">: <ref target="cl270">see on 270</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συμβέβηκεν_1151">, impers., it has come to pass: the subject to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχειν_1151"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτήν</quote>) can easily be supplied, and the whole phrase =<quote xml:lang="grc">τυγχάνει ἕδραν ἔχουσα</quote>. — For </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὥστε_1151">, cp. <ref n="Aristot. Pol. 2.1261a"> Arist. <title>Pol.</title>2. 2. 5</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ συμβαίνει δὴ τὸν τρόπον τοῦτον ὥστε πάντας ἄρχειν</quote>.— Not, ‘she has come to terms (with Eurystheus), so that she should dwell,’ etc.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυλλαβοῦς_1151">, here simply=<quote xml:lang="grc">λαβοῦσα μεθ’ ἑαυτῆς</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 971"> <title>O. T.</title>971</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_ἄστυ_1154">. Thebes, the birthplace of Heracles (116), and his early home (510), was a place where some of his children might well find friends. Sophocles has perhaps taken a hint here from his elder contemporary, the logographer Pherecydes, who related that, after the death of Eurystheus, Thebes became the home of the Heracleidae; fr. 39 (Müller <title>Frag. Hist.</title> 1. p. 82) <quote xml:lang="grc">Ὕλλος δὲ καὶ οἱ ἄλλοι Ἡρακλεῖδαι καὶ οἱ σὺν αὐτοῖς ἀποθανόντος Εὐρυσθέως κατοικίζονται πάλιν ἐν Θήβαις.—<emph>ἂν … μάθοις</emph> </quote> : <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ πύθοιο</quote>: since he has been so long abroad. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅσοι_πάρεσμεν_1155">. The plural includes those who had accompanied Heracles from Euboea: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1194">cp. 1194</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξὺν οἷς χρῄζεις φίλων</quote>. We are not obliged to suppose that any <emph>son</emph> besides Hyllus was at home; though verses 54 f. implied that. Nauck rejects v. 1156 because (1) Hyllus could not say <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσοι</quote>, and (2) Heracles has not yet asked him to do anything.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξυπηρετήσομεν_1155">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 217"> <title>O. T.</title>217</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺ_ἄκουε_1157">: there is no emphasis on <quote xml:lang="grc">σύ</quote> (as if it referred to the absence of the others): rather the sense is, ‘Well, then (since you are ready to help), hear the task.’ For this use of <quote xml:lang="grc">δ’ οὖν</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 669"> <title>O. T.</title>669</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὔργον_1157">=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὅ τι δραστέον ἐστιν</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 26"> <title>Ph.</title>26</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὔργον οὐ μακρὰν λέγεις.—<emph>ἐξήκεις</emph> </quote>, you have reached a point, a situation: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1515"> <title>O. T.</title>1515</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅλις ἵν’ ἐξήκεις δακρύων</quote>: <emph>ib.</emph> 1158 <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς τόδ’ ἥξεις. —<emph>ἐμὸς</emph> </quote> without <quote xml:lang="grc">παῖς</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1205">cp. 1205</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 547"> <title>Ai.</title>547</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴπερ δικαίως ἔστ’ ἐμὸς τὰ πατρόθεν</quote>. The stress falls on the participial clause (592 n.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γὰρ_1159"> is merely prefatory.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρόφαντον_πατρὸς_1159">: this oracle, given by Zeus at an unspecified time, and place, is not mentioned elsewhere in the play. Nor is it noticed by any other writer. Sophocles may, however, have found it in some earlier treatment of the fable. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_ἐμπνεόντων_1160">, Erfurdt's correction of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_πνεόντων_1160">, is the most probable. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμπνεῖν</quote> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">ζῆν</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 883"> <title>Ph.</title>883</ref>) is frequent, while <quote xml:lang="grc">πνεῖν</quote> has that sense only in the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">πνείει τε καὶ ἕρπει</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 17.447"> <title>Il.</title>17. 447</ref>, <ref n="Hom. Od. 18.131"> <title>Od.</title>18. 131</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὕπο_1160"> might, no doubt, have arisen from <quote xml:lang="grc">πο</quote>[<quote xml:lang="grc">τέ</quote>], but is presumably genuine: it closes a verse in 1077, <ref n="Soph. OT 949"> <title>O. T.</title>949</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 334"> <title>Ph.</title>334</ref> <ref n="Soph. Phil. 583"> Ph., 583</ref> , <ref n="Soph. El. 553"> <title>El.</title>553</ref>: and it is associated with <quote xml:lang="grc">θνῄσκω</quote> in <ref n="Soph. OT 1246"> <title>O. T.</title>1246</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 334"> <title>Ph.</title>334</ref>, <ref n="Soph. El. 444."> <title>El.</title>444.</ref>The combination of <quote xml:lang="grc">πρός</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπό</quote> cannot be defended as=‘to be slain by no one on the part of (<quote xml:lang="grc">πρός</quote>) the living.’ Paley, quoting <ref n="Eur. Orest. 407">Eur. <title>Or.</title>407</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ φασμάτων δὲ τάδε νοσεῖς ποίων ὕπο</quote> <emph>;</emph> describes <quote xml:lang="grc">φαντασμάτων</quote> as Nauck's conjecture; but that word stands in most of the more recent MSS., and in Porson's text. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_ὅστις_1161">: for the ellipse of the antecedent (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκείνου</quote>), cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1050"> <title>Ai.</title>1050</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δοκοῦντ’ ἐμοί, δοκοῦντα δ’ ὃς κραίνει στρατοῦ</quote>. <ref n="Eur. Ion 560">Eur. <title> Ion</title>560</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ θίγω δῆθ’ οἵ μ’ ἔφυσαν<emph> ;</emph>—<emph>Ἅιδου … οἰκήτωρ</emph> </quote>: 282.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅστις_πέλοι_1161">: if we suppose that Nessus was alive when Zeus spoke, then this is oblique for <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅστις ἂν πέλῃ</quote>, as <ref n="Soph. OT 714"> <title>O. T.</title>714</ref>(in a prophecy) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅστις γένοιτ’</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅστις ἂν γένηται</quote>. But if Nessus was then dead, it is oblique for <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅστις πέλει</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θὴρ_1162"> (556) is in appos. with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Κένταυρος_1162">. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ζῶντά_θανών_1162">: as the dead Hector brought death on Ajax ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1027"> <title>Ai.</title>1027</ref>). For other examples of this favourite antithesis, cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1453"> <title>O. T.</title>1453</ref> n.: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 871"> <title>Ant.</title>871</ref> n.: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 901"> <title>Ai.</title>901</ref>: <ref n="Soph. El. 808."> <title>El.</title>808.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τούτοισι_1164"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> The </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μαντεῖα_καινὰ_1164"> denote the oracle given at Dodona twelve years before this time, saying that at the end of the twelfth year Heracles should have rest. This is the oracle to which allusion was made in 44, 164 ff., 824 ff. The other and earlier oracle (1159 ff.) had predicted the <emph>agency:</emph> this Dodonaean oracle, ‘recent’ in a relative sense, predicted the <emph>time.</emph> The two oracles ‘agree,’ because each verifies the other. The thing has come to pass by the right agency at the right time.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συμβαίνοντ_ἴσα_1164">, ‘coming out in agreement with them,’—yielding the same result,—viz., that <emph>this</emph> is the predicted end. For <quote xml:lang="grc">συμβαίνειν</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 173">cp. 173</ref> n. The idea is emphatically repeated in </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_ξυνήγορα_1164">, ‘pleading on the side of the older oracle,’—upholding its truth. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 814">Cp. 814</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυνηγορεῖς</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἃ_ὀρείων_1166"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> The </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Σελλῶν_ἄλσος_1166"> is the sacred precinct at Dodona, including the temple of Zeus, with its temenos; its limits have been traced by Carapanos (<title>Dodone</title>, pp. 16— 23): see Appendix, note on this passage, § 2.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀρείων_1166"> refers to the site of Dodona in a valley, more than 1600 feet above sea-level, surrounded by hills. See Appendix, § 1.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χαμαικοιτῶν_1166">, a trait of barbarism, surviving as a mark of sanctity. According to Philostratus (<title>Imag.</title> 2. 33), the Selli were ‘men of a rude life’ (<quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοσχέδιοί τινες</quote>), who held that their austerities were pleasing to Zeus. Cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 16.235"> <title>Il.</title>16. 235</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνιπτόποδες χαμαιεῦναι</quote>: Eur. fr. 355 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ἀστρώτῳ πέδῳ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">εὕδουσι, πηγαῖς δ’ οὐχ ὑγραίνουσιν πόδας</quote>. Callimachus <ref n="Call. Del. 284"> <title>Del.</title>284</ref> calls them <quote xml:lang="grc"> Πελασγοὶ...γηλεχέες</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰσεγραψάμην_1166">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, wrote for his own use in the <quote xml:lang="grc">δέλτος</quote> (157). Cp. <ref n="Hdt. 8. 135">Her.8. 135</ref>, where Greeks accompany the Carian Mŷs on his visit to the oracle of Apollo at Ptôon, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἀπογραψομένους τὰ θεσπιεῖν ἔμελλε</quote>: then Mŷs snatches the <quote xml:lang="grc">δέλτος</quote> from them, and makes an abstract for himself (<quote xml:lang="grc">συγγραψάμενον</quote> ). <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 982">Ar. <title>Av.</title>982</ref>(<quote xml:lang="grc">χρησμὸς</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃν ἐγὼ παρὰ τἀπόλλωνος ἐξεγραψάμην</quote>. At Dodona, in later times at least, the inquirer gave his question in writing to the Peleiades, and received a written answer: many of the leaden plates thus used have been found (Carapanos, pp. 68—83): Appendix, § 6. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρόνῳ_ζῶντι_1169">: the past can be described as dead ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 141"> <title>Ai.</title>141</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς νῦν φθιμένης νυκτός</quote>); the future, as unborn ( <ref n="Soph. OC 618"> <title>O. C.</title>618</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χρόνος τεκνοῦται...ἡμέρας</quote>); the present is here called <quote xml:lang="grc">ζῶν</quote>, not merely in the sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">παρών</quote>, but with the thought that this is the moment for the oracle to become operative. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐφεστώτων_1170">, ‘imposed’ as a doom: cp. <ref n="Hom. Il. 12.326"> <title>Il.</title>12. 326</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κῆρες ἐφεστᾶσιν θανάτοιο.—<emph>τελεῖσθαι</emph> </quote>, fut., with pass. sense, as in <ref n="Hom. Od. 23.284"> <title>Od.</title>23. 284</ref>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_δ_1172"> refers to <quote xml:lang="grc">λύσιν τελεῖσθαι</quote>: ‘but <emph>that</emph> (the promised release) was, it seems, only my death.’ Cp. <ref n="Plat. Rep. 357A">Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 357A</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ μὲν οὖν...ὤμην λόγου ἀπηλλάχθαι· τὸ δ’ ἦν ἄρα, ὡς ἔοικε, προοίμιον.— <emph>θανεῖν</emph> </quote>: for the simple aor. inf., though the ref. is to the future, cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 503">Soph. <title>Ph.</title>503</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">παθεῖν</quote> (n.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συμβαίνει_1174">, are coming true: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 173">cp. 173</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπιμεῖναι_ὀξῦναι_1176">, to wait on, (so as) to sharpen. (In <ref n="Thuc. 3. 26">Thuc.3. 26</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιμένοντες...πεύσεσθαι</quote>, the fut. inf. stands as after <quote xml:lang="grc">προσδοκῶντες</quote>: it does not, like <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀξῦναι</quote> here, express the result.) Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 584">Soph. <title>Ai.</title>584</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">γλῶσσά σου τεθηγμένη</quote>. For the aor. inf. of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιμένω</quote>, cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 11.350">Hom. <title> Od.</title>11. 350</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τλήτω</quote>... | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιμεῖναι ἐς αὔριον</quote>. The delay is viewed as a whole, not as a process.— Others, less well, make <quote xml:lang="grc"> στόμα</quote> the subject to <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀξῦναι</quote>: ‘Do not wait for my words to goad thee.’</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτὸν_1176">, of thine own accord.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰκαθόντα_1176">: as to these forms, see <title> O.T.</title> 651 n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξευρόντα_1178"> is illustrated by the words <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτὸν εἰκαθόντα</quote>. He is not to wait until this law has been brought home to his mind by a rebuke. He is to ‘find it out’ in the light of his own reason. <quote xml:lang="grc">εὑρίσκειν</quote> often expresses the result of reflection. <ref n="Soph. OT 441"> <title>O. T.</title>441</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"><*>αῦτ’ ὀνείδιζ’ οἷς ἔμ’ εὑρήσεις μέγαν</quote> (<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, when you look deeper). <ref n="Hdt. 7. 194">Her.7. 194</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">λογιζόμενος ὁ Δαρεῖος εὖρέ οἱ πλέω ἀγαθὰ τῶν ἁμαρτημάτων πεποιημένα</quote>: <emph>id.</emph> 1. 125 <quote xml:lang="grc">φροντίζων δὲ εὕρισκέ τε</quote> (<emph>v. l.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὑρίσκεται</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα καιριώτατα εἶναι, καὶ ἐποίεε δὴ ταῦτα</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_πάτερ_1179">: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ</quote> here prefaces assent, as <ref n="Soph. Phil. 48"> <title>Ph.</title>48</ref> <ref n="Soph. Phil. 524."> Ph., 524.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταρβῶ_μὲν_1179"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> The sense is: ‘I am alarmed, indeed, at the issue to which you have brought me,—a choice between disobedience, and a blind promise; but I will obey.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγου_τοιάνδε_1179">, ‘such a <emph> situation</emph> in our converse’: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπελθών_1179">, ‘having advanced,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, having been drawn on to it, by the progress of the dialogue. It may be noted that the sing. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγου_1179"> suits this sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">στάσις</quote>: if the meaning had been, ‘<emph> strife</emph> of words’ (the <quote xml:lang="grc">στάσις γλώσσης</quote> of <title>O.T.</title> 634), we should have expected rather the plur. <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγων</quote>, as in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1460">Soph. <title> Ph.</title>1460</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς ἔριν λόγων</quote>. For this general sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">στάσις</quote>, <emph>status</emph>, cp. <title>Phaedr.</title> p. 253 D <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ...ἐν τῇ καλλίονι στάσει ὤν</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔμβαλλε_1181">: see on <ref n="Soph. Phil. 813"> <title>Ph.</title>813</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμβαλλε χειρὸς πίστιν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_τί_1182">: <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote>=‘in your intention’: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1174"> <title>O. T.</title>1174</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς πρὸς τί χρείας;</quote> <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 58">Ph. 58</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πλεῖς δ’ ὡς πρὸς οἶκον.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπιστρέφεις_1182">: the primary notion is that of <emph> turning</emph> some constraining force <emph>upon</emph> a person,—bringing it to bear on him: so, ‘press,’ ‘urge,’ upon him: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπάγεις μοι</quote>. It is a stronger equiv. for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπισκήπτεις</quote>.—Not, ‘regard’ (Musgrave): this would be <quote xml:lang="grc">πίστεως ἐπιστρέφει</quote> (midd.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_θᾶσσον_1183"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote> : <ref n="Soph. Aj. 75"> <title>Ai.</title>75</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ σῖγ’ ἀνέξει μηδὲ δειλίαν ἀρεῖ</quote> <emph>; O.T.</emph> 637 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ εἶ σύ τ’ οἴκους σύ τε, Κρέον, κατὰ στέγας</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ μὴ τὸ μηδὲν ἄλγος εἰς μέγ’ οἴσετε</quote> <emph>;</emph> <ref n="Eur. Ba. 343">Eur. <title>Bacch.</title>343</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ μὴ προσοίσεις χεῖρα, βακχεύσεις δ’ ἰών</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδ’ ἐξομόρξει μωρίαν τὴν σὴν ἐμοί</quote> <emph>; ib.</emph> 792 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ μὴ φρενώσεις μ’, ἀλλὰ δέσμιος φυγὼν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">σώσει τόδ’</quote> <emph>;</emph> For <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ μὴ</quote> with fut. ind., <ref n="Soph. Trach. 978">cp. 978</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἴσεις_1183">, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">χεῖρα δεξιὰν</quote>: but the choice of the verb may have been influenced by <quote xml:lang="grc">πίστιν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄμνυ_κάρα_1185">: so <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀμνύναι θεούς, Στυγὸς ὕδωρ</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 14.271"> <title>Il.</title>14. 271</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">πέδον</quote> ( <ref n="Eur. Med. 746">Eur. <title>Med.</title>746</ref>), etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξειρήσεται_1186">; This is clearly the right punctuation; for Hyllus is most anxious to know <emph>what</emph> will be asked of him. Heracles evades the question by replying, <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ λεχθὲν ἔργον</quote>,—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> , <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃ ἂν λεχθῇ</quote>: just as in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 528"> <title>Ai.</title>528</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐὰν μόνον τὸ ταχθὲν εὖ τολμᾷ τελεῖν</quote>, the partic.=<quote xml:lang="grc">ὃ ἂν ταχθῇ</quote>.—With a full stop at <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξειρήσεται</quote>, the sense would be merely, ‘and this promise shall be given.’ </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπώμοτον_1188"> (427) here = <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρκιον</quote> (schol.), <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ὅρκου ἐγγυητήν</quote> ( <emph>Suid. s. v.</emph>). Cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1324">Soph. <title>Ph.</title>1324</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζῆνα δ’ ὅρκιον καλῶ</quote> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκτὸς_ἔλθοις_1189">, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ ὅρκου</quote> <emph>;</emph> cp. <ref n="Plat. Sym. 183B">Plat. <title>Symp.</title> 183B</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκβάντι τὸν ὅρκον.— <emph>πημονὰς εὔχου λαβεῖν</emph> </quote>: the usual sanction of a solemn oath; cp. <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 12.10">Lys. or. 12 § 10</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤμοσεν ἐξώλειαν ἑαυτῷ καὶ τοῖς παισὶν ἐπαρώμενος, λαβὼν τὸ τάλαντόν με σώσειν</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
ὤμοσεν ἐξώλειαν ἑαυτῷ καὶ τοῖς παισὶν
							ἐπαρώμενος, λαβὼν τὸ τάλαντόν με σώσειν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_λάβω_1190">: for the pause <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1146">cp. 1146</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_πάγον_1191">; cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 489"> <title>Ph.</title>489</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ Χαλκώδοντος Εὐβοίας σταθμά</quote>. The change of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὕψιστον_1191"> to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑψίστου_1191"> is a plausible one. Pausanias mentions statues of <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζεὺς Ὕψιστος</quote> at Corinth (<ref n="Paus. 2.2.8">2. 2. 8</ref>), Olympia (<ref n="Paus. 5.15.5">5. 15. 5</ref>), and Thebes (<ref n="Paus. 9.8.5">9. 8. 5</ref>); the title occurs, too, in an Attic inscr. (<ref> <title>C. I. G.</title> 497—506</ref>), and was frequent in poetry. I prefer, however, to keep the reading of the MSS., because, here, we seem to need an epithet for <quote xml:lang="grc">πάγον</quote> rather than for the god. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 436">Cp. 436</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ κατ’ ἄκρον Οἰταῖον νάπος</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">Διὸς καταστράπτοντος</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θυτήρ_1192"> (613), slightly emphasised by </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_1192">, implies that he is familiar with the place.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σταθεὶς_1192">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 608">cp. 608</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐνταῦθα_1193"> properly refers to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμβαλεῖν</quote> (1197), but, since the inf. is so long delayed, is more conveniently taken with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξάραντα_1193">, in the sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνταυθοῖ</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 380"> <title>El.</title>380</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνταῦθα πέμψειν</quote>. For the sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξάραντα</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 799">cp. 799</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆρον ἔξω</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_1194"> can be prefixed to <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ξὺν οἷς</emph>, κ.τ.λ.</quote>, since </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτόχειρα_1194"> implies <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῖς σεαυτοῦ χερσί</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κείραντα_1195">, like <ref n="Hom. Il. 24.450"> <title>Il.</title>24. 450</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δοῦρ’ ἐλάτης κέρσαντες</quote>. In Attic prose, <quote xml:lang="grc">κείρειν</quote>, ‘to shear,’ is said only of cutting off hair, or devastating land. The prose word here would be <quote xml:lang="grc">κόψαντα.—<emph>ἐκτεμόνθ’</emph> </quote>, cutting it from the stump, close to the ground: <ref n="Hom.
