Il. 12.148"> <title>Il.</title>12. 148</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγνυτον ὕλην</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">πρυμνὴν ἐκτάμνοντες</quote> (‘at the root’). In <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 7.19">Lys. or. 7 § 19</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξέτεμνον τὰ πρέμνα</quote> </cit> refers to cutting the roots of an olive out of the ground.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄγριον_ἔλαιον_1195">: the <quote xml:lang="grc"> κότινος</quote> was also called <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγριος ἔλαιος</quote> ( Pind. fr. 21), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγριέλαιος</quote>, or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγριελαία</quote>. The epithet </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄρσενα_1195"> expresses its sturdy vigour. Acc. to Theophrastus (<title>Hist. Plant.</title> 4. 13) the <quote xml:lang="grc">κότινος</quote> lives longer than the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλαία</quote>. Ovid says, <cit> <quote xml:lang="lat">Ure mares oleas</quote> <bibl n="Ov. Fast. 4. 741">(<title>Fast.</title>4. 741)</bibl> </cit> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολλὸν_1195">=<quote xml:lang="grc">πολύν</quote>, as <ref n="Soph. Ant. 86"> <title>Ant.</title>86</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὸν</quote>=<quote xml:lang="grc">πολύ</quote>: the only instance of this Ionic form in tragedy.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σῶμα_τοὐμὸν_1195"> is repeated, the sentence having become so long: cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">νιν</quote> in 289, after <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκεῖνον</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πευκίνης_1198">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 766">cp. 766</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πιείρας δρυός</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρῆσαι_1198">, made emphatic by place and pause: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 72"> <title>Ant.</title>72</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θάψω.— <emph>γόου … δάκρυ</emph> </quote>, the tear that belongs to, accompanies, lamentation; as <quote xml:lang="grc">δάκρυα</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">γόοι</quote> are so often associated ( <ref n="Eur. Orest. 320">Eur. <title>Or.</title>320</ref>, <title>I. T.</title> 860, etc.). (Not, ‘a mournful tear,’ as opp. to <quote xml:lang="grc">δάκρυ χαρᾶς</quote>.)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰσίτω_1198">, abs., ‘come in,’ ‘find a place’ there: cp. <title>Phaedr.</title> p. 270 A <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ γὰρ ὑψηλόνουν τοῦτο...ἔοικεν ἐντεῦθέν ποθεν εἰσιέν<*></quote>. We ought not to supply <quote xml:lang="grc">σε</quote>, as if the sense were, ‘come into thy thoughts’ ( <title>Phaedo</title>p. 58 E <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε...με...ἔλεος εἰσῄει</quote>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀστένακτος_1200">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1074">cp: 1074</ref>.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴπερ_εἶ_1200"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1158">cp. 1158</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μενῶ_ἐγὼ_1200"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>, ‘I will await thee with my curse’; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘my curse will be in store for thee,’ attending on thee thenceforth. (Not merely, ‘I will await thee in the nether world,’ to punish thee when thou comest <*>hither.) <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1240">Cp. 1240</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θεῶν ἀρὰ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μενεῖ σ’</quote>. So <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1075"> <title>Ant.</title>1075</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> λοχῶσιν...Ἐρινύες.—<emph>ἀραῖος</emph> </quote>, here, ‘<emph>bringing</emph> a curse’: cp. <title>I. T.</title> 778 <quote xml:lang="grc">ῆ σοῖς ἀραία δώμασιν γενήσομαι</quote>. (But in <title> O.T.</title> 1291, ‘under a curse’).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰσαεὶ_1200">, because the power of the Erinyes over a mortal did not end with his life: it was their part, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁμαρτεῖν, ὄφρ’ ἂν</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">γᾶν ὑπέλθῃ· θανὼν δ’ οὐκ ἄγαν ἐλεύθερος</quote>. ( <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 340.">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title>340.</ref>)</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βαρύς_1200">, as in <ref n="Soph. OT 546"> <title>O. T.</title>546</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσμενῆ τε καὶ βαρύν</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_εἶπας_1200"> is supported by the MSS. here, but appears as uncongenial to the poet's style as in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 917"> <title>Ph.</title>917</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴμοι, τί εἶπας</quote> <emph> ;</emph> Here, as there, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_εἶπας_1200_2"> seems inadmissible. It could mean only, ‘What hast thou said of me?’ —and we can hardly justify this as meaning, ‘hast thou said that, if I refuse, I shall be no true son?’ The alternative is to insert </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δ_1200">: cp. <title>O.C.</title> 332 <quote xml:lang="grc">τέκνον, τί δ’ ἦλθες</quote> <emph>;</emph> See Append. on <ref n="Soph. Phil. 100."> <title>Ph.</title>100.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁποῖα_ἐστίν_1204">, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶπον</quote>. The reply passes over <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴργασαι</quote>, and refers to <quote xml:lang="grc"> εἶπας</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 423">cp. 423</ref>, where <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλοῖσιν ἀστῶν</quote> answers the earlier of two queries.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_μή_1204">, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">δράσεις.—<emph>γεν<*>ῦ</emph> </quote>, ‘become’, as if by adoption (<quote xml:lang="grc">εἰσποίησις</quote>) into another family. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 13.91">Lys. or. 13 § 91</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τόν τε γόνῳ πατέρα...τόν τε ποιητὸν πατέρα</quote> </cit>. So Oedipus to Polyneices, <title>O.C.</title> 1383 <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺ δ’ ἔρρ’ ἀπόπτυστός τε κἀπάτωρ ἐμοῦ</quote>. </gloss>

data.quote: τόν τε γόνῳ πατέρα...τόν τε ποιητὸν πατέρα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἷά_ἐκκαλεῖ_1206">, ‘what dost thou call upon me to do.’ For the double acc., cp. <title>Euthyphr.</title> 5 A <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτὰ ταῦτα προκαλεῖσθαι αὐτόν</quote>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παλαμναῖον_1206"> is not weak after </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φονέα_1206">, because, as used in poetry, it often implies the <emph>defilement</emph> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγος</quote>) of blood-guiltiness,—meaning, ‘accursed wretch,’ rather than merely ‘slayer.’ Cp. <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 448">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title>448</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄφθογγον εἶναι τὸν παλαμναῖον νόμος, κ.τ.λ.</quote> Hence, like <quote xml:lang="grc">μιάστωρ</quote>, it can denote also the <emph>avenger</emph> of guilt ( <title>I. T.</title> 1218). Photius had this in view when he explained <quote xml:lang="grc">παλαμναῖος</quote> by <quote xml:lang="grc">φονεὺς ἢ μιαρός</quote>. Properly the word means merely ‘a man of violent hand’: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1206"> <title>Ph.</title>1206</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">παλάμαν</quote>, n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_ἀλλ_1208">: the same formula as in <ref n="Soph. OT 1161"> <title>O. T.</title>1161</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 735."> <title>Ph.</title>735.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧν_ἔχω_1208"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">κακῶν</quote> ) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παιώνιον_1208"> refers more especially to bodily sufferings; while </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰατῆρα_κακῶν_1208"> is rather, ‘physician of my woes’ generally. After <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧν ἔχω, τῶν ἐμῶν</quote> is awkward; but it is partly excused (1) by the slight pause which might follow <quote xml:lang="grc">παιώνιον</quote>, and (2) by the emphasis on <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰατῆρα</quote>. It might, indeed, be suggested that <quote xml:lang="grc">κακῶν</quote> belongs to <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ἐμῶν</quote> only, while <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧν ἔχω</quote> should be taken separately, ‘what I suffer’: this, however, is less natural. Hermann's emendation, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ἔχω_1208"> (‘considering my state’), is possible, but slightly weak. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φοβεῖ_τοῦτο_1211">: cp. <title>O.T.</title> 980 <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺ δ’ εἰς τὰ μητρὸς μὴ φοβοῦ νυμφεύματα</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φθόνησις_1212"> is found only here. Cp. Plat <ref n="Plat. Phaedo 61"> <title>Phaedo</title>61</ref>D <quote xml:lang="grc">ἃ...τυγχάνω ἀκηκοώς, φθόνος οὐδεὶς λέγειν</quote>. <ref n="Plat. Ion 530"> <title>Ion</title>530</ref>D <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ φθονήσεις μοι ἐπιδεῖξαι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλήρωμα_1213"> (nom.), <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">γενήσεται</quote>: —cp. <ref n="Eur. Hec. 574">Eur. <title> Hec.</title>574</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ δὲ πληροῦσιν πυράν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">κορμοὺς φέροντες πευκίνους</quote>. Though <quote xml:lang="grc">πλήρωσις</quote> would have been more natural, <quote xml:lang="grc">πλήρωμα</quote>, expressing the result, is equally correct here.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅσον_γε_1213"> (<quote xml:lang="grc">πληρώσαιμ’</quote> ) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἂν_ποτιψ_1213">.: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 347"> <title>O. T.</title>347</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰργάσθαι θ’, ὅσον</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ χερσὶ καίνων</quote> (<emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶχες εἰργάσθαι</quote>). Hyllus will help to hew the wood, but not to build the pyre. The pyre was kindled by Philoctetes, or, acc. to another version, by Poeas ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 802"> <title>Ph.</title>802</ref> n.).— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποτιψαύων_1213">: tragic lyrics admit <quote xml:lang="grc">ποτί</quote> (fr. 225), and its compounds (1030 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποτίβατος</quote>: <ref n="Aesch. Seven 94">Aesch. <title>Theb.</title>94</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποτιπέσω</quote>, etc.). But tragic dialogue presents no other example, except <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 79">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 79</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποτὶ πτόλιν</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοὐ_καμεῖ_1215">, 2nd pers. sing. midd., thou shalt have no difficulty, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὐμὸν_μέρος_1215">, on my part (acc. of respect: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1062"> <title>Ant.</title>1062</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ σὸν μέρος</quote>, n.).—Most editors take <quote xml:lang="grc">καμεῖ</quote> as 3rd pers. sing. act.: ‘and my part of the work shall not flag.’ But <quote xml:lang="grc">καμοῦμαι</quote> is the regular fut.: indeed, the only trace of the act. form is in Hesych., <quote xml:lang="grc">καμῶ· ἐργάσομαι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρκέσει_ταῦτα_1216">, <emph>even</emph> this: so <ref n="Soph. Phil. 339"> <title>Ph.</title>339</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶμαι μὲν ἀρκεῖν σοί γε καὶ τὰ ς’, ὦ τάλας</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλγήμαθ’.—<emph> πρόσνειμαι</emph> </quote>: the midd. is noteworthy, as we should have expected <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσνειμον</quote>: cp., however, <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 563">Ar. <title>Av.</title>563</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">προσνείμασθαι δὲ πρεπόντως</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖσι θεοῖσιν τῶν ὀρνίθων ὂς ἂν ἁρμόττῃ καθ’ ἕκαστον</quote>,—where, as here, the act. might have been expected. The accentuation </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσνεῖμαι_1216"> (cr. n.) represents a wish to read the aor. inf. act. as an imperative. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βραχεῖαν_1217">, small (<title>O.C.</title> 586 n.): </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μακροῖς_1217">, large ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 130"> <title>Ai.</title>130</ref>, etc.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διδούς_1217">, <emph>sc.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτά</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 475"> <title>O. C.</title>475</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νεοπόκῳ μαλλῷ λαβών</quote> (n.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Εὐρυτείαν_1219">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 267"> <title>O. T.</title>267</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ Λαβδακείῳ παιδί</quote> (n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρθένον_1219">, an unmarried woman: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1225">cp. 1225</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπεικάζειν_1220"> has here much better authority than <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπεικάζειν</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 141">cp. 141</ref> n.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὥς_γ_1220">, as a correction of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὥστ_1220">, is preferable to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_1220">, not only as accounting for <quote xml:lang="grc">τ’</quote>, but because </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμέ_1220"> is added: cp. <ref n="Eur. Alc. 801">Eur. <title>Alc.</title>801</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥς γ’ ἐμοὶ χρῆσθαι κριτῇ</quote>: <title>Plut.</title> 736 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥς γ’ ἐμοὶ δοκεῖν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπισκήπτω_1221"> with double acc., like <quote xml:lang="grc">κελεύω τινά τι</quote>: so <title>I.T.</title> 701 <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς δεξιᾶς δε τῆσδ’ ἐπισκήπτω τάδε</quote>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατρῴων_ὁρκίων_1223">, the oath imposed on thee by thy father. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσθοῦ_1224">, associate with thyself: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 404"> <title>O. C.</title>404</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δάμαρτα_1224">. This passage concerning Iolè and Hyllus (1216—1251) was rendered indispensable by the plot, if the poet was to avoid a contradiction which must otherwise have perplexed the spectators.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλος_σοῦ_1225">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 444"> <title>Ai.</title>444</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἄν τις αὔτ’ ἔμαρψεν ἄλλος ἀντ’ ἐμοῦ.—<emph>ὁμοῦ</emph> </quote>, prep. with dat., in the sense of ‘near’ (<title>O.T.</title> 1007), a specially Attic use ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1218"> <title>Ph.</title>1218</ref>Append.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λάβῃ_1225">, in this command, is clearly right: the mere wish, <quote xml:lang="grc">λάβοι</quote>, would be unsuitable. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 331">Cp. 331</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_αὐτός_1227">, <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>: this third clause reiterates the sense of the first, <quote xml:lang="grc">προσθοῦ δάμαρτα</quote>: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 433">cp. 433</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτο_λέχος_1227">=<quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτο κῆδος</quote>, cogn. acc. to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κήδευσον_1227"> (‘contract this marriage’): cp. <ref n="Aristot. Pol. 5.1307a">Arist. <title> Pol.</title>5. 7. 10</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κηδεύειν ὅτῳ θέλωσιν</quote>.— Not, ‘cherish this bride,’ as in <ref n="Eur. Med. 888">Eur. <title> Med.</title>888</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κηδεύουσαν</quote> is said of Medea ‘tending’ Iason's new wife. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πιθοῦ_1228">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">πείθου</quote>: it is a peremptory summons: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 470">cp. 470</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πιστεύσαντ_1228">=<quote xml:lang="grc"> πιθόμενον</quote>, as in 1251. These are perhaps the only clear examples of <quote xml:lang="grc">πιστεύω</quote> as=‘to obey,’ though <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπιστεῖν</quote> as=<quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπειθεῖν</quote> was frequent.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμοὶ_1228">, not </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοι_1228">, because the pron., though it has no strong emphasis, implies, ‘to me, your father.’ A son's obedience should be complete.— </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σμικροῖς_1228">, dat. of respect: cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 342"> <title>Ph.</title>342</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρᾶγμ’, ὅτῳ σ’ ἐνύβρισαν</quote>: Eur. fr. 1051 <quote xml:lang="grc">χρήμασιν λελείμμεθα.—<emph>συγχεῖ</emph> </quote>, obliterates, as if it had been traced in sand: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 609">Soph. <title>O. C.</title>609</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_νοσοῦντι_1230"> <quote xml:lang="grc"> κ.τ.λ.</quote> <ref n="Soph. Trach. 543">Cp. 543</ref> ff. <quote xml:lang="grc">θυμοῦσθαι μὲν οὐκ ἐπίσταμαι</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">νοσοῦντι κείνῳ</quote>... | <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ δ’ αὖ ξυνοικεῖν τῇδ’ ὁμοῦ τίς ἂν γυνὴ</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc"> δύναιτο...<emph>;</emph>—<emph>ὧδε … φρονοῦντα</emph> </quote>, in a state of mind so deplorable as is argued by the bare suggestion of such a marriage. This is not an ‘aside’; but the speaker's amazement precludes a direct reply. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ἐργασείων_1232">: cp. <title>O.T.</title> 625 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς οὐχ ὑπείξων οὐδὲ πιστεύσων λέγεις</quote> <emph>;</emph> For the desiderative verb, see <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1001"> <title>Ph.</title>1001</ref> n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίς_ποθ_1233">, the indignant exordium, is immediately followed by the relative clause concerning Iolè, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥ_1233"> having a causal force,—as we might say, ‘What! <emph>when</emph> she...’ etc. Cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. OC 263"> <title>O. C.</title>263</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἄμοιγε ποῦ ταῦτ’ ἐστίν<emph>;</emph> οἵτινες βάθρων κ.τ.λ.</quote> Then, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ταύτην</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">τήνδε</quote>, as antecedent to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥ_1233_2">, the speaker bitterly says, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταῦτ_1233">, ‘all this,’—the horrors which, for him, are embodied in Iolè. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1492"> <title>O. T.</title>1492</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἡνίκ’ ἂν δὴ πρὸς γάμων ἥκητ ἀκμάς</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς οὗτος ἔσται, τίς παραρρίψει, τέκνα</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιαῦτ’ ὀνείδη λαμβάνων</quote> <emph>;</emph> It would miserably enfeeble the passage to alter </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταῦτ_1233_2">.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοι_1233"> implies, ‘as I have seen.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θανεῖν_1233">, without <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1173"> <title>Ant.</title>1173</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἴτιοι θανεῖν</quote> (n.): </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόνη_μεταίτιος_1233"> means that she alone shared the blame with Heracles (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 260">cp. 260</ref> n.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοὶ_δ_1233"> is more probable than </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοί_τ_1233"> here, where the antithesis is marked.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ἔχειν_1233">: <ref n="Dem. 3.8">Dem. or. 3 § 8</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐχόντων ὡς ἔχουσι Θηβαίων</quote>: <ref n="Soph. OC 273">Soph. <title>O. C.</title>273</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱκόμην ἵν’ ἱκόμην</quote>: <title>O.T.</title> 13<*> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅστις_νοσοῖ_1233">: the optat., on a<*> count of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕλοιτ’ ἄν</quote>: the relative clause is equiv. in sense to a protasis, <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ μὴ νοσοῖ</quote>: see on <ref n="Soph. OC 560."> <title>O. C.</title>560.</ref>For the form <quote xml:lang="grc"> νοσοῖ</quote> (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">νοσοίη</quote>), <ref n="Soph. Phil. 895"> <title>Ph.</title>895</ref> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλαστόρων_1233">: <ref n="Soph. OC 788"> <title>O. C.</title>788</ref> n. Such a marriage would imply that some <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄτη</quote> had deranged his mind ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 622"> <title>Ant.</title>622</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖσιν_ἐχθίστοισι_1237">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 366"> <title>O. T.</title>366</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς φιλτάτοις</quote> (Iocasta): <ref n="Soph. El. 594"> <title>El.</title>594</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐχθροῖς γαμεῖσθαι</quote> (Aegisthus).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁμοῦ_1237"> added to <quote xml:lang="grc"> συνναίειν</quote>, as in 545 to <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυνοικεῖν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_νεμεῖν_1238">, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ νεμεῖ</quote>. The verb which ought to have been principal is attracted into the relative clause. Cp. <ref n="Hdt. 4. 5">Her.4. 5</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς δὲ Σκύθαι λέγουσι, νεώτατον ἁπάντων ἐθνέων εἶναι</quote> (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστὶ</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ σφέτερον</quote>. <ref n="Hdt. 6. 137">Id. 6. 137</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς δὲ αὐτοὶ Ἀθηναῖοι λέγουσι, δικαίως ἐξελάσαι</quote> (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξήλασαν</quote>). <title>Sophist.</title> 263 D <quote xml:lang="grc">παντάπασιν, ὡς ἔοικεν, ἡ τοιαύτη σύνθεσις...γίγνεσθαι</quote> (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">γίγνεται</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγος ψευδής</quote>. <ref n="Plat. Phileb. 20"> <title>Phileb.</title>20</ref>D <quote xml:lang="grc">τόδε γε μήν, ὡς οἶμαι, περὶ αὐτοῦ ἀναγκαιότατον εἶναι</quote> (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστὶ</quote>) <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγειν</quote> [for <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶναι</quote> can hardly depend on the word <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνάγκη</quote> higher up]. <title>I. T.</title> 52 <quote xml:lang="grc">καθεῖναι</quote> (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc"> καθῆκε</quote>) after <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἔδοξε</quote>. But <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 188">Aesch. <title>Pers.</title>188</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτω στάσιν τιν’, ὡς ἐγὼ )δόκουν ὁρᾶν</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">τεύχειν</quote>, is more complex, as the fusion is between (1) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐδόκουν</quote> (3<emph>rd plur.</emph> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">τεύχειν</quote>, and (2) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔτευχον, ὡς ἐγὼ ἐδόκουν ὁρᾶν</quote>. In Latin, too, this natural laxity occurs: <title> Offic.</title> 1. 7 § 22 <emph>ut placet Stoicis</emph>, <emph>quae in terris gignantur ad usum hominum omnia creari</emph> (instead of <emph> creantur</emph>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_1238"> exclamatory (‘how!’): but this is impossible. The text is clearly sound, though it has been much suspected (cr. n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νεμεῖν_μοῖραν_1238">: <quote xml:lang="grc"> μοῖρα</quote> is the share of respect due to a person: cp. <ref n="Plat. Crat. 398C">Plat. <title>Crat.</title> 398C</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">μεγάλην μοῖραν καὶ τιμὴν ἔχει</quote>: and <ref n="Soph. OC 277">Soph. <title>O. C.</title>277</ref>Append. For <quote xml:lang="grc">νεμεῖν</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 57">cp. 57</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νέμοι...ὤραν</quote>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεῶν_ἀρὰ_1238"> (like <quote xml:lang="grc">θεῶν Ἐρινυες</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1075"> <title>Ant.</title>1075</ref>), —the vengeance sent by the gods, in answer to the father's imprecation (1202). In this objective sense, the Curse is itself the agent of retribution: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 418"> <title>O. T.</title>418</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινόπους ἀρά</quote> : <ref n="Aesch. Seven 70">Aesch. <title>Theb.</title>70</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀρά τ’, Ἐρινὺς πατρὸς ἡ μεγασθενής</quote>: and the Eumenides call themselves <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀραί</quote> ( <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 417">Aesch. <title>Eum.</title>417</ref>). Sometimes, again, the <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀρά</quote> is distinguished from the power which it calls into action: <ref n="Soph. El. 111">Soph. <title> El.</title>111</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πότνἰ Ἀρά</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">σεμναί τε θεῶν παῖδες Ἐρινύες</quote>. A transition from the latter idea to the former may be seen in <ref n="Soph. OC 1375">Soph. <title>O. C.</title>1375</ref> f., where Oed. summons his own imprecations to be his <quote xml:lang="grc"> ξυμμάχους</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ἔοικας_1241"> (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἔοικεν</quote>), as in <ref n="Soph. El. 516"> <title>El.</title>516</ref>: so <title>Helen.</title> 497 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς εἴξασιν.—<emph>φράσεις</emph> </quote>, wilt ‘show,’ ‘make it clear’ (by acts, or words, or both). After such words—Hyllus means—some violent outburst of madness may be expected. <quote xml:lang="grc">φράζειν</quote>, ‘to declare,’ does not necessarily imply speech: <ref n="Hdt. 4. 113">Her.4. 113</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φωνῆσαι μὲν οὐκ εἶχε, οὐ γὰρ συνίεσαν ἀλλήλων, τῇ δὲ χειρὶ ἔφραζε</quote>. <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1061">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1061</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺ δ’ ἀντὶ φωνῆς φράζε καρβάνῳ χερί</quote>. There is a shade of mournful irony in <quote xml:lang="grc">φράσεις</quote> (‘make it plain enough’), which is lost in the conjecture </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φανεῖς_1241">.—For the double <quote xml:lang="grc"> ‐εις</quote>, cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 682">Soph. <title>Ant.</title>682</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧν λέγεις δοκεῖς πέρι</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπ_κακοῦ_1242">, lit., ‘from a lulled plague,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, from the repose allowed to me by its subsidence. This is simpler than to understand, ‘after the plague had been lulled,’ when <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπὸ</quote> would be used like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</quote> (Theocr. 15. 106 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀθανάταν ἀπὸ θνατᾶς</quote>).—The <emph>v. l.</emph> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπευνασθέντος_1242"> would be rather a gen. absolute. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπευνάζειν</quote> does not occur. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐς_πολλὰ_1243">, ‘with regard to’ them: cp. n. on 1211. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλύειν_1244">: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, as to the marriage. The question as to the pyre has been settled (1215). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δυσσεβεῖν_1245">. He regards Iolè as virtually the destroyer of both his parents (1233): it is not <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐσεβές</quote> for him to marry her. Heracles replies that the <emph>supreme</emph> duty is to do a father's pleasure (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 1177">cp. 1177</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πράσσειν_πανδίκως_1247">, to do these things ‘with full justification,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, as a duty imposed by a father's solemn command.—Others explain, ‘command me absolutely’: but <quote xml:lang="grc">πανδίκως</quote> could not mean <quote xml:lang="grc">παντελῶς</quote>: <ref target="cl611">see on 611</ref>.—For the place of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὖν_1247"> in the verse, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1205"> <title>O. C.</title>1205</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 121."> <title>Ph.</title>121.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔγωγε_1248">, ‘that I do’; cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 104"> <title>Ai.</title>104</ref> <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1347"> Ai., 1347</ref> <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1365."> Ai., 1365.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποήσω_1249">. He will marry Iolè. But he will call the gods to witness that it is his father's doing, and not an act of his own choice.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_ἔργον_1249">: the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔργον</quote> is not, strictly, the marriage itself, but the act of Heracles in prescribing it. Hence the words, ‘showing <emph>thy deed</emph> to the gods,’ mean properly, ‘pleading, before the gods, the constraint which you have put upon me’: not, ‘protesting that my act in marrying Iolè is really your act.’ Accordingly we have <quote xml:lang="grc">δεικνὺς τὸ σὸν ἔ<*>ον</quote>, not <quote xml:lang="grc">δεικνὺς τὸ ἔργον σὸν</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc"> ὄν</quote>). The text has been suspected (cr. n.) only because it has not been fully understood. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καλῶς_τελευτᾷς_1252">, after threatening disobedience (1230 ff.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀπὶ_τοῖσδε_1252">: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, crown the promise with the deed. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 813"> <title>Ai.</title>813</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κοὐ λόγῳ δείξω μόνον·</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">τάχος γὰρ ἔργου καὶ ποδῶν ἅμ’ ἕψεται.— <emph>ταχεῖαν</emph> </quote>, adverbial: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 617"> <title>O. T.</title>617</ref> f.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὶν_ἐμπεσεῖν_1252"> <foreign xml:lang="grc"> κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: his fear is not so much of the pain, or of increased difficulty for his bearers, but rather of the illomened cries which would be wrung from him on the brink of death. <ref target="cl1260">See on 1260</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σπαραγμὸν_1252"> (778)</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἶστρον_1252">: a similar combination occurs in Aesch. fr. 163 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ ποδῶν δ’ ἄνω</quote> | <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπέρχεται σπαραγμὸς εἰς ἄκρον κάρα</quote>, | <quote xml:lang="grc">κέντημα λύσσης, σκορπίου βέλος λέγω</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄγ_1255"> is said to all the bystanders, rather than to Hyllus alone: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 821">cp. 821</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴδ’</quote>, n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγκονεῖτ_1255">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 811"> <title>Ai.</title>811</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">χωρῶμεν, ἐγκονῶμεν</quote> : <emph>ib.</emph> 988 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴθ’ ἐγκόνει, σύγκαμνε</quote>. The derivation of the verb is uncertain: the only part of it used by Homer is <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγκονέουσαι.—<emph>αἴρεσθε</emph> </quote>: this literal sense of the midd. <quote xml:lang="grc">αἴρομαι</quote> is much rarer than the figurative; see, however, <ref n="Soph. El. 54"> <title>El.</title>54</ref>, <ref n="Hom. Il. 20.247"> <title>Il.</title>20. 247</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Cycl. 473.">Eur. <title> Cycl.</title>473.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὕτη_1255">, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc"> τοῦτο</quote>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 88"> <title>O. C.</title>88</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταύτην ἔλεξε παῦλαν</quote> (n.). He does not mean, ‘this is the rest promised by the oracle’ (1170): but merely,—‘this is the true release for me.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τελευτὴ_ὑστάτη_1255">, like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔσχατον τέρμα</quote> ( <ref n="Eur. Andr. 1081"> Eur. <title>Andr.</title>1081</ref>), <emph>extremus finis</emph> ( <title> G.</title> 4. 116). He has no presentiment of immortality. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_1257">: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1179">cp. 1179</ref> n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδὲν_εἴργει_1257"> like <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν κωλύει</quote>. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 344">Cp. 344</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τελειοῦσθαι_1257">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1089"> <title>O. C.</title>1089</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τελειῶσαι</quote>: but <ref n="Soph. El. 1510"> <title>El.</title>1510</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τελεωθέν</quote>. Both forms were current in Attic prose.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπεὶ_κελεύεις_1257">: he again disclaims responsibility: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1247">cp. 1247</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄγε_νυν_1259">. Either <quote xml:lang="grc">νυν</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">νυν</quote> would be fitting here, but the former is better: it refers to the consent of Hyllus. The scholiast read <quote xml:lang="grc">νυν</quote> (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγε οὖν</quote>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὶν_νόσον_1260">, ‘before thou hast aroused this plague,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘allowed it to arise,’—by <emph>delay.</emph> The attacks recur at intervals; and he wishes to reach the pyre speedily (1253). The meaning is not that vehement laments might bring on the pain.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_σκληρά_1260">: this phrase has a bad sense in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1361"> <title>Ai.</title>1361</ref>; just as the epithet <quote xml:lang="grc">κρατερόφρων</quote>, given to Heracles in <ref n="Hom. Il. 14.324"> <title>Il.</title>14. 324</ref>, is applied in <ref n="Hes. WD 147">Hes. <title>Op.</title>147</ref> to the <quote xml:lang="grc">χάλκειον γένος</quote>.—Cp. <ref n="Hom. Od. 20.18"> Hom. <title>Od.</title>20. 18</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τέτλαθι δὴ κραδίη</quote>: Aristophanes parodies such passages, <ref n="Aristoph. Ach. 483"> <title>Ach.</title>483</ref> ff. <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόβαινε νῦν, ὦ θυμέ...ἄγε νυν, ὦ τάλαινα καρδία</quote>: as Voltaire said of like apostrophes in Corneille, ‘nous ne sommes plus dans un temps où l'on parle à son bras et à son âme.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χάλυβος_στόμιον_1260">. This has been explained:—(1) ‘A curb of steel, set with sharp stones’—to make it more severe. (2) ‘A curb of steel, ornamented with costly stones.’ (3) ‘A steel <emph>clamp</emph> for binding stones together.’ See Appendix.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνάπαυε_βοήν_1260">. Pythagoras said that a dying man,—like one who is putting out to sea,—should avoid words of ill omen: —<quote xml:lang="grc">κατὰ τὸν ὕστατον καιρὸν παρήγγελλε μὴ βλασφημεῖν, ἀλλ’ ὥσπερ ἐν ταῖς ἀναγωγαῖσοἰωνίζεσθαι μετ’ εὐφημίας</quote> (Iamblich. <title> Pyth.</title> § 257).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_1260"> with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τελέους_1260"> (fut. part.), marking the intention (<ref n="Soph. Trach. 160">cp. 160</ref> ): </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπίχαρτον_1260">, predicative: (‘as being about to do a compulsory deed with a feeling of joy.’) The end has been imposed by fate; but it brings victory over pain. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀπαδοί_1264">, ‘attendants,’ ‘followers,’—the men who have come with him from Euboea (964). In <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1108"> <title>Ant.</title>1108</ref>Creon addresses his servants as <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀπάονες. —<emph>μεγάλην μὲν ἐμοὶ</emph> κ.τ.λ.</quote>: the meaning is, ‘Pardon <emph>me</emph> for helping my father to destroy himself; and note that the real cruelty here is that of <title>Zeus</title>, who allows his son to perish thus.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συγγνωμοσύνην_1264"> (=<quote xml:lang="grc"> συγγνώμην</quote>) a word found only here; cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 151"> <title>Ant.</title>151</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θέσθαι</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">λησμοσύναν.—<emph>εἰδότες θεῶν μεγ. ἀγνωμοσύνην ἔργων</emph> κ.τ.λ.</quote>, ‘recognising the great harshness of the gods in the deeds,’ etc.: for the double gen., cp. Andoc. or. 3 § 33 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν ... ἀσφάλειαν ἡμῶν τῆς ἐπαναφορᾶς</quote>. For <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγνωμοσύνη</quote>, prop. ‘want of considerateness,’ cp. <ref n="Dem. 18.252">Dem. or. 18 § 252</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πανταχόθεν μὲν ἄν τις ἴδοι τὴν ἀγνωμοσύνην αὐτοῦ καὶ τὴν βασκανίαν</quote> (‘unfairness and malevolence’): <emph>ib.</emph> 207 <quote xml:lang="grc">τῇ τῆς τύχης ἀγνωμοσύνῃ</quote>, its ‘cruelty.’ The likesounding words end two successive verses, as <quote xml:lang="grc"> παρῄνεσα</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">συνῄνεσα</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 121">Soph. <title>Ph.</title>121</ref> f.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλῃζόμενοι_1268"> is more than <quote xml:lang="grc">καλούμενοι</quote>: it implies invocation and praise: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 659">cp. 659</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐφορῶσι_1268">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, look calmly down upon them; just as in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 825"> <title>El.</title> 825</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτ’ ἐφορῶντες | κρύπτουσιν ἕκηλοι</quote> </cit>: cp. <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1270">Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1270</ref> (Cassandra complains of Apollo) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐποπτεύσας</quote>... | ...<quote xml:lang="grc">καταγελωμένην</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_μέλλοντ_1274"> <quote xml:lang="grc"> κ.τ.λ.</quote>: Hyllus means:—‘No one, indeed, commands a view (<quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφορᾷ</quote>) of the <emph>future</emph> (and so it is possible that Zeus may yet make some amend); but, as to the <emph> present</emph> situation, it is miserable for us, shameful for Zeus (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκείνοις_1274">), and supremely cruel for the victim.’</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χαλεπώτατα_οὖν_1274">. Here <quote xml:lang="grc">δ’ οὖν</quote> marks the return to the foremost subject of his thoughts. ‘Be the pity or the shame what it may, there can be no doubt who <emph>suffers</emph> most.’ Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 688"> <title>Ant.</title> 688</ref> n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρθέν_1275">, compare <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ παρθένοι</quote> in <ref n="Soph. Trach. 211">211</ref>. The Chorus has been silent since <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1113">1113</ref>; and it seems dramatically right that its silence should be maintained in this last scene. The young maidens of Trachis may well leave the son of Heracles, at this solemn moment, to sum up the lesson of his father's fate.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπ_οἴκων_1275"> (see cr. n.) is clearly right. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ</quote> is often thus used with the gen., of position: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Charm. 163b">Plat. <title>Charm.</title> 163 B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπ’ οἰκήματος καθημένῳ</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 4.118">Thuc. 4. 118</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μένειν ἐπὶ τῆς αὑτῶν</quote> </cit>.—The Vulg. <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ἀπ’</emph> οἴκων</quote> has been explained in three ways, each of which appears untenable:—(1) ‘Do not stay behind,—leaving the house,’ <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘leave it.’ (2) ‘Do not fail from the house,’—<foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, ‘stay there.’ (3) With <quote xml:lang="grc">παρθέν’</quote>: ‘O maiden from the house, do not stay behind.’ </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θανάτους_1276">, Deianeira's violent death (for the plur., cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 497"> <title>O. T.</title> 497</ref> n.). The bold use of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μεγάλους_1276"> is softened by the poetical plur., which brings out the notion of a ‘great’ or ‘awful’ <emph>calamity.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέγας</quote> is often nearly equivalent to <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινός</quote>, as in <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέγα τι παθεῖν</quote> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 5.8.17">(Xen. <title>An.</title> 5. 8. 17)</bibl> </cit>, etc.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πήματα_καινοπαθῆ_1276">, the strange and terrible sufferings of Heracles. This adjdoes not occur elsewhere: but <ref n="Aesch. Seven 363">Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 363</ref> has <quote xml:lang="grc">καινοπήμων</quote> as=‘new to woe.’ (For the bad sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">καινός</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 867">cp. 867</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 873">873</ref>.) The second part of the compound is akin in sense to <quote xml:lang="grc">πῆμα</quote>: <ref target="cl756">cp. 756 n.</ref> —The <foreign xml:lang="lat">v. l.</foreign> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καινοπαγῆ_1276"> was a prosaic conjecture, suggested by such words as <quote xml:lang="grc">νεοπαγής</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ζεύς_1278"> the schol. supplies <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπραξεν</quote>: but it seems truer to supply <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστίν</quote>. ‘There is nothing in all this that <emph>is not</emph> Zeus’: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>, he is <emph> manifested</emph> in each and all of these events. </gloss>
